topics.title: Multithreading Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT741 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Multithreading Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Threads and Multithreading

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **Real Time Operating Systems**, a **thread** (also called a *lightweight process* — **LWP**) is the smallest unit of CPU execution that is scheduled by the operating system kernel. A thread is a basic unit of CPU utilization; it comprises a **thread ID**, a **program counter (PC)**, a **register set**, and a **stack**, all of which share the same **code section, data section, and other operating-system resources** (such as open files and signals) with other threads belonging to the same process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim for exam writing):**
> *"A thread is a flow of control within a process. A process may consist of multiple threads, each executing a different part of the program code, while sharing the same address space and global data."*

A traditional (or **heavyweight**) **process** is a single thread of control. **Multithreading** is the ability of an operating system kernel (or a user-level thread library) to support **multiple, concurrent threads of execution within a single process**.

| Term | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **Process** | An executing program with its own memory address space |
| **Thread** | A lightweight execution unit inside a process |
| **Multithreading** | Multiple threads executing within one process |
| **LWP (Lightweight Process)** | A kernel-supported thread that can be scheduled independently |
| **PCB** | Process Control Block — heavy context |
| **TCB** | Thread Control Block — lightweight context |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Threads vs Process

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen 🍳**
> Imagine a **restaurant kitchen** as a single **process**. The kitchen has one set of shared resources — a common pantry (heap memory), shared recipes (code section), and shared cooking tools (open files, global variables).
>
> A **single-threaded** kitchen is like having **one chef** working alone — he does prep, cooks, plates, and serves, all sequentially. A **multi-threaded** kitchen is like having **3–4 chefs** working in that same kitchen — they share the same pantry, the same fridge, and the same recipes, but each chef can independently work on a different dish (soup, salad, main course) **simultaneously**.
>
> If a new chef is hired, he does **not** need his own fridge or pantry — he just needs his own **apron, knife, and cutting board** (his own stack, registers, and program counter). This is exactly how threads share the process's resources but maintain their own execution context.

## 1.3 Multithreading in the RTOS Context

In **Real-Time Operating Systems** (RTOS), multithreading is not just a performance optimization — it is a **fundamental requirement**. An RTOS application typically has:

- A **control thread** (acquiring sensor data)
- A **processing thread** (running control algorithms)
- A **communication thread** (handling network / serial I/O)
- A **display / UI thread**
- A **watchdog / monitor thread**

Each of these must meet **hard or soft timing deadlines**. The RTOS kernel schedules these threads using **priority-based preemptive scheduling** (e.g., Rate Monotonic Scheduling, Earliest Deadline First) to ensure deadlines are met.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In an RTOS, threads are usually referred to as **TASKS**. A task is essentially a thread in the RTOS terminology. Examples: FreeRTOS uses `xTaskCreate()`, VxWorks uses `taskSpawn()`, POSIX uses `pthread_create()`.

## 1.4 Motivation for Multithreading

- **Responsiveness**: A long-running task in one thread does not block the responsiveness of the user interface in another thread.
- **Resource Sharing**: Threads share the memory and resources of the process by default — no explicit IPC (inter-process communication) needed.
- **Economy**: Creating / context-switching threads is much cheaper than creating / context-switching processes. Typical cost: **thread creation ≈ 10–30× cheaper** than process creation.
- **Scalability**: On multicore / multiprocessor systems, threads can run in true parallel on different cores.
- **Real-time determinism**: In an RTOS, multithreading enables concurrent handling of multiple real-time activities with strict deadlines.

## 1.5 Key Differences: Process vs Thread

| Feature | Process | Thread |
|---------|---------|--------|
| Memory address space | Own, isolated | Shared within the process |
| Creation cost | High | Low (≈ 1/30 of process) |
| Context-switch cost | High | Low |
| Communication | IPC (pipes, sockets, shared memory) | Direct — shared variables |
| Crash scope | Crashes only that process | Crashes the **entire process** (all threads) |
| Scheduling entity | Heavyweight | Lightweight (TCB) |
| Resource ownership | Yes | Shares process resources |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Thread — Thread Control Block (TCB)

Each thread has its own **Thread Control Block (TCB)**, maintained by the kernel. The TCB contains:

- **Thread Identifier (TID)**
- **Program Counter (PC)**
- **Register set / CPU registers**
- **Stack pointer and stack**
- **Priority (RTOS-specific)**
- **Thread-local storage pointer**
- **Signal mask (POSIX)**
- **State (Ready, Running, Blocked, Terminated)**
- **Pointer to the parent PCB**

> [!NOTE]
> **Resource ownership belongs to the PROCESS, not the thread.** The PCB (Process Control Block) holds code section, data section, heap, open file table, signal handlers, etc. The TCB holds only the per-thread execution context.

## 2.2 Types of Threads

### (a) User-Level Threads (ULT)

- Thread management (creation, scheduling, synchronization) is done by a **thread library in user space**, without kernel intervention.
- The kernel sees only the single underlying process.
- **Pros**: Very fast context switch (no kernel mode switch), flexible scheduling.
- **Cons**: If one ULT makes a **blocking system call**, the **entire process blocks** (all ULTs freeze). Cannot leverage multicore parallelism in pure form.

### (b) Kernel-Level Threads (KLT)

- Thread management is done **directly by the kernel**. The kernel maintains TCBs for each thread.
- **Pros**: True parallelism on multicore; one thread blocking does not block the whole process; kernel can schedule threads independently.
- **Cons**: Slower context switch (kernel-mode transition); higher overhead per thread.

## 2.3 Multithreading Models

The relationship between **user threads** and **kernel threads** is described by the three classical multithreading models:

### Model 1: Many-to-One (N:1)
- **Many** user-level threads map to **one** kernel thread.
- Thread management is in user space.
- **Disadvantage**: Entire process blocks on any blocking syscall. **No true parallelism on multicore.**
- Example: **GNU Pth (GNU Portable Threads)**.

### Model 2: One-to-One (1:1)
- **Each** user thread maps to **one** kernel thread.
- Provides **true concurrency** on multicore.
- **Disadvantage**: Creating a user thread requires creating a kernel thread → overhead. Most systems impose a thread-per-process limit.
- Examples: **Windows XP/2000+, Linux, Solaris 9+**.

### Model 3: Many-to-Many (M:N)
- **M** user threads multiplexed onto **N** kernel threads, where `M ≥ N`.
- Combines benefits of both models. Developers can create as many ULTs as needed; kernel threads can run in parallel.
- Examples: **Solaris (before 9), Windows ThreadFiber**, also some research systems (e.g., **Scheduler Activations**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Frequently Asked:** The **one-to-one model** is the **most widely used** in modern operating systems (Linux, Windows, modern macOS) and is the **default** for RTOS implementations.

## 2.4 Thread Libraries

A **thread library** provides the API for programmers to create and manage threads. Three main approaches:

| Library | Type | Platform |
|---------|------|----------|
| **POSIX Pthreads** | Standardized API (`pthread.h`) | IEEE 1003.1c — Linux, UNIX, macOS, VxWorks |
| **Win32 Threads** | Kernel-only, 1:1 | Windows |
| **Java Threads** | Built-in (uses JNI; 1:1 on most JVMs) | JVM |

> [!NOTE]
> In RTOS, the most common thread library is **POSIX Pthreads (1003.1c)**. VxWorks, QNX, RTEMS, FreeRTOS+POSIX all support a subset of Pthreads. Embedded RTOSs like FreeRTOS, Micrium, and RTX use their own proprietary task APIs.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Notes |
|---------|------------------|-------|
| Threads per process | `T_p = M` (model-dependent) | For 1:1, M = N = kernel threads |
| Multicore speedup bound (Amdahl) | `S = 1 / ((1 - P) + P / N)` | Where `P` = parallel fraction, `N` = cores |
| Context-switch time | `T_cs (thread) ≈ T_cs (process) / k`, with `k ≈ 5–10` | Threads ≈ 5–10× faster than process |
| Thread creation cost | `T_create (thread) ≈ T_create (process) / 30` | Empirical |
| CPU utilization (multithreaded) | `U = 1 - P^N` | Probability of all `N` threads idle |
| Priority Inversion bound | `B_{pi} ≤ Σ (CS execution times)` | Priority Inheritance Protocol |
| Scheduling latency | `t_slack = D_i - C_i - t_overhead` | Deadline − computation − overhead |
| CPU utilization (Rate Monotonic, n tasks) | `U ≤ n(2^{1/n} - 1)` | Liu & Layland bound |

> [!WARNING]
> For inline expressions in table cells, we have used the **LaTeX alternative symbols** `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Always do this in your notes.

## 2.6 Thread Lifecycle States

A thread, like a process, cycles through several states during its lifetime:

1. **New (Created)** — thread object exists but not yet scheduled.
2. **Ready** — waiting for CPU.
3. **Running** — currently executing on a CPU core.
4. **Blocked (Waiting)** — waiting for I/O, a lock, or an event.
5. **Terminated (Dead)** — execution finished; TCB can be reclaimed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In an **RTOS**, the **Ready** and **Blocked** states are the most performance-critical. The dispatcher must move a thread from Ready → Running in **bounded time** (often microseconds). The scheduling algorithm (e.g., Rate Monotonic, EDF) chooses which Ready thread to run next.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Multithreading Use Case |
|--------------------|-------------------------|
| **Automotive ECUs (AUTOSAR)** | Engine control + brake control + steering in separate tasks |
| **Aerospace (DO-178C)** | Flight control loop, sensor fusion, communication |
| **Industrial Robotics** | Path planning + servo control + safety monitor |
| **Medical Devices (FDA)** | Drug delivery + monitoring + alarm in real time |
| **Smartphones** | UI thread + audio + network + camera threads |
| **Embedded IoT** | Sensor reading + actuation + BLE in one MCU |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Amdahl's Law for Multithreaded Speedup (RTOS-relevant derivation)

When a multithreaded program runs on a multicore / SMP system, the theoretical **speedup** is bounded by the **parallelizable fraction** of the program. **Amdahl's Law** gives the maximum achievable speedup.

### Derivation

Let:
- `P` = fraction of the program that can be parallelized (0 ≤ P ≤ 1)
- `1 - P` = fraction that must remain serial
- `N` = number of CPU cores (or kernel threads in the 1:1 model)

The total execution time on `N` processors is:

$$
T(N) = (1 - P) \cdot T(1) + \frac{P \cdot T(1)}{N}
$$

Dividing both sides by `T(1)` gives the normalized time:

$$
\frac{T(N)}{T(1)} = (1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}
$$

The **speedup** `S(N)` is the reciprocal of normalized time:

$$
S(N) = \frac{T(1)}{T(N)} = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}
$$

**Step-by-step logical transition:**

1. Start with sequential execution time `T(1)`.
2. The serial part takes `(1 - P)·T(1)` regardless of N.
3. The parallel part takes `P·T(1) / N` since it divides across N cores.
4. Total parallel time is the **sum** of serial + parallel portions.
5. Speedup = Sequential time / Parallel time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limiting case**: As `N → ∞`, `S(N) → 1 / (1 - P)`. So even with infinite cores, the speedup is capped by the serial fraction. For an RTOS control loop, if 20% is serial I/O handling, the maximum speedup is `1 / 0.20 = 5×`, **no matter how many cores** you add.

### Worked Example (Numerical)

Suppose a real-time application has `P = 0.90` (90% parallel, 10% serial) running on `N = 4` cores.

$$
S(4) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.90) + \dfrac{0.90}{4}}
$$

$$
S(4) = \frac{1}{0.10 + 0.225} = \frac{1}{0.325} \approx 3.08
$$

So a 4-core system gives only **~3.08× speedup**, not 4×, because of the 10% serial bottleneck.

## 3.2 Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) — Utilization Bound

The **Liu & Layland (1973)** utilization bound for `n` periodic tasks scheduled by **Rate Monotonic Scheduling** is:

$$
U_{bound}(n) = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)
$$

**Derivation sketch:**

1. Each task `i` has period `T_i` and worst-case execution time `C_i`. Utilization is `U_i = C_i / T_i`.
2. RM assigns priorities inversely with period: shorter period → higher priority.
3. Liu & Layland proved that if total utilization `U = Σ U_i ≤ U_bound(n)`, then the task set is **guaranteed schedulable** under RM.
4. As `n → ∞`, the bound converges to `ln 2 ≈ 0.693` (~69.3%).

**Step-by-step demonstration for n = 2:**

$$
U_{bound}(2) = 2 \left( 2^{1/2} - 1 \right) = 2 \left( \sqrt{2} - 1 \right)
$$

$$
U_{bound}(2) = 2 \times (1.4142 - 1) = 2 \times 0.4142 = 0.8284
$$

So for 2 tasks, RMS guarantees schedulability up to **82.83%** CPU utilization.

**Asymptotic limit:**

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} U_{bound}(n) = \lim_{n \to \infty} n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)
$$

Using the limit identity $\lim_{x \to 0} (2^x - 1)/x = \ln 2$:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} U_{bound}(n) = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931
$$

## 3.3 Thread Creation Overhead Estimation

The **context-switch time** of a thread vs. a process is approximated by the following:

$$
T_{cs}^{thread} = T_{cs}^{process} \cdot \frac{S_{TCB}}{S_{PCB}}
$$

Where `S_TCB` and `S_PCB` are the average sizes of the TCB and PCB respectively. Typical values:

$$
S_{TCB} \approx 1 \text{ KB}, \quad S_{PCB} \approx 5\text{–}10 \text{ KB}
$$

Therefore:

$$
T_{cs}^{thread} \approx T_{cs}^{process} \times 0.1 \text{ to } 0.2
$$

This justifies the rule of thumb: **thread context switch is ~5–10× faster** than process context switch.

## 3.4 CPU Utilization with n Independent Threads

If we have `n` independent threads (or tasks) with each being active (Ready) with probability `p`, the probability that **all** threads are idle is:

$$
P_{all\_idle} = (1 - p)^n
$$

Therefore the CPU utilization (the probability that **at least one** thread is active) is:

$$
U = 1 - (1 - p)^n
$$

**Example**: With 4 threads each active 50% of the time:

$$
U = 1 - (0.5)^4 = 1 - 0.0625 = 0.9375 = 93.75\%
$$

In a **single-core** system, however, this theoretical 93.75% is reduced by context-switch overhead and by the inability to truly parallelize; only 1 thread runs at any instant.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Multithreading Model Trade-offs

| Metric | Many-to-One (N:1) | One-to-One (1:1) | Many-to-Many (M:N) |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| True parallelism on multicore | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Blocking syscall behaviour | ❌ Whole process blocks | ✅ Only that thread blocks | ✅ Only kernel thread blocks |
| Context-switch speed | ✅ Very fast (user-space) | ⚠️ Slower (kernel mode) | ⚠️ Mixed |
| Scalability of user threads | ✅ Unlimited | ⚠️ Limited by kernel | ✅ High |
| Implementation complexity | ✅ Simple | ✅ Simple | ❌ Complex (scheduler activations) |
| Used in production today | Rare (GNU Pth) | ✅ Linux, Windows, RTOS | Some research / Solaris legacy |
| **Best for RTOS** | ❌ | ✅ **Yes** | ⚠️ Niche |

## 3.6 Thread Cancellation & Termination Math

In POSIX, a thread can be cancelled in two ways:

- **Asynchronous cancellation** — terminates the target thread immediately. Risky; may leave shared resources in inconsistent state.
- **Deferred cancellation** — target thread checks at cancellation points (e.g., `pthread_testcancel()`) and decides whether to terminate.

The probability of a deferred cancellation happening **at a cancellation point** during a thread's execution is:

$$
P_{cancel} = 1 - e^{-\lambda \cdot t_{interval}}
$$

where `λ` is the cancellation request rate and `t_interval` is the time between cancellation points (Poisson arrival model). For deterministic RTOS behavior, **deferred cancellation is preferred**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process vs Threads — Memory Layout Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PCB_A["PROCESS A (Address Space)"]
        direction TB
        codeA["CODE SECTION\n(shared by all threads)"]
        dataA["DATA SECTION\n(shared by all threads)"]
        heapA["HEAP\n(shared by all threads)"]
        filesA["OPEN FILES / OS RESOURCES\n(shared by all threads)"]
        subgraph T1["THREAD 1 (TCB)"]
            t1_id["TID = 1001"]
            t1_pc["Program Counter"]
            t1_regs["Register Set"]
            t1_stack["Private Stack"]
        end
        subgraph T2["THREAD 2 (TCB)"]
            t2_id["TID = 1002"]
            t2_pc["Program Counter"]
            t2_regs["Register Set"]
            t2_stack["Private Stack"]
        end
        subgraph T3["THREAD 3 (TCB)"]
            t3_id["TID = 1003"]
            t3_pc["Program Counter"]
            t3_regs["Register Set"]
            t3_stack["Private Stack"]
        end
    end
    codeA -.shared.-> T1
    codeA -.shared.-> T2
    codeA -.shared.-> T3
    dataA -.shared.-> T1
    dataA -.shared.-> T2
    dataA -.shared.-> T3
    heapA -.shared.-> T1
    heapA -.shared.-> T2
    heapA -.shared.-> T3
    filesA -.shared.-> T1
    filesA -.shared.-> T2
    filesA -.shared.-> T3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The outer box represents the **Process / PCB** (with code, data, heap, files). The three inner boxes (T1, T2, T3) represent the **Threads / TCBs**, each with their **own** PC, registers, and stack, but **sharing** the outer process resources.

## 4.2 Multithreading Models — Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Mto1["MANY-TO-ONE (N:1) MODEL"]
        ut1a["User Thread 1"]
        ut2a["User Thread 2"]
        ut3a["User Thread N"]
        kta["Single Kernel Thread"]
        cpu1["CPU Core"]
    end
    ut1a --> kta
    ut2a --> kta
    ut3a --> kta
    kta --> cpu1

    subgraph Oto1["ONE-TO-ONE (1:1) MODEL"]
        ut1b["User Thread 1"]
        ut2b["User Thread 2"]
        ut3b["User Thread N"]
        kt1b["Kernel Thread 1"]
        kt2b["Kernel Thread 2"]
        ktNb["Kernel Thread N"]
        cpu1b["Core 1"]
        cpu2b["Core 2"]
        cpuNb["Core N"]
    end
    ut1b --> kt1b --> cpu1b
    ut2b --> kt2b --> cpu2b
    ut3b --> ktNb --> cpuNb

    subgraph MtoN["MANY-TO-MANY (M:N) MODEL"]
        ut1c["User Thread 1"]
        ut2c["User Thread 2"]
        ut3c["User Thread 3"]
        utMc["User Thread M"]
        kt1c["Kernel Thread 1"]
        kt2c["Kernel Thread 2"]
        ktNc["Kernel Thread N (N less than M)"]
        cpu1c["Core 1"]
        cpu2c["Core 2"]
    end
    ut1c --> kt1c
    ut2c --> kt1c
    ut3c --> kt2c
    utMc --> ktNc
    kt1c --> cpu1c
    kt2c --> cpu2c
    ktNc --> cpu1c
```

## 4.3 Thread Lifecycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New : pthread_create
    New --> Ready : thread initialized
    Ready --> Running : dispatcher / scheduler
    Running --> Ready : preempted (time slice / higher priority)
    Running --> Blocked : I/O wait / lock wait / sleep
    Blocked --> Ready : I/O complete / lock released / timeout
    Running --> Terminated : function returns / pthread_exit
    Terminated --> [*] : TCB reclaimed (joined or detached)
```

> [!NOTE]
> In an **RTOS**, the transition `Ready → Running` is called the **dispatch latency**, a critical metric. The transition `Running → Blocked → Ready` is a typical I/O wait. The transition `Running → Terminated` is a thread exit; in POSIX, the thread is not fully reaped until `pthread_join()` is called (or the thread is detached with `pthread_detach()`).

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Multithreaded RTOS Application

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HARDWARE["HARDWARE PLATFORM (MCU / SoC)"]
        CPU["CPU Core(s)"]
        TIMER["Hardware Timer / SysTick"]
        IRQ["Interrupt Controller (NVIC)"]
        IO["I/O Peripherals (UART, ADC, GPIO)"]
    end

    subgraph KERNEL["RTOS KERNEL"]
        SCHED["Scheduler\n(Rate Monotonic / EDF / Priority)"]
        DISP["Dispatcher"]
        IPC["IPC & Synchronization\n(Queues, Semaphores, Mutex)"]
        TMR["Software Timers"]
    end

    subgraph TASKS["RTOS TASKS (Threads)"]
        T1["TASK 1\nSensor Acquisition\n(Highest Priority)"]
        T2["TASK 2\nControl Algorithm\n(Mid Priority)"]
        T3["TASK 3\nCommunication\n(Lower Priority)"]
        T4["TASK 4\nWatchdog / Monitor\n(Background)"]
    end

    subgraph APP["APPLICATION CODE"]
        CTRL["Control Logic (shared)"]
        STATE["Shared State / Buffers"]
    end

    CPU --> SCHED
    TIMER --> TMR
    IRQ --> DISP
    IO --> T1
    IO --> T2

    SCHED --> DISP
    DISP --> T1
    DISP --> T2
    DISP --> T3
    DISP --> T4

    T1 --> STATE
    T2 --> STATE
    T3 --> STATE
    T4 --> STATE

    T1 <--> IPC
    T2 <--> IPC
    T3 <--> IPC
    T4 <--> IPC
    STATE --> CTRL
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-level architectural reading:** The **hardware platform** feeds the **RTOS kernel**, which schedules **multiple tasks (threads)**. Each task is a unit of execution with its own TCB. Tasks communicate via **IPC mechanisms** (queues, semaphores, mutexes — covered in Module 2). They share common **state / buffers** in memory, with synchronization provided by the kernel's IPC primitives.

## 4.5 Thread Pool Architecture (used in RTOS for short-lived tasks)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    REQ["Incoming Task Requests\n(network, sensors, events)"]
    QUEUE["Task Queue\n(FIFO)"]
    subgraph POOL["THREAD POOL"]
        W1["Worker Thread 1"]
        W2["Worker Thread 2"]
        W3["Worker Thread 3"]
        W4["Worker Thread N"]
    end
    DONE["Results / Responses"]

    REQ --> QUEUE
    QUEUE --> W1
    QUEUE --> W2
    QUEUE --> W3
    QUEUE --> W4
    W1 --> DONE
    W2 --> DONE
    W3 --> DONE
    W4 --> DONE
```

> [!NOTE]
> A **thread pool** pre-creates a fixed number of worker threads. New work items are dispatched to a free worker, avoiding the cost of dynamic thread creation. This is common in **server-side RTOS applications** and in the **Linux kernel** (`workqueue` subsystem).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part A questions test **Remember / Understand** levels of **Bloom's Taxonomy**. Answers should be concise (3–5 lines).

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a thread. How is it different from a process?*

**Model Answer:**

A **thread** is a basic unit of CPU utilization, consisting of a **thread ID, program counter, register set, and stack**, sharing the **code section, data section, and OS resources** (open files, signals) with other threads of the same process.

**Differences:**

| Aspect | Process | Thread |
|--------|---------|--------|
| Memory | Own address space | Shared within process |
| Context-switch cost | High | Low (~5–10× faster) |
| Communication | Requires IPC | Direct — shared variables |
| Creation cost | Heavy (PCB) | Light (TCB) |
| Crash scope | Only that process | Entire process (all threads) |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the difference between user-level threads and kernel-level threads? List two advantages of each.*

**Model Answer:**

- **User-Level Threads (ULT):** Managed entirely by a **user-space thread library**; kernel is unaware of them. **Advantages:** (i) very fast context switch (no kernel mode transition), (ii) flexible scheduling policy (application-defined).
- **Kernel-Level Threads (KLT):** Managed **directly by the OS kernel** with each thread having its own TCB. **Advantages:** (i) true parallelism on multicore, (ii) one thread blocking on syscall does **not** block the entire process.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE, Module 1 carries one 14-mark question. The question has **internal choice** — you must answer EITHER (a) OR (b). Sub-parts are typically 7 + 7 marks.

---

### **Q3. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Module 1]**
**Question A (14 marks):**
*(a)* Explain the three multithreading models — Many-to-One, One-to-One, and Many-to-Many — with neat diagrams. Compare their advantages and disadvantages. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* With a neat diagram, explain the thread lifecycle. Discuss how an RTOS handles the **Ready → Running** transition. **(7 marks)**

**OR**

**Question B (14 marks):**
*(a)* What are the benefits of multithreading? Discuss why multithreading is **especially important** in real-time systems with at least three application examples. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* Describe the **POSIX Pthreads** library. Write the syntax of `pthread_create()` and `pthread_join()` and explain each parameter. **(7 marks)**

---

### Detailed Model Solution — Question 3, A(a): Multithreading Models

**Step 1 — Define the concept:**
A multithreading model describes the **mapping relationship** between **user threads** (managed by a thread library) and **kernel threads** (managed by the OS kernel).

**Step 2 — Many-to-One (N:1):**
- All `N` user threads map to a **single** kernel thread.
- Thread management is in **user space**.
- **Advantages:** Fast context switch; any number of ULTs possible; portable across kernels.
- **Disadvantages:** If one ULT issues a **blocking syscall**, the **entire process blocks**. **Cannot** exploit multicore parallelism.
- **Example:** GNU Portable Threads (Pth), early Solaris Green Threads.

**Step 3 — One-to-One (1:1):**
- Each user thread maps to **one** kernel thread.
- **Advantages:** True parallelism on multicore; blocking on one thread does not affect others; simple model.
- **Disadvantages:** Creating a user thread ⇒ creating a kernel thread; kernel imposes a limit on threads per process; slower context switch.
- **Examples:** Windows NT/XP/2000+, Linux, modern macOS, **most RTOS implementations** (VxWorks, QNX, RTEMS).

**Step 4 — Many-to-Many (M:N):**
- `M` user threads multiplexed onto `N` kernel threads, with `M ≥ N`.
- The kernel schedules the `N` kernel threads; the thread library schedules `M` user threads on top.
- **Advantages:** Combines flexibility of ULTs with parallelism of KLTs. Developer can create many ULTs; kernel can run many in parallel.
- **Disadvantages:** Very complex to implement (requires coordination between user scheduler and kernel scheduler). **Examples:** Solaris (before 9), Scheduler Activations (Anderson et al.).

**Step 5 — Comparative table:**

| Property | Many-to-One | One-to-One | Many-to-Many |
|----------|-------------|------------|---------------|
| Parallelism | No | Yes | Yes |
| Block resilience | No (whole process) | Yes (per-thread) | Yes |
| Switch speed | Fastest | Slower | Mixed |
| Complexity | Low | Low | High |
| Used today | Rare | **Most OSes & RTOS** | Niche |

**Step 6 — Conclusion:** The **one-to-one model** is the most widely adopted in modern OSes and is the **default for RTOS** due to its predictability and true concurrency on multicore SoCs.

*(Refer to Mermaid diagrams in Section 4.2 for visual aid.)*

---

### Detailed Model Solution — Question 3, A(b): Thread Lifecycle and RTOS Dispatch

**Step 1 — States:**
A thread passes through **5 states**: `New`, `Ready`, `Running`, `Blocked`, `Terminated`.

**Step 2 — Transitions explained:**
- `New → Ready`: thread object is initialized; stack and TCB allocated.
- `Ready → Running`: the **dispatcher** picks this thread (highest-priority ready thread on the chosen core).
- `Running → Ready`: **preemption** — either a higher-priority thread became ready, or the time slice expired.
- `Running → Blocked`: thread issues a blocking syscall, takes a held mutex, or calls `taskDelay()` (FreeRTOS) / `nanosleep()` (POSIX).
- `Blocked → Ready`: the awaited I/O completes, mutex is released, or timeout fires.
- `Running → Terminated`: thread function returns or calls `pthread_exit()` / `taskDelete()`.

**Step 3 — RTOS-specific handling of Ready → Running:**
- The RTOS uses a **priority-based preemptive scheduler** (e.g., Rate Monotonic, Fixed Priority, or EDF).
- When a higher-priority task becomes ready (e.g., interrupt signals completion of I/O), the scheduler immediately triggers a **context switch** to that task.
- The **dispatch latency** = time between the event occurring and the new task beginning execution. This is bounded in hard RTOS.
- The dispatcher saves the current task's context (PC, registers, stack pointer) into its TCB and restores the new task's context.
- All TCBs of equal-priority tasks are organized in **ready queues** (often one queue per priority level, e.g., bitmap-based scheduler in uC/OS-II).

**Step 4 — Why this matters in RTOS:**
- **Hard real-time** systems must meet deadlines deterministically; the **dispatch latency** must be bounded and small (typically < 10 µs in modern RTOS).
- **Soft real-time** systems aim for "best effort" deadline satisfaction.

---

### Detailed Model Solution — Question 3, B(a): Multithreading Benefits in RTOS

**Step 1 — Generic benefits of multithreading:**
- **Responsiveness** (UI doesn't freeze)
- **Resource sharing** (no IPC overhead)
- **Economy** (faster creation & switching)
- **Scalability** (true parallelism on multicore)

**Step 2 — Why multithreading is critical in RTOS:**
- A real-time system inherently has **multiple concurrent activities** (sensors, actuators, communication, user interface, monitoring).
- Each must meet **timing deadlines**.
- Multithreading (tasks) allows **logical concurrency** even on a single-core MCU, by **time-sharing** the CPU at fine granularity.

**Step 3 — Application examples:**
1. **Automotive ECU (e.g., engine control):** Separate tasks for crankshaft position sensing, fuel injection calculation, oxygen sensor reading, and CAN bus communication. Each has different periods (e.g., crankshaft every 6 ms, O2 sensor every 100 ms).
2. **Quadcopter flight controller (e.g., Pixhawk/PX4):** Tasks for IMU sampling (1 kHz), PID control loop (500 Hz), attitude estimation, motor PWM output, telemetry over MAVLink. Deadline misses cause crashes.
3. **Medical infusion pump (FDA Class II):** Tasks for flow-rate sensing, alarm monitoring, keypad/display UI, drug delivery control, and wireless nurse-station communication. Safety-critical threads (alarms) have highest priority.

**Step 4 — Multicore extension:** In multicore SoCs (e.g., NXP i.MX8, TI Sitara), threads can run **in true parallel** on separate cores, improving throughput while preserving real-time guarantees.

---

### Detailed Model Solution — Question 3, B(b): POSIX Pthreads

**Step 1 — Introduction to Pthreads:**
**POSIX Pthreads** (IEEE 1003.1c standard) is the most widely used **thread library** for C/C++ on UNIX-like systems. It provides a standardized API for thread creation, termination, synchronization (mutexes, condition variables), and more.

**Step 2 — Header file and linking:**
- Include: `#include <pthread.h>`
- Compile/link: add `-lpthread` flag (Linux)

**Step 3 — `pthread_create()` signature:**

```c
int pthread_create(pthread_t *thread,
                   const pthread_attr_t *attr,
                   void *(*start_routine)(void *),
                   void *arg);
```

**Parameter explanation:**

| Parameter | Meaning |
|-----------|---------|
| `thread` | Pointer to a `pthread_t` variable that will receive the new thread's ID |
| `attr` | Pointer to thread attributes (e.g., stack size, scheduling policy) — `NULL` for defaults |
| `start_routine` | Function pointer to the **thread function**; takes `void*`, returns `void*` |
| `arg` | Argument passed to the thread function; can be a pointer to any data |

**Return value:** `0` on success; positive error number on failure.

**Step 4 — `pthread_join()` signature:**

```c
int pthread_join(pthread_t thread, void **retval);
```

**Parameter explanation:**

| Parameter | Meaning |
|-----------|---------|
| `thread` | Thread ID of the thread to wait for |
| `retval` | Pointer to a variable that will receive the thread's return value (can be `NULL`) |

**Effect:** Blocks the calling thread until the specified thread terminates. The terminated thread's resources (TCB, stack) are released.

**Step 5 — Minimal complete example (POSIX):**

```c
#include <pthread.h>
#include <stdio.h>

void* worker(void* arg) {
    int id = *(int*)arg;
    printf("Hello from thread %d\n", id);
    return NULL;
}

int main(void) {
    pthread_t t1, t2;
    int a = 1, b = 2;
    pthread_create(&t1, NULL, worker, &a);
    pthread_create(&t2, NULL, worker, &b);
    pthread_join(t1, NULL);
    pthread_join(t2, NULL);
    return 0;
}
```

**Step 6 — Compiling and running:**

```bash
gcc -o hello hello.c -lpthread
./hello
```

> [!NOTE]
> In **RTOS** like **VxWorks, QNX, RTEMS**, Pthreads is supported as the threading API. In **FreeRTOS**, the equivalent is `xTaskCreate()` (a task is a thread in FreeRTOS terminology).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in Multithreading questions — KTU Board 2024 valuation pattern observed:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "thread" with "process"** in definitions. Always specify that a thread **shares the address space** of its parent process, while a process has its **own** address space. Marks lost: **1 mark per incorrect statement**.
>
> 2. **Not drawing diagrams** in multithreading model questions. KTU examiners award **2–3 marks** for a clear diagram of N:1, 1:1, M:N mappings. Drawing a text-only description loses those marks.
>
> 3. **Forgetting RTOS context.** When the question says "in real-time systems", you MUST mention **deadlines, dispatch latency, priority-based scheduling, and at least one example** (e.g., automotive ECU, flight controller). A generic OS answer is **incomplete** for a PECPT741 paper.
>
> 4. **Skipping parameter explanation** in `pthread_create` questions. Each of the 4 parameters must be explained — 1.5 marks each, totalling 6 marks out of 7. Writing just the syntax without parameter meaning **loses ~5 marks**.
>
> 5. **Confusing "join" with "detach"**. `pthread_join()` **waits and reaps** the thread. `pthread_detach()` **lets the thread self-reap** when it exits. They are NOT the same. Examiners check this specifically.
>
> 6. **Calculating Amdahl's Law incorrectly**: forgetting to add the serial fraction `(1-P)` before dividing. Always show: `S = 1 / ((1 - P) + P/N)`.
>
> 7. **In RMS bound calculation**, students often write the formula `n(2^(1/n) - 1)` but forget the **asymptotic limit** `ln(2) ≈ 0.693`. If the question asks for "large n", this limit is required.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Thread = lightweight execution unit** sharing process resources; has its own PC, registers, stack, and TCB.
- **Process = heavyweight execution unit** with its own PCB, address space, and resources.
- **Thread creation** is **~30× cheaper** than process creation; thread context switch is **~5–10× faster** than process context switch.
- **Three multithreading models:** Many-to-One (N:1), One-to-One (1:1), Many-to-Many (M:N). **One-to-One is the most widely used** today, including in RTOS.
- **User-Level Threads (ULT)** are managed in user space; **fast switching** but **block whole process on syscall**.
- **Kernel-Level Threads (KLT)** are managed by the OS; **slower switching** but **true parallelism** and **per-thread blocking**.
- **POSIX Pthreads** is the standard thread library (`pthread_create`, `pthread_join`, `pthread_exit`, `pthread_cancel`, `pthread_detach`).
- **Thread lifecycle**: `New → Ready → Running → Blocked → Terminated`. In RTOS, the **Ready → Running** transition is bounded by **dispatch latency**.
- **Amdahl's Law**: `S(N) = 1 / ((1 - P) + P/N)`. The serial fraction caps the speedup.
- **Rate Monotonic Utilization Bound**: `U ≤ n(2^(1/n) - 1)`. Asymptotic limit `→ ln(2) ≈ 69.3%`.
- **Thread pool** = a fixed set of pre-created worker threads consuming work from a queue — efficient for handling many short-lived tasks.
- **In RTOS terminology**: a "task" ≈ a "thread". Examples: FreeRTOS `xTaskCreate`, VxWorks `taskSpawn`, POSIX `pthread_create`.
- **Why multithreading is essential in RTOS**: to handle multiple concurrent real-time activities (sensors, control, comms, UI, monitoring) with predictable deadlines.
- **Common KTU exam focus areas**:
  - Definition of thread with 4 components (TID, PC, registers, stack)
  - Process vs Thread comparison table
  - Three multithreading models with diagrams
  - POSIX Pthreads API (`pthread_create` parameters, `pthread_join`)
  - Thread lifecycle state diagram
  - Amdahl's Law and RMS bound (numerical problems)
  - Real-time application examples (ECU, flight controller, medical device)
- **Pitfalls to avoid in exam**: confusing thread and process, missing diagrams, missing RTOS context, incomplete parameter explanation in Pthread questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of an **Operating System (OS)** and specifically a **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)**, a **Process** is formally defined as a **program in execution**. It is the fundamental unit of work scheduled by the kernel and represents a running instance of a program code that may be invoked, suspended, resumed, and terminated by the scheduler.

A process is more than just the executable code (the *text section*). It is a dynamic, active entity that consists of:

- **Program Code (Text Section):** The static, compiled instructions stored in memory.
- **Current Activity (Program Counter & Registers):** The dynamic execution state, including the instruction pointer, stack pointer, general-purpose registers, and status flags.
- **Process Stack:** A Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) data structure holding temporary data such as function parameters, return addresses, and local variables.
- **Data Section:** Global and static variables associated with the process.
- **Heap:** Dynamically allocated memory acquired during run-time (e.g., via `malloc()` in C).
- **Process Control Block (PCB):** A kernel-managed data structure (also called a *Task Control Block* or *TCB* in many RTOSs like FreeRTOS) that encapsulates all metadata describing the process.

In an **RTOS**, a process (commonly called a *task*) has a strict timing contract. Each task is typically associated with a **deadline**, a **period** (for periodic tasks), and a **worst-case execution time (WCET)**. The scheduler must guarantee that tasks meet their timing constraints — a property termed **temporal determinism**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECPT741 Module 1):**
> A *process* in RTOS is a *task*. The terms "task" and "process" are used interchangeably in most textbooks, but technically, a *task* refers to a single thread of execution, while a *process* may contain multiple tasks (threads). For Module 1 of PECPT741, treat them as synonymous unless explicitly discussing multi-threaded processes.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen" Model

Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen as the operating system, and the **chefs** as the **processes**.

- Each **chef** (process) has a personal **recipe card** (the *program code* / text section).
- The chef also carries a **clipboard** (the *Process Control Block*) that tracks: *which step of the recipe they are on* (Program Counter), *what ingredients they are holding* (CPU registers), *how long since they started* (accounting info), and *whether they are waiting for something* (process state).
- The **head chef** (the *scheduler / dispatcher*) decides which chef gets to use the **stove** (the *CPU*) at any given moment.
- A chef waiting for an ingredient (e.g., an oven preheating) is in a **Blocked/Waiting** state — they are alive but cannot proceed.
- A chef standing idle with their recipe ready is in a **Ready** state — waiting for the head chef to call them.
- A chef currently cooking is in the **Running** state — actively using the stove.
- A chef whose dish is served and recipe completed is **Terminated** (exit state).

When the head chef switches from one chef to another (e.g., one is waiting for the oven, and another has ingredients ready), there is a brief moment of **context switching** — the head chef must read the clipboard of the leaving chef (save state) and hand the new chef's clipboard to the stove (load state). This overhead is precisely why **RTOS designers minimize context-switch latency** to meet hard real-time deadlines.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Key Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Metrics in RTOS Process Management:**
> - **CPU Utilization (U):** Fraction of time the CPU is busy executing processes. $0 \le U \le 1$.
> - **Throughput (η):** Number of processes completed per unit time, typically measured in **processes/second**.
> - **Turnaround Time (TAT):** $T_{AT} = T_{completion} - T_{arrival}$. Measured in **milliseconds (ms)**.
> - **Waiting Time (WT):** Time spent in the **Ready Queue**, $WT = T_{AT} - Burst Time$.
> - **Response Time (RT):** Time from arrival to first CPU allocation. Crucial in interactive and real-time systems.
> - **Deadline (D):** Absolute time by which a real-time task *must* complete execution.
> - **Period (P):** Time interval between successive activations of a *periodic* task.
> - **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET, C):** Upper bound on the execution time of a task on the target hardware.

---

## 1.4 Why "Processes" Are Central to RTOS

Unlike general-purpose OSs (Windows, Linux) where processes compete for *fairness* and *throughput*, RTOS processes compete to meet **deadlines**. A missed deadline in a **hard real-time system** (e.g., anti-lock braking system, pacemakers, nuclear reactor control) can be **catastrophic — causing loss of life or equipment**. Therefore, RTOS process management prioritizes:

1. **Bounded Response Time:** The worst-case time between an event (e.g., interrupt) and process response is strictly bounded and *known a priori*.
2. **Minimal Kernel Latency:** Interrupt disable time, scheduler run-time, and context-switch overhead are minimized to **microseconds** or even **nanoseconds**.
3. **Preemptive Priority Scheduling:** Higher-priority processes (e.g., the brake controller) can immediately preempt lower-priority ones (e.g., the infotainment display).

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Process — The Process Control Block (PCB)

The **Process Control Block (PCB)** — or in many RTOS kernels, the **Task Control Block (TCB)** — is a kernel data structure created when a process is admitted into the system. It is the *identity card* and *execution state container* of the process.

### 2.1.1 Standard PCB Fields

| Field Name | Purpose / Significance |
|---|---|
| **Process ID (PID)** | Unique integer identifier assigned by the kernel. |
| **Process State** | Current state — `New`, `Ready`, `Running`, `Blocked`, `Terminated`. |
| **Program Counter (PC)** | Address of the next instruction to execute. |
| **CPU Registers** | General-purpose registers, accumulator, index registers — the "live" execution snapshot. |
| **Stack Pointer (SP)** | Points to the top of the process's stack frame in memory. |
| **Memory Management Info** | Base/limit registers, page tables, segment tables — defines the process's address space. |
| **Scheduling Information** | Priority, quantum, scheduling algorithm parameters, pointers to Ready/Wait queues. |
| **I/O Status Information** | Outstanding I/O requests, allocated I/O devices, open file descriptors. |
| **Accounting Information** | CPU time used, time limits, process IDs of parent/children. |
| **Pointer to Parent Process** | Required for process tree / hierarchy and signal delivery. |
| **Open File Table Pointer** | Links to files currently held by the process. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Examiner questions frequently ask students to "Draw and explain the PCB" or "Explain the role of PCB during context switching." Always include *all* the fields above and explicitly mention that the PC and registers are what the **dispatcher** saves/restores.

---

## 2.2 The Five-State Process Model

The most widely taught model in KTU and in textbook treatments of process management is the **Five-State Model**, originally formalized by researchers at IBM and adopted in Silberschatz' *Operating System Concepts*.

### 2.2.1 The Five States

1. **New (Created):** The process is being created. The OS is allocating a PCB, setting up memory, and initializing data structures. It has not yet been admitted to the pool of executable processes.
2. **Ready:** The process has been loaded into main memory and is waiting in the **Ready Queue** to be assigned to the CPU. It is *prepared* to run but is *not* currently running.
3. **Running:** The process is currently executing on a CPU core. On a single-core system, exactly **one** process is in this state at any instant.
4. **Blocked (Waiting / Sleeping):** The process cannot proceed because it is waiting for some event to occur — typically completion of I/O, receipt of a signal, or availability of a resource (semaphore, mutex, message).
5. **Terminated (Exit / Zombie):** The process has finished execution. Its PCB and resources may not yet be fully deallocated (the parent may still need to read the exit status).

### 2.2.2 State Transition Rules (Triggers)

A transition between states occurs only when a specific kernel event takes place:

- **New → Ready:** *Admission.* The OS has finished process creation and admits the process to the Ready Queue.
- **Ready → Running:** *Dispatch (Schedule).* The scheduler has selected this process and the dispatcher has loaded its context (PC, registers) onto the CPU.
- **Running → Ready:** *Preemption / Time-out / Yield.* A higher-priority process becomes runnable, the time quantum expires, or the process voluntarily yields.
- **Running → Blocked:** *Wait (I/O or Event).* The process issues an I/O request, requests a held resource, or invokes a `wait()` system call.
- **Blocked → Ready:** *Wake-up (I/O Completion or Signal).* The awaited event has occurred (e.g., disk I/O completed, semaphore signaled).
- **Running → Terminated:** *Exit.* The process executes its final statement, calls `exit()`, or is killed by a signal/abort.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (KTU Pitfall):** A process in the *Blocked* state goes to *Ready* — **not directly to Running**. The scheduler must first re-admit it to the Ready Queue, and only then can it be selected for execution. Many students write incorrect arrows like "Blocked → Running" — this is a guaranteed **2–3 mark deduction** in the KTU valuation key.

---

## 2.3 Real-Time Process Classification

In an RTOS, processes (tasks) are additionally classified by their temporal behavior:

| Classification | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Periodic Task** | Activated at fixed intervals of period **P** (e.g., every 10 ms). | Reading a sensor in an airbag controller. |
| **Aperiodic Task** | Activated at irregular, unpredictable times. | Emergency alarm interrupt. |
| **Sporadic Task** | Aperiodic but with a minimum inter-arrival time bound. | User button press. |
| **Hard Real-Time Task** | Missing a deadline = **system failure / catastrophe**. | ABS brake control, pacemaker. |
| **Soft Real-Time Task** | Missing a deadline = **degraded performance, no catastrophe**. | Video frame decoding, audio playback. |

### 2.3.1 The Periodic Task Model (Liu & Layland)

For a periodic task $\tau_i$:

$$C_i \le D_i \le P_i$$

where:
- $C_i$ = Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)
- $D_i$ = Relative deadline
- $P_i$ = Period
- $U_i = \dfrac{C_i}{P_i}$ = Utilization of task $\tau_i$

> For a set of $n$ independent, preemptible periodic tasks to be **schedulable** under the **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** algorithm, the Liu & Layland sufficient condition is:
>
> $$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{P_i} \le n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

This bound approaches $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$ as $n \to \infty$ — meaning the CPU can never be loaded above ~69.3% under RMS without *risking* a missed deadline (though it is a sufficient, not necessary, condition).

---

## 2.4 Context Switching — The Heart of Multitasking

A **context switch** is the procedure of saving the state of the currently running process (its *context* — PC, registers, stack pointer, etc.) and loading the state of the next process selected by the scheduler. It is the *fundamental mechanism* that creates the illusion of parallel execution on a single-core CPU.

### 2.4.1 Context Switch Sequence

1. The scheduler is invoked (timer interrupt, I/O completion, system call, etc.).
2. The CPU saves the registers, PC, and other state of the *old* process into its PCB.
3. The scheduler selects the *new* process from the Ready Queue.
4. The dispatcher loads the new process's PCB — restoring PC, registers, and memory map.
5. Execution resumes at the new process's saved PC.

### 2.4.2 Context Switch Overhead

Pure context switch time $T_{cs}$ in a typical RTOS ranges from **1 µs to 10 µs**. Total time "wasted" per unit time due to context switching at rate $f_{cs}$ is:

$$T_{wasted} = f_{cs} \times T_{cs}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RTOS Design Imperative:** The context-switch time is part of the *system overhead* and directly reduces the CPU time available for real-time work. In **hard real-time** schedulability analysis, the *worst-case* context-switch overhead is **always added to the WCET** of every task: $C_i^{effective} = C_i + 2 \cdot T_{cs}$ (one save + one load).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{AT} = T_{completion} - T_{arrival}$ | Turnaround Time | ms |
| 2 | $WT = T_{AT} - Burst\ Time$ | Waiting Time (Ready Queue only) | ms |
| 3 | $RT = T_{first\ CPU} - T_{arrival}$ | Response Time | ms |
| 4 | $T_{AT}^{avg} = \frac{1}{n}\sum T_{AT_i}$ | Average Turnaround Time | ms |
| 5 | $WT^{avg} = \frac{1}{n}\sum WT_i$ | Average Waiting Time | ms |
| 6 | $\eta = \frac{n_{completed}}{T_{total}}$ | Throughput | processes/s |
| 7 | $U_i = \frac{C_i}{P_i}$ | Per-Task CPU Utilization | dimensionless, $0 \le U_i \le 1$ |
| 8 | $U_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} U_i$ | Total CPU Utilization | dimensionless |
| 9 | $U_{RMS}^{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Liu-Layland RMS schedulability bound | dimensionless |
| 10 | $C_i^{eff} = C_i + 2 T_{cs}$ | Effective WCET including context switch | ms / µs |
| 11 | $C_i \le D_i \le P_i$ | Periodic task constraint | ms |
| 12 | $T_{wasted} = f_{cs} \cdot T_{cs}$ | Time lost to context switching | ms / s |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Average Waiting & Turnaround Time (FCFS Scheduling)

> **Problem Statement (Modeled on KTU 2024 Past Paper style):**
> Three processes arrive in the system at time 0 with the following CPU burst times. Compute the **Average Waiting Time** and **Average Turnaround Time** using **First-Come-First-Served (FCFS)** scheduling. Processes are non-preemptive.
>
> | Process | Arrival Time (ms) | Burst Time (ms) |
> |---|---|---|
> | P1 | 0 | 8 |
> | P2 | 1 | 4 |
> | P3 | 2 | 9 |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Construct the Gantt Chart (FCFS Order: P1 → P2 → P3)**

Using the order of arrival, the Gantt chart timeline is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Time: } 0 \rightarrow 8 \rightarrow 12 \rightarrow 21 \\
&\text{Run: } \;\; P1 \;\;\;\; P2 \;\;\;\;\; P3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Completion Time for each process**

$P1$ finishes at $t = 8$ ms, $P2$ finishes at $t = 8 + 4 = 12$ ms, $P3$ finishes at $t = 12 + 9 = 21$ ms.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{comp}(P1) &= 8\ \text{ms} \\
T_{comp}(P2) &= 12\ \text{ms} \\
T_{comp}(P3) &= 21\ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Turnaround Time (TAT) for each process**

Using the formula $T_{AT} = T_{completion} - T_{arrival}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{AT}(P1) &= 8 - 0 = 8\ \text{ms} \\
T_{AT}(P2) &= 12 - 1 = 11\ \text{ms} \\
T_{AT}(P3) &= 21 - 2 = 19\ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Waiting Time (WT) for each process**

Using the formula $WT = T_{AT} - Burst\ Time$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
WT(P1) &= 8 - 8 = 0\ \text{ms} \\
WT(P2) &= 11 - 4 = 7\ \text{ms} \\
WT(P3) &= 19 - 9 = 10\ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note (Logic Explanation):** $P2$ arrived at $t = 1$ but had to wait until $P1$ finished at $t = 8$ — therefore it waited $8 - 1 = 7$ ms in the Ready Queue. $P3$ arrived at $t = 2$ and waited until $P2$ finished at $t = 12$ — so its wait is $12 - 2 = 10$ ms. $P1$ did not wait because it started immediately.

**Step 5 — Compute the Averages**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{AT}^{avg} &= \frac{8 + 11 + 19}{3} = \frac{38}{3} \approx 12.67\ \text{ms} \\
WT^{avg} &= \frac{0 + 7 + 10}{3} = \frac{17}{3} \approx 5.67\ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Throughput**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{3\ \text{processes}}{21\ \text{ms}} = \frac{3}{21 \times 10^{-3}}\ \text{processes/s} \approx 142.86\ \text{processes/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — RMS Schedulability Bound (Liu & Layland)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A real-time system has $n = 3$ independent periodic tasks with the following parameters:
>
> | Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $P_i$ (ms) |
> |---|---|---|
> | $\tau_1$ | 1 | 4 |
> | $\tau_2$ | 2 | 6 |
> | $\tau_3$ | 3 | 10 |
>
> Using **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** priority (shorter period = higher priority), determine whether the task set is **guaranteed schedulable** using the Liu-Layland sufficient condition.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Verify the Periodic Task Constraint $C_i \le D_i \le P_i$**

For all three tasks, we assume $D_i = P_i$ (deadline equals period), and $C_i \le P_i$ holds in all cases:
$\tau_1: 1 \le 4$, $\tau_2: 2 \le 6$, $\tau_3: 3 \le 10$ — all satisfied.

**Step 2 — Compute Total CPU Utilization**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{total} &= \frac{C_1}{P_1} + \frac{C_2}{P_2} + \frac{C_3}{P_3} \\
&= \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to a Common Denominator (LCD = 60)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{total} &= \frac{15}{60} + \frac{20}{60} + \frac{18}{60} \\
&= \frac{15 + 20 + 18}{60} \\
&= \frac{53}{60} \approx 0.8833
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Liu-Layland Sufficient Bound for $n = 3$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{bound} &= n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right) \\
&= 3 \times \left(2^{1/3} - 1\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the Exponential Numerically**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2^{1/3} &= \sqrt[3]{2} \approx 1.2599 \\
2^{1/3} - 1 &\approx 0.2599 \\
U_{bound} &= 3 \times 0.2599 \approx 0.7798
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare and Conclude**

$$
U_{total} = 0.8833 \quad \text{vs.} \quad U_{bound} = 0.7798
$$

Since $U_{total} > U_{bound}$, the **Liu-Layland sufficient condition is NOT satisfied**. Therefore, the system is *not guaranteed* to be schedulable by RMS using this condition.

> **Important Caveat:** The Liu-Layland bound is *sufficient* but **not necessary**. The task set might still be schedulable under RMS — this would require a more precise analysis (e.g., *exact response-time analysis* or *hyperbolic bound*). For KTU 2024 scheme, stating both the *negative* verdict and the *caveat* scores **full marks**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Process States

| Property | New | Ready | Running | Blocked | Terminated |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| In Ready Queue? | No | Yes | No | No | No |
| Has PCB? | Being Created | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes (until deallocated) |
| Consuming CPU? | No | No | **Yes** | No | No |
| Waiting for I/O? | No | No | No | **Yes** | No |
| Can transition to Terminated? | Yes (abort) | No (typically) | Yes (exit) | Yes (abort) | — |
| Can be scheduled? | No | Yes | Already chosen | No | No |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process State Transition Diagram (Five-State Model)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[New] -- Admission --> B[Ready]
    B -- Dispatch / Schedule --> C[Running]
    C -- Preemption / Time-out / Yield --> B
    C -- Wait for I/O or Event --> D[Blocked]
    D -- I/O Completion / Event Occurs --> B
    C -- Exit or Abort --> E[Terminated]
    A -- Abort --> E
```

> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - Solid arrows = legal, well-defined state transitions triggered by kernel events.
> - The cycle *Ready → Running → Ready* (preemption) is the heartbeat of multitasking.
> - There is **no direct arrow** between *Blocked* and *Running* — a blocked process must first go to *Ready* before being dispatched.

---

## 4.2 Process Control Block (PCB) — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    PCB[Process Control Block PCB]
    PCB --> PID[Process ID PID]
    PCB --> STATE[Process State]
    PCB --> PC[Program Counter]
    PCB --> REG[CPU Registers]
    PCB --> SP[Stack Pointer]
    PCB --> MEM[Memory Management Info]
    PCB --> SCHED[Scheduling Info Priority Quantum]
    PCB --> IO[I/O Status Open Files]
    PCB --> ACCT[Accounting CPU Time Used]
    PCB --> PARENT[Parent Process Pointer]
```

> **Functional Notes:**
> - On a **context switch OUT**, the kernel *writes* into the fields: `PC`, `REG`, `SP`, `STATE`.
> - On a **context switch IN**, the kernel *reads* from these same fields to restore execution.
> - The `MEM` block typically holds base/limit registers or a pointer to the page table.

---

## 4.3 Context Switch Data Flow (Save → Schedule → Restore)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[CPU Registers Live State] -- Save on Preemption / Time-out --> B[PCB of Old Process]
    B -- Stored in Memory --> C[Kernel Scheduler]
    C -- Selects Next Process --> D[Ready Queue]
    D -- Picks Highest Priority Ready Process --> E[PCB of New Process]
    E -- Loaded to CPU --> A
```

> **Key Insight:** The PC, registers, and stack pointer are *the only* fields that physically need to be saved/restored on every context switch. Other PCB fields (priority, parent PID, etc.) are *not* volatile during execution — they are metadata read by the scheduler before dispatch.

---

## 4.4 Periodic Task Timeline (RTOS Temporal Behavior)

```mermaid
graph LR
    T0[Time 0 Release tau1 tau2 tau3] -- Period P1 --> T1[Time P1 Release tau1]
    T1 -- Period P1 --> T2[Time 2P1 Release tau1]
    T0 -- Period P2 --> T3[Time P2 Release tau2]
    T0 -- Period P3 --> T4[Time P3 Release tau3]
```

> **Interpretation:** A periodic task $\tau_i$ is *released* (becomes ready) at $t = 0, P_i, 2P_i, 3P_i, \ldots$ It must complete by its **absolute deadline** $D_i = kP_i$ for its $k$-th invocation.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a process. How is it different from a program?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 mark) Definition:** A *process* is a program in execution. It is a dynamic, active entity that includes the program code (text), current activity (program counter, registers), stack, data section, heap, and a Process Control Block (PCB).
- **(1 mark) Program vs Process:** A *program* is a passive entity — a static file of compiled instructions stored on disk. A *process* is an active entity — a running instance of a program with a live execution state.
- **(1 mark) Example:** A `.exe` file on disk is a *program*; double-clicking it creates a *process* with its own PID, stack, and allocated memory.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a Process Control Block (PCB)? List any four fields of a PCB.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 mark) Definition:** A PCB is a kernel-maintained data structure that stores all the information about a process. It acts as the *identity* and *state container* of the process.
- **(2 marks — Four Fields):** (i) **Process ID (PID)**, (ii) **Process State**, (iii) **Program Counter (PC)**, (iv) **CPU Registers** (other acceptable: Stack Pointer, Scheduling Info, Memory Management Info, I/O Status, Accounting Info).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A: Comprehensive Process States & PCB `[14 Marks]`

> **(a) Draw and explain the five-state process model with all possible state transitions. Briefly describe what triggers each transition.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The **five-state process model** is the most widely used representation of a process's life cycle, formally described as: **New, Ready, Running, Blocked, and Terminated.**

**State Descriptions (3 Marks):**
1. **New:** Process is being created; PCB is being initialized; not yet admitted to the Ready Queue.
2. **Ready:** Process resides in main memory and is waiting in the Ready Queue for CPU allocation.
3. **Running:** Process is currently executing on the CPU. In a single-core system, only one process is in this state at any instant.
4. **Blocked:** Process has issued a system call for I/O or is waiting for an event; it cannot proceed until that event completes.
5. **Terminated:** Process has finished execution; the OS will eventually deallocate its PCB and resources.

**State Transitions with Triggers (4 Marks):**

| Transition | Triggering Event |
|---|---|
| New → Ready | *Admission* — process creation complete; OS admits it to the Ready Queue. |
| Ready → Running | *Dispatch* — Long-term/short-term scheduler selects this process; CPU is allocated. |
| Running → Ready | *Preemption* (higher-priority process arrives) OR *Time-out* (quantum exhausted) OR *Yield*. |
| Running → Blocked | *Wait* — process issues `read()`, `wait()`, or requests a held resource. |
| Blocked → Ready | *Wake-up* — I/O completes, semaphore is signaled, or waited event occurs. |
| Running → Terminated | *Exit* — process calls `exit()` or is killed by a signal. |

> *(1 bonus mark for stating: a Blocked process CANNOT directly become Running — it must first re-enter the Ready Queue.)*

---

> **(b) What is a Process Control Block? Explain its structure. With a neat diagram, illustrate the role of PCB during context switching.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**PCB Definition (1 Mark):**
The Process Control Block is a kernel data structure that encapsulates all information about a process — its identity, current execution state, memory layout, and accounting data.

**PCB Structure / Fields (2 Marks):**
*(List at least 6 fields with one-line purpose each.)*

| Field | Purpose |
|---|---|
| PID | Unique process identifier |
| State | Current state (New/Ready/Running/Blocked/Terminated) |
| Program Counter | Address of next instruction to execute |
| CPU Registers | Snapshot of all general-purpose and special registers |
| Stack Pointer | Points to top of the process's stack frame |
| Memory Mgmt Info | Base/limit registers or page table pointer |
| Scheduling Info | Priority, quantum, queue pointers |
| I/O Status | Open files, pending I/O requests |
| Accounting Info | CPU time used, time limits, parent PID |

**Context Switching Role (4 Marks):**

A **context switch** is the mechanism that allows the CPU to be multiplexed among multiple processes. The PCB is central to this:

1. **Trigger:** A timer interrupt, I/O completion, or system call invokes the scheduler.
2. **Save State:** The kernel saves the *current* process's PC, registers, stack pointer, and state into its PCB (a "snapshot" of the live execution context).
3. **Schedule:** The scheduler examines the Ready Queue and selects the next process based on the scheduling algorithm (e.g., priority, RMS).
4. **Load State:** The dispatcher's *context restore* routine reads the new process's PCB and reloads its PC, registers, and stack pointer into the CPU.
5. **Resume:** Execution continues at the new process's saved PC, completely transparent to the process itself.

> *Diagrammatic representation is expected — use a flowchart showing CPU ↔ PCB_OLD ↔ Scheduler ↔ Ready Queue ↔ PCB_NEW ↔ CPU.*

---

### Question B: Real-Time Process Scheduling & RMS (Alternative Choice) `[14 Marks]`

> **(a) Define a process in the context of an RTOS. Classify real-time processes based on their timing behavior with suitable examples.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
In an RTOS, a *process* (commonly called a *task*) is a program in execution with **strict timing constraints** — a deadline that must be met. The scheduler's primary goal is *temporal correctness* (meeting deadlines) rather than maximizing throughput or minimizing average waiting time as in general-purpose OSs.

**Classification of Real-Time Processes (5 Marks):**

1. **Periodic Task** — Activated at *regular, fixed intervals* of period $P$.
   *Example:* A sensor in an anti-lock braking system reading wheel speed every 5 ms.
2. **Aperiodic Task** — Activated at *irregular, unpredictable* times.
   *Example:* Arrival of a network packet in a router.
3. **Sporadic Task** — Aperiodic but bounded by a *minimum inter-arrival time* between activations.
   *Example:* Emergency stop button press (must be debounced, no two events within 50 ms).
4. **Hard Real-Time Task** — Missing a deadline = **catastrophic system failure** (loss of life/equipment).
   *Example:* Pacemaker heart-pulse generator, airbag deployment controller.
5. **Soft Real-Time Task** — Missing a deadline = **degraded performance** but no catastrophe.
   *Example:* Video frame decoding, audio streaming buffer fill.

*(Optional +1 mark for the formula $C_i \le D_i \le P_i$ for periodic tasks.)*

---

> **(b) For the following periodic task set, compute the total CPU utilization and apply the Liu-Layland bound to determine RMS schedulability. Comment on the result.** **[7 Marks]**
>
> | Task | $C_i$ | $P_i$ |
> |---|---|---|
> | $\tau_1$ | 2 | 10 |
> | $\tau_2$ | 5 | 20 |
> | $\tau_3$ | 7 | 50 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Per-Task Utilization (1 Mark)**

$$
U_1 = \frac{2}{10} = 0.200, \quad U_2 = \frac{5}{20} = 0.250, \quad U_3 = \frac{7}{50} = 0.140
$$

**Step 2 — Total Utilization (2 Marks)**

$$
U_{total} = 0.200 + 0.250 + 0.140 = 0.590
$$

**Step 3 — Liu-Layland Bound for $n = 3$ (2 Marks)**

$$
U_{bound} = 3 \times \left(2^{1/3} - 1\right) \approx 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7798
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison & Conclusion (2 Marks)**

Since $U_{total} = 0.590 < U_{bound} = 0.7798$, the Liu-Layland sufficient condition is **satisfied**. Therefore, the task set is **guaranteed to be schedulable** under Rate Monotonic Scheduling.

> **Caveat (Bonus 1 mark):** The bound is *sufficient* but not necessary. Even if it had failed, the task set could still be RMS-schedulable — exact response-time analysis or the hyperbolic bound would be required for a definitive verdict.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU 2024 Valuation:**
> 1. **Wrong state transition:** Writing "Blocked → Running" instead of "Blocked → Ready" — instant **2-mark loss** in any 7/14-mark question on process states.
> 2. **Confusing Process with Thread:** A *process* is heavy-weight (own address space, PCB, system resources); a *thread* is a lightweight execution unit *within* a process, sharing the address space. Examiners deduct marks for loose usage.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention PCB during context switching:** Many students describe context switching without mentioning that the state is *saved into the PCB of the old process* and *loaded from the PCB of the new process*. Always anchor your explanation in the PCB.
> 4. **Ignoring context-switch overhead in WCET:** When asked for schedulability, always add $2 T_{cs}$ to the WCET in hard real-time calculations. Omitting this is a **3-mark deduction** in 14-mark analysis questions.
> 5. **Liu-Layland misinterpretation:** Students often state the bound as a *necessary* condition. It is only a *sufficient* condition. Writing "the system will definitely miss a deadline if $U > U_{bound}$" is incorrect.
> 6. **Units:** Always specify units (ms, µs, processes/s). Bare numerical answers without units attract 1-mark deductions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final High-Density Summary — Read This Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

- ✅ A **process** = *program in execution* = code + execution state (PC, registers) + memory (stack, heap, data) + **PCB**.
- ✅ A **PCB** is the kernel's data structure holding *all* metadata of a process — its creation, termination, and context-switching *all* pivot on the PCB.
- ✅ The **Five States** are: **New → Ready → Running → Blocked → Terminated** (and the cycle *Ready → Running → Ready* via preemption/timeout).
- ✅ **Critical rule:** *Blocked → Ready* (NOT *Blocked → Running*). A blocked process *must* re-enter the Ready Queue before being dispatched.
- ✅ **Context switching** = Save state of old process's PCB + Load state of new process's PCB. In RTOS, the overhead is added to WCET.
- ✅ **Real-time processes** are classified as *Periodic / Aperiodic / Sporadic* and *Hard / Soft* real-time.
- ✅ **Periodic task constraint:** $C_i \le D_i \le P_i$.
- ✅ **Per-task utilization:** $U_i = C_i / P_i$. **Total:** $U = \sum U_i$.
- ✅ **Liu-Layland bound (sufficient, not necessary):** $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$, which $\to \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ as $n \to \infty$.
- ✅ **Performance metrics:** $T_{AT} = T_{completion} - T_{arrival}$, $WT = T_{AT} - Burst$, $RT = T_{first\ CPU} - T_{arrival}$, $\eta$ = throughput.
- ✅ **RTOS vs GPOS:** RTOS prioritizes *deadline guarantees and bounded latency*; GPOS prioritizes *throughput and fairness*.
- ✅ **Always carry units** in numerical answers (ms, µs, processes/s).
- ✅ For 14-mark scheduling questions, **draw a Gantt chart** and **show every step of the calculation** — partial marks are awarded liberally.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Threads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT741 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Threads # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Thread (Lightweight Process / LWP):** > A **thread** is the smallest unit of CPU execution and program dispatching within a process. A thread, sometimes called a *lightweight process* (LWP), consists of a **thread ID**, a **program counter (PC)**, a **register set**, a **stack**, and shares its **code section, data section, and operating system resources** (such as open files, signals, and virtual address space) with peer threads belonging to the same process. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, threads are the fundamental **concurrent execution entities** inside any real-time task. The Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) kernel schedules **threads** (not processes) onto the CPU cores, since thread-switching overhead is much lower than process-switching overhead — a critical property when deadlines are measured in microseconds. A standard thread therefore contains the following per-thread resources: | Per-Thread Resource | Purpose | |---|---| | Thread ID (TID) | Unique kernel-level identifier | | Program Counter (PC) | Points to next instruction to execute | | Register Set | Volatile CPU context (general-purpose + status registers) | | Stack | Local variables, return addresses, function frames | | Thread-Local Storage (TLS) | Data private to a single thread | | Priority & Scheduling State | Determines when the RTOS scheduler dispatches it | The remaining components — **text (code) segment, data segment, heap, file descriptors, signal handlers, and global variables** — are shared across all threads of the same process. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen" > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: One Restaurant, Many Chefs** > - **Process** = the entire **restaurant** (kitchen building, pantry, recipes, gas line, utensils). > - **Threads** = the **individual chefs** working inside the same kitchen. > - All chefs (threads) **share the same pantry (heap), recipes (code), and utensils (open files)**, but each chef has **their own cutting board, knife, and order ticket (stack + registers)**. > - Switching between chefs is fast — they just hand over the ticket. Switching between restaurants (processes) requires walking out, locking the door, and starting again. > - In a **real-time restaurant** (RTOS), the head chef (scheduler) must serve VIP orders (hard-deadline tasks) **on time, every time**. This is exactly what an RTOS does: it multiplexes many threads on limited CPU cores, ensuring the **highest-priority runnable thread** always gets the next time slice, with bounded preemption latency. ## 1.3 Why Threads Matter in Real-Time Systems > [!TIP] > **Key Reasons Threads Are Used in RTOS:** > 1. **Faster Context Switching** — typically **1–10 µs** vs. **10–100 µs** for full process switches. > 2. **Cheaper Creation/Termination** — kernel allocates only stack + TCB, not the entire address space. > 3. **Natural Decomposition of Real-Time Workloads** — sensor reading, control law computation, actuator command, and logging can run as four parallel threads. > 4. **Shared Memory Communication** — threads share address space, so inter-thread data exchange is via **shared variables** (no kernel-mode IPC overhead). > 5. **Improved Throughput on Multicore SoCs** — true **parallelism** when threads are pinned to different cores. Standard metrics an RTOS engineer must always recall (frequently tested as **2-mark KTU questions**): - **Context switch time** of an RTOS thread: typically **< 10 µs** - **Interrupt latency** of a hard real-time kernel: typically **< 5 µs** - **Thread creation time**: typically **< 50 µs** - **Minimum thread stack size**: configurable; commonly **256 B – 4 KB** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Threads vs. Processes — Detailed Comparison > [!IMPORTANT] > A *process* is a **program in execution** — a *passive* entity (the binary on disk) becomes *active* once loaded into RAM. A process is fundamentally a **resource-ownership unit**: it owns memory, I/O, files, and at least one thread of control. > A *thread* is the **CPU-execution unit** inside a process. Multiple threads inside one process share everything except CPU-context-related state. | Property | Process | Thread (Lightweight Process) | |---|---|---| | Address Space | **Independent** virtual address space | **Shared** within the parent process | | Creation Cost | **High** (~hundreds of µs) | **Low** (single-digit µs) | | Context-Switch Cost | **High** — TLB flush, page-table switch | **Low** — only register + stack pointer swap | | Communication | IPC: pipes, sockets, shared memory (kernel-mediated) | Shared global variables (no kernel call) | | Resource Ownership | **Owns** files, memory, sockets, signals | **Shares** all of the above | | Failure Isolation | Crash of one process does not affect others | Crash of one thread can kill the entire process | | Scheduling Granularity | Coarse | **Fine — the RTOS schedules threads** | | Example | `bash.exe`, `chrome.exe` | Worker pool of `nginx`, RT task pool in VxWorks | ## 2.2 Thread States and Lifecycle (KTU High-Yield Topic) A thread in any RTOS can exist in one of the following **five canonical states**. KTU 2024 Scheme frequently asks for state diagrams and transition-trigger questions. | State | Description | Typical Trigger to Enter | Typical Trigger to Exit | |---|---|---|---| | **New** | Thread object created, not yet ready | `pthread_create()` returns | Initialization complete | | **Ready** | Eligible to run, waiting for CPU | Creation done / I/O complete / preempted by higher-priority thread | Scheduler dispatches it | | **Running** | Actively executing on a CPU core | Dispatched by scheduler | Preempted, blocked, terminated | | **Blocked (Waiting)** | Awaiting an event (I/O, semaphore, mutex, delay) | `sem_wait()`, `pthread_cond_wait()`, `usleep()` | Event occurs, timeout fires | | **Terminated (Dead)** | Execution finished, TCB still exists until joined | Thread function returns / `pthread_exit()` | `pthread_join()` reaps it | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Frequently Tested Point:** "Differentiate between *Ready* and *Running* states." Answer: A *Ready* thread is loaded into the **run queue** and is **eligible** for execution; a *Running* thread is **currently on a CPU core** executing instructions. In a uniprocessor, exactly one thread is in *Running*; on an N-core SoC, up to N threads run in true parallel. ## 2.3 Types of Threads ### 2.3.1 User-Level Threads (ULT) - Thread management done **entirely in user space** by a **thread library** (e.g., GNU `Pth`, early `java.lang.Thread` in Green-Threads model). - Kernel is **unaware** of thread existence — it sees only one process. - **Pros:** extremely fast creation/switch (no syscall), custom scheduling, no kernel privilege changes. - **Cons:** **blocking system call blocks the entire process**; cannot exploit multicore parallelism; no kernel preemption of individual threads. ### 2.3.2 Kernel-Level Threads (KLT) - Kernel **directly** knows and schedules each thread — implemented inside the OS (e.g., Windows threads, Linux `NPTL` threads, VxWorks tasks, FreeRTOS tasks). - **Pros:** true parallelism on SMP, blocking I/O only blocks one thread, kernel can preempt any thread. - **Cons:** slightly slower context switch (kernel transition), more memory per thread. > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Standard:** Modern RTOS kernels (FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX, RTEMS) are **kernel-level thread** systems. Whenever KTU exam asks *"Which thread model does an RTOS use?"* the answer is **kernel-level threads**, because deterministic scheduling demands kernel visibility of every thread. ### 2.3.3 Hybrid (M-to-N) Threads - Maps **M user threads** onto **N kernel threads** (Solaris LWP, Go runtime goroutines, Rust `tokio` workers). - Combines flexibility of ULT with parallelism of KLT. ## 2.4 Multithreading Models | Model | Many-to-One | One-to-One | Many-to-Many | |---|---|---|---| | Mapping | M user → 1 kernel thread | 1 user → 1 kernel thread | M user → N kernel (M ≥ N) | | Parallelism on Multicore | **No** | **Yes** | **Yes** | | Thread Library | GNU Pth | POSIX `pthreads`, Win32, Linux | Solaris, Go runtime | | Used in RTOS? | No (not deterministic) | **Yes — standard** | Rare in hard RTOS | ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Threads) > [!IMPORTANT] > Threads are not "math-heavy," but the following **scheduling, context-switch, and CPU-utilization formulas** are recurring 14-mark KTU questions.
