Use `\vert` instead of `|` in any markdown table cell. | # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | CPU Utilization under n threads | $U = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - p_i)$ | $p_i$ = fraction of time thread $i$ computes | | 2 | **Context-Switch Time** | $T_{cs} = T_{save} + T_{load} + T_{cache} + T_{TLB}$ | All in **µs**; for RTOS $T_{cs} < 10\,\mu s$ | | 3 | **Thread Throughput** | $\theta = \dfrac{N_{threads}}{T_{total}}$ | threads completed per second | | 4 | **Thread Turnaround Time** | $T_{turn} = T_{finish} - T_{arrive}$ | seconds | | 5 | **Response Time** | $T_{resp} = T_{first\_run} - T_{arrive}$ | seconds | | 6 | **Waiting Time** | $T_{wait} = T_{turn} - T_{burst}$ | seconds | | 7 | **Amdahl's Law (speedup)** | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$ | $f$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = cores/threads | | 8 | **Hard Deadline Guarantee (Liu & Layland)** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{P_i} \le U_{bound}$ | RMS: $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | | 9 | **Worst-Case Execution Time** | $WCET = T_{compute} + T_{preempt} + T_{interrupt}$ | seconds | | 10 | **Preemptive Priority Inversion Bound** (Priority Ceiling Protocol) | $B_{inv} \le C_{cs} \cdot n_{low}$ | $C_{cs}$ = critical-section time | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse $T_{turn}$ (turnaround) with $T_{resp}$ (response). Turnaround counts from arrival to **completion**; response counts from arrival to **first scheduling**. In real-time, **response time** is what determines whether a deadline is met. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive ECU** (AUTOSAR OS / OSEK-OS): a separate thread per control function — engine, brake-by-wire, ADAS perception. - **Aerospace flight control** (VxWorks 653): time- and space-partitioned threads, ARINC-653 scheduling. - **Industrial PLCs**: ladder logic compiled to RTOS threads, deterministic cycle time of 1–10 ms. - **IoT edge devices** (FreeRTOS on ESP32, Zephyr on Nordic): sensor threads, comm-stack threads, OTA-update threads running cooperatively. - **Robotics** (ROS 2 on Xenomai): real-time control loop threads + non-real-time perception threads. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > Threads involve **analytical reasoning** more than algebra. This section therefore presents the **step-by-step derivations** of (a) the **Amdahl speedup law** as it applies to multithreaded RTOS workloads, and (b) the **Rate-Monotonic Utilization Bound** for thread sets — both classical KTU 14-mark derivations. --- ## 3.1 Derivation: Amdahl's Law for Multithreaded Speedup **Problem statement:** A real-time control application has a parallelizable fraction $f$ of its workload distributed across $N$ identical CPU cores (each core running one thread). Derive the maximum achievable speedup $S$. **Step 1 — Decompose execution time.** Let the total sequential execution time on a single core be normalized to $1$. Then: $$ T_{total} = T_{seq} + T_{par} = (1 - f) + f $$ **Step 2 — Parallelize the parallel fraction across N cores.** When $f$ is split evenly across $N$ threads running on $N$ cores, the parallel portion completes in $f / N$ of the original time: $$ T_{parallel}(N) = (1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N} $$ **Step 3 — Define speedup as the ratio of single-core time to $N$-core time.** $$ S(N) = \dfrac{T_{total}}{T_{parallel}(N)} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}} $$ **Step 4 — Take the limit as $N \to \infty$ to find the asymptotic ceiling.** $$ \lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \dfrac{1}{1 - f} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion (Amdahl, 1967):** Even with *infinite* cores, the speedup of a multithreaded program is bounded by $\dfrac{1}{1 - f}$. If only 5% of the code is sequential, the maximum possible speedup — no matter how many threads — is $\dfrac{1}{0.95} \approx 5.26\times$. **Step 5 — RTOS-specific refinement.** In an RTOS, additional overheads — context-switch time $T_{cs}$, scheduler decision time $T_{sch}$, and cache-miss penalty $T_{cm}$ — further reduce the effective speedup: $$ S_{RTOS}(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N} + \dfrac{N \cdot T_{cs} + T_{sch} + T_{cm}}{T_{total}}} $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked *"Why doesn't doubling cores double RTOS performance?"*, write the Amdahl equation above and isolate the $T_{cs} \cdot N$ term — that is the **scheduling-tax** of multithreading on a real-time kernel. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Liu & Layland Rate-Monotonic Utilization Bound **Problem statement:** For $n$ periodic real-time threads scheduled by **fixed-priority Rate-Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)**, derive the least upper bound on total CPU utilization $U$ that still guarantees all deadlines are met. **Step 1 — Assumptions (Liu & Layland 1973).** - $n$ independent, periodic threads. - Thread $T_i$ has period $P_i$ and worst-case execution time $C_i$. - Priority is the **inverse of period**: shorter period → higher priority. - Zero context-switch overhead, no blocking, deadlines equal periods. **Step 2 — Define the utilization.** $$ U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{P_i} $$ **Step 3 — Critical instant analysis.** The worst-case response time of any thread $T_i$ occurs at the *critical instant* — when all higher-priority threads release a job at the same instant. The total demand on $T_i$ in the window $[0, P_i]$ is: $$ W_i = C_i + \sum_{j : P_j < P_i} \left\lceil \dfrac{P_i}{P_j} \right\rceil C_j $$ **Step 4 — Schedulability condition.** $$ W_i \le P_i \quad \forall\, i $$ **Step 5 — Convert to utilization bound.** For a set of $n$ threads whose utilization is **at most** $U_{bound}$, the above condition is **always** satisfied. Liu & Layland showed: $$ U_{bound}(n) = n \cdot \left(2^{1/n} - 1 \right) $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Numerical values to memorize (KTU favorite 2-mark question):** > > $$\begin{aligned} > U_{bound}(1) &= 1.000 \\ > U_{bound}(2) &= 0.828 \\ > U_{bound}(3) &= 0.780 \\ > U_{bound}(4) &= 0.757 \\ > U_{bound}(5) &= 0.743 \\ > U_{bound}(n \to \infty) &= \ln 2 \approx 0.693 > \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6 — Worked numerical example.** Consider 3 threads with $C_1=1, C_2=2, C_3=3$ ms and $P_1=4, P_2=6, P_3=10$ ms. Is the task set schedulable under RMS? $$ U = \dfrac{1}{4} + \dfrac{2}{6} + \dfrac{3}{10} = 0.250 + 0.333 + 0.300 = 0.883 $$ Bound for $n=3$ is $0.780$. Since $0.883 > 0.780$, the **bound is insufficient to guarantee** schedulability — but it does **not** prove failure. We must perform the **exact response-time test**: $$ W_3 = C_3 + \left\lceil \tfrac{10}{4} \right\rceil C_1 + \left\lceil \tfrac{10}{6} \right\rceil C_2 = 3 + 3(1) + 2(2) = 10 = P_3 $$ So $T_3$ is **just barely** schedulable. ✅ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Thread Properties vs. KTU Question Pattern | KTU Question Type | Key Property to Apply | Marks | |---|---|---| | "Define thread. Differentiate from process." | Section 2.1 table | 3 or 7 | | "Draw and explain thread state diagram." | Section 2.2 + Mermaid §4 | 7 | | "Compare ULT vs KLT." | Section 2.3 | 7 | | "Justify hard real-time use of kernel threads." | Section 2.3.2 | 3 | | "Derive Amdahl's law for an RTOS workload." | Section 3.1 | 14 | | "Compute utilization bound under RMS." | Section 3.2, memorize $U_{bound}$ values | 7 or 14 | | "Why does infinite cores not give infinite speedup?" | Section 3.1, step 4 | 3 | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Process vs. Threads — Shared vs. Per-Thread Resources ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PROC["PROCESS A — VIRTUAL ADDRESS SPACE"] direction TB C["SHARED CODE SEGMENT
.text"] D["SHARED DATA SEGMENT
.data .bss globals"] H["SHARED HEAP
malloc region"] F["SHARED FILE TABLE
open fds, sockets"] subgraph T1["THREAD T1"] R1["Registers + PC"] S1["Stack T1"] TID1["TID = 1"] end subgraph T2["THREAD T2"] R2["Registers + PC"] S2["Stack T2"] TID2["TID = 2"] end subgraph T3["THREAD T3"] R3["Registers + PC"] S3["Stack T3"] TID3["TID = 3"] end end C --- T1 C --- T2 C --- T3 D --- T1 D --- T2 D --- T3 H --- T1 H --- T2 H --- T3 F --- T1 F --- T2 F --- T3 ``` ## 4.2 Thread State Transition Diagram (RTOS Kernel View) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> NEW : pthread_create / task spawn NEW --> READY : initialization complete READY --> RUNNING : scheduler dispatch RUNNING --> READY : preempted by higher-priority thread OR time slice expired RUNNING --> BLOCKED : mutex wait / semaphore wait / delay / I/O BLOCKED --> READY : event occurs / timeout / signal RUNNING --> TERMINATED : thread function returns / pthread_exit TERMINATED --> [*] : resources reaped by join or kernel ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Callout:** Always label the *trigger* for each transition (preemption, blocking call, event, termination). A diagram without triggers loses at least 2 of 7 marks. ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Multithreaded RTOS Application ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Hard Real Time Thread
PRIORITY 99
10 kHz control loop] -->|shared heap| B[Sensor Fusion Thread
PRIORITY 80
1 kHz] A -->|signal / msgq| C[Actuator Driver Thread
PRIORITY 90
triggered] B -->|shared ring buffer| D[Communication Thread
PRIORITY 50
CAN / Ethernet] D -->|UDP / TCP| E[Cloud Gateway] F[Watchdog Thread
PRIORITY 1
lowest] -.->|heartbeat| A F -.->|reset| G[Hardware Reboot] subgraph KERNEL["RTOS KERNEL"] SCH["Fixed-Priority Preemptive Scheduler"] TMR["High-Resolution Timer Tick 1 us"] IPC["Inter-Thread IPC: queues, semaphores, mutexes"] end A -.-> SCH B -.-> SCH C -.-> SCH D -.-> SCH F -.-> SCH ``` ## 4.4 Multithreading Models Comparison (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph MTO["MANY-TO-ONE MODEL"] U1a[User Thread 1] --> K1[Kernel Thread 1] U1b[User Thread 2] --> K1 U1c[User Thread 3] --> K1 end subgraph OTO["ONE-TO-ONE MODEL — USED IN RTOS"] U2a[User Thread 1] --> K2a[Kernel Thread 1] U2b[User Thread 2] --> K2b[Kernel Thread 2] U2c[User Thread 3] --> K2c[Kernel Thread 3] end subgraph MTM["MANY-TO-MANY MODEL"] U3a[User Thread 1] --> K3a[Kernel Thread 1] U3b[User Thread 2] --> K3a U3c[User Thread 3] --> K3b[Kernel Thread 2] end ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks Equivalent) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: Define a thread. Why are threads called "lightweight processes"?** **Model Answer:** A **thread** is the smallest unit of CPU execution within a process. It consists of a thread ID, program counter, register set, and a private stack, and shares the code, data, heap, and OS resources with other threads of the same process. Threads are called *lightweight processes* because creating, switching, and terminating a thread requires far less kernel work than doing the same for a full process — there is **no address-space switch, no TLB flush, and no page-table reload** involved. Typical thread creation takes **single-digit microseconds** vs. **hundreds of microseconds** for a process, and context-switch time drops by an order of magnitude. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]` **Q: List any three differences between user-level threads and kernel-level threads.** **Model Answer:** | # | User-Level Thread (ULT) | Kernel-Level Thread (KLT) | |---|---|---| | 1 | Managed entirely by a user-space thread library; kernel is unaware. | Managed and scheduled directly by the OS kernel. | | 2 | A blocking system call blocks the **entire process** (all ULTs). | A blocking system call blocks **only that one KLT**; others continue. | | 3 | Cannot exploit true hardware parallelism on a multicore SoC. | Provides **true parallel execution** on SMP/multicore systems. | > [!NOTE] > **One-line bonus (gains +1 mark):** Hard real-time systems (VxWorks, QNX, FreeRTOS) use **kernel-level threads** because deterministic scheduling requires kernel visibility of every thread. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > - When asked for a **state diagram**, students often draw boxes without labeling **transition triggers** — deducting up to 2 of 7 marks. > - In a **comparison question**, the verdict line ("Therefore, KLT is preferred for RTOS") is **mandatory**; missing it costs 1 mark. > - For **Amdahl's law**, you must show the **limit as N → ∞** explicitly — a common omission. --- ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **five states of a thread** and the events that cause transitions between them. (7 marks) **(b)** Compare **user-level threads, kernel-level threads, and hybrid threads** in detail. Justify which model is used in a hard real-time operating system. (7 marks) #### Model Solution — Part (a) The **five canonical thread states** (see §4.2 Mermaid diagram) are: 1. **New** — Thread Control Block (TCB) is allocated, stack is reserved, but the thread is not yet eligible to run. Trigger to leave *New*: `pthread_create()` finishes initialization. 2. **Ready** — Thread is loaded in the run queue and waiting for the CPU. Trigger to leave *Ready*: the **scheduler** dispatches it. 3. **Running** — Thread is currently executing on a CPU core. Trigger to leave *Running*: either **preempted by a higher-priority thread** (preemptive scheduling), **time slice expired**, **blocked on a synchronization primitive**, or **terminated**. 4. **Blocked (Waiting)** — Thread cannot run because it is awaiting an event — a semaphore, mutex, message-queue receive, I/O completion, or a `delay()` call. Trigger to leave *Blocked*: the awaited event occurs (or a timeout fires). 5. **Terminated** — Thread function has returned or called `pthread_exit()`. The TCB and stack still exist until another thread calls `pthread_join()` (or the kernel reaps it). **Key transitions to highlight in the answer:** - *Running → Ready*: preemption by a higher-priority thread (this is the heart of real-time preemptive scheduling). - *Running → Blocked*: voluntary yield to a synchronization object. - *Blocked → Ready*: not directly to Running — the scheduler must re-dispatch. #### Model Solution — Part (b) | Property | User-Level Threads (ULT) | Kernel-Level Threads (KLT) | Hybrid (M-to-N) | |---|---|---|---| | Thread mgmt | User library | Kernel | Both | | Blocking syscall | Blocks **all** ULTs | Blocks only **one** KLT | Blocks only **one** bound KLT | | True parallelism | **No** | **Yes** | Yes (up to N) | | Context-switch speed | **Fastest** (no syscall) | Slower (kernel-mode switch) | Fast for user-thread switch | | Custom scheduler | **Yes** | No (kernel decides) | Yes for user threads | | Used in RTOS? | **No** | **Yes — standard** | Rare | | Example | GNU Pth, early Java Green Threads | POSIX pthreads, VxWorks tasks, FreeRTOS tasks | Solaris LWP, Go goroutines | **Justification (verdict — mandatory):** A *hard real-time* system requires **deterministic, bounded response times** and the ability to run on **multicore SoCs in parallel**. Only **kernel-level threads** satisfy both requirements — the kernel must be aware of, and be able to preempt, every individual thread. Therefore, **all certified hard real-time operating systems (VxWorks 653, QNX Neutrino, RTEMS, LynxOS-178, FreeRTOS) implement the kernel-level thread model**. --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **(a)** Derive **Amdahl's Law** for the speedup of a multithreaded workload, and explain why doubling CPU cores does **not** double performance in an RTOS. (7 marks) **(b)** For a set of three periodic real-time threads scheduled by **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)**, derive the **Liu & Layland utilization bound** and state its numerical values for $n = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ and $n \to \infty$. (7 marks) #### Model Solution — Part (a) (Reproduce the derivation in §3.1, then conclude:) $$ S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}} \quad ; \quad \lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \dfrac{1}{1 - f} $$ **Why doubling cores ≠ doubling performance in RTOS:** 1. **Sequential bottleneck:** the term $(1-f)$ in the denominator is unaffected by $N$. 2. **Scheduling overhead:** each additional thread introduces context-switch time $T_{cs}$ per scheduling decision, so the effective speedup becomes $$ S_{RTOS}(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N} + \dfrac{N \cdot T_{cs} + T_{sch} + T_{cm}}{T_{total}}} $$ 3. **Cache and TLB contention:** running N threads on N cores dilutes L1/L2 cache, raising $T_{cm}$. 4. **Synchronization blocking:** mutexes and spinlocks serialize threads, increasing the effective sequential fraction. #### Model Solution — Part (b) The Liu & Layland bound is $$ U_{bound}(n) = n \cdot \left(2^{1/n} - 1 \right) $$ Numerical values: $$ \begin{aligned} U_{bound}(1) &= 1.000 \\ U_{bound}(2) &= 0.828 \\ U_{bound}(3) &= 0.780 \\ U_{bound}(4) &= 0.757 \\ U_{bound}(5) &= 0.743 \\ \lim_{n \to \infty} U_{bound}(n) &= \ln 2 \approx 0.693 \end{aligned} $$ The derivation uses **critical-instant analysis**: at $t=0$ all higher-priority threads release a job simultaneously, and the demand on the lowest-priority thread is $$ W_i = C_i + \sum_{j: P_j < P_i} \left\lceil \dfrac{P_i}{P_j} \right\rceil C_j \le P_i $$ A utilization of $U \le n(2^{1/n}-1)$ **guarantees** that the inequality holds for every thread. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Card — Threads in RTOS (Module 1)** > > ✅ **Definition**: Thread = TC + registers + PC + stack, sharing process address space. > ✅ **Lightweight**: Context-switch < 10 µs vs. process switch 10–100 µs. > ✅ **States**: New → Ready → Running → Blocked/Terminated. **Six valid transitions** — know triggers. > ✅ **Thread vs. Process**: Thread shares; process owns. Same address space = shared globals; different stacks. > ✅ **ULT vs. KLT**: KLT preferred in RTOS for determinism + SMP parallelism. > ✅ **Multithreading models**: M-to-1, 1-to-1, M-to-N. RTOS uses 1-to-1. > ✅ **Amdahl's Law**: $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + f/N}$, ceiling $\dfrac{1}{1-f}$ as $N \to \infty$. > ✅ **RMS Utilization Bound**: $U_{bound}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$; values 1.000, 0.828, 0.780, 0.757, 0.743, 0.693. > ✅ **Response time vs. Turnaround time**: response = arrival → first dispatch; turnaround = arrival → finish. > ✅ **Hard real-time RTOSes** (VxWorks, QNX, FreeRTOS, RTEMS): kernel-level threads, fixed-priority preemptive scheduling, bounded latency. > ✅ **Typical metrics** (memorize): context-switch < 10 µs, interrupt latency < 5 µs, thread create < 50 µs. > ✅ **Pitfall**: ULT cannot exploit multicore — always state this in comparisons. > ✅ **Pitfall**: Amdahl's law must include $N \cdot T_{cs}$ term for RTOS-grade analysis. > ✅ **Pitfall**: $U \le U_{bound}$ is **sufficient but not necessary** for RMS schedulability — exact response-time test is the final arbiter.

topics.title: Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT741 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Scheduling in an RTOS?

In the context of **Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS)**, **Scheduling** is the formal process of determining the *order* in which a set of competing computational tasks (or *jobs*) are assigned processor time, such that every job completes its execution **before or at** its specified *deadline*. Unlike general-purpose operating systems that optimize for **throughput** or **fairness**, RTOS scheduling optimizes for **temporal correctness** — meeting deadlines is non-negotiable, especially in **hard real-time systems**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Mandatory to memorize):**
> *Real-time scheduling is the allocation of processor time to a set of concurrent tasks subject to temporal constraints (deadlines, periods, jitter), under the objective of guaranteeing that every task instance (job) finishes before its deadline.*

## 1.2 Fundamental Task Terminology (KTU High-Frequency)

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| **Job** | $J_i$ | A single unit of work / instance of a task |
| **Task** | $T_i$ | A set of related jobs with a common purpose |
| **Release Time (Arrival)** | $a_i$ or $r_i$ | Time at which a job becomes ready |
| **Execution Time (WCET)** | $e_i$ or $C_i$ | Worst-case execution time on the processor |
| **Absolute Deadline** | $d_i$ | Time by which the job must finish |
| **Relative Deadline** | $D_i$ | $D_i = d_i - r_i$ |
| **Period** | $p_i$ | Time between successive releases of a periodic task |
| **Jitter** | — | Variation in release/completion times |

> [!NOTE]
> For *periodic tasks*, deadline is typically equal to period: **$D_i = p_i$**. This is the **implicit deadline** model heavily used in KTU problems.

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The Hospital Emergency Room

Imagine a hospital **Triage Nurse** who must decide which patient the doctor sees next. Patients arrive continuously, each with a *severity level* (priority), a *required consultation time* (WCET), and a *critical window* (deadline — e.g., a heart-attack patient must be seen within 10 minutes).

- If the nurse simply serves patients **First-Come-First-Served (FCFS)** → some critical patients may die waiting. (Bad scheduling)
- If she prioritizes the patient with the **closest critical window first** → she is doing **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)**.
- If she has a fixed rule: *“The more critical (higher rate of deterioration), the higher the priority”* → she is doing **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)**.

This nurse is the **Scheduler**, the doctor is the **CPU**, and the patients are the **Tasks**. The hospital's survival depends on her logic — just like a rocket flight controller or anti-lock braking system depends on its RTOS scheduler.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU:**
> - **Hard Real-Time**: Missing a deadline = **system failure** (e.g., airbag, pacemakers).
> - **Soft Real-Time**: Missing a deadline = degraded quality, but no catastrophe (e.g., video streaming).
> - **Firm Real-Time**: Late result is discarded (e.g., weather forecasting data).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Real-Time Scheduling Algorithms

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes a structured classification. Every scheduling algorithm can be categorized along **three orthogonal axes**:

1. **Static vs. Dynamic Priority**
   - *Static*: Priority is fixed at design time (e.g., Rate Monotonic).
   - *Dynamic*: Priority can change at runtime (e.g., EDF).
2. **Preemptive vs. Non-Preemptive**
   - *Preemptive*: A higher-priority job can interrupt a running lower-priority one.
   - *Non-Preemptive*: Once a job starts, it runs to completion.
3. **Offline (Table-Driven) vs. Online (Clock/Event-Driven)**
   - *Offline*: Schedule is computed before system starts and stored as a table.
   - *Online*: Decisions are made dynamically during execution.

## 2.2 The Canonical Algorithms (Module 1 Core)

### A. Clock-Driven (Time-Triggered) Scheduling
- Decisions are made at **specific time instants** (clock ticks).
- The schedule is essentially a **precomputed lookup table** or a cyclic executive.
- Best for **highly predictable** systems with static workload (e.g., automotive ECUs).
- **Disadvantage**: Fragile if aperiodic events dominate.

### B. Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) — *Liu & Layland, 1973*
- **Static priority**, **preemptive**.
- **Rule**: Shorter period → higher priority.
- **Assumption**: Tasks are *independent*, *periodic*, $D_i = p_i$, and have *known WCET*.
- **Optimal**: Among all *static-priority* algorithms, RMS is optimal (Liu & Layland proved it).
- **Utilization Bound** (Sufficient but not necessary):

$$U_{RMS}(n) = n \cdot \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

As $n \to \infty$, $U_{RMS} \to \ln(2) \approx 0.693$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** For small $n$ values:
> - $n=1$: bound = **1.000**
> - $n=2$: bound = **0.828**
> - $n=3$: bound = **0.779**
> - $n=4$: bound = **0.757**

### C. Earliest Deadline First (EDF)
- **Dynamic priority**, **preemptive**.
- **Rule**: The job with the **earliest absolute deadline** runs next.
- **Optimal**: EDF can schedule *any* task set with utilization $U \le 1$ (a *necessary and sufficient* condition).
- **Drawback**: Higher runtime overhead due to dynamic priority computation.

### D. Least Laxity First (LLF)
- **Dynamic priority**, **preemptive**.
- **Rule**: Task with the **smallest laxity** (slack time) is scheduled first.
- **Laxity** at time $t$: $L_i(t) = d_i - t - e_i^{remaining}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute values and ceiling functions are written using `\vert` and `\lceil \rceil` to preserve the markdown table structure. No raw `|` symbols appear inside cells.

| # | Formula / Theorem | Mathematical Expression | Variables | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Liu & Layland Utilization Bound (RMS)** | $U_{RMS}(n) = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$ | $n$ = number of tasks | Quick pass/fail test for RM schedulability |
| 2 | **Total Processor Utilization** | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | $C_i$ = WCET, $T_i$ = period | Computes the load |
| 3 | **Hyperbolic Bound (RMS — tighter)** | $\prod_{i=1}^{n} \left( U_i + 1 \right) \le 2$ | $U_i = C_i/T_i$ | Less pessimistic than Liu-Layland |
| 4 | **Exact Response Time (RMS)** | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \dfrac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | $hp(i)$ = higher-priority tasks | Solved by fixed-point iteration |
| 5 | **Response-Time Test** | Task set schedulable iff $\forall i, R_i \le D_i$ | — | Exact schedulability test |
| 6 | **Laxity (Slack)** | $L_i(t) = d_i - t - C_i^{rem}$ | — | Used in LLF algorithm |
| 7 | **Idle Time Fraction (EDF)** | $1 - U$ | — | Slack capacity for aperiodic tasks |
| 8 | **Time Demand Function (TDF)** | $W_i(t) = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \dfrac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | — | Plotting method for analysis |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

Real-time scheduling theory is the **mathematical backbone** of:
- **Aerospace flight control** (NASA uses RMS-derived schedulers in CubeSats).
- **Automotive AUTOSAR** systems in modern cars.
- **Medical devices** — pacemakers, infusion pumps.
- **Industrial robotics** — synchronized arm controllers.
- **5G baseband processing** — deadline-bound radio frame scheduling.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 viva voce, you may be asked: *"Why is RMS optimal among static-priority algorithms?"*
> **Answer**: Liu & Layland (1973) proved via a *critical instant* argument that any task set schedulable by *any* static-priority algorithm is also schedulable by RMS. This is because RMS assigns priorities proportional to urgency (shorter period = more frequent = more urgent).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Liu & Layland Utilization Bound

The classical proof rests on the **critical-instant theorem** and an induction on the number of tasks. We present a structured walk-through for KTU-level understanding.

### Step 1: The Critical Instant
For a static-priority preemptive scheduler, the **worst-case completion time** of a job of task $T_k$ occurs when:
- All higher-priority tasks $T_j$ (with $j \in hp(k)$) release a job **at the same instant** (the critical instant).
- The job of $T_k$ is also released at that instant.
- We need to check whether this job of $T_k$ can complete before its deadline $D_k$.

### Step 2: Constructing the Worst-Case Response Time
In a time window of length $t$, the number of jobs released by $T_j$ is at most $\left\lceil t / T_j \right\rceil$. Each such job demands $C_j$ time units. So the total processor demand on $T_k$ within time $t$ is:

$$W_k(t) = C_k + \sum_{j \in hp(k)} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

### Step 3: The Fixed-Point Iteration
The actual worst-case response time $R_k$ is the smallest $t$ such that $W_k(t) \le t$. Equivalently:

$$R_k = C_k + \sum_{j \in hp(k)} \left\lceil \frac{R_k}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

We solve this by **iteration**:
- **Initialize** $R_k^{(0)} = C_k$.
- **Iterate** $R_k^{(m+1)} = W_k\!\left(R_k^{(m)}\right)$.
- **Stop** when $R_k^{(m+1)} = R_k^{(m)}$ (fixed point reached) or when $R_k^{(m)} > D_k$ (failure).

### Step 4: Sufficient Bound (Liu & Layland)
A *closed-form* sufficient bound is obtained by a worst-case summation over the smallest period. The result is:

$$U_{RMS}(n) = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$

### Step 5: Numerical Limiting Behaviour
Using L'Hôpital's rule on $\lim_{n \to \infty} n(2^{1/n}-1)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty} n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)
&= \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{2^{1/n} - 1}{1/n}  \\
&\text{Let } x = 1/n \text{ so } x \to 0: \\
&= \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{2^{x} - 1}{x} \\
&\text{Using the standard limit } \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{a^x - 1}{x} = \ln a: \\
&= \ln 2 \approx 0.6931
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, **no static-priority scheduler can guarantee 100% utilization** — the theoretical ceiling for large task sets is ~**69.3%**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Consider three periodic tasks under Rate Monotonic Scheduling:

| Task | Period $T_i$ | WCET $C_i$ | Priority (by RMS) |
|------|--------------|------------|--------------------|
| T1   | 20 ms        | 5 ms       | Highest            |
| T2   | 50 ms        | 10 ms      | Middle             |
| T3   | 100 ms       | 20 ms      | Lowest             |

**Sub-question (a):** Compute total utilization and check Liu–Layland bound.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_1 &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{5}{20} = 0.25 \\
U_2 &= \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{10}{50} = 0.20 \\
U_3 &= \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{20}{100} = 0.20 \\
U_{total} &= 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.20 = 0.65
\end{aligned}
$$

Liu–Layland bound for $n = 3$:

$$U_{RMS}(3) = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7798$$

Since $0.65 < 0.7798$, the task set is **schedulable under RMS**. ✓

**Sub-question (b):** Compute exact worst-case response time of T3 via iteration.

Higher-priority set: $hp(3) = \{1, 2\}$.

**Iteration 1:** $R_3^{(1)} = C_3 = 20$ ms.

**Iteration 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(2)} &= C_3 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3^{(1)}}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3^{(1)}}{T_2} \right\rceil C_2 \\
&= 20 + \left\lceil \frac{20}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 5 + \left\lceil \frac{20}{50} \right\rceil \cdot 10 \\
&= 20 + (1)(5) + (1)(10) = 35 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(3)} &= 20 + \left\lceil \frac{35}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 5 + \left\lceil \frac{35}{50} \right\rceil \cdot 10 \\
&= 20 + (2)(5) + (1)(10) = 20 + 10 + 10 = 40 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 4:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(4)} &= 20 + \left\lceil \frac{40}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 5 + \left\lceil \frac{40}{50} \right\rceil \cdot 10 \\
&= 20 + (2)(5) + (1)(10) = 40 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $R_3^{(4)} = R_3^{(3)} = 40$ ms, the iteration has converged. **Fixed point: $R_3 = 40$ ms**. Since $D_3 = T_3 = 100$ ms and $40 < 100$, T3 meets its deadline. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Matrix for KTU Problem Identification**
>
> | If the question gives... | Then use... |
> |--------------------------|-------------|
> | Utilization only, asks pass/fail | **Liu-Layland bound** |
> | Tighter pass/fail with same info | **Hyperbolic bound** |
> | Specific jobs, asks for $R_i$ | **Fixed-point iteration** |
> | Multiple algorithms to compare | **Time-Demand Function plot** |
> | Aperiodic + periodic mixed | **Background/Polling/Deferrable server** (advanced module) |

---

## 3.3 Derivation Outline: Why EDF is Optimal (Horn's Theorem)

EDF is *dominant* — it never misses a deadline that any other algorithm could meet, provided no idle time exists before a deadline. Proof sketch:

1. **Assume** a feasible schedule $S$ exists. Construct an *earliest-deadline schedule* $S'$.
2. Whenever $S$ schedules a job $J_a$ with later deadline before a job $J_b$ with earlier deadline, **swap** them.
3. Swapping cannot increase the maximum lateness $L_{max}$ because earlier deadlines are *moved forward*.
4. By induction, $S'$ is at least as good as $S$. Hence EDF is optimal.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree of Real-Time Scheduling Algorithms

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Real-Time Scheduling Algorithms]:::root --> B[Static Priority]
    A --> C[Dynamic Priority]
    A --> D[Hybrid / Mixed]

    B --> B1[Rate Monotonic RMS]
    B --> B2[Deadline Monotonic DMS]

    C --> C1[Earliest Deadline First EDF]
    C --> C2[Least Laxity First LLF]
    C --> C3[Maximum Urgency First MUF]

    D --> D1[Proportional Share]
    D --> D2[Table Driven Cyclic Executive]

    B1 --> B1a[Liu Layland 1973]
    C1 --> C1a[Optimal when U le 1]

    classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef staticNode fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef dynamicNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000
    classDef hybridNode fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000

    class A root
    class B,B1,B2,B1a staticNode
    class C,C1,C2,C3,C1a dynamicNode
    class D,D1,D2 hybridNode
```

## 4.2 Task Lifecycle State Machine in an RTOS Scheduler

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Released: Job arrives at time a_i
    Released --> Ready: Placed in priority queue
    Ready --> Running: Scheduler dispatches job
    Running --> Ready: Preempted by higher priority job
    Running --> Completed: Execution finished
    Completed --> [*]: Deadline d_i met
    Running --> DeadlineMissed: Time exceeds d_i

    DeadlineMissed --> [*]: System reports failure
    note right of Running
        Context switch occurs
        when preemption is
        triggered
    end note

    note right of DeadlineMissed
        In HARD real-time systems
        this is a CATASTROPHIC
        failure event
    end note
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture — How a Scheduler Interacts with the RTOS Kernel

```mermaid
graph LR
    APP1[Application Task T1] --> KQ[Kernel Ready Queue]
    APP2[Application Task T2] --> KQ
    APP3[Application Task T3] --> KQ

    TIC[Hardware Timer Tick] --> SCH[Scheduling Algorithm Module]
    EVT[Event Interrupt] --> SCH

    KQ --> SCH
    SCH --> DEC{Scheduling Decision}
    DEC -->|Dispatch| CPU[CPU Core]
    CPU --> CTC[Context Switch Handler]
    CTC -->|Save state| KQ
    CPU --> EXE[Task Execution]
    EXE --> CTC

    classDef cpuNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef schedNode fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#FF4500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class DEC,CPU cpuNode
    class SCH,KQ,CTC schedNode
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Timeline: RMS vs EDF Behavior

```mermaid
gantt
    title Rate Monotonic vs EDF on a 3 Task Set
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %S
    section RMS Schedule
    T1 exec1 :a1, 0, 5
    T2 exec1 :a2, 5, 10
    T3 exec1 :a3, 15, 20
    T1 exec2 :a4, 20, 5
    T2 exec2 :a5, 25, 10
    T1 exec3 :a7, 40, 5
    T3 exec2 :a8, 45, 20
    section EDF Schedule
    T1 e1 :b1, 0, 5
    T2 e1 :b2, 5, 10
    T3 e1 :b3, 15, 20
    T1 e2 :b4, 20, 5
    T2 e2 :b5, 25, 10
    T1 e3 :b6, 35, 5
    T3 e2 :b7, 40, 20
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Gantt comparison illustrates how EDF reorders jobs dynamically as new deadlines appear, while RMS rigidly follows the static priority order. In low utilization regimes both work; under high utilization, RMS may fail while EDF still succeeds.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the terms: (i) Release time, (ii) Relative deadline, (iii) Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET). Why is WCET preferred over average execution time in real-time scheduling analysis?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(i) Release time ($a_i$):** The instant at which a job becomes available for execution. (0.5 mark)
- **(ii) Relative deadline ($D_i$):** The maximum allowable time from release to completion, i.e., $D_i = d_i - a_i$. (0.5 mark)
- **(iii) WCET ($C_i$):** The upper bound on the execution time of a job across all possible input data and execution paths. (0.5 mark)
- **Why WCET, not average?** Real-time systems must *guarantee* meeting deadlines in the *worst* case, not on average. Using average times would lead to optimistic schedules and catastrophic deadline misses under peak load. WCET is a *safe upper bound* required for formal schedulability proofs. (1.5 marks)

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the assumptions made in the Liu & Layland Rate Monotonic Scheduling model. State the Liu & Layland utilization bound for n = 3.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Assumptions:** (2 marks)
1. All tasks are **periodic** with known periods.
2. Tasks are **independent** (no shared resources, no precedence).
3. Relative deadline equals the period ($D_i = T_i$) — implicit deadlines.
4. WCET $C_i$ is **known and constant**.
5. Scheduling is **preemptive**.
6. Context switch overhead is **negligible**.

**Bound for n = 3:** (1 mark)

$$U_{RMS}(3) = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) = 3(1.2599 - 1) = 3 \times 0.2599 \approx 0.7798$$

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]`

Consider the following set of three periodic real-time tasks to be scheduled on a uniprocessor using Rate Monotonic Scheduling:

| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | WCET $C_i$ (ms) |
|------|-------------------|------------------|
| T1   | 8                 | 2                |
| T2   | 16                | 4                |
| T3   | 32                | 8                |

**(a)** Assign priorities using Rate Monotonic rule. Compute the total utilization and check whether the task set is schedulable using the Liu & Layland bound. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the exact worst-case response time of T3 using the response-time iteration. Verify whether T3 meets its deadline. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

RMS assigns higher priority to **shorter period**. So priority order: T1 > T2 > T3. (1 mark)

Compute individual utilizations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_1 &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{2}{8} = 0.25 \\
U_2 &= \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{4}{16} = 0.25 \\
U_3 &= \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{8}{32} = 0.25 \\
U_{total} &= 0.25 + 0.25 + 0.25 = 0.75
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

Liu–Layland bound for $n = 3$:

$$U_{RMS}(3) = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) = 3(0.2599) = 0.7798$$

(2 marks)

**Comparison:** $U_{total} = 0.75 < U_{RMS}(3) = 0.7798$.

**Conclusion:** The task set satisfies the sufficient condition, hence is **schedulable under RMS**. (2 marks)

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

We compute $R_3$ iteratively. Higher-priority set: $hp(3) = \{1, 2\}$.

**Iteration 1** (seed): $R_3^{(1)} = C_3 = 8$ ms. (1 mark)

**Iteration 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(2)} &= C_3 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3^{(1)}}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3^{(1)}}{T_2} \right\rceil C_2 \\
&= 8 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{8} \right\rceil (2) + \left\lceil \frac{8}{16} \right\rceil (4) \\
&= 8 + (1)(2) + (1)(4) = 14 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

**Iteration 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(3)} &= 8 + \left\lceil \frac{14}{8} \right\rceil (2) + \left\lceil \frac{14}{16} \right\rceil (4) \\
&= 8 + (2)(2) + (1)(4) = 8 + 4 + 4 = 16 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

**Iteration 4:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(4)} &= 8 + \left\lceil \frac{16}{8} \right\rceil (2) + \left\lceil \frac{16}{16} \right\rceil (4) \\
&= 8 + (2)(2) + (1)(4) = 16 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

(1 mark)

Since $R_3^{(4)} = R_3^{(3)} = 16$ ms, **convergence is reached**. The worst-case response time of T3 is:

$$R_3 = 16 \text{ ms}$$

Deadline $D_3 = T_3 = 32$ ms. Since $R_3 = 16 \le 32$, T3 **meets its deadline** with a slack of 16 ms. (1 mark)

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**(a)** Compare Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) and Earliest Deadline First (EDF) along the dimensions: priority type, optimality, utilization bound, runtime overhead, and implementation complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider two tasks with $T_1 = 4, C_1 = 1$ and $T_2 = 7, C_2 = 3$. Show that this task set is schedulable by EDF but may be unschedulable by RMS. Compute both Liu-Layland bound and exact response time of T2. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

| Dimension | **Rate Monotonic (RMS)** | **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** |
|-----------|--------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Priority type | Static, fixed at design | Dynamic, recomputed at each arrival |
| Optimality | Optimal among static-priority algorithms | Optimal *overall* (any algorithm) |
| Utilization bound | $n(2^{1/n}-1)$, max **0.693** as $n \to \infty$ | 1.0 (i.e., up to 100% utilization) |
| Runtime overhead | **Low** — priorities are precomputed | **High** — re-sorting on every event |
| Implementation complexity | Simple, predictable | Complex; needs accurate runtime clocks |
| Aperiodic handling | Poor (no native support) | Excellent (background capacity) |

(6 marks — 1 mark per row × 6 rows, or pro-rata)

**Key Conclusion (1 mark):** EDF dominates RMS in *schedulability* but RMS dominates EDF in *implementation simplicity and predictability*. KTU 2024 favours RMS

topics.title: Structures: Simple Batch
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT741 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Structures: Simple Batch

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition

A **Simple Batch System** (also called a **Batch Monitor System**) is one of the earliest structures of an operating system, in which jobs (programs along with their control data, originally on punch cards or magnetic tape) having **similar requirements** are grouped into *batches* and submitted to the computer for sequential execution by a resident software program called the **Batch Monitor**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):**
> This topic belongs to the foundational study of **Operating System Structures** which forms the historical and architectural basis of modern Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS). Understanding batch structures is essential before studying scheduling, interrupts, and task management in RTOS.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, simple batch systems are studied because they represent the **first systematic attempt to automate job sequencing**, the precursor to modern schedulers used in RTOS kernels.

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **college photocopy shop** in the 1960s:

- A student walks in with **100 copies of an assignment** to be printed. The shopkeeper does not start the machine immediately.
- Instead, the shopkeeper **collects requests from all students** in a pile (a "batch"), and when the pile becomes large enough, he **runs the machine once for the entire batch** of similar print jobs.
- The shopkeeper here acts as the **Monitor**, the pile is the **Batch**, and the print jobs are the **User Programs**.

Just as the shopkeeper processes similar print jobs sequentially to reduce setup time, a Simple Batch OS groups jobs with similar needs and runs them one after another with **minimum human intervention between jobs**, thereby maximizing the use of expensive mainframes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Idea:** The single most important metric of a Simple Batch System is the **utilization of the CPU**. Since early computers cost millions, the goal was to keep the CPU as busy as possible by reducing the time wasted in manual setup between jobs.

---

## Why Simple Batch?

Before batch systems, computers used **Hard-Wired Programmed Systems** (plugboard-based). Operators had to:
1. Mount tapes manually,
2. Set switches,
3. Load each program.

This caused the CPU to be **idle for long durations** between jobs. The Simple Batch OS automated this handoff using a small resident program — the **Monitor** — which always resided in memory and handed control from one job to the next automatically.

---

## Standard Metrics in a Simple Batch System

| Metric | Typical Value (Historical) | Modern Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **CPU Idle Time** | Reduced to a few **percent** | Aim: 0% idle in modern OS |
| **Job Turnaround Time** | **Hours** | Milliseconds in RTOS |
| **Monitor Memory Footprint** | A few **KB** of core memory | KBs of kernel code |
| **Off-line vs On-line** | Early: **Off-line batching** | Modern: Direct submission |

---

## Architectural Place of Simple Batch in OS Evolution

$$
\text{Plugboard} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Simple Batch} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Multiprogrammed Batch} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Time-Sharing} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Real-Time OS}
$$

The **Simple Batch** structure introduces three critical concepts that survive in RTOS today:
1. **Resident Monitor / Kernel**
2. **Job Control Language (JCL)** — forerunner of system calls
3. **Automatic job-to-job transition** — forerunner of the scheduler/dispatcher

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Simple Batch System

A simple batch system is composed of the following logical components, each residing in a well-defined region of memory:

### 1. The **Batch Monitor** (Resident Software)
- A small program that **always resides in main memory** (typically at the lowest memory addresses).
- It controls the sequence of jobs, performs I/O on behalf of the user job, and provides basic operator-like functions.
- Modern equivalent: the **OS kernel**.

### 2. **Job Control Language (JCL)**
- A control language (similar to today's shell scripts) embedded with every job to:
  - Identify the job,
  - Specify required I/O devices,
  - Indicate program language translator to be used (e.g., FORTRAN compiler).
- Modern equivalent: **Command-line arguments / system calls**.

### 3. **User Programs (Jobs)**
- Each job is a self-contained program written in a high-level language, accompanied by its JCL.

### 4. **Peripherals**
- **Card readers** (input), **line printers** (output), **magnetic tape drives** (storage between jobs).

---

## 2.2 Operational Flow — Step-by-Step Logic

The operational flow of a Simple Batch OS follows these exact steps:

- **Step 1 — Job Submission:** The programmer submits the source code (punch cards) and JCL to a **data entry clerk** or directly to the operator.
- **Step 2 — Conversion to Batch Tape:** The operator transfers the jobs onto a **magnetic tape** (called the *input batch tape*) using a smaller auxiliary computer.
- **Step 3 — Loading the Monitor:** When the main computer boots, the **Batch Monitor is loaded into low memory** and gains control.
- **Step 4 — Job Read:** The Monitor reads one job at a time from the input device (card reader or tape) into the **user program area** of memory.
- **Step 5 — Control Transfer to Job:** The Monitor passes control to the user program. The job executes to completion (no preemption in simple batch).
- **Step 6 — Control Return to Monitor:** When the job finishes (or errors out), control returns to the Monitor.
- **Step 7 — Output Dumping:** The Monitor dumps the job's output to a **line printer** or output tape.
- **Step 8 — Loop:** Steps 4–7 are repeated for the next job in the batch.
- **Step 9 — Batch Completion:** Once all jobs in the batch are processed, the Monitor signals the operator to mount the next batch tape.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Non-Multiprogrammed"?** Notice that only **one job resides in memory at a time**. This is the key reason simple batch systems had poor CPU utilization — whenever a job performed I/O, the **CPU sat idle** until the I/O completed. This limitation directly motivated **Multiprogrammed Batch Systems**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Below is the high-yield formula and metric table for KTU examinations on this topic. **Note:** All pipes are rendered using `\vert` to maintain table integrity.

| # | Concept / Metric | Expression / Definition | Typical Value | Significance for RTOS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CPU Utilization** | $U_{cpu} = 1 - P_{idle}$ | Higher the better | Core metric of any OS |
| 2 | **Throughput** | $T = \dfrac{N_{jobs}}{\Delta t_{total}}$ | Jobs per unit time | Measures batch efficiency |
| 3 | **Turnaround Time** | $TAT = T_{completion} - T_{submission}$ | Hours (historical) | Critical in RTOS as **deadline** |
| 4 | **Memory Footprint of Monitor** | $M_{mon} \approx 5\text{–}10\%$ of core | Few KB | Sets minimum RAM for OS |
| 5 | **I/O vs CPU Burst Ratio** | $\dfrac{t_{IO}}{t_{CPU}}$ | $\gg 1$ in early jobs | Justifies multiprogramming |
| 6 | **Idle Time Fraction** | $f_{idle} = \dfrac{\sum t_{idle}}{\sum t_{total}}$ | High in simple batch | What multiprogramming solves |
| 7 | **Job Sequencing Rule** | $J_1 \rightarrow J_2 \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow J_n$ | FIFO | No preemption allowed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme, the question "Compare Simple Batch with Multiprogrammed Batch" almost always comes for **7 marks**. Memorize the difference in CPU utilization, memory layout, and the number of resident jobs.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Although obsolete in modern times, the principles pioneered by simple batch systems live on in:
- **Cron jobs** in Unix/Linux — scheduled batch execution.
- **AT jobs** in Windows — legacy task scheduling.
- **Factory PLC batch controllers** — process a "batch" of similar I/O operations in industrial automation.
- **Data pipeline ETL systems** — Apache Spark, Hadoop batch jobs.
- **RTOS background tasks** — periodic batch processing of telemetry packets in embedded satellites.

In an **RTOS context**, the simple batch model maps to the **"Background task + Interrupt Service Routine"** paradigm of small embedded controllers, where non-time-critical jobs are batched and processed when the CPU is free.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section develops the key analytical results used in KTU numerical and analytical questions on simple batch systems.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: CPU Utilization in Simple Batch System

Consider a single user job that alternates between **CPU bursts** and **I/O bursts** during its lifetime.

Let:
- $t_{CPU}$ = total time the job spends computing on the CPU,
- $t_{IO}$  = total time the job spends waiting for I/O to complete,
- $T_{job}$ = total execution time of the job.

Then:

$$
T_{job} = t_{CPU} + t_{IO}
$$

**Step 1 — CPU Busy Time:** Since the system runs only this one job at a time, the CPU is busy **only during the CPU bursts**:

$$
T_{CPU\_busy} = t_{CPU}
$$

**Step 2 — CPU Idle Time:** The CPU is **idle** while the job is performing I/O:

$$
T_{CPU\_idle} = t_{IO}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Wall Clock Time:** The total time observed from outside is:

$$
T_{total} = T_{CPU\_busy} + T_{CPU\_idle} = t_{CPU} + t_{IO}
$$

**Step 4 — CPU Utilization Fraction:**

$$
U_{cpu} = \frac{T_{CPU\_busy}}{T_{total}} = \frac{t_{CPU}}{t_{CPU} + t_{IO}}
$$

**Step 5 — Idle Time Fraction:**

$$
f_{idle} = 1 - U_{cpu} = \frac{t_{IO}}{t_{CPU} + t_{IO}}
$$

**Interpretation:** In a simple batch system, if a job spends **80% of its time doing I/O**, the CPU is utilized only **20% of the time** — a severe wastage. This is the central motivation for **multiprogramming**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A simple batch system executes a single job whose total CPU burst time is **30 seconds** and total I/O wait time is **70 seconds**. Calculate: (a) the CPU utilization, (b) the idle time fraction, (c) the throughput if 120 such jobs are processed in one run.

**Given:**
- $t_{CPU} = 30 \text{ s}$
- $t_{IO} = 70 \text{ s}$
- $N = 120$ jobs

**Solution:**

**(a) CPU Utilization:**

$$
U_{cpu} = \frac{t_{CPU}}{t_{CPU} + t_{IO}} = \frac{30}{30 + 70} = \frac{30}{100} = 0.30
$$

Therefore $U_{cpu} = 30\%$.

**(b) Idle Time Fraction:**

$$
f_{idle} = 1 - 0.30 = 0.70 = 70\%
$$

**(c) Throughput:**

$$
T_{total\_per\_job} = t_{CPU} + t_{IO} = 30 + 70 = 100 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{total\_batch} = N \times T_{total\_per\_job} = 120 \times 100 = 12{,}000 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{throughput} = \frac{N}{T_{total\_batch}} = \frac{120}{12{,}000} = 0.01 \text{ jobs/s}
$$

This is equivalent to:

$$
T_{throughput} = 0.01 \times 3600 = 36 \text{ jobs/hour}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Limitations → Subsequent Solution

| # | Limitation of Simple Batch | Resulting Problem | OS Structure that Solved It |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Only one job in memory | CPU idle during I/O | **Multiprogrammed Batch** |
| 2 | No interaction with user | Long debugging cycle | **Time-Sharing System** |
| 3 | No deadline awareness | Unacceptable in control systems | **Real-Time OS (RTOS)** |
| 4 | Sequential FIFO only | No priority handling | **Priority-based Scheduling** |
| 5 | No memory protection | One job can crash monitor | **Protected Kernel Mode** |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Memory Map of a Simple Batch System

The memory of a simple batch computer is divided into two fixed regions:

- **Region A — Monitor (Resident):** Always loaded, from address $0$ to $M_{mon} - 1$.
- **Region B — User Program Area:** From address $M_{mon}$ to $M_{total} - 1$.

$$
M_{used} = M_{mon} + M_{user\_job}
$$

The remaining memory is **not used**:

$$
M_{free} = M_{total} - M_{used} = M_{total} - M_{mon} - M_{user\_job}
$$

In simple batch, only one user job is loaded, so $M_{free}$ is significant. **Multiprogramming** would divide Region B into multiple slots to hold several jobs simultaneously.

---

## 3.5 Quantitative Comparison Table (High-Yield for KTU)

| Parameter | Simple Batch | Multiprogrammed Batch | Time-Sharing |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resident jobs in memory | **1** | **n (multiple)** | n (multiple) |
| CPU idle time | High ($\approx 60\text{–}80\%$) | Low ($<20\%$) | Very low |
| User interaction | None | None | Yes |
| Response time | Hours | Minutes | Seconds |
| Pre-emption | No | Optional (later) | Yes (time-slice) |
| Complexity of monitor | Low | High | Very high |
| Job Control Language | JCL | JCL | Command interpreter |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture of a Simple Batch System

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the functional architecture and data flow of a simple batch system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Programmer] -->|Submits punch cards and JCL| B[Data Entry Clerk]
    B -->|Records onto magnetic tape| C[Input Batch Tape]
    C -->|Read by main computer| D[Batch Monitor in Core Memory]
    D -->|Loads job into user area| E[User Program Area]
    E -->|CPU executes job| F[Job in Execution]
    F -->|Generates output| G[Line Printer or Output Tape]
    F -->|Returns control| D
    D -->|Reads next job| C
    G -->|Collected by operator| H[Final Output to User]
    D -.->|On job end| I[Read Next Job from Batch]
```

**Description of the flow:**
- The **Batch Monitor (D)** is the central control node.
- It pulls jobs one by one from the **Input Batch Tape (C)** into the **User Program Area (E)**.
- The job runs to completion in **F**, produces output to **G**, and returns control to **D**.
- The monitor then loops to read the next job.

---

## 4.2 Memory Layout of a Simple Batch System

The following block diagram shows the fixed partitioning of physical memory.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Address 0] --> B[Batch Monitor Region]
    B --> C[User Program Region - Job 1]
    C --> D[Unused Memory - Free Area]
    D --> E[Address M total]
    F[Monitor Functions] -.-> B
    G[Operating System Services] -.-> B
    H[Single User Job] -.-> C
    I[Wasted in Simple Batch] -.-> D
```

**Key observations:**
- **Batch Monitor Region** holds the resident OS code.
- **User Program Region** holds exactly one job at any time.
- **Unused Memory** is wasted — this is what multiprogramming later exploited.

---

## 4.3 Job Lifecycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Submitted: User submits punch cards
    Submitted --> Batched: Operator groups into batch
    Batched --> LoadedIntoMemory: Monitor reads job
    LoadedIntoMemory --> Executing: CPU control transferred
    Executing --> Completed: Job finishes
    Executing --> Error: Job fails
    Completed --> OutputDump: Monitor dumps output
    Error --> OutputDump: Error message printed
    OutputDump --> [*]: Next job loaded
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Detailed)

The following Mermaid diagram provides a more detailed functional view of all the interacting subsystems inside a simple batch system.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputSubsystem [Input Subsystem]
        IN1[Card Reader]
        IN2[Magnetic Tape Drive]
    end
    subgraph CoreMemory [Core Memory]
        MON[Batch Monitor - Resident Kernel]
        JOB[User Job Area]
    end
    subgraph CPUSubsystem [CPU Subsystem]
        CPU[Central Processing Unit]
        REG[Registers and ALU]
    end
    subgraph OutputSubsystem [Output Subsystem]
        OUT1[Line Printer]
        OUT2[Output Tape Drive]
    end
    subgraph ControlSubsystem [Control Subsystem]
        CTL[Job Control Language Interpreter]
        SEQ[Sequencer and Dispatcher]
    end

    IN1 --> MON
    IN2 --> MON
    MON --> CTL
    CTL --> JOB
    JOB --> CPU
    CPU --> REG
    JOB --> OUT1
    JOB --> OUT2
    CPU --> MON
    MON --> SEQ
    SEQ --> JOB
```

**Component Roles:**

| Subsystem | Role | RTOS Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Input Subsystem | Brings jobs from external media into memory | Task input queue |
| Batch Monitor | Central controller, always resident | RTOS kernel / scheduler |
| User Job Area | Holds the current job's code and data | Task stack / TCB |
| CPU Subsystem | Executes the loaded job | CPU core |
| Output Subsystem | Records job output | Output buffer / log |
| Control Subsystem | Interprets JCL and sequences jobs | System call interface |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define a Simple Batch Operating System. Mention any two of its disadvantages.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

A **Simple Batch Operating System** is a system in which similar jobs are grouped into batches and submitted to the computer, where a resident program called the **Batch Monitor** automatically sequences and executes them one after another, with minimal human intervention.

**Two disadvantages:**
1. **CPU Idle Time:** Since only one job resides in memory at a time, the CPU remains idle during the I/O operations of the job, leading to poor CPU utilization.
2. **No User Interaction:** The user cannot interact with the program during execution. Debugging is done only after the entire batch is processed, increasing turnaround time.

*(Allocate: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for two disadvantages.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is a Batch Monitor? List any two of its functions.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

A **Batch Monitor** is a small, always-resident software program that controls the sequencing of jobs in a simple batch system. It acts as the earliest form of an operating system kernel.

**Two functions:**
1. **Job Sequencing:** It reads jobs one by one from the input device, transfers control to each, and then proceeds to the next.
2. **I/O Handling on Behalf of Jobs:** It manages the input (card reader / tape) and output (line printer) devices so that user programs can run without directly controlling them.

*(Allocate: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for two functions.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> As per KTU 2024 Scheme guidelines, the module-level choice pattern is followed: **either Question A or Question B must be answered**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **architecture and working of a Simple Batch Operating System**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Simple Batch** and **Multiprogrammed Batch** Operating Systems on the basis of **CPU utilization, memory layout, number of resident jobs, complexity, and response time**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (a):

The **Simple Batch OS architecture** consists of the **Batch Monitor** at the low end of memory and a **single user program area** in higher memory.

**Working (Step-by-step):**
1. The operator collects jobs from various users and groups similar jobs into a **batch on magnetic tape**.
2. The **Batch Monitor** is loaded into memory at boot time and gains CPU control.
3. The monitor reads one job from the input device and loads it into the **user program area**.
4. Control is transferred to the user program, which executes to completion (no preemption).
5. The monitor regains control upon job completion, dumps the output to the **line printer**, and reads the next job.
6. The cycle repeats until the batch is exhausted.

**Diagram (as drawn in SECTION 4.1):**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Programmer] -->|Punch cards and JCL| B[Operator Batches Jobs]
    B --> C[Input Batch Tape]
    C --> D[Batch Monitor in Core Memory]
    D --> E[User Program Area]
    E --> F[Job Executes on CPU]
    F --> G[Output to Line Printer]
    F -->|Returns Control| D
    D -.->|Loop| C
```

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- 2 marks — Architecture explanation
- 2 marks — Working step-by-step
- 2 marks — Diagram
- 1 mark — Clarity and conclusion

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (b):

| Parameter | Simple Batch | Multiprogrammed Batch |
|---|---|---|
| **CPU Utilization** | Low (CPU idle during I/O) | High (overlaps I/O of one job with CPU of another) |
| **Memory Layout** | One user program area | Multiple user program partitions |
| **Resident Jobs** | Only 1 | Multiple (n) jobs simultaneously |
| **Monitor Complexity** | Simple — only sequencing | Complex — needs job selection, protection |
| **Response Time** | Long (entire batch must finish) | Improved due to overlapping I/O |
| **Job Control** | JCL | JCL + memory manager + scheduler |

**Conclusion:** Multiprogrammed batch systems overcome the central limitation of simple batch systems — CPU idle time — by keeping multiple jobs in memory and switching between them when one performs I/O. This is the direct conceptual ancestor of modern **RTOS task scheduling**.

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- 1 mark per parameter × 6 parameters (3 for each OS) = 6 marks
- 1 mark for the conclusion

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **memory layout and job execution flow** in a Simple Batch System with a diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A simple batch system executes a job whose total CPU burst time is **20 seconds** and total I/O wait time is **80 seconds**. Calculate the **CPU utilization, idle time fraction, and throughput** for a batch of **90 such jobs**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (a):

The memory of a simple batch system is divided into two main regions:
- **Monitor Region:** Located at the low end of memory, from address 0 to $M_{mon} - 1$. The batch monitor permanently resides here.
- **User Program Region:** Located above the monitor, holding a single user job.

**Job Execution Flow:**
1. Operator prepares a batch tape of similar jobs.
2. The computer is booted and the monitor is loaded into the monitor region.
3. The monitor reads the first job into the user program region.
4. The monitor transfers CPU control to the job.
5. The job runs to completion (no preemption).
6. Control returns to the monitor, which dumps the output and loads the next job.
7. The cycle repeats until all jobs in the batch are done.

**Diagram (as drawn in SECTION 4.2):**
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Address 0] --> B[Batch Monitor Region]
    B --> C[User Program Region - Job]
    C --> D[Unused Memory - Free Area]
    D --> E[Address M total]
```

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- 2 marks — Memory layout explanation
- 2 marks — Job execution flow steps
- 2 marks — Diagram
- 1 mark — Neatness

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (b):

**Given:**
- $t_{CPU} = 20 \text{ s}$ per job
- $t_{IO} = 80 \text{ s}$ per job
- $N = 90$ jobs

**Step 1 — CPU Utilization:**

$$
U_{cpu} = \frac{t_{CPU}}{t_{CPU} + t_{IO}} = \frac{20}{20 + 80} = \frac{20}{100} = 0.20
$$

Therefore, $U_{cpu} = 20\%$.

**Step 2 — Idle Time Fraction:**

$$
f_{idle} = 1 - 0.20 = 0.80 = 80\%
$$

**Step 3 — Total Time Per Job:**

$$
T_{per\_job} = t_{CPU} + t_{IO} = 20 + 80 = 100 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Batch Time:**

$$
T_{total} = 90 \times 100 = 9{,}000 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5 — Throughput:**

$$
T_{throughput} = \frac{90}{9{,}000} = 0.01 \text{ jobs/s} = 36 \text{ jobs/hour}
$$

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- 1 mark — Writing the formulas
- 2 marks — CPU utilization
- 1 mark — Idle fraction
- 2 marks — Throughput
- 1 mark — Final answer with units

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Batch Monitor" with "Compiler":** The monitor is NOT a translator. It is the *control program* that loads and sequences jobs. Marks are deducted if students describe the monitor as a compiler.
> 2. **Forgetting units in throughput:** KTU strictly requires units like "jobs/second" or "jobs/hour". Writing only the numerical value loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Drawing a flat rectangular box for memory layout:** Examiners expect a **partitioned memory diagram** showing the **monitor region** and the **user program region** as distinct blocks. A single rectangle gets only **partial marks**.
> 4. **Confusing JCL with high-level programming language:** JCL is a *control language* for the monitor, not the programming language in which the job is written.
> 5. **Stating "Real-time" features for simple batch:** Simple batch systems have **no real-time guarantees**. This is a frequent conceptual error.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **Simple Batch System** groups similar jobs into a batch and runs them sequentially using a **Batch Monitor**.
- The **Batch Monitor** is the resident OS kernel — it loads jobs, transfers control, dumps output, and loops.
- **Job Control Language (JCL)** accompanies every job to specify resources and translator.
- **No preemption, no user interaction, no overlapping of I/O and CPU.**
- **Memory layout:** Monitor at low addresses, single user program area above it, free memory is wasted.
- **CPU Utilization formula:** $U_{cpu} = \dfrac{t_{CPU}}{t_{CPU} + t_{IO}}$.
- **Idle Fraction formula:** $f_{idle} = 1 - U_{cpu}$.
- **Throughput formula:** $T = \dfrac{N_{jobs}}{T_{total}}$.
- **Turnaround time** is in **hours** in simple batch; modern RTOS reduces it to **milliseconds** with deadline guarantees.
- **Limitation:** Single job in memory → CPU idle during I/O → motivated **Multiprogrammed Batch**.
- **Succession:** Simple Batch → Multiprogrammed Batch → Time-Sharing → Real-Time OS.
- **Modern echoes:** Cron jobs, AT jobs, factory batch controllers, ETL pipelines, RTOS background tasks.
- **CPU utilization in simple batch is poor (often below 30%)** because I/O-bound jobs leave the CPU waiting.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multi-Programmed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT741 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Multi-Programmed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Multiprogramming** is a foundational computing paradigm in which two or more independent programs are concurrently held in the main memory of a computer system, such that the Central Processing Unit (CPU) always has at least one ready-to-execute program to run, thereby minimizing CPU idle time. In the context of a **Real Time Operating System (RTOS)**, multiprogramming extends beyond simple CPU optimization — it becomes a deterministic mechanism for ensuring that multiple time-critical tasks can co-exist, share resources, and meet their respective deadlines within bounded temporal windows.

The classical academic definition (per Silberschatz, Galvin & Gagne, *Operating System Concepts*) is:

> *"Multiprogramming is the technique of keeping multiple programs in main memory at the same time, so that when one program is waiting for I/O, another can be assigned to the CPU, thereby increasing system efficiency."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Hospital Triage Counter**
>
> Imagine a small clinic with **one doctor** and **three patients** waiting:
> - *Patient A* needs a blood test (results take time → he must wait).
> - *Patient B* needs a quick consultation.
> - *Patient C* needs an X-ray (also takes time).
>
> A **single-programmed** doctor would treat Patient A, sit idle for 20 minutes while waiting for the lab, then treat B, sit idle again, etc.
>
> A **multiprogrammed** doctor treats A, but while A is in the lab, he sees B; while B is in X-ray, he calls C; the moment A returns, the doctor switches back. **The doctor (CPU) is never idle if there is a ready patient (process).**
>
> In an RTOS, this switching must occur within **strict time bounds** — the doctor must see each patient not just *eventually*, but *on time*.

### 1.3 Key Metrics and Standard Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Performance Metrics in Multiprogramming**
>
> - **CPU Utilization (U):** Fraction of time the CPU is doing useful work. Target in batch systems: **> 90%**.
> - **Throughput (T):** Number of processes completed per unit time (**processes/second**).
> - **Turnaround Time (TAT):** Time from process submission to completion (**seconds**).
> - **Waiting Time (WT):** Time a process spends in the ready queue (**seconds**).
> - **Response Time (RT):** Time from submission to first response (critical for **interactive RTOS**).
> - **Degree of Multiprogramming (DMP):** The number of processes resident in memory at any instant.

### 1.4 Multiprogramming vs. Multitasking vs. Multiprocessing

| Feature | Multiprogramming | Multitasking | Multiprocessing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Goal** | Maximize CPU utilization | Provide responsive interactive system | Increase computational throughput via parallelism |
| **CPU Count** | Single CPU | Single CPU (logical) | Multiple CPUs / Cores |
| **Switching** | Coarse-grained (I/O wait) | Fine-grained (time slicing) | True parallel execution |
| **Context** | Batch era foundation | Modern desktop/mobile | Servers, HPC, embedded SoCs |
| **RTOS Relevance** | Foundational for task coexistence | Core mechanism (preemptive) | Used in multi-core RTOS (e.g., AUTOSAR) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Many students conflate *multiprogramming* with *multitasking*. In an **RTOS Module 1 context**, the question is almost always: *"How does multiprogramming enable the system to handle multiple real-time tasks?"* — focus on **coexistence, context switching, and resource sharing**, not on parallel hardware.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Multiprogramming — Stepwise Breakdown

Multiprogramming is not a single event but a **continuous feedback loop** between the OS kernel and hardware. The operational logic can be decomposed as follows:

1. **Memory Loading Phase (Job Pool → RAM):**
   - The OS loader (or long-term scheduler) admits a *degree of multiprogramming (DMP)* number of processes from the secondary storage job pool into **main memory**.
   - Each admitted process is placed in its own partition and assigned a **Process Control Block (PCB)**.

2. **Ready Queue Formation:**
   - All admitted processes that are not waiting on I/O are placed in the **Ready Queue**, a FIFO or priority-based linked list maintained by the kernel.

3. **CPU Dispatch:**
   - The **Short-Term Scheduler (STS / Dispatcher)** selects ONE process from the ready queue and loads it onto the CPU by restoring its registers, program counter, and stack pointer from its PCB.

4. **Execution / Interrupt / System Call:**
   - The selected process runs until one of four events:
     - It issues an **I/O system call** (becomes *Blocked/Waiting*).
     - It requests a resource that is currently unavailable.
     - A **timer interrupt** fires (in preemptive systems).
     - It terminates normally or abnormally.

5. **Context Save & Switch:**
   - The kernel saves the state of the currently running process back into its PCB and loads the next chosen process — this is the **Context Switch**.

6. **Loop Closure:**
   - The newly loaded process executes, and the cycle repeats indefinitely.

### 2.2 The "Why" — The Mathematical Justification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Multiprogramming Theorem (Foundational Equation)**
>
> A program in a typical uniprogrammed system spends only a fraction **p** of its time using the CPU and a fraction **(1 − p)** waiting for I/O. With **n** programs loaded in memory, the probability that **all n** programs are simultaneously waiting for I/O is **p^n** (since each is independently idle with probability 1−p, the chance all are idle is (1−p)^n, and thus the chance the CPU is idle is (1−p)^n).

This leads to the **CPU Utilization Formula**:

$$U_{\text{CPU}} = 1 - (1 - p)^n$$

> Where:
> - $U_{\text{CPU}}$ = CPU utilization (0 ≤ U ≤ 1)
> - $p$ = fraction of CPU time a process uses actively (i.e., 1−p is the I/O wait fraction)
> - $n$ = degree of multiprogramming (number of resident processes)

**Why this matters in RTOS:** In a real-time embedded controller (e.g., an automotive ECU), if the *braking task*, *steering task*, and *engine task* are all loaded, and each spends ~60% of its time on I/O (sensors/actuators), then the CPU is guaranteed to be active **> 99%** of the time — leaving deterministic slack for the scheduler to meet all deadlines.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.3 — Master Formula Reference for Multiprogramming (KTU Module 1)**

| # | Formula / Concept | LaTeX Expression | Variables & Units | RTOS Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CPU Utilization (Multiprogramming Theorem) | $U_{\text{CPU}} = 1 - (1 - p)^n$ | $p$ = CPU-active fraction, $n$ = process count | Predicts deadline headroom |
| 2 | Degree of Multiprogramming | $\text{DMP} = \frac{M_{\text{avail}}}{M_{\text{per-proc}}}$ | $M$ = memory (KB/MB) | Bounds concurrent task count |
| 3 | Throughput | $T = \frac{N_{\text{proc}}}{t_{\text{total}}}$ | $N_{\text{proc}}$ = completed processes, $t_{\text{total}}$ in seconds | Real-time jobs completed/sec |
| 4 | Turnaround Time | $\text{TAT} = t_{\text{finish}} - t_{\text{arrival}}$ | Time in seconds | Worst-case for offline analysis |
| 5 | Waiting Time | $\text{WT} = \text{TAT} - t_{\text{burst}}$ | $t_{\text{burst}}$ = CPU time used | Lower WT = fairer scheduler |
| 6 | Response Time | $\text{RT} = t_{\text{first-resp}} - t_{\text{arrival}}$ | Time in seconds | Critical for interactive RT tasks |
| 7 | Context Switch Overhead | $T_{\text{cs}} = T_{\text{save}} + T_{\text{load}}$ | Time in microseconds ($\mu$s) | Must be bounded in RTOS |
| 8 | Effective CPU Time | $U_{\text{eff}} = 1 - (1-p)^n - f_{\text{cs}}$ | $f_{\text{cs}}$ = fraction lost to context switches | Real achievable utilization |
| 9 | Amdahl's Law Bound (RT extension) | $S_{\text{max}} = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{n}}$ | $f$ = parallelizable fraction | Limits multi-core RT benefit |
| 10 | Memory Utilization | $U_{\text{mem}} = \frac{\sum M_{\text{used}}}{M_{\text{total}}} \cdot 100\%$ | Percentage (%) | Avoids thrashing |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Multiprogramming is the **invisible backbone** of every modern computing device:

- **Automotive RTOS (AUTOSAR / OSEK):** Multiple ECUs multiprogram braking, engine, and infotainment tasks on shared cores.
- **Aerospace (DO-178C certified systems):** Flight control + navigation + communication tasks multiprogrammed under ARINC 653 partitioning.
- **Industrial PLCs:** Real-time scan cycles multiprogrammed with HMI tasks.
- **IoT Edge Devices (FreeRTOS, Zephyr):** Sensor sampling, BLE transmission, and OTA updates multiprogrammed on Cortex-M microcontrollers.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Linkage:** Module 1 sets the *philosophical base* for all subsequent RTOS concepts (tasks, scheduling, interrupts, semaphores). If a student cannot articulate the multiprogramming theorem fluently, downstream chapters on **Rate Monotonic Scheduling** and **Earliest Deadline First** become conceptually fragile.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — The Multiprogramming Theorem

We now derive the CPU utilization formula from **first principles**. This derivation is high-yield for KTU problems worth 7–14 marks.

**Setup:**
Let there be $n$ independent programs resident in main memory. For any single program, define:
- $p$ = probability that the program is using the CPU (i.e., in the *Running* state) at a randomly chosen instant.
- $(1 - p)$ = probability that the program is waiting for I/O (i.e., in the *Blocked/Waiting* state) at the same instant.

**Assumption:** The I/O behavior of each program is **statistically independent** of the others. This is the standard simplifying assumption used in OS textbooks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why independence matters:** In a real RTOS, tasks share buses and may contend for peripherals, so true independence is an idealization. However, for a high-level CPU utilization analysis (which is what KTU questions demand), independence gives a clean, tractable bound.

**Step 1 — Probability that ONE process is idle:**
$$P(\text{one process idle}) = 1 - p$$

**Step 2 — Probability that ALL n processes are simultaneously idle:**
Since the $n$ processes are independent, we multiply their individual idle probabilities:

$$P(\text{all } n \text{ idle}) = (1 - p)^n$$

**Step 3 — Probability that AT LEAST ONE process is ready (CPU is busy):**
This is the complement of "all idle":

$$P(\text{CPU busy}) = 1 - P(\text{all idle})$$

Substituting the result of Step 2:

$$P(\text{CPU busy}) = 1 - (1 - p)^n$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation as CPU Utilization:**
Since $P(\text{CPU busy})$ is, by definition, the fraction of time the CPU is doing useful work, we equate it with CPU utilization:

$$\boxed{U_{\text{CPU}} = 1 - (1 - p)^n}$$

**Step 5 — Asymptotic Validation:**
We can verify this formula at the boundary conditions:

- If $n = 1$ (uniprogrammed), then $U_{\text{CPU}} = 1 - (1 - p)^1 = p$. ✓ (CPU is busy exactly when the single process is running.)
- If $p \to 1$ (pure CPU-bound task, never waits for I/O), then $U_{\text{CPU}} = 1 - 0^n = 1$. ✓ (CPU is always busy.)
- If $p \to 0$ (process is always idle), then $U_{\text{CPU}} = 1 - 1^n = 0$. ✓ (CPU is always idle.)
- As $n \to \infty$, $(1 - p)^n \to 0$ for any $p \in (0, 1)$, so $U_{\text{CPU}} \to 1$. ✓ (Infinite multiprogramming → 100% utilization — but in practice, memory and overhead caps this.)

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (3-Mark / 7-Mark KTU Style):**
