> A real-time embedded controller has a single CPU running three independent tasks. Each task uses the CPU for 30% of its time and waits for sensor I/O for the remaining 70%. Compute the CPU utilization under multiprogramming.

**Given:**
$$n = 3, \quad p = 0.30$$

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify $p$ and $n$ from the problem statement. The CPU-active fraction is 30%, so $p = 0.30$. The number of resident tasks is $n = 3$.

**Step 2:** Compute $(1 - p)$:
$$1 - p = 1 - 0.30 = 0.70$$

**Step 3:** Raise $(1 - p)$ to the power $n$:
$$(1 - p)^n = (0.70)^3$$

**Step 4:** Expand the cube:
$$(0.70)^3 = 0.70 \times 0.70 \times 0.70$$
$$= 0.49 \times 0.70$$
$$= 0.343$$

**Step 5:** Subtract from 1 to obtain CPU utilization:
$$U_{\text{CPU}} = 1 - 0.343 = 0.657$$

**Step 6:** Express as a percentage:
$$U_{\text{CPU}} = 65.7\%$$

**Step 7 — Comparison with uniprogrammed case (for contrast):**
If only one task ran (DMP = 1), then $U_{\text{CPU}} = p = 0.30 = 30\%$. Multiprogramming has **more than doubled** the CPU utilization.

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{U_{\text{CPU}} = 0.657 \approx 65.7\%}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Varying $n$ and $p$

> [!NOTE]
> **Table 3.3 — CPU Utilization $U_{\text{CPU}} = 1 - (1-p)^n$ for Various $p$ and $n$**

| $p$ \ $n$ | $n=1$ | $n=2$ | $n=3$ | $n=5$ | $n=10$ | $n \to \infty$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **$p = 0.10$** | 0.1000 | 0.1900 | 0.2710 | 0.4095 | 0.6513 | 1.0000 |
| **$p = 0.30$** | 0.3000 | 0.5100 | 0.6570 | 0.8319 | 0.9718 | 1.0000 |
| **$p = 0.50$** | 0.5000 | 0.7500 | 0.8750 | 0.9688 | 0.9990 | 1.0000 |
| **$p = 0.80$** | 0.8000 | 0.9600 | 0.9920 | 0.9997 | ~1.0000 | 1.0000 |
| **$p = 0.95$** | 0.9500 | 0.9975 | 0.9999 | ~1.0000 | ~1.0000 | 1.0000 |

**Reading the matrix:** For $p = 0.30$, going from $n=1$ to $n=3$ increases $U$ from 30% to 65.7% — a substantial gain. However, going from $n=3$ to $n=10$ yields only a marginal gain (65.7% → 97.2%) and consumes 3.3× more memory. **This is the Law of Diminishing Returns in multiprogramming.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often assume "more programs = better utilization, always." In real-time systems, increasing $n$ beyond a threshold causes **thrashing** (excessive context switching, cache pollution) which can *decrease* the effective utilization $U_{\text{eff}} = 1 - (1-p)^n - f_{\text{cs}}$. Always quote the **effective** utilization in an RTOS context.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Multiprogramming Memory Layout — Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **Memory Layout of a Multiprogrammed RTOS**, showing how multiple task partitions co-exist in physical RAM and how the dispatcher multiplexes them onto a single CPU.

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below represents the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of a multiprogrammed uniprocessor RTOS, mapping the interactions between the job pool, memory partitions, ready queue, dispatcher, CPU, and I/O subsystem. This is the most commonly tested schematic in KTU Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["JOB POOL\nSecondary Storage HDD SSD"]
    B["LONG TERM SCHEDULER\nLT S Admission Control"]
    C["MAIN MEMORY RAM\nPartition P1 Partition P2 Partition P3"]
    D["SHORT TERM SCHEDULER\nDispatcher Priority Logic"]
    E["CPU CORE\nSingle Execution Unit"]
    F["READY QUEUE\nFIFO or Priority List"]
    G["I O SUBSYSTEM\nSensors Actuators Peripherals"]
    H["WAITING QUEUE\nBlocked Processes List"]
    I["TERMINATION\nProcess Exit Handler"]
    J["TIMER INTERRUPT\nPreemption Source"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> F
    F --> D
    D --> E
    E --> G
    G --> H
    H --> F
    E --> J
    J --> D
    E --> I
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **Job Pool** holds candidate programs on secondary storage.
- The **Long-Term Scheduler (LTS)** admits processes into **Main Memory** based on the degree of multiprogramming (DMP).
- The **Ready Queue** holds admitted processes that are not blocked.
- The **Short-Term Scheduler / Dispatcher** selects ONE process and dispatches it to the CPU.
- The **CPU** executes the process until it (a) issues an I/O call → goes to **Waiting Queue**, (b) is preempted by the **Timer Interrupt**, or (c) terminates.
- The **Waiting Queue** feeds processes back to the Ready Queue once their I/O completes — completing the **multiprogramming loop**.

### 4.2 State Transition Diagram for a Multiprogrammed Process

The following Mermaid state diagram captures the **lifecycle of a process** in a multiprogrammed RTOS, including the preemption arrow driven by the timer interrupt.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New: Process Created
    New --> Ready: LT Scheduler Admit
    Ready --> Running: Dispatcher Selects
    Running --> Waiting: I O or Resource Request
    Waiting --> Ready: I O Complete or Resource Granted
    Running --> Ready: Timer Interrupt Preemption
    Running --> Terminated: Exit or Fatal Error
    Terminated --> [*]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Interpretation:** The arrow `Running → Ready` driven by the **Timer Interrupt** is the *preemptive* extension that distinguishes **multitasking** (modern multiprogramming) from classical batch multiprogramming. In a hard real-time RTOS, the preemption arrow must respect **bounded interrupt latency** and **bounded context switch time**, or the system fails its temporal guarantees.

### 4.3 Comparative Schematic — Uniprogrammed vs. Multiprogrammed CPU Timeline

The following Gantt-style Mermaid diagram contrasts the CPU's active/idle pattern under uniprogrammed vs. multiprogrammed execution of three tasks (T1, T2, T3). Each `R` denotes a CPU-running burst and each `W` denotes a wait/I/O interval.

```mermaid
gantt
    title CPU Timeline Comparison
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %s

    section Uniprogrammed
    T1 Run       :a1, 0, 3
    T1 Wait      :crit, a2, 3, 5
    T1 Run       :a3, 8, 2
    T1 Wait      :crit, a4, 10, 5
    T2 Run       :a5, 15, 4
    T2 Wait      :crit, a6, 19, 5
    T2 Run       :a7, 24, 1

    section Multiprogrammed
    T1 Run       :b1, 0, 3
    T2 Run       :b2, 3, 4
    T3 Run       :b3, 7, 2
    T1 Run       :b4, 9, 2
    T2 Run       :b5, 11, 1
    T3 Run       :b6, 12, 3
    T1 Run       :b7, 15, 1
```

**Interpretation:**
- In the **Uniprogrammed** timeline, the CPU is idle for long `W` (wait) blocks while tasks perform I/O.
- In the **Multiprogrammed** timeline, whenever a task enters a wait state, the dispatcher immediately switches to another ready task — **eliminating CPU idle gaps** and increasing utilization.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU diagrams:** Always label the axis (time in ms or μs), the tasks (T1, T2, T3), and explicitly mark the idle vs. busy CPU intervals. Examiners reward clarity and the visible absence of CPU idle time in the multiprogrammed view.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Define multiprogramming. How does it differ from multitasking?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Multiprogramming is a memory-management and CPU-scheduling strategy in which multiple independent programs are held simultaneously in main memory so that the CPU always has a ready process to execute, thus minimizing idle time.* (1 mark)

*The key difference is the switching granularity and the trigger:*
- *Multiprogramming switches the CPU only when the running program **voluntarily** blocks on I/O or a system call (non-preemptive, coarse-grained).* (1 mark)
- *Multitasking uses a **timer interrupt** to forcibly preempt the running program after a fixed time quantum and switch to another ready process (preemptive, fine-grained).* (1 mark)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> **State the multiprogramming theorem. Explain its significance in an RTOS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*The multiprogramming theorem states that with $n$ resident programs, each using the CPU for a fraction $p$ of its time, the CPU utilization is:* (1 mark)

$$U_{\text{CPU}} = 1 - (1 - p)^n$$

*Significance in RTOS:* (2 marks)
- *It provides an **analytical upper bound** on how much CPU bandwidth is available to schedule real-time tasks.*
- *It justifies why RTOS designers carefully select the **degree of multiprogramming (DMP)**: too few tasks leave the CPU idle (wasted real-time capacity); too many tasks cause thrashing and unbounded context-switch latency, violating deadline guarantees.*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for KTU 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a multiprogrammed operating system. Describe the roles of the long-term scheduler, short-term scheduler, and dispatcher. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the multiprogramming theorem $U_{\text{CPU}} = 1 - (1-p)^n$ from first principles and verify it for the boundary cases. A real-time system has 4 tasks, each spending 75% of its time on I/O. Calculate the CPU utilization. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q3(a) — Architecture of Multiprogrammed OS (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Block Diagram:**
Draw a block diagram with the following components (refer to Section 4.1 above for the canonical Mermaid schematic):
- **Job Pool** (secondary storage) → **Long-Term Scheduler** → **Main Memory (partitions)** → **Ready Queue** → **Short-Term Scheduler (Dispatcher)** → **CPU**.
- **CPU** is bidirectionally connected to the **I/O Subsystem** and the **Waiting Queue**, which feeds back to the **Ready Queue** upon I/O completion.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Long-Term Scheduler (LTS):**
- Controls the **degree of multiprogramming (DMP)**.
- Decides *which* processes are admitted from the job pool to main memory.
- Runs *infrequently*; its decisions affect the long-term mix of I/O-bound vs. CPU-bound processes.
- In an RTOS, the LTS enforces the **task set** declared at system design time and rejects any overload scenario.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Short-Term Scheduler (STS):**
- Runs *very frequently* (every context switch).
- Selects *which ready process* to dispatch next, based on priority, deadline, or policy (e.g., Rate Monotonic, EDF).
- Must execute in **bounded time** (typically < 10 μs) in a hard RTOS.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Dispatcher:**
- The actual mechanism that performs the **context switch** — saving the old process's state into its PCB and loading the new process's state from its PCB into the CPU registers.
- Dispatcher latency = (1) saving state of old process, (2) loading state of new process, (3) switching to user mode, (4) jumping to the new process's PC.
- In RTOS, this latency is part of the system's **interrupt response time** budget.

#### Model Solution for Q3(b) — Derivation + Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Statement:**
Define $p$ as the probability that a single process is using the CPU at a random instant, and $(1-p)$ as the probability it is waiting for I/O. Assume $n$ processes are resident and **statistically independent**.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Derivation:**
- Probability a single process is idle: $1 - p$
- Probability all $n$ processes are simultaneously idle: $(1-p)^n$ (by independence)
- Probability the CPU is busy: $1 - (1-p)^n$
- Therefore: $U_{\text{CPU}} = 1 - (1-p)^n$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Boundary Verification:**
- $n = 1$: $U = 1 - (1-p) = p$ ✓
- $p = 1$: $U = 1 - 0 = 1$ ✓
- $p = 0$: $U = 1 - 1 = 0$ ✓

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Numerical Computation:**
Given: $n = 4$, each task spends 75% on I/O, so the CPU-active fraction is:
$$p = 1 - 0.75 = 0.25$$

Substitute into the theorem:
$$U_{\text{CPU}} = 1 - (1 - 0.25)^4 = 1 - (0.75)^4$$

Compute $(0.75)^4$ step by step:
- $(0.75)^2 = 0.5625$
- $(0.75)^4 = (0.5625)^2 = 0.31640625$

Therefore:
$$U_{\text{CPU}} = 1 - 0.31640625 = 0.68359375 \approx 0.6836$$

$$\boxed{U_{\text{CPU}} \approx 68.36\%}$$

---

> **Q4. [Alternative Choice for KTU 2024 Scheme — Internal Choice Option B]**
> **(a)** Explain the concept of **Degree of Multiprogramming (DMP)**. How is it related to memory size and per-process memory requirement? Discuss the role of **thrashing** in limiting DMP in real-time systems. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A batch-oriented system has 5 processes, each with $p = 0.40$. Compare the CPU utilization when (i) DMP = 5 vs. (ii) DMP = 10. Justify why an RTOS designer would not simply maximize DMP. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q4(a) — DMP & Thrashing (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Definition of DMP:**
- The **Degree of Multiprogramming** is the number of processes resident in main memory at any given instant.
- It is bounded by available memory:
$$\text{DMP}_{\text{max}} = \left\lfloor \frac{M_{\text{avail}}}{M_{\text{per-proc}}} \right\rfloor$$
where $M_{\text{avail}}$ is the available RAM and $M_{\text{per-proc}}$ is the per-process memory footprint (code + data + stack + PCB).

**Step 2 (2 marks) — DMP and Multiprogramming Theorem Linkage:**
As DMP ($n$) increases, the CPU utilization $U_{\text{CPU}} = 1 - (1-p)^n$ increases monotonically toward 1. This is the *upside* of increasing DMP.

**Step 3 (3 marks) — Thrashing and Its RTOS Implications:**
- **Thrashing** occurs when the system spends more time in **context switching and memory swapping** than in productive execution.
- As DMP rises, the working set of each process may exceed available memory → frequent page faults → CPU spends most of its time servicing page faults rather than tasks.
- **Effective utilization** in the presence of context-switch overhead:
$$U_{\text{eff}} = 1 - (1-p)^n - f_{\text{cs}}$$
where $f_{\text{cs}}$ is the fraction of CPU time consumed by context switches.
- In a **hard real-time system**, thrashing is catastrophic: deadlines are missed deterministically, leading to system failure (e.g., missed airbag deployment window, missed flight-control loop).
- Therefore, RTOS designers bound DMP at the **schedulability analysis** stage (e.g., Liu & Layland's utilization bound test for RMS), not at the memory saturation point.

#### Model Solution for Q4(b) — Numerical Comparison + Justification (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Case (i): DMP = 5, $p = 0.40$:**
$$U_{5} = 1 - (1 - 0.40)^5 = 1 - (0.60)^5$$

Compute $(0.60)^5$ step by step:
- $(0.60)^2 = 0.36$
- $(0.60)^3 = 0.36 \times 0.60 = 0.216$
- $(0.60)^4 = 0.216 \times 0.60 = 0.1296$
- $(0.60)^5 = 0.1296 \times 0.60 = 0.07776$

Therefore:
$$U_{5} = 1 - 0.07776 = 0.92224 \approx 92.22\%$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Case (ii): DMP = 10, $p = 0.40$:**
$$U_{10} = 1 - (1 - 0.40)^{10} = 1 - (0.60)^{10}$$

Compute $(0.60)^{10}$ step by step:
- $(0.60)^5 = 0.07776$ (from Step 1)
- $(0.60)^{10} = (0.07776)^2$
- $(0.07776)^2 = 0.0060466$ (approx.)

Therefore:
$$U_{10} = 1 - 0.0060466 = 0.9939534 \approx 99.40\%$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Quantitative Comparison:**
$$\Delta U = U_{10} - U_{5} = 99.40\% - 92.22\% = 7.18\%$$

So doubling DMP from 5 to 10 yields only a **7.18 percentage-point gain** in theoretical utilization.

**Step 4 (3 marks) — Why an RTOS Designer Would Not Maximize DMP:**
- *Diminishing returns:* As shown, large increases in DMP yield marginal utilization gains.
- *Memory cost:* Doubling DMP doubles RAM footprint, which is often infeasible in embedded RT targets (Cortex-M class devices have < 1 MB RAM).
- *Context switch overhead:* More tasks → more preemptions → $f_{\text{cs}}$ grows. $U_{\text{eff}}$ may actually *decrease* past the optimal DMP.
- *Schedulability violation:* Hard real-time guarantees (e.g., Liu & Layland bound: $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ for RMS) are violated if DMP is increased without re-analyzing the task set's worst-case execution time (WCET).
- *Determinism loss:* More concurrent tasks mean more interference, more priority inversions, and harder worst-case latency bounding — antithetical to the RTOS goal of *bounded* temporal behavior.

$$\boxed{\text{RTOS Principle: Choose the } \textbf{minimum DMP} \text{ that meets the utilization bound, not the maximum.}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Formula Mis-quotation:** Writing $U_{\text{CPU}} = 1 - p^n$ (WRONG) instead of $U_{\text{CPU}} = 1 - (1-p)^n$ (CORRECT) costs 1–2 marks instantly. The exponent is on the **idle** probability, not the **active** probability.
> 2. **Confusing Multiprogramming with Multiprocessing:** A 14-mark question that asks for "multiprogrammed OS architecture" and receives an answer about "multi-core CPUs" will receive **at most 2–3 marks**, even if technically accurate.
> 3. **Skipping Boundary Verification:** The boundary case check ($n=1$, $p=0$, $p=1$) is a **frequently awarded bonus point**. Omitting it costs a full mark.
> 4. **No Units in Final Answer:** Express $U_{\text{CPU}}$ as a **percentage (e.g., 65.7%)** AND a fraction. A bare decimal without context loses a mark.
> 5. **Ignoring RTOS Context:** For KTU PECPT741, every multiprogramming answer must explicitly connect to **real-time constraints, deadlines, or determinism**. A purely generic OS answer (e.g., "increases throughput") will be marked down.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Multiprogramming (Module 1)**

- **Core Definition:** Multiprogramming = *multiple programs in memory + always keep CPU busy*.
- **Differentiator:** Multiprogramming is **non-preemptive** (waits for I/O); Multitasking is **preemptive** (timer interrupt).
- **The Multiprogramming Theorem (MUST memorize):**
  $$U_{\text{CPU}} = 1 - (1 - p)^n$$
- **Key Components of a Multiprogrammed OS:**
  1. Job Pool (secondary storage)
  2. Long-Term Scheduler (admits processes, controls DMP)
  3. Main Memory (partitions)
  4. Ready Queue
  5. Short-Term Scheduler / Dispatcher
  6. CPU
  7. Waiting Queue (I/O blocked)
  8. I/O Subsystem
- **Five Process States:** New, Ready, Running, Waiting (Blocked), Terminated.
- **Degree of Multiprogramming (DMP):** $\text{DMP} = M_{\text{avail}} / M_{\text{per-proc}}$.
- **Performance Metrics:** CPU Utilization, Throughput, Turnaround Time, Waiting Time, Response Time, Context Switch Time.
- **Law of Diminishing Returns:** As $n$ increases, marginal $U$ gain shrinks — never blindly maximize DMP.
- **Thrashing:** Excessive context switching / page faults that *reduce* effective utilization. The nemesis of real-time systems.
- **RTOS-Specific Concern:** Bounded context-switch time, bounded interrupt latency, schedulability utilization bound (Liu & Layland: $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ for Rate Monotonic).
- **Boundary Verifications (memorize):**
  - $n=1 \Rightarrow U = p$
  - $p=0 \Rightarrow U = 0$
  - $p=1 \Rightarrow U = 1$
  - $n \to \infty \Rightarrow U \to 1$ (theoretical only)
- **Common Numerical Setup:** 3–5 processes, $p$ in range 0.2–0.8, compute $U_{\text{CPU}}$ and comment on it.
- **Engineering Examples to Quote:** AUTOSAR automotive ECUs, FreeRTOS IoT nodes, ARINC 653 avionics, industrial PLC scan cycles.
- **One-Sentence Exam Closer:** *"Multiprogramming transforms an idle CPU into a continuously executing pipeline of real-time tasks — but in an RTOS, the *determinism* of that pipeline matters far more than its raw utilization."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time-Shared
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT741 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Time-Shared

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time-Shared Operating System (TSOS):** A multiprogrammed, interactive operating system architecture in which the available **CPU time** is partitioned into small, fixed-duration intervals called **time slices** (or **time quanta**), and these slices are rapidly and cyclically allocated to multiple concurrent users/processes, giving each the *illusion* of dedicated, continuous CPU ownership.

In the context of KTU's PECPT741 (Real Time Operating Systems) syllabus, the study of **Time-Shared Systems** is foundational because it establishes the *baseline model* of multitasking from which Real-Time OS principles are contrasted. The defining features, as per KTU Module 1, are:

- **Multi-user / Multi-programmed** execution
- **Interactive** user response
- **Preemptive** scheduling with a fixed time quantum
- **Fast context switching** to maintain the illusion of simultaneity
- **Resource sharing** (CPU, memory, I/O) among many processes

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Roll-Call Teacher"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a school teacher with 60 students in a classroom, and only **one teacher** to answer every question. The teacher cannot speak to all 60 students at once. Instead, the teacher gives each student exactly **1 minute** of undivided attention, then moves to the next student. After cycling through all 60, the teacher comes back to the first student. To the students, it *feels* like the teacher is talking to them continuously, but in reality, the teacher's voice is being **time-sliced** among everyone.

In this analogy:
- **Teacher** → CPU (single processing resource)
- **Students** → Processes / Users competing for CPU
- **1 minute** → Time quantum (slice)
- **Cycling through class** → Round-Robin scheduling
- **Switching attention** → Context switch
- **"Feels continuous"** → Illusion of simultaneity (pseudo-parallelism)

### 1.3 Core Characteristics (At a Glance)

| Characteristic | Value / Behavior |
|---|---|
| **CPU Allocation Unit** | Time Quantum (e.g., **10–100 ms**) |
| **User Count** | Hundreds to thousands simultaneously |
| **Response Goal** | Best-effort, *not* deterministic |
| **Primary Scheduler** | Round-Robin (Preemptive) |
| **Hard Deadlines** | **Not guaranteed** (this is the key RTOS contrast) |
| **Fairness** | Maximized — every process gets equal CPU share |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principles — The "Why" and "How"

A time-shared OS operates on a layered set of mechanisms. Let us dissect each layer:

**(a) The Fundamental Problem**
The CPU, at any given clock cycle, can execute exactly **one** instruction stream from **one** process. With N processes waiting, we have a resource contention problem.

**(b) The Solution — Time Slicing**
The OS divides time into discrete, fixed-length intervals called **time quanta** (denoted $q$). Each process is allowed to execute for a duration of at most $q$ before the OS *preempts* it.

**(c) The Round-Robin Scheduler**
Processes are organized in a **circular ready queue**. The scheduler dispatches the head of the queue, sets a timer for $q$ time units, and on timer expiry performs a **context switch** to the next process.

**(d) The Context Switch**
Saving the entire state of the currently running process (registers, program counter, stack pointer, memory maps) into its **Process Control Block (PCB)**, and loading the state of the next process. This costs **overhead time** $\tau_{cs}$.

**(e) Fairness and the Illusion of Parallelism**
If the time quantum $q$ is sufficiently small (typically **< 100 ms**), the human eye / user perception cannot detect the gaps, producing the **illusion of simultaneous execution**. This is called **pseudo-parallelism**.

### 2.2 Why Time-Shared Systems Are NOT Real-Time Systems

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Distinction:** A time-shared OS optimizes for **throughput** and **fairness**. A real-time OS optimizes for **deadline adherence**. In TSOS, a process may wait arbitrarily long before getting the CPU — this is **unacceptable** in hard real-time systems (e.g., anti-lock braking, pacemakers).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Metric | Formula | Units | KTU Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CPU Utilization** $U$ | $U = \dfrac{T_{\text{busy}}}{T_{\text{busy}} + T_{\text{idle}}} \times 100\%$ | **%** | Includes context switch overhead |
| 2 | **Throughput** $\lambda$ | $\lambda = \dfrac{N_{\text{completed}}}{T_{\text{total}}}$ | **processes/sec** | Higher is better |
| 3 | **Turnaround Time (TAT)** | $T_{TAT} = T_{\text{completion}} - T_{\text{arrival}}$ | **ms or s** | Total time in system |
| 4 | **Waiting Time (WT)** | $T_{WT} = T_{TAT} - T_{\text{burst}}$ | **ms or s** | Time spent in ready queue |
| 5 | **Response Time (RT)** | $T_{RT} = T_{\text{first response}} - T_{\text{arrival}}$ | **ms or s** | Crucial for interactive TSOS |
| 6 | **Context Switch Overhead** $f_{cs}$ | $f_{cs} = \dfrac{\tau_{cs}}{q} \times 100\%$ | **%** | Should be < 10% of $q$ |
| 7 | **Effective CPU Time** | $T_{\text{eff}} = N \cdot (q - \tau_{cs})$ | **ms** | Net useful work per round |
| 8 | **Average Waiting Time (RR)** | $\overline{WT} = \dfrac{(N-1) \cdot q_{\text{residual}} - \text{burst residuals}}{N}$ | **ms** | Used in numerical problems |
| 9 | **Time Quantum Lower Bound** | $q \gg \tau_{cs}$ | **ms** | Must be >> context switch time |
| 10 | **Time Quantum Upper Bound** | $q < T_{\text{interactive limit}}$ (≈ **100 ms**) | **ms** | Human perception threshold |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Rule of Time Quantum:** The time quantum $q$ must be **large enough** to make context switch overhead negligible, but **small enough** to feel instantaneous to the interactive user. The KTU-recommended balance is $q$ such that **context switch overhead $f_{cs} \le 10\%$**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Early Mainframes (1960s–1980s):** CTSS, MULTICS, UNIX — hundreds of users per machine
- **Modern Cloud Servers:** Virtual machines and containers are time-sliced on shared hardware
- **Linux/Windows Desktops:** Even single-user systems are time-shared across apps (browser, IDE, music player)
- **Educational Importance:** Establishes the conceptual baseline for understanding *why* RTOS exists — RTOS adds **deterministic deadlines** on top of the time-sharing framework.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Waiting Time in Pure Round-Robin (KTU Standard Problem)

**Given:**
- $N$ processes in the ready queue
- Time quantum $= q$
- All processes arrive at time $t = 0$
- Process $i$ has total CPU burst $B_i$

**Step 1: Determine the number of full quanta consumed by process $i$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
k_i &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{B_i}{q} \right\rfloor
\end{aligned}$$

Each process $i$ will be preempted exactly $k_i$ times *during* its execution (it is not preempted on its final, partial quantum).

**Step 2: Calculate the waiting time component from full quanta.**

In every quantum other than its own, the process waits one full quantum $q$. A process completing $k_i$ full quanta waits $k_i$ other quanta in the *worst* scheduling sense. Across $N$ processes, this gives:

$$\begin{aligned}
WT_{i,\text{full}} &= k_i \cdot q
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Add the residual partial quantum waiting.**

The last (partial) quantum of process $i$ is the $(k_i+1)$-th quantum. By then, the other $N-1$ processes have all completed $k_i$ full quanta. So in that final round, process $i$ waits for $(N-1) \cdot q$ *less* the residual burst it has left.

$$\begin{aligned}
r_i &= B_i - k_i \cdot q \quad \text{(residual burst, where } 0 \le r_i < q \text{)} \\
WT_{i,\text{residual}} &= (N-1) \cdot q - (q - r_i) = (N-2)\cdot q + r_i
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Total waiting time for process $i$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
WT_i &= k_i \cdot q + (N-2)\cdot q + r_i \\
WT_i &= k_i \cdot q + (N-2)\cdot q + (B_i - k_i \cdot q) \\
WT_i &= (N-2)\cdot q + B_i
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** For all processes that complete within the first round (i.e., $k_i = 0$ and $r_i = B_i < q$), the waiting time simplifies to a uniform $(N-1)\cdot q$ across all such processes — **perfect fairness**.

**Step 5: Average waiting time across all $N$ processes.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{WT} &= \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} WT_i
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — CPU Efficiency Under Context Switch Overload

**Given:** Time quantum $q = 20$ ms, Context switch time $\tau_{cs} = 1$ ms, $N$ processes all CPU-bound.

**Step 1: Each process loses 1 ms per quantum for context switching.**

The *effective* useful time per quantum is:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{eff per quantum}} &= q - \tau_{cs} = 20 - 1 = 19 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: CPU efficiency $\eta$ is the ratio of useful time to total quantum time.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \dfrac{T_{\text{eff per quantum}}}{q} \times 100\% \\
\eta &= \dfrac{q - \tau_{cs}}{q} \times 100\% \\
\eta &= \left(1 - \dfrac{\tau_{cs}}{q}\right) \times 100\% \\
\eta &= \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{20}\right) \times 100\% \\
\eta &= 0.95 \times 100\% = \mathbf{95\%}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever $\tau_{cs} > 0.1 \cdot q$, the system is considered **inefficient**, and KTU examiners may ask you to recommend a new quantum size. Always verify the **10% rule**.

**Step 3: Minimum time quantum to maintain $\eta \ge 90\%$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
1 - \dfrac{\tau_{cs}}{q} &\ge 0.90 \\
\dfrac{\tau_{cs}}{q} &\le 0.10 \\
q &\ge \dfrac{\tau_{cs}}{0.10} = 10 \cdot \tau_{cs}
\end{aligned}$$

So $q$ must be at least **10 times** the context switch time. For $\tau_{cs} = 1$ ms, the minimum is $q_{\min} = 10$ ms.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Example (KTU Standard Format)

**Problem:** Consider 4 processes arriving at $t=0$ with CPU bursts: $P_1 = 24$ ms, $P_2 = 3$ ms, $P_3 = 3$ ms, $P_4 = 7$ ms. Time quantum $q = 4$ ms. Compute average waiting time.

| Step | Action | Ready Queue After | $P_1$ | $P_2$ | $P_3$ | $P_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_1$ runs 4 ms (0–4) | $P_2, P_3, P_4, P_1$ | 20 | 3 | 3 | 7 |
| 2 | $P_2$ runs 3 ms (4–7) | $P_3, P_4, P_1$ | 20 | 0 ✓ | 3 | 7 |
| 3 | $P_3$ runs 3 ms (7–10) | $P_4, P_1$ | 20 | 0 | 0 ✓ | 7 |
| 4 | $P_4$ runs 4 ms (10–14) | $P_1$ | 20 | 0 | 0 | 3 |
| 5 | $P_1$ runs 4 ms (14–18) | $P_4$ | 16 | 0 | 0 | 3 |
| 6 | $P_4$ runs 3 ms (18–21) | $P_1$ | 16 | 0 | 0 | 0 ✓ |
| 7 | $P_1$ runs 4 ms (21–25) | $P_1$ | 12 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 8 | $P_1$ runs 4 ms (25–29) | $P_1$ | 8 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 9 | $P_1$ runs 4 ms (29–33) | $P_1$ | 4 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 10 | $P_1$ runs 4 ms (33–37) | — | 0 ✓ | 0 | 0 | 0 |

**Completion times:** $P_1 = 37$, $P_2 = 7$, $P_3 = 10$, $P_4 = 21$.

**Turnaround times (all arrive at 0):** TAT = CT - 0 → $37, 7, 10, 21$.

**Waiting times:** $WT_i = TAT_i - B_i$:

$$\begin{aligned}
WT_1 &= 37 - 24 = 13 \text{ ms} \\
WT_2 &= 7 - 3 = 4 \text{ ms} \\
WT_3 &= 10 - 3 = 7 \text{ ms} \\
WT_4 &= 21 - 7 = 14 \text{ ms} \\
\overline{WT} &= \dfrac{13 + 4 + 7 + 14}{4} = \dfrac{38}{4} = \mathbf{9.5 \text{ ms}}
\end{aligned}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Time-Shared OS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USER_LAYER["USER LAYER - Multiple Interactive Terminals"]
        U1["User 1 Terminal"]
        U2["User 2 Terminal"]
        U3["User N Terminal"]
    end

    subgraph KERNEL_LAYER["KERNEL LAYER - OS Core"]
        SCHED["Round Robin Scheduler"]
        TIMER["Hardware Timer Interrupt"]
        DISP["Dispatcher Module"]
        CSW["Context Switch Engine"]
        PCB_MGR["Process Control Block Manager"]
    end

    subgraph HW_LAYER["HARDWARE LAYER"]
        CPU["Single CPU Core"]
        MEM["Shared Main Memory"]
        IO["Shared I O Devices"]
    end

    U1 --> SCHED
    U2 --> SCHED
    U3 --> SCHED

    SCHED --> DISP
    TIMER --> SCHED
    DISP --> CSW
    CSW --> PCB_MGR

    DISP --> CPU
    PCB_MGR --> MEM
    CPU --> IO

    CSW -. saves and loads state .-> PCB_MGR
    TIMER -. triggers every q ms .-> CSW
```

### 4.2 Round-Robin Scheduling Flow (Execution Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Start Scheduler"] --> B["Pick Head of Ready Queue"]
    B --> C["Load PCB of Selected Process"]
    C --> D["Start Hardware Timer q ms"]
    D --> E{"Process Blocked or Quantum Expired"}
    E -->|Quantum Expired| F["Save PCB to Ready Queue Tail"]
    E -->|Process Blocked I O| G["Move to Waiting Queue"]
    F --> H["Context Switch to Next Process"]
    G --> H
    H --> B
```

### 4.3 Time Slice vs. Context Switch Overhead — The "10% Rule" Visualization

```mermaid
gantt
    title Time Quantum vs Context Switch Overhead
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s

    section Efficient Case q = 100ms, tcs = 5ms
    Process A Useful Work   :a1, 0, 95
    Context Switch Overhead :a2, 95, 100

    section Inefficient Case q = 10ms, tcs = 3ms
    Process A Useful Work   :b1, 0, 7
    Context Switch Overhead :b2, 7, 10
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Gantt chart:** In the *efficient case*, only 5% of the quantum is wasted on context switching. In the *inefficient case*, 30% is wasted — making the time-shared system appear sluggish and reducing throughput dramatically. The KTU exam often asks: *"What is the minimum quantum to keep overhead < 10%?"* — the answer is $q \ge 10 \cdot \tau_{cs}$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECPT741]`
**Define a Time-Shared Operating System. List any four key characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Time-Shared Operating System (TSOS)** is a multiprogrammed, interactive OS in which the CPU is allocated to multiple users/processes in a round-robin fashion, with each process receiving a fixed-duration time quantum. The system uses rapid context switching to create the *illusion* of simultaneous, dedicated CPU access for every user.

**Four key characteristics:**
1. **Multi-user support** — Hundreds of users interact concurrently.
2. **Preemptive round-robin scheduling** — Each process gets a fixed time quantum.
3. **Fast context switching** — Quantum kept small (< 100 ms) for responsiveness.
4. **Resource sharing** — CPU, memory, and I/O devices are multiplexed among all users.

---

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECPT741]`
**Distinguish between a Time-Shared System and a Real-Time Operating System in 4 points.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Parameter | Time-Shared OS | Real-Time OS (RTOS) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Primary Goal** | Maximize throughput & fairness | Meet strict **deadlines** |
| 2 | **Response Time** | Best-effort, variable | Deterministic, bounded |
| 3 | **Scheduling** | Round-Robin, time-slice based | Priority / Deadline-driven (Rate Monotonic, EDF) |
| 4 | **Failure Consequence** | Tolerable (e.g., slow UI) | Catastrophic (e.g., airbag delay) |
| 5 | **Example** | Linux desktop, Windows server | VxWorks, QNX, FreeRTOS |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] **Explain the architecture and working of a Time-Shared Operating System with a neat block diagram. Discuss how the Round-Robin scheduler achieves fairness.**

**Model Solution:**

A Time-Shared Operating System (TSOS) is built around the principle of *multiplexing the CPU* across many concurrent users. Its architecture consists of three primary layers: (1) the **User Layer** with multiple interactive terminals, (2) the **Kernel Layer** housing the scheduler, dispatcher, and PCB manager, and (3) the **Hardware Layer** containing the CPU, memory, and I/O devices.

The **working cycle** proceeds as follows:

- The **Round-Robin Scheduler** maintains a circular ready queue of all runnable processes.
- A **Hardware Timer** is configured to fire every $q$ milliseconds (the time quantum).
- The **Dispatcher** loads the Process Control Block (PCB) of the head-of-queue process onto the CPU and starts execution.
- On either (i) quantum expiry, or (ii) voluntary blocking (e.g., I/O wait), the CPU is preempted.
- The **Context Switch Engine** saves the current process's state to its PCB, places the PCB at the queue tail, and loads the next process.

**Fairness is achieved because:**
- Every process receives *exactly* one time quantum per full round.
- No process is starved — even low-priority processes get CPU time.
- The wait time for any process is bounded by $(N-1) \cdot q$, where $N$ is the queue length.

---

**(b)** [7 Marks] **Consider 4 processes arriving at time 0 with CPU bursts: $P_1 = 17$ ms, $P_2 = 6$ ms, $P_3 = 11$ ms, $P_4 = 4$ ms. Using Round-Robin scheduling with time quantum $q = 5$ ms, draw the Gantt chart and calculate the average waiting time and average turnaround time.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Construct the Gantt chart.**

| Step | Time Interval | Running Process | Remaining Bursts (P1,P2,P3,P4) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 – 5 | P1 | 12, 6, 11, 4 |
| 2 | 5 – 10 | P2 | 12, 1, 11, 4 |
| 3 | 10 – 15 | P3 | 12, 1, 6, 4 |
| 4 | 15 – 20 | P4 | 12, 1, 6, 0 ✓ |
| 5 | 20 – 25 | P1 | 7, 1, 6, 0 |
| 6 | 25 – 26 | P2 | 7, 0 ✓, 6, 0 |
| 7 | 26 – 31 | P3 | 7, 0, 1, 0 |
| 8 | 31 – 36 | P1 | 2, 0, 1, 0 |
| 9 | 36 – 37 | P3 | 2, 0, 0 ✓, 0 |
| 10 | 37 – 39 | P1 | 0 ✓, 0, 0, 0 |

**Gantt:** `[P1 | P2 | P3 | P4 | P1 | P2 | P3 | P1 | P3 | P1]`
Times: `0--5--10--15--20--25--26--31--36--37--39`

**Step 2: Calculate Completion Times (CT).**

$$\begin{aligned}
CT_1 &= 39, \quad CT_2 = 26, \quad CT_3 = 37, \quad CT_4 = 20
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Turnaround Time** $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$ (all arrived at 0):

$$\begin{aligned}
TAT_1 &= 39, \quad TAT_2 = 26, \quad TAT_3 = 37, \quad TAT_4 = 20
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Waiting Time** $WT_i = TAT_i - B_i$:

$$\begin{aligned}
WT_1 &= 39 - 17 = 22 \text{ ms} \\
WT_2 &= 26 - 6 = 20 \text{ ms} \\
WT_3 &= 37 - 11 = 26 \text{ ms} \\
WT_4 &= 20 - 4 = 16 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Averages.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{WT} &= \dfrac{22 + 20 + 26 + 16}{4} = \dfrac{84}{4} = \mathbf{21 \text{ ms}} \\
\overline{TAT} &= \dfrac{39 + 26 + 37 + 20}{4} = \dfrac{122}{4} = \mathbf{30.5 \text{ ms}}
\end{aligned}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] **With suitable examples, explain how context switching overhead affects CPU efficiency in a time-shared system. Derive the relationship between time quantum $q$, context switch time $\tau_{cs}$, and CPU efficiency $\eta$.**

**Model Solution:**

In a time-shared OS, the CPU is constantly switching between processes. Each switch incurs a **context switch overhead** $\tau_{cs}$ — the time spent saving and loading PCB state during which **no useful work is done**.

**Derivation:**

Consider a single quantum $q$ allocated to a process. The effective useful CPU work done is $(q - \tau_{cs})$. The CPU efficiency $\eta$ is defined as the ratio of useful work to total quantum time:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \dfrac{q - \tau_{cs}}{q} \\
\eta &= 1 - \dfrac{\tau_{cs}}{q}
\end{aligned}$$

**Numerical Example 1:** If $q = 20$ ms and $\tau_{cs} = 1$ ms:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= 1 - \dfrac{1}{20} = 0.95 = 95\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Numerical Example 2:** If $q = 5$ ms and $\tau_{cs} = 1$ ms (poor choice):

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= 1 - \dfrac{1}{5} = 0.80 = 80\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Implication:** A very small quantum causes severe efficiency loss. A very large quantum makes the system feel unresponsive. The **optimal quantum** satisfies:

$$\begin{aligned}
q &\ge 10 \cdot \tau_{cs} \quad \text{(to keep } \eta \ge 90\% \text{)} \\
q &\le 100 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(to keep response time perceivable as instant)}
\end{aligned}$$

---

**(b)** [7 Marks] **Explain the concept of "pseudo-parallelism" in time-shared systems. Why does it fail to be acceptable in hard real-time systems? Illustrate with an example.**

**Model Solution:**

**Pseudo-parallelism** is the phenomenon where multiple processes appear to execute simultaneously on a single-CPU system, even though only one instruction stream runs at any instant. This illusion is created by rapidly switching the CPU between processes, each receiving tiny time slices (< 100 ms), faster than human perception.

**Why it fails in hard real-time systems:**

A hard real-time system has **strict deadlines** that, if missed, cause catastrophic failure (e.g., loss of life, system damage). Pseudo-parallelism is *best-effort* — it does not guarantee that a critical process will receive the CPU *in time* to meet its deadline.

**Illustrative Example — Anti-lock Braking System (ABS):**

| Process | Deadline | Worst-case wait in TSOS |
|---|---|---|
| Brake sensor sampling | **5 ms** (every cycle) | Could be up to $(N-1) \cdot q$ |
| Airbag deployment check | **10 ms** | Could exceed 10 ms if $N$ is large |
| Display refresh | 100 ms | Acceptable (TSOS can handle this) |

If a TSOS is used for ABS and the ready queue has $N = 200$ processes with $q = 5$ ms, the worst-case wait for a critical sensor process is $(200-1) \cdot 5 = 995$ ms — **200× longer** than the 5 ms deadline! This makes TSOS **categorically unsuitable** for hard real-time safety-critical applications. Hence RTOS uses **priority-based preemptive scheduling** (e.g., Rate Monotonic Scheduling) to guarantee deadline compliance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing TAT and WT:** Turnaround Time = CT − AT, Waiting Time = TAT − Burst. Many students mistakenly subtract in the wrong order.
> 2. **Forgetting the partial quantum:** In Round-Robin, the *last* quantum of each process is a partial one — you must NOT assume all quanta are full-length $q$.
> 3. **Ignoring context switch overhead in $\eta$ calculation:** If the question mentions $\tau_{cs}$, your efficiency formula *must* subtract it.
> 4. **Not stating the assumption $AT = 0$:** When all processes arrive at $t = 0$, explicitly write this. Examiners deduct 1 mark if assumed implicitly.
> 5. **Mixing up time-shared and real-time goals:** TSOS optimizes for *throughput/fairness*; RTOS optimizes for *deadlines*. Mixing these in a comparison answer is a common 2-mark loss.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Time-Shared OS = Round-Robin + Fast Context Switch + Multi-User.**
- **Time quantum $q$** is the single most important tunable parameter.
- **The 10% rule:** $q \ge 10 \cdot \tau_{cs}$ to keep efficiency above **90%**.
- **Upper limit of $q$:** **100 ms** to maintain interactive feel.
- **Pseudo-parallelism** is an *illusion* of simultaneity created by fast switching — not true parallel execution.
- **Fairness in RR:** Every process gets one quantum per round; max wait = $(N-1) \cdot q$.
- **CPU efficiency formula:** $\eta = 1 - (\tau_{cs} / q)$.
- **Waiting Time** in RR for process $i$: $WT_i = (N-2)\cdot q + B_i$ *(when $B_i > q$ and all arrive at 0)*.
- **Turnaround Time:** $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$.
- **TSOS vs RTOS — key contrast:** TSOS is *best-effort*; RTOS is *deadline-bound*. RTOS rejects the TSOS model and replaces Round-Robin with **priority preemption**.
- **KTU Board Exam Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Expect 1 definition-type (3 marks) + 1 numerical on Gantt chart + WT/TAT (14 marks) almost every semester.
- **Watch your units:** ms vs s — KTU examiners are strict.
- **Always draw a Gantt chart** for RR problems; a textual schedule without Gantt loses easy marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Personal Computer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT741 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Personal Computer ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Personal Computer (PC)** is a general-purpose, single-user, microprocessor-based computing system designed for individual use, built around a **Von Neumann architecture** that integrates a Central Processing Unit (CPU), main memory (RAM/ROM), Input/Output (I/O) subsystems, and a hierarchical bus system (system, address, data, and control buses) on a motherboard. In the context of **Real Time Operating Systems (RTOS)**, the PC architecture is studied as the *baseline computational model* against which resource-constrained, deterministic **embedded systems** are contrasted, because the PC's non-deterministic OS behaviour (e.g., caches, virtual memory, DMA bursts) violates the **bounded response time** requirement fundamental to real-time computing. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight (Module 1):** The "Personal Computer" topic is the *gateway concept* of RTOS. Examiners frequently ask students to compare a PC with an embedded system to highlight *why RTOS exists in the first place*. Do not skip this foundational contrast. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Office Building Imagine a large corporate office building: - **The CEO (CPU)** sits in a corner office, gives orders, and processes decisions. - **The Filing Cabinet (RAM/ROM)** is right next to the CEO — fast to access but limited in size. - **The Warehouse (Hard Disk / SSD)** is in the basement — huge storage but slow to retrieve from. - **The Reception Desk (I/O Controller)** receives mail (keyboard input), sends letters (printer output), and handles visitors (USB devices). - **The Internal Telephone System (System Bus)** connects every room; the CEO dials the *address* of any room through the address line and exchanges *data* through the data line. - **The Pneumatic Tube (Control Bus)** carries small command slips like "READ", "WRITE", or "INTERRUPT" that tell each department what to do. Just as a real office is *general-purpose* (can handle any kind of business), a Personal Computer is *general-purpose* — it is **not optimised** for any single task, unlike an embedded controller in a washing machine or a car ECU. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Metric | Standard Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | Word size of early PCs | **8 bits / 16 bits** | Foundational in 8086/8088 era | | Modern PC word size | **64 bits** | Register width in x86-64 CPUs | | Data bus width (modern) | **64 bits** | Transfers per memory cycle | | Address bus width (modern 64-bit) | **48 bits (effective) / 64 bits (physical)** | Up to **256 TiB** of virtual address space | | Standard I/O port count (x86) | **65,536 ports (16-bit I/O address space)** | Used in I/O-mapped I/O | | Typical system clock | **1 GHz – 5 GHz** | Determines instruction execution rate | > [!NOTE] > **Why a PC is NOT a Real-Time System (Examiner Favourite):** A modern PC uses cache memory, virtual memory paging, DMA controllers, and a multi-tasking OS — all of which introduce *unbounded latency*. A hard real-time system requires a *deterministic upper bound* on response time, which a general-purpose PC cannot guarantee. This is the single most important reason we study PC architecture *before* moving to RTOS design. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Block-Level Anatomy of a Personal Computer A PC is organised into three major subsystems communicating through a shared **system bus**: 1. **CPU (Central Processing Unit)** — the brain, executes instructions. 2. **Main Memory (Primary Storage)** — RAM + ROM, holds active program/data. 3. **I/O Subsystem** — keyboard, mouse, display, disk, network, etc. The internal structure of the CPU itself contains: - **ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit)** — performs arithmetic (+, −, ×, ÷) and logical (AND, OR, NOT) operations. - **Control Unit (CU)** — decodes instructions and generates control signals. - **Register File** — small, ultra-fast on-chip storage (e.g., Program Counter, Instruction Register, Accumulator, General Purpose Registers). - **Cache (L1, L2, L3)** — high-speed buffer between CPU and RAM. ### 2.2 The Three Buses — Structured Logic #### (a) Address Bus - **Unidirectional** (CPU → Memory/I/O). - Width = **n bits** ⇒ CPU can directly address **2ⁿ unique locations**. - Example: 8086 has a 20-bit address bus → can address **2²⁰ = 1,048,576 bytes = 1 MB** of memory. - Modern 64-bit CPUs have 48-bit effective address bus → **2⁴⁸ = 256 TiB** virtual space. #### (b) Data Bus - **Bidirectional**. - Width = **n bits** ⇒ transfers **n bits per memory cycle**. - Example: 8088 has a 16-bit internal ALU but an **8-bit external data bus** → "16-bit processor with 8-bit bus" — a classic KTU question. #### (c) Control Bus - **Collection of control lines**: $\overline{\text{RD}}$ (read), $\overline{\text{WR}}$ (write), $\overline{\text{MREQ}}$ (memory request), $\overline{\text{IORQ}}$ (I/O request), $\overline{\text{INT}}$ (interrupt), $\overline{\text{NMI}}$ (non-maskable interrupt), $\overline{\text{BUSREQ}}$, $\overline{\text{BUSACK}}$, clock, reset. ### 2.3 Memory Hierarchy in a PC (Latency Ladder) | Level | Storage | Typical Capacity | Access Time | Managed By | |---|---|---|---|---| | L0 | CPU Registers | ~64 × 64 bits | ~1 ns | Hardware | | L1 Cache | SRAM on-die | 32 – 64 KB per core | ~1–2 ns | Hardware | | L2 Cache | SRAM on-die | 256 KB – 1 MB | ~3–10 ns | Hardware | | L3 Cache | Shared SRAM | 4 – 32 MB | ~10–20 ns | Hardware | | Main Memory | DRAM | 8 – 64 GB | ~50–100 ns | OS (virtual memory) | | Secondary Storage | SSD / HDD | 512 GB – 4 TB | SSD: ~50 µs / HDD: ~5 ms | OS / Firmware | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** The cache and virtual-memory systems are the *primary sources of non-determinism* in a PC. A cache miss or a page fault can stall the CPU for **microseconds to milliseconds** — catastrophic in a hard real-time system. RTOS designers therefore often *disable caches* or use **deterministic memory layouts** in safety-critical systems (e.g., automotive, avionics). ### 2.4 I/O Addressing — Two Models #### Memory-Mapped I/O - I/O devices share the **same address space** as memory. - Any memory instruction (LOAD/STORE) can access I/O. - **Advantage:** Uniform instruction set; full instruction set usable for I/O. - **Disadvantage:** Reduces available memory space. - **Used in:** ARM, MIPS, RISC-V, most RISC processors. #### I/O-Mapped I/O (Isolated I/O) - Separate address space for I/O, accessed via special instructions (`IN`, `OUT` in x86). - Limited to 16-bit I/O address space → **65,536 ports** in x86. - **Advantage:** Full memory space available; faster I/O decoding. - **Disadvantage:** Requires dedicated I/O instructions. - **Used in:** Intel 8086, x86 family. ### 2.5 Interrupts in a PC — The "Knock on the CEO's Door" A PC handles asynchronous events via a **prioritised interrupt mechanism**: 1. **Maskable Interrupts (IRQ)** — can be disabled by setting the **Interrupt Enable (IE) flag**. Routed through the **PIC (Programmable Interrupt Controller)** — historically the 8259A, now integrated into APIC on modern chipsets. 2. **Non-Maskable Interrupts (NMI)** — cannot be disabled; used for catastrophic events (e.g., memory parity error, watchdog timeout). 3. **Software Interrupts** — triggered by `INT n` instruction (x86) or `SVC/SWI` (ARM); used for **system calls** into the OS kernel. 4. **Exceptions** — internally generated by the CPU (divide by zero, page fault, illegal opcode). > [!NOTE] > **RTOS Connection:** Real-time kernels use a *reentrant interrupt service routine (ISR)* model with a **fixed priority scheme** and bounded interrupt latency. The PC's general-purpose interrupt architecture (APIC with message-signalled interrupts) prioritises *fairness* and *throughput*, not *worst-case latency* — another reason a stock PC is unsuitable for hard real-time. ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Addressable memory | $M = 2^n$ bytes | $n$ = address bus width (bits) | | 2 | Data transferred per bus cycle | $D = w$ bits | $w$ = data bus width | | 3 | Total memory transfer time | $T = \dfrac{N \times c}{f}$ | $N$ = bytes, $c$ = cycles/byte, $f$ = clock | | 4 | Bus bandwidth | $BW = f_{\text{bus}} \times w$ | bits/sec | | 5 | Memory-mapped I/O address space | $= 2^n$ (shared with memory) | unified | | 6 | Isolated I/O address space | $= 2^{16} = 65{,}536$ ports (x86) | separate | | 7 | CPU clock period | $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$ | seconds | | 8 | Instruction execution time | $T_i = (\text{cycles per instruction}) \times T_{clk}$ | seconds | | 9 | Average memory access time (2-level cache) | $T_{avg} = h_1 T_1 + (1-h_1) h_2 T_2 + (1-h_1)(1-h_2) T_m$ | $h$ = hit rate | | 10 | Interrupt latency bound (theoretical) | $L_{IRQ} \leq T_{max\ instr} + T_{context\ save}$ | bounded ⇒ real-time | > [!IMPORTANT] > **LaTeX Note:** Absolute values and conditional bars are written using `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the literal `|` to preserve the markdown table structure. ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Hardware Diagnostics:** PC architecture knowledge underpins BIOS/UEFI firmware, device driver development, and OS kernel engineering. - **Embedded Bridge:** Understanding the PC's *non-determinism* motivates the *determinism guarantees* of RTOS (VxWorks, QNX, FreeRTOS, RTEMS). - **SoC Design:** Modern System-on-Chip designs (smartphone chips, automotive MCUs) integrate PC-style components (CPU, cache, memory controller, DMA) on a single die — same principles, different scale. - **Cyber-Physical Systems:** The PC is the *development host* on which RTOS-based firmware is compiled, debugged (JTAG, GDB), and validated before deployment to an embedded target. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Addressable Memory from Address Bus Width **Problem:** A Personal Computer uses an Intel 8086 microprocessor with a 20-bit address bus. Calculate the maximum directly addressable primary memory. **Step 1 — Identify the given parameter.** The address bus width $n = 20$ bits. **Step 2 — Apply the addressing formula.** Each unique binary pattern on the address bus selects one memory location. The total number of unique patterns of $n$ bits is $2^n$. $$ M = 2^{n} = 2^{20} $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the exponent.** Since $2^{10} = 1024 \approx 1\ \text{Ki}$ (kibibyte), we get: $$ 2^{20} = (2^{10})^2 = 1024^2 = 1{,}048{,}576\ \text{bytes} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to standard units.** $$ M = 1{,}048{,}576\ \text{B} = 1024\ \text{KiB} = 1\ \text{MiB}\ \text{(mebibyte)} $$ > **Result:** The 8086 can directly address **1 MiB (1,048,576 bytes)** of memory — which is exactly why early PCs were limited to the "640 KiB conventional + 384 KiB upper memory" DOS map. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Bus Bandwidth **Problem:** A PC's memory bus operates at a clock frequency $f_{\text{bus}} = 800\ \text{MHz}$ with a data bus width of $w = 64$ bits. One word is transferred per clock cycle. Compute the peak bus bandwidth in GB/s. **Step 1 — State the bandwidth formula.** $$ BW = f_{\text{bus}} \times w $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the values.** $$ BW = 800 \times 10^6\ \text{Hz} \times 64\ \text{bits/cycle} $$ **Step 3 — Multiply.** $$ BW = 51{,}200 \times 10^6\ \text{bits/s} = 5.12 \times 10^{10}\ \text{bits/s} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to bytes per second.** $$ BW = \dfrac{5.12 \times 10^{10}}{8} = 6.4 \times 10^{9}\ \text{bytes/s} $$ **Step 5 — Convert to GB/s.** $$ BW = 6.4\ \text{GB/s} $$ > **Result:** Peak memory bandwidth = **6.4 GB/s**. (Real-world sustained bandwidth is typically 60–80 % of peak due to refresh, command overhead, and contention.) --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) with Two-Level Cache **Problem:** A PC has an L1 cache with hit rate $h_1 = 0.95$ and access time $T_1 = 2\ \text{ns}$, an L2 cache with hit rate $h_2 = 0.90$ (for L1 misses) and access time $T_2 = 10\ \text{ns}$, and main memory access time $T_m = 100\ \text{ns}$. Compute the average memory access time $T_{avg}$. **Step 1 — Write the AMAT formula for a 2-level cache hierarchy.** $$ T_{avg} = h_1 \cdot T_1 + (1 - h_1) \cdot h_2 \cdot T_2 + (1 - h_1)(1 - h_2) \cdot T_m $$ **Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.** $$ T_{avg} = (0.95)(2) + (0.05)(0.90)(10) + (0.05)(0.10)(100) $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate each term.** - L1 contribution: $(0.95)(2) = 1.90\ \text{ns}$ - L2 contribution: $(0.05)(0.90)(10) = 0.45\ \text{ns}$ - Main memory contribution: $(0.05)(0.10)(100) = 0.50\ \text{ns}$ **Step 4 — Sum the contributions.** $$ T_{avg} = 1.90 + 0.45 + 0.50 = 2.85\ \text{ns} $$ > **Result:** $T_{avg} = 2.85\ \text{ns}$. Notice that the **main memory contribution (0.50 ns)** is comparable to L2 (0.45 ns) — a clear illustration that even a 5 % L1 miss rate dominates the average. In an RTOS context, this *unbounded penalty* of a main memory access is precisely what must be eliminated. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Von Neumann vs Harvard Architecture | Parameter | Von Neumann (PC default) | Harvard (Used in DSPs, some MCUs) | |---|---|---| | Memory for instructions and data | **Shared** bus and memory | **Separate** buses and memory | | Bus count | 1 (unified) | 2 (instruction + data) | | Bottleneck | **Von Neumann bottleneck** — fetch & execute share bus | No such bottleneck | | Speed | Slower for data-heavy workloads | Faster concurrent fetch + data access | | Determinism | Lower (shared contention) | **Higher** — preferred in RTOS targets | | Examples | 8086, x86, ARM (in Von Neumann mode) | AVR, PIC, ARM Cortex-M (modified Harvard) | | PC relevance | Modern desktop/laptop CPU | Embedded controllers, DSPs | --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: PC vs Embedded System (Examination Favourite) | Parameter | Personal Computer | Embedded System (RTOS Target) | |---|---|---| | Purpose | General-purpose computation | Single dedicated function | | User interface | Rich GUI, keyboard, mouse | Often none or minimal (LED, button) | | OS | Windows / Linux / macOS (GPOS) | RTOS (VxWorks, QNX, FreeRTOS) | | Determinism | **Non-deterministic** (caches, paging) | **Deterministic / bounded** | | Power | High (100s of W) | Low (mW – few W) | | Real-time guarantee | None | **Hard or soft real-time** | | Hardware cost | High | Low (often < $10 BOM) | | Resource constraints | Abundant RAM, disk | Tight (KB RAM, KB Flash) | | Update mechanism | User-installed | Often firmware OTA or in-factory | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Personal Computer Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CPU["CPU (Central Processing Unit)"] ALU["ALU
Arithmetic Logic Unit"] CU["Control Unit
Instruction Decoder"] REG["Register File
PC, IR, ACC, GPR"] CACHE["Cache L1 / L2 / L3
High Speed SRAM"] end subgraph MEM["Main Memory (Primary Storage)"] RAM["RAM
Volatile Read Write"] ROM["ROM / BIOS / UEFI
Non Volatile Boot Code"] end subgraph IO["I/O Subsystem"] KBD["Keyboard / Mouse"] DISP["Display / GPU"] STORAGE["SSD / HDD
Secondary Storage"] NIC["Network Card / USB"] PRINTER["Printer / Audio"] end subgraph BUSES["System Buses"] ABUS["Address Bus
Unidirectional n bits"] DBUS["Data Bus
Bidirectional w bits"] CBUS["Control Bus
RD WR INT NMI CLK"] end CPU <--> BUSES BUSES <--> MEM BUSES <--> IO CPU -.-> CACHE ALU --- CU CU --- REG REG --- CACHE classDef cpuStyle fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef memStyle fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 classDef ioStyle fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 classDef busStyle fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 class CPU cpuStyle class MEM memStyle class IO ioStyle class BUSES busStyle ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The CPU sits at the top, holding the ALU, Control Unit, Register File, and on-die Cache. It communicates with main memory (RAM/ROM) and the I/O subsystem through the three buses (address, data, control). The arrows are intentionally bidirectional for the data bus but conceptually unidirectional for the address bus. --- ### 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Interrupt Handling Sequence in a PC ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant APP as User Application participant CPU as CPU Core participant PIC as PIC / APIC participant DEV as I/O Device APP->>CPU: Execute normal instruction stream DEV->>PIC: Raise IRQ line (electrical signal) PIC->>CPU: Assert INT pin with vector number CPU->>CPU: Sample INT at end of instruction CPU->>CPU: Check IE flag (maskable?) CPU->>CPU: Save PC, flags, CS to stack CPU->>CPU: Disable further interrupts (IE = 0) CPU->>PIC: Send INTA pulse, read vector CPU->>CPU: Jump to ISR from IVT / IDT CPU->>DEV: Service the device (read buffer) CPU->>CPU: Execute IRET CPU->>APP: Resume normal instruction stream ``` > **Reading the diagram:** When an I/O device (e.g., keyboard, network card) needs CPU attention, it raises an Interrupt Request (IRQ) line to the Programmable Interrupt Controller (PIC) or APIC. The PIC resolves priorities, sends the vector number to the CPU, and the CPU saves its state, transfers control to the ISR, services the device, and returns via the `IRET` instruction. --- ### 4.3 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Memory Hierarchy Access Pattern ```mermaid flowchart LR A["CPU Request
for memory address X"] --> B{"In L1 Cache?"} B -- Yes --> C["Hit L1
Access Time ~1 ns"] B -- No --> D{"In L2 Cache?"} D -- Yes --> E["Hit L2
Access Time ~10 ns"] D -- No --> F{"In L3 Cache?"} F -- Yes --> G["Hit L3
Access Time ~20 ns"] F -- No --> H["Miss to RAM
Access Time ~100 ns"] H --> I["Fetch block from RAM
into L3, L2, L1"] C --> J["Return data to CPU"] E --> J G --> J I --> J ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The CPU issues a request for memory address $X$. The request is checked successively against L1, L2, and L3 caches. On a miss at all cache levels (a *cache miss*), the data is fetched from main memory (DRAM) and propagated back up the hierarchy. Each level down increases latency by an order of magnitude. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define a Personal Computer. List its three major hardware subsystems.* **Model Answer:** A **Personal Computer (PC)** is a general-purpose, single-user, microprocessor-based computing system designed for individual productivity, communication, and entertainment. The three major hardware subsystems are: 1. **CPU (Central Processing Unit)** – executes instructions and controls the system. 2. **Main Memory (RAM + ROM)** – stores active programs and data; ROM holds boot firmware. 3. **I/O Subsystem** – keyboard, mouse, display, disk, network, and other peripherals, coordinated by I/O controllers. > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Tip (3-mark):** Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for listing CPU + Memory + I/O, and 1 mark for the role of each. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Differentiate between memory-mapped I/O and I/O-mapped I/O. Give one example processor for each.* **Model Answer:** | Feature | Memory-Mapped I/O | I/O-Mapped I/O | |---|---|---| | Address space | Shared with memory | Separate from memory | | Instructions used | Any memory instruction (LOAD/STORE) | Special I/O instructions (IN/OUT) | | Address space size | Full $2^n$ (where $n$ = address bus width) | Limited (e.g., 65,536 ports in x86) | | Example processor | ARM, MIPS, RISC-V | Intel 8086, x86 | --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks and offer an *internal choice* between two questions. We model both options below. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Architecture Deep Dive** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Personal Computer. Discuss the role of the system, address, data, and control buses. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** A Personal Computer is built around the **Von Neumann architecture**, where the CPU, main memory, and I/O devices share common buses. The block diagram consists of the CPU (containing the ALU, Control Unit, Register File, and Cache), main memory (RAM and ROM), and the I/O subsystem, all interconnected through the three buses. - **System Bus:** The collective term for the address, data, and control buses — the *highway* of the PC. - **Address Bus:** Unidirectional; carries the memory or I/O address from the CPU to memory/I/O. Width $n$ bits → $2^n$ unique locations addressable. - **Data Bus:** Bidirectional; carries the actual data being read or written. Width $w$ bits → $w$ bits transferred per bus cycle. - **Control Bus:** Carries timing and command signals: $\overline{\text{RD}}$, $\overline{\text{WR}}$, $\overline{\text{MREQ}}$, $\overline{\text{IORQ}}$, $\overline{\text{INT}}$, $\overline{\text{NMI}}$, clock, reset. When the CPU needs to read a memory location, it places the address on the address bus, asserts $\overline{\text{RD}}$ and $\overline{\text{MREQ}}$ on the control bus, and the memory places the data on the data bus. For a write, $\overline{\text{WR}}$ is asserted instead. This single-bus model is the *Von Neumann bottleneck* — instruction fetch and data access share the same bus, limiting throughput. **(b)** A PC uses a 32-bit address bus and a 16-bit data bus. Calculate (i) the maximum addressable memory, (ii) the memory transferred per bus cycle, and (iii) the peak bus bandwidth if the bus clock is 100 MHz. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Maximum addressable memory:** $$ M = 2^{32}\ \text{bytes} = 4\ \text{GiB} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296\ \text{bytes} $$ **(ii) Memory transferred per bus cycle:** The data bus is 16 bits wide, so 16 bits = **2 bytes** are transferred per bus cycle. **(iii) Peak bus bandwidth:** $$ BW = f_{\text{bus}} \times w = 100 \times 10^6\ \text{Hz} \times 16\ \text{bits/cycle} = 1.6 \times 10^9\ \text{bits/s} $$ Converting to bytes per second: $$ BW = \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^9}{8} = 0.2 \times 10^9\ \text{bytes/s} = 200\ \text{MB/s} $$ > **Final Answers:** (i) **4 GiB**, (ii) **2 bytes/cycle**, (iii) **200 MB/s**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — PC vs Embedded System Comparison** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]` **(a)** Compare and contrast a Personal Computer with an Embedded System in terms of purpose, hardware resources, operating system, real-time behaviour, and power consumption. Why is a general-purpose PC unsuitable for hard real-time applications? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | Parameter | Personal Computer | Embedded System | |---|---|---| | Purpose | General-purpose (office, browsing, gaming) | Single dedicated function (washing machine, ECU) | | Hardware resources | GB of RAM, TB of storage | KB of RAM, KB–MB of flash | | Operating system | GPOS (Windows, Linux) | RTOS (FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX) | | Real-time behaviour | Non-deterministic; uses caches, paging, DMA | Deterministic; bounded interrupt latency | | Power consumption | High (100s of watts) | Low (milliwatts to a few watts) | | Cost | Hundreds to thousands of dollars | Often less than $10 BOM | | User interface | Rich GUI | Minimal or none | | Update mechanism | User-initiated | OTA / in-factory firmware | **Why a PC is unsuitable for hard real-time:** A hard real-time system requires a *guaranteed upper bound on response time* (typically microseconds). A general-purpose PC introduces unbounded latency through: - **Cache misses** (L1 hit ~1 ns, RAM miss ~100 ns — 100× variation). - **Virtual memory paging** (a page fault can stall the CPU for milliseconds). - **DMA contention** (peripherals can hold the bus for unpredictable durations). - **OS scheduling** (GPOS schedulers favour throughput and fairness, not deadline adherence). - **Interrupt prioritisation ambiguity** (APIC balances load rather than guaranteeing deadlines). In a hard real-time system (e.g., anti-lock brake controller, pacemaker), missing a deadline can be fatal. Hence, RTOS-based embedded controllers replace the PC in such applications. **(b)** Explain the role of interrupts in a Personal Computer. Differentiate between maskable and non-maskable interrupts with examples. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Role of Interrupts:** Interrupts are *asynchronous signals* from hardware (or software) that temporarily divert the CPU from its current execution to handle a higher-priority event. They are the PC's mechanism for *responsive I/O*, *exception handling*, and *multitasking*. Without interrupts, the CPU would waste cycles polling devices (busy-wait), degrading throughput. When an interrupt occurs, the CPU: 1. Completes the current instruction. 2. Checks the Interrupt Enable (IE) flag — if clear, the interrupt is ignored (for maskable). 3. Saves the **Program Counter (PC)**, **status flags**, and **code segment (CS)** on the stack. 4. Loads the new PC and CS from the **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** or **Interrupt Descriptor Table (IDT)**. 5. Jumps to the Interrupt Service Routine (ISR). 6. After the ISR executes `IRET`, restores state and resumes. **Maskable vs Non-Maskable Interrupts:** | Feature | Maskable Interrupt (IRQ) | Non-Maskable Interrupt (NMI) | |---|---|---| | Can be disabled? | Yes, by clearing the IE flag | No, always serviced | | Priority | Set by PIC/APIC | Fixed highest priority | | Vector source | IVT entry from PIC | Fixed vector (INT 2 on 8086) | | Use cases | Keyboard, mouse, disk, network | Memory parity error, watchdog timeout, power failure | | Examples | IRQ 1 (keyboard), IRQ 12 (mouse) | System board NMI, bus parity error | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing "interrupt" with "exception":** Exceptions are *internally* generated by the CPU (e.g., divide by zero, page fault). Interrupts are *externally* generated by hardware. Examiners deduct 1 mark if you mix them up. > 2. **Forgetting to mention the IE flag:** A 3-mark question on maskable interrupts *must* mention the Interrupt Enable flag as the masking mechanism. Simply writing "it can be disabled" is incomplete. > 3. **Writing `INT 1` for NMI:** The NMI vector on 8086/x86 is **INT 2**, not INT 1 (which is the single-step trap). This is a frequently tested micro-fact. > 4. **Omitting the stack save/restore step:** The ISR mechanism is incomplete without the context save and `IRET` restore. Examiners allocate 2 marks specifically for this. > 5. **In the PC vs Embedded question, writing "PC is fast" as a reason for unsuitability:** Speed is *not* the issue. The issue is *determinism* (worst-case latency), not average performance. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision — KTU 2024 Module 1: Personal Computer** - **Definition:** A PC is a general-purpose, single-user, Von Neumann architecture machine integrating CPU + Memory + I/O via shared buses. - **Three Subsystems:** CPU, Main Memory, I/O — connected by the system bus. - **Three Buses:** Address (unidirectional, $2^n$ locations), Data (bidirectional, $w$ bits per cycle), Control ($\overline{\text{RD}}$, $\overline{\text{WR}}$, $\overline{\text{INT}}$, $\overline{\text{NMI}}$, clock, reset). - **Key formula:** $M = 2^n$ where $n$ = address bus width. 8086 → 20-bit → 1 MiB. - **Cache Hierarchy:** L1 (ns) → L2 → L3 → RAM (100 ns). Cache misses cause *non-determinism* — the *core reason* a PC fails hard real-time. - **Two I/O Models:** Memory-mapped (ARM/MIPS) vs I/O-mapped (x86, 65,536 ports). - **Interrupt Types:** Maskable (IRQ, can be disabled via IE flag) vs Non-maskable (NMI, cannot be disabled, e.g., parity error). - **PC vs Embedded:** PC is general-purpose, non-deterministic, resource-rich, high-power. Embedded is single-function, deterministic (RTOS), resource-constrained, low-power. - **Von Neumann Bottleneck:** Shared bus for instruction and data access. Mitigated by Harvard/modified Harvard in embedded targets. - **Examiners' Favourite Comparisons:** (a) Von Neumann vs Harvard, (b) PC vs Embedded System, (c) Memory-mapped vs I/O-mapped I/O, (d) Maskable vs NMI, (e) Cache vs RAM latency. - **High-Yield Numbers to Memorise:** 8086 = 20-bit address / 16-bit data / 1 MiB. 8088 = 16-bit internal / 8-bit external. Modern = 64-bit / 48-bit effective / 256 TiB virtual. - **RTOS Bridge:** The PC's non-determinism is the *justification* for studying RTOS in the rest of this course. Always tie your PC-related answer back to *why we need a Real-Time OS*.

topics.title: Parallel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT741 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Parallel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Real Time Operating Systems (RTOS)**, **Parallelism** refers to the simultaneous execution of multiple tasks, processes, or computational operations on distinct processing resources to meet stringent timing constraints. Formally stated by the KTU 2024 syllabus, parallelism in RTOS is the architectural property whereby two or more real-time tasks (jobs) execute *physically* at the same instant of time $t$ on independent execution units (cores, processors, or hardware accelerators), while still satisfying their individual deadlines.

Mathematically, given a set of $n$ real-time tasks $T = \{T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n\}$ with deadlines $d_i$, parallelism holds if there exists a schedule $\sigma$ such that for at least two tasks $T_i$ and $T_j$ where $i \neq j$:

$$[\sigma_{start}(T_i), \sigma_{end}(T_i)] \cap [\sigma_{start}(T_j), \sigma_{end}(T_j)] \neq \emptyset$$

and the processor allocation function $\pi: T \rightarrow P$ is non-injective (i.e., $\pi(T_i) \neq \pi(T_j)$) on the overlapping interval.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Distinction: Concurrency vs. Parallelism**
> - **Concurrency** = *logical* interleaving on a *single* core (context switching illusion).
> - **Parallelism** = *physical* simultaneous execution on *multiple* cores.
> In RTOS, **hard real-time** systems demand parallelism to be *deterministic* and *bounded*, with Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET) guarantees.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **🍳 Real-World Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen**
> Imagine a single chef (single-core CPU) preparing a 5-course meal. He switches between soups, salads, and main courses rapidly — that is **concurrency** (logical, but not simultaneous). Now imagine 5 chefs, each responsible for one course, all working *at the same instant* on their own burners. That is **parallelism** — *true* simultaneous execution on independent hardware.
>
> In a **Hard Real-Time** kitchen, the customer expects the soup within 5 minutes **no matter what**. A parallel kitchen can guarantee this only if the task assignment and the chef's speed (WCET) are deterministic. A slow chef or a non-deterministic schedule ruins the deadline.

## 1.3 Standard Real-Time Parallelism Metrics (KTU 2024 Highlight)

The following key metrics must be memorized for board exams:

- **Speedup ($S$)** — ratio of single-core to multi-core execution time.
- **Efficiency ($E$)** — useful work per processor.
- **Utilization ($U$)** — fraction of time the processor is busy.
- **Scalability** — ability to maintain performance as $N$ increases.
- **Determinism** — bounded and predictable execution time (a *sine qua non* in RTOS).
- **Deadline Miss Ratio (DMR)** — fraction of tasks missing their deadlines.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Granularity Levels of Parallelism

Parallelism in RTOS exists at multiple granularities. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the first three as high-yield:

| # | Granularity Level | Unit of Parallelism | Typical RTOS Use Case |
|---|-------------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Bit-level Parallelism** | Bit slicing / word width | 8/16/32/64-bit ALU width |
| 2 | **Instruction-level (ILP)** | Instructions | VLIW, superscalar pipelines |
| 3 | **Task-level / Thread-level** | Tasks/Threads | Multi-core real-time scheduling |
| 4 | **Data-level (DLP)** | Data partitions | SIMD, GPU-based signal processing |
| 5 | **Job-level (Cluster)** | Sub-programs | Distributed real-time clusters |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Focus:** In Module 1, the primary concern is **Task-level Parallelism** because it directly impacts schedulability, deadline guarantees, and processor allocation.

## 2.2 Amdahl's Law — The Heart of Parallel Speedup

**Gene Amdahl (1967)** formulated the fundamental bound on speedup for a fixed workload. The proof follows the principle that *the serial fraction of a program cannot be parallelized*.

### Logical Steps Behind Amdahl's Law

1. Decompose total execution time $T_1$ into a **serial component** and a **parallel component**.
2. The serial fraction $(1 - P)$ remains unaffected by adding more processors.
3. The parallel fraction $P$ scales as $1/N$ with $N$ processors.
4. Total time is the **sum** of these two (they are sequential to each other).
5. Speedup = ratio of original time to new time.
6. Take the limit as $N \to \infty$ to find the *theoretical ceiling*.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL: Avoid the `vert` symbol inside table cells to preserve markdown rendering.**

| # | Formula | Symbols | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$ | $S$ = speedup, $N$ = processors, $P$ = parallel fraction | Amdahl's Law (fixed workload) |
| 2 | $S_{\max} = \lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \dfrac{1}{1-P}$ | $P$ = parallel fraction | Maximum theoretical speedup |
| 3 | $E(N) = \dfrac{S(N)}{N} = \dfrac{1}{N(1-P) + P}$ | $E$ = efficiency, $0 \le E \le 1$ | Fraction of useful work per processor |
| 4 | $S_{G}(N) = N - (N-1)(1-P) = P \cdot N + (1-P)$ | $N$ = processors, $P$ = parallel fraction | Gustafson's Law (scaled workload) |
| 5 | $e_{KF} = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{S} - \dfrac{1}{N}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{N}}$ | $e_{KF}$ = experimental parallel fraction | Karp-Flatt metric (detects serial bottlenecks) |
| 6 | $U_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} C_i / P_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} P_i}$ | $C_i$ = WCET of task $i$, $P_i$ = period, $m$ = tasks | Real-time utilization bound |
| 7 | $DMR = \dfrac{\text{Missed Tasks}}{\text{Total Tasks}}$ | DMR ∈ $[0, 1]$ | Deadline miss ratio for hard RT |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECU Networks:** Modern cars use 100+ ECUs (Electronic Control Units) executing in parallel. Engine control (50 µs deadline) and brake control (100 µs) must run in parallel on multi-core ARM Cortex-R processors.
- **Aerospace Avionics:** F-35 fighter jets run parallel real-time tasks on a multi-core F-35 Mission System. ARINC 653 standard mandates **strict spatial and temporal partitioning** to guarantee independence between parallel tasks.
- **Medical Robotics:** da Vinci surgical robot uses parallel real-time control loops for instrument kinematics at **1 kHz** to ensure no perceptible lag.
- **5G Base Stations:** Massive MIMO beamforming uses **Data-Level Parallelism** (DSP + GPU) to process antenna arrays within 1 ms sub-frame deadlines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Amdahl's Law

> [!NOTE]
> Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. The blank lines around `$$` blocks are mandatory per KTU formatting rules.

### Step 1 — Define the Workload

Let $T_1$ be the execution time of a real-time task on a single processor (1 CPU). Decompose the workload into two complementary fractions:

$$P = \text{parallelizable fraction}, \quad 1 - P = \text{serial fraction}$$

with the boundary condition $0 \le P \le 1$.

### Step 2 — Component-wise Time on a Single Processor
