# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 RTOS Architecture — Formal Definition

In the context of **Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS)**, the term *Architecture* refers to the **structured, layered design** that defines how the kernel, hardware abstraction layer (HAL), task management module, scheduler, inter-process communication (IPC) primitives, and memory management unit interact to deliver **deterministic, time-bounded execution** for time-critical applications.

An **RTOS Architecture** is therefore a **blueprint of software-hardware cooperation** that guarantees:
- **Bounded Response Time** (typically in **microseconds** to **milliseconds**)
- **Predictable Task Scheduling** under worst-case load
- **Deterministic Interrupt Handling**
- **Minimal Kernel Latency**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Architecture in RTOS is not just *system design* — it specifically refers to **kernel architecture**, **task architecture**, **scheduling architecture**, and **IPC architecture**. You MUST know the trade-offs of each design choice for the **14-mark ESE questions**.

### Classification of RTOS Architectures

| # | Architecture Type | Core Principle | Typical Use Case |
|---|-------------------|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Monolithic Kernel Architecture** | All OS services (scheduler, drivers, FS, networking) run in **kernel/supervisor mode** in a single address space | Embedded controllers, automotive ECUs |
| 2 | **Microkernel Architecture** | Only essential services (scheduler, IPC) in kernel; rest in user space | High-reliability systems, QNX, MINIX |
| 3 | **Hybrid (Modular) Kernel Architecture** | Selective placement of modules in kernel/user space | Real-time Linux (PREEMPT-RT), Windows NT |
| 4 | **Layered Architecture** | Strict hierarchical layering with controlled interfaces | Legacy RTOS like VxWorks |
| 5 | **Component-Based / Nano-Kernel** | Pluggable, reusable modules | Modern IoT RTOS like Zephyr, FreeRTOS variants |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Emergency Room"

Imagine a **hospital emergency room (ER)** that must treat patients (tasks) within strict time limits.

- **The Triage Nurse = Kernel/Scheduler**: Decides *who* gets attention *next* based on priority and urgency (just like Rate Monotonic or EDF scheduling).
- **The Doctors (Specialists) = User Tasks**: They do the actual work (diagnosis, surgery).
- **The Shared Operation Theatre = Critical Section / Shared Resource**: Only one doctor can operate at a time → requires **mutual exclusion** (Mutex).
- **The Pager System = Inter-Task Communication (IPC)**: Doctors inform each other about patient status (semaphores, message queues, mailboxes).
- **The Hospital Building = Hardware Platform** (CPU, memory, I/O).
- **The Hospital Administrator = Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)**: Hides the complexity of vendors and machines from the doctors.

> **In an RTOS, the architecture is the "design of this hospital"** — how the nurse, doctors, theatre, and pagers are organized so that **no patient waits beyond their maximum allowed time** (deadline miss = system failure in hard real-time).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Architectural Decision:** A *hard real-time* system (e.g., anti-lock braking) **cannot tolerate ANY deadline miss**, while a *soft real-time* system (e.g., video streaming) can tolerate occasional misses with graceful degradation. The architecture must be chosen accordingly.

---

## 1.3 Architecture Building Blocks — The Five Pillars

Every RTOS architecture is constructed from these **five fundamental building blocks**:

1. **Kernel Core (Microkernel / Nanokernel)**
   - The heart that handles scheduling, context switching, and IPC.
   - Should be **as small as possible** to minimize latency.

2. **Task Management Module**
   - Creates, deletes, suspends, and resumes tasks.
   - Maintains the **Task Control Block (TCB)** for each task.

3. **Scheduler**
   - Implements the chosen **scheduling algorithm** (RM, EDF, LLF, etc.).
   - Decides *which task runs next* and *for how long*.

4. **Inter-Process Communication (IPC) Subsystem**
   - Provides primitives: **Semaphores, Mutex, Message Queues, Mailboxes, Pipes, Event Flags, Signals**.
   - The "nervous system" of the architecture.

5. **Memory & I/O Management**
   - Bounded allocation (no virtual memory thrashing).
   - Predictable I/O drivers with deterministic response.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Exam:** When asked to "explain RTOS architecture", always structure your answer around these **5 blocks** + cite at least one **scheduling algorithm** and one **IPC mechanism**. This guarantees full marks.

---

## 1.4 Kernel Architecture Variants — Detailed Comparison

### 1.4.1 Monolithic Kernel Architecture

In a **monolithic RTOS kernel** (e.g., traditional µC/OS-II, older VxWorks), the entire operating system — scheduler, file system, network stack, drivers — runs in a **single privileged address space**.

**Advantages:**
- ⚡ **Low overhead** due to shared address space (no mode switches)
- ⚡ **High performance** for I/O-heavy workloads
- ⚡ Simpler kernel design

**Disadvantages:**
- ❌ A bug in *any* module can crash the **entire system** (no fault isolation)
- ❌ Poor modularity; difficult to extend
- ❌ Not suitable for **safety-critical** systems needing certification (e.g., DO-178C, ISO 26262)

### 1.4.2 Microkernel Architecture

In a **microkernel architecture** (e.g., QNX Neutrino, MINIX 3), only **four essential services** run in kernel mode:
1. **IPC (Inter-Process Communication)**
2. **Scheduling**
3. **Memory Management (basic)**
4. **Interrupt Handling**

All other services (drivers, file systems, networking, protocol stacks) run as **user-space processes**.

**Advantages:**
- ✅ **High reliability** — driver crash does NOT crash the OS
- ✅ Excellent **fault isolation** and **security**
- ✅ Easier to certify for safety-critical domains
- ✅ Modular and extensible

**Disadvantages:**
- ❌ Higher overhead due to **context switches** and **message passing** across user/kernel boundary
- ❌ Can introduce **non-determinism** if not carefully designed
- ❌ Performance hit for I/O-intensive workloads

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favorite:** You will likely be asked to **compare monolithic vs microkernel** in a 7-mark sub-question. Memorize the table above.

### 1.4.3 Hybrid Kernel Architecture

The **hybrid kernel** is a pragmatic compromise. It **selectively** places performance-critical modules (like network stacks in Windows, or real-time extensions in Linux PREEMPT-RT) in kernel space, while keeping other services in user space.

- **Examples:** Real-Time Linux (PREEMPT-RT patch), Windows NT, BeOS
- **Used when:** Some non-determinism is acceptable, but real-time performance is still required.

---

## 1.5 Task Architecture — The Foundation of RTOS Design

A **Task** (also called a *thread* in some RTOS literature) is the **fundamental unit of execution** in an RTOS.

### 1.5.1 Task States and Transitions

A task in an RTOS cycles through **five canonical states**:

| State | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| **Ready (R)** | Task is eligible to run, waiting for CPU allocation |
| **Running (RUN)** | Task currently executing on the CPU |
| **Blocked / Waiting (B)** | Task waiting for an event (semaphore, I/O, delay) |
| **Suspended (S)** | Task explicitly paused by another task or itself |
| **Terminated / Dormant (D)** | Task has finished or not yet started |

**State Transition Triggers:**
- **R → RUN** : Scheduler dispatches task (preemption or voluntary yield)
- **RUN → R** : Task preempted by higher-priority task or time-slice expired
- **RUN → B** : Task calls blocking primitive (e.g., `sem_wait`, `taskDelay`)
- **B → R** : Event occurs (semaphore signaled, I/O complete, timeout)
- **RUN → S** : Task explicitly suspended (e.g., `OSTaskSuspend()`)
- **S → R** : Another task resumes it
- **RUN → D** : Task completes its function and deletes itself
- **D → R** : Task re-created

### 1.5.2 Task Control Block (TCB)

The **TCB** is the *data structure* the kernel maintains **for each task**. It is the "identity card" of a task in the architecture.

**Typical TCB Fields:**
- `TaskID` — unique identifier
- `TaskState` — current state (Ready, Running, Blocked, etc.)
- `Priority` — static (compile-time) or dynamic (run-time)
- `StackPointer (SP)` — current top of stack
- `TaskFunctionPointer` — entry point
- `StackBase` / `StackLimit` — stack boundaries
- `Registers` — saved CPU context (for context switch)
- `Delay/Timeout` — for `taskDelay()` calls
- `ErrorCode` — last error
- `MessageQueue` / `EventFlags` — for IPC
- `Next` / `Previous` — for linked-list scheduling queues

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** The TCB is like a **patient's medical chart** in the ER analogy. The triage nurse (scheduler) flips through these charts (TCBs) to decide who gets attention next, and notes the current status (state), priority (severity), and history (registers, stack).

### 1.5.3 Context Switching

**Context switching** is the process of **saving the state of a running task** and **loading the state of another task** to make it run on the CPU.

**The 5-step procedure:**
1. Save current task's **CPU registers** into its TCB
2. Update current task's **stack pointer** in TCB
3. Move current task from **RUN → Ready** queue
4. Select **next task** via scheduler
5. Load next task's **registers and stack pointer** from its TCB
6. Jump to the new task's PC (Program Counter)

**Context Switch Time (CST)** is the single most important latency metric in RTOS architecture.

$$CST = T_{save} + T_{scheduler} + T_{load}$$

Where:
- $T_{save}$ = time to save current context
- $T_{scheduler}$ = time for scheduler to pick next task
- $T_{load}$ = time to load new context

Typical CST values:
- **FreeRTOS:** ~1-5 µs on Cortex-M4 @ 168 MHz
- **QNX Neutrino:** ~2-10 µs
- **VxWorks:** ~1-3 µs

---

## 1.6 Scheduling Architecture — The Brain of RTOS

The **scheduling architecture** is what truly differentiates an RTOS from a GPOS (General Purpose OS).

### 1.6.1 Classification by Preemption

| Type | Description | Real-Time Suitability |
|------|-------------|----------------------|
| **Non-Preemptive (Cooperative)** | Task runs until it voluntarily yields | Poor for hard real-time |
| **Preemptive (Priority-Based)** | Higher-priority task can interrupt lower | **Standard for RTOS** |
| **Preemptive with Time-Slicing** | Equal-priority tasks share CPU in round-robin | Soft real-time |
| **Hybrid** | Preemption + time-slicing for same-priority | Most modern RTOS |

### 1.6.2 Classification by Algorithm

| Algorithm | Type | Optimality |
|-----------|------|------------|
| **Rate Monotonic (RM)** | Static-priority | Optimal among static-priority algorithms |
| **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** | Dynamic-priority | Optimal among all algorithms (for uniprocessor) |
| **Least Laxity First (LLF)** | Dynamic-priority | Optimal for minimizing lateness |
| **Deadline Monotonic (DM)** | Static-priority | Used when deadlines ≠ periods |
| **Round Robin (RR)** | None | Not real-time; for fairness only |

---

## 1.7 Inter-Task Communication (IPC) Architecture

Tasks in an RTOS rarely work in isolation. They must **communicate and synchronize**. The IPC architecture provides these primitives:

| Primitive | Purpose | Key Property |
|-----------|---------|--------------|
| **Semaphore (Binary)** | Signaling, mutual exclusion | Counting; no ownership concept |
| **Mutex** | Mutual exclusion | **Ownership-based**; supports priority inheritance |
| **Message Queue** | Pass data between tasks | Fixed-size messages; FIFO by default |
| **Mailbox** | Single-slot message passing | Lightweight, one-slot |
| **Pipe** | Byte-stream communication | Stream-oriented |
| **Event Flags** | Multi-event synchronization | Bitmask of events |
| **Signal** | Asynchronous notification | Software interrupt to a task |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Priority Inversion Problem** — A classic RTOS architecture pitfall:
> - **Low-priority task L** holds a **Mutex M**.
> - **High-priority task H** waits for M → blocked.
> - **Medium-priority task M** preempts L → L never runs → H never runs.
> - This is **unbounded priority inversion** (the "Mars Pathfinder" bug, 1997).
> - **Solution:** **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** — when a high-priority task waits on a mutex held by a lower-priority task, the lower-priority task **temporarily inherits** the higher priority.
> - **Stronger solution:** **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)** — each mutex has a ceiling priority equal to the highest priority of any task that may lock it.

---

## 1.8 Memory Architecture in RTOS

Unlike GPOS, RTOS memory architecture avoids:
- ❌ Virtual memory / demand paging (non-deterministic)
- ❌ Dynamic heap fragmentation in critical paths
- ❌ Swapping

**Preferred RTOS memory techniques:**
- ✅ **Static memory allocation** for critical tasks
- ✅ **Memory pools** (fixed-size blocks) for deterministic allocation
- ✅ **Stack-based allocation** for short-lived data
- ✅ **Memory Protection Units (MPU)** for fault isolation
- ✅ Bounded execution time for `malloc`/`free`

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Kernel Architecture — Operational Logic

The kernel architecture decides **where** each OS function executes. This has **direct, measurable impact on determinism**.

### 2.1.1 Mode-Switch Cost Model

In a typical protected-mode processor (ARM Cortex-M, x86):

| Operation | Approximate Cost |
|-----------|------------------|
| **Function call (same mode)** | ~10 ns |
| **System call (user → kernel)** | ~50-200 ns |
| **Context switch (same process)** | ~0.5-5 µs |
| **Context switch (process to process)** | ~1-10 µs |
| **Interrupt latency** | ~100 ns - 5 µs |
| **Interrupt response** | ~500 ns - 20 µs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule of Thumb:** An RTOS architecture should minimize the number of mode switches in the **critical path** of the highest-priority task. This is why a microkernel can sometimes be **slower** than a monolithic kernel, even though it is "more elegant".

### 2.1.2 Architectural Decision Matrix

| Criterion | Monolithic | Microkernel | Hybrid |
|-----------|------------|-------------|--------|
| **Performance** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Reliability** | ⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Modularity** | ⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Real-Time Determinism** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ (if well-designed) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Certification Ease** | ⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Code Size** | Small (good for MCUs) | Larger | Medium |
| **Examples** | µC/OS-II, FreeRTOS | QNX, MINIX 3 | Linux PREEMPT-RT, VxWorks (modular) |

---

## 2.2 Scheduling Architecture — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)

**Liu & Layland (1973)** proved that RMS is **optimal among all static-priority algorithms** for **preemptive, independent, periodic tasks** where:
$$D_i = T_i \quad \text{(Deadline = Period)}$$

**RMS Assumptions:**
1. All tasks are **periodic** with period $T_i$
2. **Deadline = Period** ($D_i = T_i$)
3. Tasks are **independent** (no shared resources or precedence)
4. **Fixed priorities** (assigned by rate — shorter period = higher priority)
5. **Preemptive** scheduling
6. **Context switch time = 0**
7. **Single processor**

**Priority Assignment Rule:**
$$\text{If } T_i < T_j \text{ then } P_i > P_j$$
(Task with **shorter period → higher priority**)

**Necessary Condition (Processor Utilization Bound):**

For $n$ tasks, RMS is schedulable **only if**:
$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$$

The bound $n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ is the **Least Upper Bound (LUB)** of CPU utilization under RMS.

As $n \to \infty$:
$$\lim_{n \to \infty} n(2^{1/n} - 1) = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical:** The bound is **sufficient but NOT necessary**. Tasks may be schedulable even if $U > n(2^{1/n} - 1)$. The bound is only a **quick test** — for KTU, you also need the **exact Response Time Analysis (RTA)**.

### 2.2.2 Earliest Deadline First (EDF)

EDF is a **dynamic-priority** algorithm where the task with the **earliest absolute deadline** is given the highest priority at every scheduling decision point.

**Schedulability Condition (Necessary and Sufficient for EDF):**

A set of $n$ independent, preemptive, periodic tasks with $D_i = T_i$ is **schedulable under EDF if and only if**:
$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq 1$$

That is, **any feasible task set is schedulable under EDF**. This is the optimality result of Liu & Layland.

### 2.2.3 Response Time Analysis (RTA) — The Exact Test

The **exact** schedulability test for RMS (and any fixed-priority system) is given by the **Response Time Analysis** recurrence:

For task $i$, its **worst-case response time** $R_i$ is the smallest fixed-point of:
$$R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$

Where:
- $C_i$ = worst-case execution time of task $i$
- $T_j$ = period of task $j$
- $hp(i)$ = set of tasks with **higher priority** than task $i$
- $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ = ceiling function

**Task $i$ is schedulable if and only if:**
$$R_i \leq D_i$$

The recurrence is solved **iteratively**:
1. Start with $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$
2. Compute $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i^{(k)} / T_j \rceil \cdot C_j$
3. Stop when $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$ (fixed point) or $R_i^{(k+1)} > D_i$ (failed)

### 2.2.4 Priority Inversion Analysis

**Worst-case blocking time** for a task $i$ using **Priority Inheritance Protocol**:
$$B_i = \max_{k \in lp(i) \cap M} (C_k)$$

Where:
- $lp(i)$ = set of tasks with **lower priority** than $i$
- $M$ = set of tasks that access at least one mutex also accessed by $i$

**Blocking-aware Response Time:**
$$R_i = C_i + B_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$

### 2.2.5 Interrupt Architecture

The interrupt architecture defines **interrupt priority levels**, **nesting**, and **latency**.

**Interrupt Latency:**
$$T_{latency} = T_{detect} + T_{ack} + T_{save\_ctx} + T_{ISR\_start}$$

**Maximum Interrupt Response Time:**
$$T_{response} = T_{latency} + T_{ISR}$$

**For KTU:** The architecture must guarantee that the **worst-case interrupt latency** plus the **longest ISR execution** does not exceed the **tightest deadline**.

---

## 2.3 IPC Architecture — Blocking Semantics

### 2.3.1 Blocking vs Non-Blocking Primitives

| Primitive | Blocking Call | Non-Blocking Alternative | Return Value |
|-----------|---------------|--------------------------|--------------|
| Semaphore | `sem_wait()` (blocks indefinitely) | `sem_trywait()` (returns -1 if unavailable) | Success or EAGAIN |
| Semaphore | `sem_timedwait()` (blocks with timeout) | — | Success, EAGAIN, or ETIMEDOUT |
| Mutex | `mutex_lock()` | `mutex_trylock()` | Success or EBUSY |
| Message Queue | `msg_receive()` (blocks if empty) | `msg_receive_nb()` | Message or empty flag |
| Event Flags | `event_wait()` (blocks on event mask) | `event_poll()` | Current event mask |

> [!NOTE]
> **Determinism Tip:** In hard real-time, **avoid indefinite blocking**. Always use timed waits (`sem_timedwait`, `msg_receive_timed`) to bound the worst-case waiting time.

### 2.3.2 IPC Throughput Model

For a **bounded queue** of size $N$ with producer/consumer tasks:

**Maximum throughput (messages/second):**
$$Th_{max} = \min\left(\frac{1}{T_p}, \frac{1}{T_c}\right)$$

Where $T_p$ and $T_c$ are the periods of producer and consumer tasks.

**Average queue length** (Little's Law):
$$\bar{L} = \lambda \cdot \bar{W}$$

Where $\lambda$ is arrival rate and $\bar{W}$ is average waiting time.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | **RMS Utilization Bound** | $U_{RMS} \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Sufficient condition for schedulability under RMS |
| 2 | **RMS Asymptotic Bound** | $\lim_{n\to\infty} U_{RMS} = \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ | Maximum utilization for infinite tasks under RMS |
| 3 | **EDF Optimality** | $U_{EDF} = \sum C_i/T_i \leq 1$ | Necessary and sufficient for EDF |
| 4 | **Response Time (RTA)** | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i/T_j \rceil C_j$ | Worst-case response time under fixed priority |
| 5 | **Schedulability Test (RTA)** | $R_i \leq D_i$ | Task $i$ meets its deadline |
| 6 | **CPU Utilization** | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i/T_i$ | Fraction of CPU time used |
| 7 | **Context Switch Time** | $CST = T_{save} + T_{sched} + T_{load}$ | Total time to switch tasks |
| 8 | **Interrupt Latency** | $T_{lat} = T_{detect} + T_{ack} + T_{save} + T_{start}$ | Time from interrupt to first ISR instruction |
| 9 | **Blocking Time (PIP)** | $B_i = \max_{k \in lp(i) \cap M} C_k$ | Worst-case blocking under Priority Inheritance |
| 10 | **Slack Time / Laxity** | $L_i(t) = D_i - (t + R_i^{rem})$ | Time before deadline minus remaining work |
| 11 | **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)** | $C_i^{WCET} = \max C_i$ | Maximum observed execution time |
| 12 | **Hyperperiod (LCM of periods)** | $H = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n)$ | Cycle length after which schedule repeats |
| 13 | **Priority Ceiling of Resource** | $\Pi(R_k) = \max_{i \in \text{users}(R_k)} P_i$ | Highest priority of any task that may lock $R_k$ |
| 14 | **Time Slice (Round Robin)** | $Q = T_{quantum}$ | CPU time slice for equal-priority tasks |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The choice of RTOS architecture has direct **engineering consequences**:

| Domain | Architectural Choice | Reason |
|--------|---------------------|--------|
| **Automotive (ECU, ADAS)** | AUTOSAR OS (OSEK/VDX) — microkernel-style, static config | ISO 26262 ASIL-D certification requires predictability |
| **Avionics (Flight Control)** | VxWorks / INTEGRITY — microkernel, ARINC 653 partitioned | DO-178C certification; spatial + temporal partitioning |
| **Industrial Robotics** | QNX Neutrino — microkernel | Fault isolation; real-time I/O |
| **IoT / Wearables** | FreeRTOS / Zephyr — monolithic, tiny footprint | Memory (< 10 KB), power efficiency |
| **Medical Devices** | ThreadX / embOS — microkernel, certified | IEC 62304, FDA approval |
| **Spacecraft** | VxWorks / RTEMS — radiation-hardened, microkernel | Long mission life, no reboots |
| **5G Base Stations** | Linux PREEMPT-RT — hybrid | High throughput + real-time guarantees |
| **Mars Rover** | VxWorks (Curiosity, Perseverance) | Certified, real-time, reliable |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 14-mark answers:** Always tie the architectural choice to the **domain requirements** (e.g., "Automotive ECU uses AUTOSAR because it needs predictable timing AND safety certification"). This shows examiner-style depth.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: RMS Utilization Bound (Liu & Layland, 1973)

**Goal:** Prove that for $n$ tasks, the maximum sustainable CPU utilization under RMS is $U(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$.

### Step 1: Define the Critical Instant

A **critical instant** for a task $\tau_i$ is the time at which **all higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously** with $\tau_i$. At this instant, the demand on the CPU is maximum, leading to the **worst-case response time** for $\tau_i$.

### Step 2: Set Up the Demand Function

Let $t$ be a time after the critical instant. The total CPU demand by task $\tau_i$ and all higher-priority tasks in $[0, t]$ is:

$$W_i(t) = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$

The first completion time of $\tau_i$ is the smallest $t$ such that $W_i(t) = t$ (CPU fully utilized in interval). This $t$ equals the worst-case response time $R_i$.

### Step 3: Construct the Worst-Case Task Set

To find the **maximum utilization** for $n$ tasks, consider the "boundary" task set where:
- All tasks have $C_i$ just large enough to **barely miss the deadline** under RMS.
- The task set is **schedulable with utilization exactly $U^*$**.

For a task set to be at the boundary, the following relationship holds (for the lowest-priority task $\tau_n$):

$$C_n + \sum_{j=1}^{n-1} \left\lceil \frac{T_n}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j = T_n$$

### Step 4: Maximize the Utilization

We want to maximize:
$$U^* = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$$

subject to the constraint that the system is **just-schedulable**. This is a constrained optimization problem.

**Liu & Layland's trick:** The worst case occurs when the periods are **harmonically related** in a specific way. For a given set of priorities (1 = highest, $n$ = lowest), the worst case is:

$$T_i = T_n \cdot \prod_{k=1}^{i-1} \alpha_k$$

where $\alpha_k$ is the ratio between consecutive periods.

### Step 5: Solve the Recurrence

Through careful algebraic manipulation (Liu & Layland 1973, Theorem 4), the **least upper bound** of utilization is:

$$U^*(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$$

### Step 6: Verify the Asymptotic Limit

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} n(2^{1/n} - 1) = \lim_{n \to \infty} n \cdot (e^{\ln 2 / n} - 1)$$

Using the Taylor series $e^x \approx 1 + x$ for small $x$:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} n \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\ln 2}{n} - 1\right) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \ln 2 = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** As the number of tasks increases, RMS can guarantee schedulability for only about **69.3%** of CPU utilization. The remaining ~30.7% may be unused even though the system is feasible. This is the **price of static priorities**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Response Time Analysis (RTA) Iteration

**Problem:** Given 3 tasks with parameters:

| Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | $D_i$ (ms) |
|------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| $\tau_1$ | 1 | 4 | 4 |
| $\tau_2$ | 2 | 6 | 6 |
| $\tau_3$ | 3 | 10 | 10 |

Priority: $\tau_1 > \tau_2 > \tau_3$ (by RMS rule, since $T_1 < T_2 < T_3$).

**Find:** Is $\tau_3$ schedulable? Compute $R_3$.

### Step 1: Set Up the RTA Recurrence for $\tau_3$

Higher-priority tasks: $hp(3) = \{\tau_1, \tau_2\}$

$$R_3 = C_3 + \sum_{j=1}^{2} \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{4} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{6} \right\rceil (2)$$

### Step 2: Iterate from Initial Guess

**Iteration 0:** Start with $R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 3$ ms

$$R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \lceil 3/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 3/6 \rceil \cdot 2$$

Calculate each term:
- $\lceil 3/4 \rceil = \lceil 0.75 \rceil = 1$
- $\lceil 3/6 \rceil = \lceil 0.5 \rceil = 1$

$$R_3^{(1)} = 3 + 1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 = 6 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 3: Continue Iteration

**Iteration 1:** $R_3^{(1)} = 6$ ms

- $\lceil 6/4 \rceil = \lceil 1.5 \rceil = 2$
- $\lceil 6/6 \rceil = \lceil 1.0 \rceil = 1$

$$R_3^{(2)} = 3 + 2 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 = 7 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 4: Check Convergence

**Iteration 2:** $R_3^{(2)} = 7$ ms

- $\lceil 7/4 \rceil = \lceil 1.75 \rceil = 2$
- $\lceil 7/6 \rceil = \lceil 1.1667 \rceil = 2$

$$R_3^{(3)} = 3 + 2 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 2 = 9 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 5: Final Convergence

**Iteration 3:** $R_3^{(3)} = 9$ ms

- $\lceil 9/4 \rceil = \lceil 2.25 \rceil = 3$
- $\lceil 9/6 \rceil = \lceil 1.5 \rceil = 2$

$$R_3^{(4)} = 3 + 3 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 2 = 10 \text{ ms}$$

**Iteration 4:** $R_3^{(4)} = 10$ ms

- $\lceil 10/4 \rceil = \lceil 2.5 \rceil = 3$
- $\lceil 10/6 \rceil = \lceil 1.667 \rceil = 2$

$$R_3^{(5)} = 3 + 3 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 2 = 10 \text{ ms}$$

✅ **Fixed point reached:** $R_3^* = 10$ ms

### Step 6: Compare with Deadline

$$R_3^* = 10 \text{ ms} \quad \text{vs} \quad D_3 = 10 \text{ ms}$$

$$R_3^* = D_3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau_3 \text{ is JUST schedulable (boundary case)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** $\tau_3$ is schedulable **exactly at its deadline**. Any increase in $C_3$ or in interference from higher-priority tasks would cause a deadline miss.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: EDF Optimality Proof (Sketch)

**Theorem (Liu & Layland, 1973):** If a set of $n$ independent, preemptive, periodic tasks with $D_i = T_i$ is **schedulable by any algorithm**, then it is **schedulable by EDF**.

### Proof Sketch:

**Step 1: Define a feasible schedule.** A schedule is *feasible* if:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq 1 \quad \text{and a schedule exists that meets all deadlines}$$

**Step 2: Construct an exchange argument.** Suppose an algorithm $A$ produces a schedule $S_A$ that meets all deadlines. We construct $S_{EDF}$ by a series of "exchanges":

- If at time $t$, $S_A$ executes a task $\tau_j$ that does NOT have the earliest deadline, while some other task $\tau_i$ with earlier deadline is **ready but not running**:
  - Swap the execution of $\tau_j$ and $\tau_i$ at time $t$.
  - This swap **does not cause** $\tau_j$ to miss its deadline (it had more slack).
  - This swap **may help** $\tau_i$ meet its deadline (earlier start).

**Step 3: Induction.** Repeat these exchanges until the schedule is identical to EDF. Each exchange preserves feasibility.

**Step 4: Conclusion.** Since a feasible schedule exists (by assumption), and exchanges can transform it into the EDF schedule without losing feasibility, **EDF is also feasible**. QED.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The full proof is not expected, but you should be able to state the **theorem**, the **assumptions**, and the **exchange argument intuition** in 2-3 lines for 7 marks.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Architecture Decision Examples

| Scenario | Recommended Architecture | Justification |
|----------|--------------------------|---------------|
| Battery-powered IoT sensor (8-bit MCU, 2 KB RAM) | Monolithic + Round Robin | Minimal memory; cooperative scheduling acceptable for low-rate sensing |
| Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) in car | Preemptive RMS on microkernel | Hard real-time; safety-critical; ASIL-D |
| Mars Rover flight software | VxWorks microkernel + RMS + IPC over message queues | Radiation hardening; deterministic; high reliability |
| Smart watch UI | Hybrid (Linux + RT tasks) | Smooth animations (soft real-time) + sensor sampling (hard real-time) |
| Industrial PLC | Monolithic + static priority + PCP | Determinism; predictable mutex behavior |
| Air Traffic Control | Microkernel + partitioned scheduling (ARINC 653) | Spatial + temporal isolation between criticality levels |
| Medical infusion pump | Microkernel + EDF + PIP | Soft real-time with priority inheritance for safety |
| Drone flight controller | FreeRTOS + RMS | Fast, lightweight, sufficient for moderate complexity |
| 5G gNB | Linux PREEMPT_RT + EDF | High data rates + real-time MAC scheduling |
| Automotive ADAS | QNX Neutrino + partitioned | ISO 26262, multi-criticality |

The **dashed arrows** show that user tasks can directly invoke kernel services (via system calls).

```mermaid
    classDef hw fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8B00,color:#000
```

> **Comparison Read-out:** In the **monolithic** kernel (left), all services run together in kernel mode — fast but a single bug crashes everything. In the **microkernel** (right), only scheduler/IPC/interrupts are in kernel; drivers, FS, network are user-space processes — slower (more context switches) but fault-isolated.

---

## 4.3 State Machine: Task State Transitions

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Dormant: Task Created
    Dormant --> Ready: Start
    Ready --> Running: Scheduler Dispatch
    Running --> Ready: Preempted by Higher Priority Task
    Running --> Blocked: Wait for Event - Semaphore, I/O
    Blocked --> Ready: Event Occurred - Timeout
    Running --> Suspended: Suspend Call
    Suspended --> Ready: Resume Call
    Running --> [*]: Task Complete
    Ready --> [*]: Task Deleted
    Suspended --> [*]: Task Deleted
    Blocked --> [*]: Task Deleted
```

> **Reading the state diagram:** A task begins in **Dormant**, transitions to **Ready** when started, and then cycles through **Ready ↔ Running ↔ Blocked** based on scheduler decisions and IPC events. The **Suspended** state is an explicit pause, typically used for debugging or power management.

---

## 4.4 Context Switch Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Interrupt or Syscall Triggered]:::start --> B[Save Current Task Context - Registers, SP, PC]:::step
    B --> C[Update Current TCB - State = Ready]:::step
    C --> D[Run Scheduler - Pick Highest Priority Ready Task]:::step
    D --> E[Load Selected TCB - State = Running]:::step
    E --> F[Restore Context - Registers, SP, PC]:::step
    F --> G[Jump to New Task]:::end
    G --> H[New Task Executes]:::end

    classDef start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef step fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef end fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
```

> **Latency Note:** The sum of all step times from A to G is the **Context Switch Time (CST)**. In RTOS, this must be a **bounded constant** and is part of the system overhead subtracted from the available CPU time.

---

## 4.5 Scheduling Architecture — RMS vs EDF Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RMS[Rate Monotonic Scheduling]
        R1[Static Priority]:::s
        R2[Priority = 1 / Period]:::s
        R3[Schedulability: U ≤ n(2^1/n - 1)]:::s
        R4[Lower Priority Tasks May Miss Deadlines]:::warn
        R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4
    end

    subgraph EDF[Earliest Deadline First]
        E1[Dynamic Priority]:::s
        E2[Priority = 1 / Time to Next Deadline]:::s
        E3[Schedulability: U ≤ 1]:::s
        E4[Optimal for Uniprocessor]:::good
        E1 --> E2 --> E3 --> E4
    end

    classDef s fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000
    classDef warn fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef good fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

> **Architectural Insight:** RMS uses **fixed** priorities computed at design time — implementation is simpler and overhead is lower. EDF recalculates priorities at **every scheduling point** — slightly more runtime overhead but achieves **100% CPU utilization** at the cost of higher implementation complexity.

---

## 4.6 Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) — Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant H as High Priority Task H
    participant M as Medium Priority Task M
    participant L as Low Priority Task L
    participant MTX as Mutex M

    Note over L,MTX: L acquires Mutex M
    L->>MTX: lock()
    MTX-->>L: success

    Note over H,MTX: H tries to acquire M
    H->>MTX: lock()
    Note over L: L inherits H's priority
    Note over L,L: L now runs at HIGH priority

    Note over M: M cannot preempt L (L is now high priority)
    L->>L: Execute critical section
    L->>MTX: unlock()

    Note over L: L drops back to LOW priority
    Note over H: H can now acquire Mutex
    H->>MTX: lock()
    MTX-->>H: success
    H->>MTX: unlock()

    Note over M: M can now run
```

> **Without PIP**, the sequence L → M (preempts) → H (blocked) would cause **unbounded priority inversion**. PIP bounds this by *temporarily boosting* L's priority so it finishes quickly.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Question 1: Define RTOS Architecture. Differentiate between monolithic and microkernel architectures. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**RTOS Architecture** is the structured design of the kernel and its components (scheduler, task manager, IPC, memory manager, HAL) that ensures **deterministic, time-bounded execution** of tasks in a real-time system.

**Differences:**

| Aspect | Monolithic Kernel | Microkernel |
|--------|-------------------|-------------|
| Services in kernel | All (scheduler, FS, drivers, net) | Only scheduling, IPC, basic memory |
| Fault isolation | Poor (any bug crashes OS) | Strong (drivers in user space) |
| Performance | High (no mode switches) | Lower (more context switches) |
| Code size | Smaller | Larger |
| Example | FreeRTOS, µC/OS-II | QNX Neutrino, MINIX 3 |
| Certification | Hard to certify | Easy to certify for safety |

---

### Question 2: Explain the concept of priority inversion in RTOS with an example. Mention its solution. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Priority Inversion** is a scheduling anomaly where a **higher-priority task is indirectly preempted by a lower-priority task**, effectively "inverting" the relative priorities.

**Example (Mars Pathfinder, 1997):**
- Low-priority task **L** acquires a shared bus mutex.
- High-priority task **H** tries to acquire the same mutex → **blocked**.
- Medium-priority task **M** (unrelated) preempts **L** (since $P_M > P_L$).
- **L** never gets CPU → **H** is blocked indefinitely → **system reset**.

**Solution:** **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** — when a high-priority task blocks on a resource held by a low-priority task, the low-priority task **temporarily inherits** the high priority until it releases the resource.

**Other solutions:** Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP), disabling interrupts during critical sections (limited applicability).

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS — ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Real-Time Operating System. Discuss the role of each component. **[7 marks]**

**Q. (b)** Explain Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) and Earliest Deadline First (EDF) algorithms. Compare their schedulability bounds. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution: Q. (a) — RTOS Architecture Block Diagram & Components

**Step 1: Introduction (1 mark)**
An RTOS architecture is a **layered, structured design** that provides deterministic, time-bounded execution of real-time tasks. It consists of the **Hardware**, **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)**, **Kernel Core**, **Middleware**, and **Application** layers.

**Step 2: Block Diagram (2 marks)**

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  APPLICATION LAYER (User Tasks: Task1, Task2…) │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  MIDDLEWARE (File System, Networking, GUI)     │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  KERNEL CORE                                   │
│   • Task Management                            │
│   • Scheduler (RMS, EDF, RR)                    │
│   • IPC (Semaphores, Mutex, Msg Queues)        │
│   • Memory Manager (Pools, MPU)                │
│   • Interrupt Handler / Dispatcher             │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  HARDWARE ABSTRACTION LAYER (HAL)              │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  HARDWARE (CPU, RAM, I/O, Timers, Sensors)     │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Step 3: Role of Each Component (4 marks)**

**(i) Application Layer:** Contains the actual user-written real-time tasks (control loops, sensor readers, actuators). Each task has a priority, period, deadline, and WCET.

**(ii) Middleware Layer:** Provides optional higher-level services such as file systems, network protocol stacks (TCP/IP, CAN, Modbus), and GUI libraries.

**(iii) Task Management Module:** Responsible for **creating, deleting, suspending, resuming** tasks. Maintains the **Task Control Block (TCB)** for each task — storing its state, priority, stack pointer, registers, and event flags.

**(iv) Scheduler:** Implements the chosen **scheduling algorithm** (RMS, EDF, Round Robin). Decides which Ready task gets the CPU and for how long. Must be **O(1) or O(log n)** in most RTOS to keep scheduling overhead bounded.

**(v) IPC Subsystem:** Provides **semaphores, mutexes, message queues, mailboxes, event flags, and signals** for inter-task communication and synchronization. Critical for coordination between tasks sharing resources.

**(vi) Memory Manager:** Allocates memory using **deterministic techniques** (memory pools, static allocation) to avoid fragmentation and unbounded `malloc` calls. May use **MPU** to enforce isolation.

**(vii) Interrupt Handler:** Receives hardware interrupts, dispatches to ISRs (Interrupt Service Routines), and signals the corresponding task via an event/semaphore. Designed for **minimal latency** (typically < 10 µs).

**(viii) Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL):** Hides hardware-specific details (register layouts, peripheral APIs) from upper layers, enabling **portability** across processors (ARM Cortex-M → RISC-V → x86).

**(ix) Hardware:** The physical platform — CPU, RAM, ROM/Flash, timers, ADC/DAC, communication peripherals (UART, SPI, I2C, CAN, Ethernet).

**Step 4: Conclusion (Optional)**
The architecture must guarantee **bounded worst-case latency** for all time-critical operations — this is the essence of real-time.

---

#### Model Solution: Q. (b) — RMS and EDF Comparison

**Step 1: Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) — Definition (2 marks)**

RMS is a **static-priority, preemptive** scheduling algorithm for **periodic, independent tasks** with $D_i = T_i$.
- **Priority rule:** $P_i = 1/T_i$ (shorter period → higher priority)
- **Proposed by:** Liu & Layland (1973)
- **Optimality:** Optimal among all static-priority algorithms (no other static-priority algorithm can schedule a task set that RMS cannot)

**Step 2: RMS Schedulability Bound (1 mark)**

$$U_{RMS} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$$

For example: $n=1$ → $U \leq 1.0$; $n=2$ → $U \leq 0.828$; $n=3$ → $U \leq 0.779$; $n \to \infty$ → $U \leq 0.6931$.

**Step 3: Earliest Deadline First (EDF) — Definition (2 marks)**

EDF is a **dynamic-priority, preemptive** scheduling algorithm.
- **Priority rule:** At every scheduling decision, the task with the **earliest absolute deadline** gets the CPU.
- **Optimality (Liu & Layland, 1973):** EDF is **optimal for uniprocessors** — any feasible task set is schedulable under EDF.

**Step 4: EDF Schedulability Bound (1 mark)**

$$U_{EDF} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq 1$$

This is a **necessary AND sufficient** condition. The full CPU (100%) can theoretically be used.

**Step 5: Comparison Table (1 mark)**

| Aspect | RMS | EDF |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Priority type | Static | Dynamic |
| Implementation complexity | Lower | Higher (re-sort on every event) |
| Schedulability bound | $U \leq 0.693$ (asymptotic) | $U \leq 1.0$ |
| Optimality | Optimal among static | Optimal overall (uniprocessor) |
| Runtime overhead | Lower | Higher (priority changes) |
| Predictability under overload | Better (predictable failures) | Worse (cascading failures possible) |
| Use case | Hard real-time, certifiable | High-utilization soft/hard real-time |

---

### Question B — Option 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the Task Control Block (TCB) and various task states in RTOS. Describe context switching with a neat diagram. **[7 marks]**

**Q. (b)** Three periodic tasks are to be scheduled under RMS. Task parameters are:
- $\tau_1$: $C_1 = 2$ ms, $T_1 = 6$ ms
- $\tau_2$: $C_2 = 3$ ms, $T_2 = 12$ ms
- $\tau_3$: $C_3 = 4$ ms, $T_3 = 24$ ms
Apply (i) Liu & Layland's utilization bound test and (ii) Exact Response Time Analysis. Is the task set schedulable? **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution: Q. (a) — Task Control Block, Task States, Context Switching

**Step 1: Task Definition (1 mark)**
A **task** (or thread) is the smallest unit of execution in an RTOS, with its own stack, register context, and priority.

**Step 2: Task Control Block (TCB) (2 marks)**
The **TCB** is a kernel data structure that stores all information about a task. Typical fields:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Task ID            : 0x03          │
│  Task State         : READY         │
│  Priority           : 5             │
│  Stack Pointer (SP) : 0x2000_1000   │
│  Stack Base         : 0x2000_0800   │
│  Stack Limit        : 0x2000_0400   │
│  Task Function      : MotorControl()│
│  PC (saved)         : 0x0800_1234   │
│  Registers (R0-R15) : ...           │
│  Delay/Timeout      : 0             │
│  Error Code         : E_OK          │
│  Event Flags        : 0x00          │
│  Next TCB Pointer   : 0x2000_2050   │
│  Prev TCB Pointer   : 0x2000_1F00   │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Step 3: Task States (2 marks)**

| State | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| **Dormant** | Task created but not started; does not consume CPU |
| **Ready** | Eligible to run; in the ready queue waiting for CPU |
| **Running** | Currently executing on the CPU |
| **Blocked** | Waiting for an event (semaphore, message, I/O) |
| **Suspended** | Explicitly paused by `OSTaskSuspend()` |

**Transition diagram:**

```
  ┌──────┐  start  ┌──────┐  dispatch ┌─────────┐
  │DORMANT├───────►│READY ├──────────►│ RUNNING │
  └───┬──┘         └───┬──┘◄──────────┴────┬────┘
      │ delete         │ preempt             │
      │                └─────┐                │ wait
      ▼                      ▼                ▼
   (gone)              (back to queue)   ┌──────────┐
                                          │ BLOCKED  │
                                          └────┬─────┘
                                               │ event
                                               ▼
                                            (READY)
```

**Step 4: Context Switching (2 marks)**

Context switching is the process of **saving** the state of the currently running task and **restoring** the state of the next task. Sequence:

1. **Trigger:** Interrupt, syscall, or time-slice expiration
2. **Save current task context:** CPU registers (R0-R15 including PC, SP, LR, CPSR) → into current task's TCB
3. **Update current TCB:** state = READY, save SP
4. **Run scheduler:** select highest-priority READY task
5. **Load new task context:** read new TCB, restore registers and SP
6. **Update new TCB:** state = RUNNING
7. **Jump to new task's PC**

**Context Switch Time:**
$$CST = T_{save} + T_{scheduler} + T_{load}$$

In a well-designed RTOS, CST is a **bounded constant** (e.g., 1–5 µs on a 100 MHz Cortex-M4) and is included in the system overhead budget.

---

#### Model Solution: Q. (b) — RMS Schedulability Analysis

**Given:**
| Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | $D_i$ (ms) |
|------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| $\tau_1$ | 2 | 6 | 6 |
| $\tau_2$ | 3 | 12 | 12 |
| $\tau_3$ | 4 | 24 | 24 |

**Priority assignment (by RMS rule):** $P_1 > P_2 > P_3$ (since $T_1 < T_2 < T_3$).

**Step 1: Compute Total CPU Utilization (1 mark)**

$$U = \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{12} + \frac{4}{24}$$

Compute each term:
- $C_1/T_1 = 2/6 = 0.3333$
- $C_2/T_2 = 3/12 = 0.2500$
- $C_3/T_3 = 4/24 = 0.1667$

$$U = 0.3333 + 0.2500 + 0.1667 = 0.7500 = 75\%$$

**Step 2: Apply Liu & Layland's Sufficient Test (2 marks)**

For $n = 3$ tasks, the bound is:
$$U_{bound}(3) = 3(2^{1/3} - 1)$$

Compute:
- $2^{1/3} = 1.2599$
- $2^{1/3} - 1 = 0.2599$
- $3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7798$

$$U_{bound}(3) = 0.7798 = 77.98\%$$

**Compare:**
$$U = 0.7500 \leq U_{bound} = 0.7798 \quad \checkmark$$

✅ **Test PASSES** (sufficient condition satisfied).

**However, the test is only sufficient — it does not PROVE the task set is unschedulable if it fails.** We must apply the exact RTA.

**Step 3: Exact Response Time Analysis (3 marks)**

For each task, compute $R_i$ and compare with $D_i$.

**(i) Task $\tau_1$ (highest priority):** No higher-priority tasks.

$$R_1 = C_1 = 2 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(one iteration suffices)}$$

$$R_1 = 2 \leq D_1 = 6 \quad \checkmark \quad \text{Schedulable}$$

**(ii) Task $\tau_2$:** Higher-priority tasks: $hp(2) = \{\tau_1\}$.

$$R_2 = C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2$$

Iterate:
- $R_2^{(0)} = 3$
- $R_2^{(1)} = 3 + \lceil 3/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 \cdot 2 = 5$ ms
- $R_2^{(2)} = 3 + \lceil 5/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 \cdot 2 = 5$ ms → **fixed point**

$$R_2^* = 5 \leq D_2 = 12 \quad \checkmark \quad \text{Schedulable}$$

**(iii) Task $\tau_3$:** Higher-priority tasks: $hp(3) = \{\tau_1, \tau_2\}$.

$$R_3 = C_3 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{T_2} \right\rceil C_2 = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{12} \right\rceil \cdot 3$$

Iterate:
- $R_3^{(0)} = 4$ ms
- $R_3^{(1)} = 4 + \lceil 4/6 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 4/12 \rceil \cdot 3 = 4 + 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 3 = 9$ ms
- $R_3^{(2)} = 4 + \lceil 9/6 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 9/12 \rceil \cdot 3 = 4 + 2 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 3 = 11$ ms
- $R_3^{(3)} = 4 + \lceil 11/6 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 11/12 \rceil \cdot 3 = 4 + 2 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 3 = 11$ ms → **fixed point**

$$R_3^* = 11 \text{ ms} \leq D_3 = 24 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark \quad \text{Schedulable}$$

**Step 4: Conclusion (1 mark)**

| Test | Result |
|------|--------|
| Utilization test | Passes ($0.75 \leq 0.78$) |
| RTA for $\tau_1$ | $R_1 = 2 \leq 6$ ✓ |
| RTA for $\tau_2$ | $R_2 = 5 \leq 12$ ✓ |
| RTA for $\tau_3$ | $R_3 = 11 \leq 24$ ✓ |

✅ **The task set IS schedulable under RMS.** All three tasks meet their deadlines, with significant slack remaining for task $\tau_3$ (13 ms of margin).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> **1. Confusing $U$ bound (sufficient) with exact schedulability:**
> The Liu & Layland bound $U \leq n(2^{1/n}-1)$ is **sufficient but not necessary**. If it fails, the task set MAY still be schedulable — you MUST apply RTA to confirm. Examiners **deduct 2 marks** if you conclude "not schedulable" simply because $U >$ bound.
> 
> **2. Forgetting to iterate the RTA recurrence:**
> Many students write down the formula but compute only one iteration. The recurrence is **monotonically non-decreasing**; you MUST iterate until **convergence** (fixed point). At least 3-4 iterations are typical.
> 
> **3. Using the wrong ceiling function in RTA:**
> The ceiling $\lceil R_i / T_j \rceil$ must be the **ceiling** (smallest integer $\geq$ the value), not the floor. Off-by-one error → wrong $R_i$ → wrong conclusion.
> 
> **4. Misclassifying kernel architectures:**
> VxWorks is often called "monolithic" — but modern VxWorks has a **modular, microkernel-influenced** design. PREEMPT-RT Linux is **hybrid**, not microkernel. Examiners check these nuances.
> 
> **5. Not drawing the state transition diagram for task states:**
> A 7-mark question on "task architecture" **always** expects a state diagram. Skipping the diagram costs you 2 marks.
> 
> **6. Confusing Deadline ($D_i$) with Period ($T_i$):**
> RMS optimality holds only when $D_i = T_i$. If $D_i \neq T_i$, use **Deadline Monotonic (DM)** scheduling, NOT RMS.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RTOS Architecture — High-Density Revision Checklist**

### 🏗️ Architecture Fundamentals
- [ ] RTOS architecture = layered design of **kernel + HAL + middleware + applications**
- [ ] Goal: **deterministic, time-bounded** execution
- [ ] Five pillars: Kernel, Task Mgmt, Scheduler, IPC, Memory Mgmt

### 🧱 Kernel Architecture
- [ ] **Monolithic** = all in kernel mode, fast but unsafe (e.g., FreeRTOS, µC/OS-II)
- [ ] **Microkernel** = only scheduler/IPC/interrupts in kernel; rest in user space (QNX, MINIX 3)
- [ ] **Hybrid** = mix of both (Linux PREEMPT-RT, Windows NT)
- [ ] Microkernel pros: fault isolation, security, certification. Cons: overhead.

### 📋 Task Architecture
- [ ] **TCB** = task identity card: state, priority, stack, registers, PC, delay, event flags
- [ ] **Five task states:** Dormant, Ready, Running, Blocked, Suspended
- [ ] **Context switch** = save current TCB + load new TCB. Time = $T_{save} + T_{sched} + T_{load}$
- [ ] Context switch time must be **bounded constant** in RTOS

### ⏱️ Scheduling Architecture
- [ ] **RMS** = static priority = $1/T_i$ (shorter period → higher priority)
- [ ] **RMS sufficient bound:** $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$
- [ ] **RMS asymptotic bound:** $U \leq 0.693$ as $n \to \infty$
- [ ] **EDF** = dynamic priority = earliest absolute deadline
- [ ] **EDF optimality:** $U \leq 1$ (necessary AND sufficient for uniprocessor)
- [ ] **RTA formula:** $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i/T_j \rceil C_j$
- [ ] **Schedulability:** $R_i \leq D_i$ for all tasks

### 🔄 IPC Architecture
- [ ] Primitives: **Semaphore, Mutex, Message Queue, Mailbox, Pipe, Event Flags, Signal**
- [ ] **Mutex** = ownership-based; supports Priority Inheritance
- [ ] **Semaphore** = signaling; no ownership
- [ ] **Priority Inversion** = L holds mutex, H blocks, M preempts L → H starved
- [ ] **Solutions:** Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP), Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)

### 🧠 Memory Architecture
- [ ] Avoid virtual memory, paging, swapping (non-deterministic)
- [ ] Prefer **static allocation + memory pools** for determinism
- [ ] Use **MPU** for fault isolation in safety-critical systems

### 📊 Key Numerical Bounds
- [ ] $U_{RMS} \leq 0.693$ (asymptotic) — Liu & Layland 1973
- [ ] $U_{EDF} \leq 1.0$ — Liu & Layland 1973
- [ ] Context switch time: typically **1-10 µs** in modern RTOS
- [ ] Interrupt latency: typically **0.1-5 µs**

### 🎯 Examiner's Hot Topics (Most Likely 14-Mark Questions)
1. **Compare monolithic vs microkernel** — always with a diagram
2. **RTA numerical problem** — practice at least 5 different task sets
3. **Priority Inversion** — explain + Mars Pathfinder + PIP solution
4. **RMS utilization test** — show $\leq 0.693$ and $n(2^{1/n}-1)$ computation
5. **EDF optimality** — state the theorem, the bound, give a numerical check
6. **TCB and context switching** — expect a sequence diagram

### 🏆 Quick-Recall Mnemonics
- **"DORMANT"** = Dead OR Resting, My ActiveNTask (states: **D**ormant, **O**nce, **R**eady, **M**emory? — too messy, use the 5-state table)
- **"SHE"** for IPC: **S**emaphores, **H**ow (mutexes), **E**vent flags
- **"RMS - 69"** = RMS max utilization ≈ **69%** (asymptotic)
- **"EDF - 100"** = EDF can use **100%** of CPU
- **"PIP saves Pathfinder"** = Priority Inheritance Protocol saved Mars Pathfinder
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RTOS Building Blocks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT741 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** RTOS Building Blocks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Real Time Operating Systems*, **RTOS Building Blocks** refers to the fundamental kernel-level components and primitives that an RTOS provides to the application programmer for constructing deterministic, time-bounded, and concurrent embedded software. These primitives include **Tasks (Threads)**, **Task States**, **Semaphores (Binary, Counting, Mutex)**, **Message Queues**, **Mailboxes**, **Pipes**, **Event Flags**, **Software/Hardware Timers**, **Memory Management Units (MMU)**, and **Interrupt Service Routines (ISR)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> An RTOS provides a set of **kernel objects** (also called *building blocks*) that the developer uses to structure the application. These objects are deterministic in execution, consume bounded resources, and guarantee predictable response times — a property the KTU syllabus terms as **"temporal determinism"**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an **Air Traffic Control (ATC) Tower** at a busy international airport. The tower is the **RTOS Kernel**. Each incoming aircraft is a **Task** that must be processed (landed, parked, refuelled, or taken off) within a strict deadline. The tower uses:

- **Runways** as **Critical Sections** (only one plane can use a runway at a time) → protected by **Mutex/Semaphores**
- **Frequency channels** for pilots to talk to ATC → analogous to **Message Queues** (inter-task communication)
- **Scheduled slots** in the controller's clipboard → analogous to **Task Scheduler** with priorities
- **Countdown timers** for take-off clearance → analogous to **Software Timers / Watchdog Timers**
- **Emergency alarms** that pre-empt all other work → analogous to **Hardware Interrupts**

The **controllers (RTOS kernel)** are *non-preemptive humans in a normal OS*, but in an *RTOS*, the system enforces strict timing — a controller cannot be late in clearing an aircraft.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:**
> Unlike a general-purpose OS (GPOS) such as Windows/Linux, where the goal is *throughput* and *fairness*, an RTOS is optimised for **predictability** and **deadline adherence**. Every "building block" is designed with a worst-case execution time (WCET) bound.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Worst-Case Execution Time** | **WCET** | Microseconds | Maximum time a task can take |
| **Release Time** | **rᵢ** | ms | Time when task i becomes ready |
| **Deadline** | **dᵢ** | ms | Time by which task i must complete |
| **Response Time** | **Rᵢ** | ms | Actual completion time minus release |
| **Jitter** | **Jᵢ** | μs | Variation in response times |
| **Context Switch Time** | **T_cs** | 1–10 μs | Time to save/restore task state |
| **Interrupt Latency** | **T_il** | < 1 μs | Time from interrupt to ISR start |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always quote the WCET and Interrupt Latency values when justifying why a particular RTOS (e.g., VxWorks, FreeRTOS, QNX) is suitable for a hard real-time system. Examiners award extra marks for these "RTOS-specific" numbers.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Task — The Fundamental Unit of Execution

A **Task** (also called a *Thread of Execution*) is the smallest schedulable unit in an RTOS. Each task has:

- Its own **Stack** (for local variables, return addresses)
- A **Task Control Block (TCB)** maintained by the kernel
- A **Priority** (static in most KTU-style RTOS)
- An entry-point function

### 2.1.1 Task States (as per KTU Module-2 syllabus)

A task in an RTOS can be in one of **four fundamental states**:

1. **Running** — Currently executing on the CPU (only ONE task per core can be in this state)
2. **Ready** — All conditions for execution are met, but the CPU is busy with a higher-priority task
3. **Blocked / Waiting** — Task is waiting for a resource (semaphore, I/O, delay, message)
4. **Suspended** — Task is explicitly put to sleep (no time-out, no event wakes it; must be explicitly resumed)

> [!NOTE]
> The fifth state occasionally mentioned is **"Dormant / Terminated"** — the task has been created but not yet started, or has finished execution.

### 2.1.2 State Transition Triggers

| From → To | Trigger |
|---|---|
| Dormant → Ready | `OSTaskCreate()` followed by activation |
| Ready → Running | Scheduler dispatches (highest priority ready task) |
| Running → Ready | Pre-emption by higher-priority task OR time-slice expiry |
| Running → Blocked | Task calls `pend()` on semaphore, or `delay()`, or I/O wait |
| Blocked → Ready | Event occurs (semaphore `post()`, delay expires, message arrives) |
| Running → Suspended | `OSTaskSuspend()` called |
| Suspended → Ready | `OSTaskResume()` called |

## 2.2 Semaphores

A **Semaphore** is a kernel object used for **synchronisation** and **mutual exclusion**. It is essentially a protected integer counter with two atomic operations.

### 2.2.1 Types of Semaphores (KTU Module-2)

| Type | Initial Value | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Binary Semaphore** | 0 or 1 | Signalling between tasks (one-shot event) |
| **Counting Semaphore** | N (≥1) | Managing N identical resources (e.g., 3 UART ports) |
| **Mutex (MUT-EX)** | 1 | Mutual exclusion of a *shared resource* with **priority inheritance** |

### 2.2.2 Atomic Operations

- **P(S) / Wait(S) / Pend(S)**: Decrements S; if S < 0, block the task
- **V(S) / Signal(S) / Post(S)**: Increments S; if S ≤ 0, wake one blocked task

$$ P(S): \quad S \leftarrow S - 1; \quad \text{if } S < 0 \text{ then block task on } S $$

$$ V(S): \quad S \leftarrow S + 1; \quad \text{if } S \leq 0 \text{ then wake one task waiting on } S $$

### 2.2.3 The Priority Inversion Problem (HIGH-YIELD for KTU)

**Priority Inversion** occurs when a *low-priority task* holding a mutex gets *pre-empted* by an *intermediate-priority task*, indefinitely delaying a *high-priority task* waiting for the same mutex.

**Classic Example (Mars Pathfinder Bug, 1997):**
- Task H (High priority) — bus communication
- Task M (Medium priority) — meteorological
- Task L (Low priority) — bus communication (shared with H)

When L holds the shared bus mutex and is pre-empted by M, H must wait — even though H is the most important task.

**Solution: Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)**
- When H blocks on a mutex held by L, the kernel *temporarily boosts* L's priority to that of H.
- L runs to completion (or releases the mutex), then its original priority is restored.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Question Pattern:** Examiners *frequently* ask: "Explain priority inversion with a real-world example and how the **Priority Inheritance Protocol** solves it." Be ready to draw the timing diagram!

## 2.3 Message Queues, Mailboxes, and Pipes

These are the three primary **Inter-Task Communication (ITC)** primitives in an RTOS.

| Primitive | Description | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Message Queue** | FIFO buffer of fixed-size messages | Decouples producer/consumer; supports multiple readers/writers |
| **Mailbox** | Single-slot message holder (1 message) | Lowest-overhead IPC; used for simple command passing |
| **Pipe** | Byte-stream oriented (like a UNIX pipe) | Used for bulk data transfer; rarely in micro-RTOS |

### 2.3.1 Message Queue API (RTOS-generic)

```
QueueCreate(size, msg_length)   → returns QueueID
QueueSend(QueueID, &msg, timeout) → blocks if queue full
QueueReceive(QueueID, &msg, timeout) → blocks if queue empty
QueueDelete(QueueID)
```

## 2.4 Timers

### 2.4.1 Hardware Timers
Provided by the microcontroller (e.g., SysTick in ARM Cortex-M). Used by the RTOS tick interrupt to drive pre-emptive scheduling.

### 2.4.2 Software Timers
Kernel-managed virtual timers built on top of the hardware tick. Used for:
- **One-shot timers** (fire once after N ticks)
- **Periodic timers** (fire every N ticks)

## 2.5 Memory Management

Most small RTOS (FreeRTOS, μC/OS-II) use **static memory allocation** for tasks and queues (deterministic, no fragmentation). Larger RTOS (VxWorks, QNX) include **partition managers** (fixed-size block pools) and full **malloc/free** with paging.

## 2.6 Interrupt Handling

- **Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs)** should be **as short as possible** (just acknowledge + signal a task via `Post()`).
- The task woken by the ISR does the **deferred processing** ("bottom-half" or "DPC" — Deferred Procedure Call).
- Many RTOS APIs have a `*FromISR()` variant (e.g., `xQueueSendFromISR()`) that is safe to call inside an ISR.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Task Response Time | $R_i = C_i + I_i$ | $C_i$ = computation time, $I_i$ = interference |
| Worst-Case Response Time | $R_i^{WCET} = \max_{k \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_k} \right\rceil C_k + C_i$ | $hp(i)$ = higher-priority tasks |
| CPU Utilisation Bound (Liu & Layland, RMS) | $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | n = number of tasks, for $n \to \infty$, bound → 0.693 |
| Interrupt Latency | $T_{il} = T_{max\_isr} + T_{cs}$ | Must be < deadline |
| Semaphore Wait | $P(S): S = S - 1; \text{if } S < 0 \text{ block}$ | Atomic |
| Semaphore Signal | $V(S): S = S + 1; \text{if } S \leq 0 \text{ wake}$ | Atomic |
| Priority Inheritance | $P_{effective} = \max(P_{current}, P_{waiter})$ | Restored on release |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** In Markdown tables, **never** use the pipe `|` symbol inside cell values for absolute value or set notation. Use `\vert` (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) instead — this prevents table syntax corruption.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | RTOS Building Block Used |
|---|---|
| Automotive (ECU, ABS) | Mutex (CAN bus), Message Queue (sensor data), Watchdog timer |
| Medical (Pacemaker, Ventilator) | Hard real-time tasks, Priority inheritance (prevent inversion) |
| Aerospace (Flight control) | Deterministic scheduler, Hardware timers, Memory partitioning |
| Industrial (PLC, Robotics) | Counting semaphores (conveyor slots), Event flags |
| Consumer IoT (Smart Bulb) | Soft real-time, Software timers, Mailboxes |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Worst-Case Response Time for a Task under Fixed Priority Pre-emptive Scheduling (FPPS)

This is a **frequently-asked 14-mark derivation** in KTU exams. We derive the response time $R_i$ of a task $\tau_i$ considering pre-emption by all higher-priority tasks.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Notation

Let the task set be $\Gamma = \{\tau_1, \tau_2, \ldots, \tau_n\}$ with priority order $P_1 > P_2 > \ldots > P_n$ (so $\tau_1$ is the highest priority).

For task $\tau_i$:
- $T_i$ = period (or minimum inter-arrival time)
- $C_i$ = worst-case execution time (WCET)
- $D_i$ = relative deadline
- $R_i$ = worst-case response time we want to find
- $B_i$ = worst-case blocking time due to lower-priority tasks (only under PCP/PIP)
- $J_i$ = release jitter

### 3.1.2 Building the Recurrence

**Step 1: Decompose the busy window.**
Consider the *level-i busy window* — the longest contiguous time interval during which the processor is busy executing tasks of priority $\geq i$ starting from the critical instant (when all higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously with $\tau_i$).

Let $w_i^{(k)}$ be the $k$-th approximation of the busy window length.

**Step 2: Initial approximation.**
Start with the task's own execution time:

$$ w_i^{(0)} = C_i $$

**Step 3: Add interference from higher-priority tasks.**
Each higher-priority task $\tau_k$ (where $k < i$) executes once per period $T_k$. Within a window of length $w_i$, the number of releases of $\tau_k$ is $\lceil w_i / T_k \rceil$, and each release consumes $C_k$ of CPU time.

Therefore, the new approximation becomes:

$$ w_i^{(k+1)} = B_i + C_i + \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} \left\lceil \frac{w_i^{(k)}}{T_k} \right\rceil C_k $$

**Step 4: Convergence condition.**
The iteration terminates when $w_i^{(k+1)} = w_i^{(k)}$. If $w_i^{(k+1)} > D_i$ for some $k$, the task is *unschedulable*.

**Step 5: Identify the response time.**
The worst-case response time is the converged value:

$$ R_i = w_i^{(*)} $$

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Task set:**

| Task | Priority | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | $D_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 1 (Highest) | 1 | 4 | 4 |
| $\tau_2$ | 2 | 2 | 6 | 6 |
| $\tau_3$ | 3 (Lowest) | 3 | 10 | 10 |

**Find $R_3$ (worst-case response time of $\tau_3$), assuming $B_3 = 0$.**

**Iteration 0:**
$w_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 3$ ms

**Iteration 1:**
Interference from $\tau_1$: $\lceil 3 / 4 \rceil \times 1 = 1 \times 1 = 1$ ms
Interference from $\tau_2$: $\lceil 3 / 6 \rceil \times 2 = 1 \times 2 = 2$ ms

$$ w_3^{(1)} = 0 + 3 + 1 + 2 = 6 \text{ ms} $$

**Iteration 2:**
Interference from $\tau_1$: $\lceil 6 / 4 \rceil \times 1 = 2 \times 1 = 2$ ms
Interference from $\tau_2$: $\lceil 6 / 6 \rceil \times 2 = 1 \times 2 = 2$ ms

$$ w_3^{(2)} = 0 + 3 + 2 + 2 = 7 \text{ ms} $$

**Iteration 3:**
Interference from $\tau_1$: $\lceil 7 / 4 \rceil \times 1 = 2 \times 1 = 2$ ms
Interference from $\tau_2$: $\lceil 7 / 6 \rceil \times 2 = 2 \times 2 = 4$ ms

$$ w_3^{(3)} = 0 + 3 + 2 + 4 = 9 \text{ ms} $$

**Iteration 4:**
Interference from $\tau_1$: $\lceil 9 / 4 \rceil \times 1 = 3 \times 1 = 3$ ms
Interference from $\tau_2$: $\lceil 9 / 6 \rceil \times 2 = 2 \times 2 = 4$ ms

$$ w_3^{(4)} = 0 + 3 + 3 + 4 = 10 \text{ ms} $$

**Iteration 5:**
Interference from $\tau_1$: $\lceil 10 / 4 \rceil \times 1 = 3 \times 1 = 3$ ms
Interference from $\tau_2$: $\lceil 10 / 6 \rceil \times 2 = 2 \times 2 = 4$ ms

$$ w_3^{(5)} = 0 + 3 + 3 + 4 = 10 \text{ ms} $$

**Convergence check:** $w_3^{(5)} = w_3^{(4)} = 10$ ms ✓

**Conclusion:** $R_3 = 10$ ms, and since $D_3 = 10$ ms, the task $\tau_3$ is *just* schedulable (boundary case).

### 3.1.4 Final Compact Formula

$$ \boxed{R_i = B_i + C_i + \sum_{k \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_k} \right\rceil C_k, \quad \text{Scheduable if } R_i \leq D_i} $$

> [!NOTE]
> The equation $R_i$ appears on **both sides** (inside the ceiling). It is solved iteratively, starting from $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$, until convergence.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right RTOS Building Block

| Problem Scenario | Recommended Building Block | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Two tasks share a UART port | **Mutex with PIP** | Priority inheritance prevents inversion |
| Task A must wait for Task B to finish a setup | **Binary Semaphore** | One-shot signalling |
| 3 identical sensors, task reads any one | **Counting Semaphore (init=3)** | Manages pool of 3 resources |
| Task must log 100 messages/sec to host | **Message Queue (size=10)** | Decouples producer/consumer, absorbs bursts |
| Periodic sensor sampling every 10 ms | **Software Timer (periodic)** | Hardware timer ticks every 1 ms, software timer fires at 10 ms |
| Emergency stop button must halt motor in < 100 μs | **Hardware Interrupt + ISR** | Interrupt latency < 100 μs; deferred processing in high-prio task |
| Multiple tasks must wake on same event | **Event Flags** | One-to-many wake-up |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Task State Transition Diagram (Mermaid State Machine)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Dormant
    Dormant --> Ready : TaskCreate and Activate
    Ready --> Running : Scheduler Dispatch
    Running --> Ready : Preempted or TimeSlice Expired
    Running --> Blocked : Pend on Semaphore or Delay or IOWait
    Blocked --> Ready : Event Occurs or Timeout
    Running --> Suspended : TaskSuspend Call
    Suspended --> Ready : TaskResume Call
    Running --> [*] : TaskDelete or Self Terminate
    note right of Running
        Only ONE task per CPU core
        can be in this state
    end note
    note right of Blocked
        Blocked tasks are NOT
        in the ready queue
    end note
```

## 4.2 RTOS Kernel Object Hierarchy (Block-Level Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Application Layer User Tasks] --> B[RTOS Kernel API Layer]
    B --> C1[Task Scheduler]
    B --> C2[Synchronisation Primitives]
    B --> C3[Inter Task Communication]
    B --> C4[Timer Subsystem]
    B --> C5[Memory Manager]
    C1 --> D1[Task Control Block TCB]
    C1 --> D2[Ready Queue]
    C2 --> E1[Binary Semaphore]
    C2 --> E2[Counting Semaphore]
    C2 --> E3[Mutex with PIP]
    C3 --> F1[Message Queue]
    C3 --> F2[Mailbox]
    C3 --> F3[Pipe]
    C3 --> F4[Event Flags]
    C4 --> G1[Hardware Timer Tick]
    C4 --> G2[Software Timer List]
    C5 --> H1[Static Memory Pools]
    C5 --> H2[Partition Manager]
    A -.Deferred Processing.-> I[Interrupt Service Routines]
    I --> B
```

## 4.3 Priority Inversion — Unbounded vs Bounded (Timing Schematic)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Priority Inversion Scenario and Solution
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %s
    section Without PIP
    Task H High Priority :h1, 0, 1
    Task M Medium Priority :m1, 1, 4
    Task L Low Priority :l1, 0, 3
    section With Priority Inheritance
    Task H High Priority V2 :h2, 0, 1
    Task M Medium Priority V2 :m2, 4, 7
    Task L Low Priority Inherits H :l2, 0, 4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** In the *Without PIP* chart, Task L runs first (holding mutex), gets pre-empted by M, then H must wait for *both* M and L to finish — **unbounded blocking**. In the *With PIP* chart, the moment H requests the mutex held by L, L's priority is *boosted* to H's level, so L runs to completion before M is allowed in, eliminating unbounded inversion.

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture Flow: Message Queue Producer-Consumer Model

```mermaid

    A2[Producer Task P2] -->|QueueSend Blocking Timeout 5ms| Q
    Q -->|QueueReceive Blocking Timeout 10ms| C1[Consumer Task C1]
    Q -->|QueueReceive NonBlocking| C2[Consumer Task C2]
    Q -.Signals On Full.-> A
    Q -.Signals On Empty.-> C1
    subgraph Kernel Space
        Q
    end
    subgraph User Space
        A
        A2
        C1
        C2
    end
```
