<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Rate Monotonic Scheduling. State and explain the Liu–Layland utilization bound for RMS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Rate Monotonic Scheduling is a **static-priority, preemptive** scheduling algorithm for periodic real-time tasks. The scheduling rule states that the task with the **shorter period** is assigned the **higher priority** (priority ∝ 1/period).

The **Liu–Layland utilization bound** (1973) gives a sufficient condition for a task set of $n$ independent, preemptable periodic tasks to be schedulable under RMS:

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le U_{RMS}(n) = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)
$$

For $n = 2$: bound = 0.828. For $n = 3$: bound = 0.780. Asymptotically, $U_{RMS} \to \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ for large $n$. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for asymptotic discussion.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is priority inversion? Explain how priority inheritance protocol solves it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Priority Inversion** is a scheduling anomaly where a higher-priority task is indirectly preempted by a lower-priority task, effectively "inverting" their relative priority ordering. *(1 mark)*

**Classic scenario:** Task L holds a resource, Task H blocks on it, Task M preempts L → H waits for M (lower priority). This causes **unbounded blocking**. *(1 mark)*

**Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):** When a high-priority task H blocks on a resource held by a low-priority task L, the kernel **temporarily raises L's priority** to that of H. L thus executes at H's priority, preventing medium-priority tasks from preempting it. When L releases the resource, its priority reverts to the original. This **bounds** the blocking time of H to at most one critical section of any lower-priority task. *(1 mark)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) (7 marks)** Consider three periodic tasks with the following parameters:

| Task | Period Tᵢ | WCET Cᵢ | Deadline Dᵢ |
|------|----------|---------|-------------|
| τ₁ | 4 | 1 | 4 |
| τ₂ | 6 | 2 | 6 |
| τ₃ | 10 | 2 | 10 |

Using **Rate Monotonic Scheduling**, determine whether the task set is schedulable. Apply both the **Liu–Layland utilization bound** and the **exact response time analysis**.

**(b) (7 marks)** Explain the **Inter-Process Communication (IPC) mechanisms** in RTOS with neat diagrams. Compare **semaphores and mutexes** with respect to ownership, priority inheritance, and use cases.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q(a):**

**Step 1 — Priority Assignment (RMS):**
Periods: $T_1=4 < T_2=6 < T_3=10$. Hence priorities: $P_1 > P_2 > P_3$.

**Step 2 — Liu–Layland Bound Test:**

$$
U = \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{2}{10}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= 0.250 + 0.333 + 0.200 \\
  &= 0.783
\end{aligned}
$$

For $n=3$: $U_{RMS}(3) = 3(2^{1/3}-1) = 0.7798$.

Since $U = 0.783 > 0.7798$, the **Liu–Layland bound is violated**. The bound test is **inconclusive**.

**Step 3 — Exact Response Time Test for τ₁:**

$$
R_1 = C_1 = 1 \le D_1 = 4 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 4 — Exact Response Time Test for τ₂:**
$R_2^{(0)} = C_2 = 2$

$$
R_2^{(1)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3
$$

$$
R_2^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3
$$

Converged. $R_2 = 3 \le D_2 = 6$ ✓

**Step 5 — Exact Response Time Test for τ₃:**
$R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 2$

$$
R_3^{(1)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 1 + 2 = 5
$$

$$
R_3^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 2 + 2 = 6
$$

$$
R_3^{(3)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 2 + 2 = 6
$$

Converged. $R_3 = 6 \le D_3 = 10$ ✓

**Conclusion:** Although the Liu–Layland bound is violated, the exact response time analysis shows all response times are within deadlines. **The task set is schedulable under RMS.**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q(b):**

**IPC Mechanisms in RTOS (Diagrams and Descriptions):**

1. **Semaphores** — A non-negative counter with atomic `wait()` (P) and `signal()` (V) operations. Used for resource counting and mutual exclusion.
2. **Mutex** — A binary lock with ownership; only the locking task can unlock. Supports priority inheritance in RTOS.
3. **Message Queues** — FIFO buffer for variable-length messages, enabling decoupled task-to-task data transfer.
4. **Mailboxes** — A single-slot message buffer; used for signaling (e.g., ISR-to-task).
5. **Shared Memory** — Common memory region accessed by multiple tasks; requires explicit synchronization.

**Comparison Table — Semaphore vs Mutex:**

| Property | Semaphore | Mutex |
|----------|-----------|-------|
| Ownership | No owner (any task can signal) | Owned by the locking task |
| Mutex Unlock Rule | Any task may signal/V | Only the owner may unlock |
| Priority Inheritance | Not by default | RTOS mutexes typically support PI |
| Use Case | Producer–consumer, resource pools | Critical sections, exclusive access |
| Type | Counting (≥0 integer) | Binary (locked/unlocked) |
| Suitable for ISR | Yes (give from ISR) | No (locking from ISR discouraged) |

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) (7 marks)** Explain the **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** scheduling algorithm. Compare EDF with Rate Monotonic Scheduling in terms of optimality, utilization bound, and runtime overhead.

**(b) (7 marks)** With a sequence diagram, explain **priority inversion** and discuss the **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)** as a solution. How does it differ from the Priority Inheritance Protocol?

---

#### **Model Solution for Q(a):**

**Earliest Deadline First (EDF):**
EDF is a **dynamic-priority, preemptive** scheduling algorithm. At every scheduling decision point (e.g., job release or job completion), the scheduler chooses the ready job with the **earliest absolute deadline**. Priority is thus *dynamic* — it changes from job to job.

**Properties of EDF:**
- **Optimality:** EDF is *optimal* on a uniprocessor. If any algorithm can feasibly schedule a task set, EDF can also schedule it.
- **Schedulability Bound (necessary and sufficient):**

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le 1
$$

- **Runtime Overhead:** The scheduler must maintain a sorted queue of ready jobs by deadline. Insertion/scheduling is $O(\log n)$ using a priority heap, which is more expensive than RMS's $O(1)$ fixed-priority selection.
- **Transient Overload Behavior:** When the system is overloaded ($U > 1$), EDF may cascade miss many deadlines; RMS degrades more gracefully.

**Comparison Table — EDF vs RMS:**

| Property | EDF | RMS |
|----------|-----|-----|
| Priority Type | Dynamic | Static (fixed) |
| Optimality | Optimal (uniprocessor) | Optimal *among fixed-priority* |
| Utilization Bound | 1.0 (exact) | $n(2^{1/n}-1)$ (sufficient) |
| Implementation | Moderate–High | Low |
| Runtime Cost | $O(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Transient Overload | Cascading misses | Predictable failure |
| Best Suited For | Soft RT, multimedia | Hard RT, safety-critical |
| KTU Significance | High (often paired with RMS) | Very High (board favorite) |

**Justification of Bound (Sketch):** The proof relies on the **processor demand criterion** of Baruah et al. A task set is EDF-schedulable iff for all $L \ge 0$:

$$
g(0, L) = \sum_{i: D_i \le L} \left\lceil \frac{L}{T_i} \right\rceil C_i \le L
$$

The bound $U \le 1$ follows because in the limit $L \to \infty$, the LHS grows as $L \cdot U$ and must be $\le L$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q(b):**

**Priority Inversion — Sequence Diagram:**

1. $\tau_H$ (high priority) is ready and waiting.
2. $\tau_L$ (low priority) acquires shared resource $R$.
3. $\tau_H$ arrives and preempts $\tau_L$; $\tau_H$ blocks on $R$.
4. $\tau_M$ (medium priority) arrives and preempts $\tau_L$.
5. $\tau_L$ cannot run; $\tau_H$ is *indirectly* blocked behind $\tau_M$.
6. $\tau_M$ finishes; $\tau_L$ resumes; $\tau_L$ releases $R$; $\tau_H$ runs.

**Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP):**
In PCP, each resource $R$ is assigned a **priority ceiling** $\lceil R \rceil$ equal to the **highest priority of any task that may access it**. A task may lock $R$ only if its *active priority* is **strictly higher** than the ceilings of all resources currently locked by *other* tasks. This prevents deadlocks and bounds blocking.

**Differences from Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):**

| Property | Priority Inheritance | Priority Ceiling |
|----------|---------------------|------------------|
| When does inheritance occur? | Only on actual blocking | On lock acquisition (prevention) |
| Deadlock Prevention | No (PIP does not prevent it) | Yes |
| Blocking Bound | ≤ 1 critical section | ≤ 1 critical section |
| Runtime Overhead | Lower | Higher (requires ceiling tracking) |
| Run-time decision | Yes (when blocking occurs) | Pre-flight check at lock time |
| Implementation Complexity | Simpler | More complex |
| KTU Examiner Focus | High | Very High (often confused) |

**Key Difference (KTU Pitfall):** PIP reacts to blocking *after* it occurs. PCP *prevents* certain blocking patterns by its pre-lock check, thus eliminating transitive/chain blocking.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in RTOS Theory Papers:**
> 1. **Confusing Priority Inheritance with Priority Ceiling:** Students often write "PI prevents deadlock" — it does *not*. PCP does. (Lose 2 marks)
> 2. **Treating the Liu–Layland bound as necessary:** It is *sufficient*, not necessary. Always state the qualifier. (Lose 1 mark)
> 3. **Forgetting the ceiling function $\lceil \cdot \rceil$** in the response time equation — examiners deduct 1 mark per instance.
> 4. **Stopping iteration at $R_i^{(1)}$** without checking convergence. The iteration must run until $R^{(k+1)} = R^{(k)}$ OR $R^{(k+1)} > D_i$ (deadline miss).
> 5. **Using "semaphore" and "mutex" interchangeably.** Mutex has ownership; semaphore does not. (Lose 1 mark)
> 6. **Forgetting to compute total utilization first** before applying the Liu–Layland bound.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3 Concepts:**

- [x] **RMS Priority Rule:** Shorter period → Higher priority. Static, fixed.
- [x] **Liu–Layland Bound:** $U_{RMS}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$. Asymptote $\to \ln 2 \approx 0.693$. **Sufficient only.**
- [x] **Exact Response Time Test:** Iterate $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i/T_j \rceil C_j$ until convergence or deadline miss.
- [x] **EDF Optimality:** Uniprocessor-optimal. Bound $U \le 1.0$ is necessary AND sufficient.
- [x] **Priority Inversion = Indirect blocking.** Solutions: PI, PCP, SRP.
- [x] **PIP = Reactive.** **PCP = Preventive (deadlock-free).** Critical distinction for KTU.
- [x] **Mutex has ownership**; **semaphore does not**. Mutex supports PI in RTOS.
- [x] **IPC Quintet:** Semaphores, Mutex, Message Queues, Mailboxes, Shared Memory.
- [x] **Hard RT** = deadline miss = failure. **Soft RT** = deadline miss = degraded quality.
- [x] **Hyperperiod** = $\text{lcm}(T_1, \ldots, T_n)$ — major cycle of all periodic tasks.
- [x] **Critical instant** = all tasks released simultaneously → worst-case scheduling scenario.
- [x] **Mars Pathfinder (1997)** = textbook case of unbounded priority inversion causing system resets.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT741 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS), **Scheduling** is the mechanism by which the kernel determines the order in which multiple competing tasks (jobs) are dispatched to the CPU for execution, subject to timing constraints such as **deadlines**, **periods**, and **release times**. A real-time scheduler is said to be *valid* if it produces a feasible schedule in which every job completes before its deadline.

According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, scheduling in RTOS is classified along three orthogonal axes:
1. **Static vs Dynamic** — whether priorities are assigned at compile-time or runtime.
2. **Preemptive vs Non-preemptive** — whether a running task can be involuntarily suspended.
3. **Offline (Clock-driven) vs Online (Event-driven)** — whether the schedule is computed prior to execution or in response to events.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Scheduling is the *core differentiating feature* of an RTOS compared to a GPOS. A GPOS aims to maximize **throughput**; an RTOS aims to guarantee **timeliness** (meeting deadlines) even at the cost of throughput.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are the **air traffic controller at a busy airport** with only one runway. Several flights (tasks) are queued up, each with a *latest landing time* (deadline). Some flights are international (high-priority, periodic, must land on schedule), and some are domestic (lower priority, but still time-sensitive). Your job is to decide the *landing order* such that:
- No plane crashes (deadline miss / catastrophic system failure)
- Fuel is not wasted waiting (bounded response time)
- Runway is never idle when a critical plane is waiting (no missed opportunity)

You follow a *schedule* (e.g., always land the plane with the earliest "must-land-by" time first) — that rule is your **scheduling algorithm**. The runway is your **CPU**. The planes are your **real-time tasks**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In RTOS, *correctness* is not just logical — it is *temporal*. A wrong answer produced too late is as bad as a wrong answer.

### 1.3 Core Terminology (Mandatory for KTU Board Exams)

| Term | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Job (Task Instance)** | A unit of work scheduled and executed by the system. |
| **Task** | A set of related jobs, characterized by temporal parameters. |
| **Release Time ($r_i$)** | The instant a job becomes available for execution. |
| **Absolute Deadline ($d_i$)** | The instant by which the job must complete. |
| **Relative Deadline ($D_i$)** | $D_i = d_i - r_i$. |
| **Period ($T_i$)** | The interval between successive releases of a periodic task. |
| **Execution Time ($e_i$ or $C_i$)** | Worst-case CPU time required to complete the job. |
| **Response Time ($R_i$)** | $R_i = f_i - r_i$ where $f_i$ is finish time. |
| **Laxity (Slack)** | $L_i = D_i - e_i$ — the maximum time a job can be delayed. |
| **Jitter** | Variation in the response time of successive job instances. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Real-Time Tasks

$$\text{Tasks} = \begin{cases} \text{Periodic} & : \text{Released at regular intervals } T_i \\ \text{Aperiodic} & : \text{Released at irregular, unpredictable times} \\ \text{Sporadic} & : \text{Aperiodic with a hard minimum inter-arrival time} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A **hard real-time system** treats any deadline miss as a *system failure* (e.g., anti-lock braking). A **soft real-time system** allows deadline misses with degraded QoS (e.g., video streaming).

### 2.2 Classification of Scheduling Algorithms

- **Clock-driven (Table-driven / Offline):** Schedule is precomputed at design time using parameters like periods. Best for purely periodic, static systems. Example: Cyclic Executive.
- **Event-driven (Priority-driven / Online):** Decisions made at runtime based on task states. Two main classes:
  - **Fixed-Priority:** Priority assigned once (e.g., at task creation) and never changes. Example: **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)**.
  - **Dynamic-Priority:** Priority may change at runtime based on current state. Example: **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)**.
- **Hybrid:** Combines both, e.g., Slack stealing.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize these equations verbatim — they appear in 14-mark problems frequently.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Liu \& Layland Utilization Bound (RMS)} &: \quad U(n) = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) \\
\text{Critical Utilization (EDF)} &: \quad U_{EDF} = 1 \quad (\text{necessary and sufficient for uniprocessor}) \\
\text{Response Time} &: \quad R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j \\
\text{Deadline Monotonic Priority} &: \quad \text{Lower } D_i \Rightarrow \text{Higher priority} \\
\text{Laxity} &: \quad L_i(t) = D_i - t - e_i^{\text{remaining}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Utilization Table (KTU-Favorite Numerical Values):**

| n (Tasks) | RMS Bound $U(n) = n(2^{1/n}-1)$ | Decimal |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | $1(2-1)$ | **1.0000** |
| 2 | $2(\sqrt{2}-1)$ | **0.8284** |
| 3 | $3(2^{1/3}-1)$ | **0.7798** |
| 4 | $4(2^{1/4}-1)$ | **0.7568** |
| ∞ | $\ln 2$ | **0.6931** |

> [!WARNING]
> The Liu-Layland bound is **sufficient but not necessary**. A task set with $U > U(n)$ can still be schedulable under RMS — you must perform **Response Time Analysis (RTA)** for exact schedulability.

### 2.4 The 'Why' Behind Each Algorithm

- **RMS** — assigns priorities inversely proportional to periods. Mathematically optimal fixed-priority policy (proven by Liu & Layland, 1973). Used in avionics, automotive ECUs.
- **EDF** — dynamic, always runs the job closest to its deadline. Optimal for uniprocessors in dynamic-priority class.
- **LLF (Least Laxity First)** — runs the job with the smallest slack. Ties broken arbitrarily. Optimal in the same class as EDF but more complex.
- **DMS (Deadline Monotonic)** — generalization of RMS where $D_i \le T_i$ is not required. Priority inversely proportional to relative deadline.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** NASA CFS (Core Flight System) uses table-driven scheduling for deterministic command sequences.
- **Automotive (AUTOSAR):** RMS and DMS dominate ECU scheduling.
- **Industrial Automation:** PLCs use cyclic executives with precomputed schedules.
- **Robotics:** ROS 2 uses a hybrid clock-driven + event-driven approach.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Liu & Layland RMS Utilization Bound

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **14-mark KTU favorite**. Understand each step — you may be asked to reproduce or extend it.

**Setup:** Consider $n$ periodic tasks with priorities ordered $P_1 > P_2 > \dots > P_n$ (i.e., $T_1 < T_2 < \dots < T_n$). The worst-case release pattern that maximally stresses task $\tau_n$ is when *all higher-priority tasks release simultaneously with $\tau_n$* at $t=0$.

The critical instant for $\tau_n$ is the time when its response time is maximized.

**Step 1 — Time Demand Function:**

The processor demand placed on $\tau_n$ over the interval $[0, t]$ is:

$$W_n(t) = C_n + \sum_{j=1}^{n-1} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$

**Step 2 — Schedulability Condition:**

A schedule is feasible iff there exists some $t \in [0, D_n]$ such that:

$$W_n(t) \le t$$

The smallest such $t$ is the worst-case response time $R_n$.

**Step 3 — Derivation of the Utilization Bound (Intuitive Sketch):**

For RMS on $n$ tasks, the worst-case scenario occurs when $T_1 = T_2 = \dots = T_{n-1}$ and $T_n \to T_{n-1}^+$ (all tasks have nearly equal periods). Under this tightest packing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{RMS}(n) &= \lim_{T_n \to T_{n-1}} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{C_j}{T_j} \\
&= n \cdot C / T \quad \text{where } C/T \text{ is the common ratio} \\
\text{From the critical instant inequality:} \quad C &\le T \left( 1 - \sum_{j=1}^{n-1} C/T \right) \\
\Rightarrow \frac{C}{T} \cdot n &\le 1 \quad \text{(after normalization)} \\
\Rightarrow U_{RMS}(n) &\le n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The closed form $n(2^{1/n}-1)$ comes from the recurrence $U(n) = 1 + U(n-1) \cdot \frac{1}{2^{1/(n-1)}}$, which when expanded yields the geometric series summing to $2^{1/n} - 1$.

**Step 4 — Asymptotic Limit:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty} n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) &= \lim_{n \to \infty} n \left( e^{\ln 2 / n} - 1 \right) \\
&= \lim_{n \to \infty} n \left( \frac{\ln 2}{n} + O(n^{-2}) \right) \\
&= \ln 2 \approx 0.6931
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, RMS can guarantee schedulability only up to ~69.3% of CPU utilization asymptotically.

### 3.2 Worked Example: RMS Schedulability Test

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Standard 14-Mark Pattern** — you will see this exact structure.

**Task Set:**

| Task $\tau_i$ | $T_i$ (ms) | $C_i$ (ms) | $D_i$ (ms) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $\tau_1$ | 4 | 1 | 4 |
| $\tau_2$ | 6 | 2 | 6 |
| $\tau_3$ | 10 | 2 | 10 |

**Step 1 — Compute Utilization:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{2}{10} \\
&= 0.2500 + 0.3333 + 0.2000 = 0.7833
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Liu-Layland Bound for $n=3$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U(3) &= 3(2^{1/3} - 1) = 3(1.2599 - 1) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7798
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare:**

$$U = 0.7833 > U(3) = 0.7798$$

The utilization **exceeds** the bound, so we **cannot conclude** schedulability from the bound alone. We must perform **Response Time Analysis (RTA)**.

**Step 4 — Response Time Analysis (Iterative):**

Compute $R_n$ by iterating $R_n^{(k+1)} = C_n + \sum_{j < n} \left\lceil R_n^{(k)} / T_j \right\rceil \cdot C_j$ until convergence.

For $\tau_3$ ($C_3=2, T_1=4, T_2=6, C_1=1, C_2=2$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(0)} &= 2 \\
R_3^{(1)} &= 2 + \lceil 2/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 2/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 1 + 2 = 5 \\
R_3^{(2)} &= 2 + \lceil 5/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 5/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 2 + 2 = 6 \\
R_3^{(3)} &= 2 + \lceil 6/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 6/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 2 + 2 = 6 \quad (\text{converged})
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $R_3 = 6 \le D_3 = 10$, the task is **schedulable**. Repeat for $\tau_2$ and $\tau_1$ to complete the analysis.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Viva + Theory Favorite)

| Criterion | RMS | EDF | LLF | Cyclic Executive |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Priority Type | Fixed (Static) | Dynamic | Dynamic | Static Table |
| Optimality (Uniprocessor) | Optimal fixed-priority | Optimal dynamic | Optimal dynamic | N/A |
| Schedulability Test | $U \le n(2^{1/n}-1)$ or RTA | $U \le 1$ | $U \le 1$ | Frame size constraint |
| Implementation Complexity | Low | Medium | High | Very Low |
| Overhead at Runtime | Low | Medium | High | None |
| Transient Behavior | Stable | Can exhibit domino effect | Can thrash | Fully deterministic |
| $U$ Limit (asymptotic) | **69.3%** | **100%** | **100%** | Depends on frame |
| Used In | VxWorks, FreeRTOS | RT-Python, Mars Pathfinder fix | Theoretical | AUTOSAR OS, OSEK |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of an RTOS Scheduler

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task Release Event] --> B[Task Arrival Queue]
    B --> C{Scheduler Decision Engine}
    C --> D[Priority Computation Module]
    C --> E[Schedulability Test Module]
    D --> F[Ready Queue Sorted by Priority]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Preemption Check}
    G -->|Yes| H[Save Context of Running Task]
    H --> I[Load Context of Highest-Priority Task]
    G -->|No| J[Continue Current Task]
    I --> K[CPU Execution]
    J --> K
    K --> L{Task Complete?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Update Task State to Finished]
    L -->|No| N{Tick Interrupt?}
    N -->|Yes| C
    N -->|No| K
    M --> O[Deadline Met?]
    O -->|Yes| P[Normal Termination]
    O -->|No| Q[Deadline Miss Handler / Error]
```

### 4.2 Scheduling Timeline — RMS Example

```mermaid
gantt
    title Rate Monotonic Schedule (3 Tasks)
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  
    section tau1 (T=4, C=1)
    tau1_job1 :a1, 0, 1
    tau1_job2 :a2, 4, 1
    tau1_job3 :a3, 8, 1
    section tau2 (T=6, C=2)
    tau2_job1 :b1, 1, 2
    tau2_job2 :b2, 7, 2
    section tau3 (T=10, C=2)
    tau3_job1 :c1, 3, 2
    tau3_job2 :c2, 9, 2
```

### 4.3 Priority Inversion Scenario (Classic KTU Question)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant H as Task H (High Priority)
    participant M as Task M (Medium Priority)
    participant L as Task L (Low Priority)
    Note over L: Acquires shared Lock X
    L->>L: Runs normally
    Note over H: H becomes ready, preempts L
    H->>L: H requests Lock X (BLOCKED)
    Note over M: M becomes ready, preempts L
    M->>M: Runs to completion
    Note over L: L finishes, releases Lock X
    Note over H: H finally acquires Lock X and runs
    Note over H,M,L: PRIORITY INVERSION: H waited for M (lower priority)
```

### 4.4 Comparison of Scheduling Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[RTOS Scheduling] --> B[Clock Driven]
    A --> C[Event Driven]
    A --> D[Hybrid]
    B --> B1[Cyclic Executive]
    B --> B2[Table Driven]
    C --> E[Fixed Priority]
    C --> F[Dynamic Priority]
    E --> E1[RMS]
    E --> E2[DMS]
    F --> F1[EDF]
    F --> F2[LLF]
    D --> D1[Slack Stealing]
    D --> D2[Servers Polling]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) and Earliest Deadline First (EDF).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | RMS | EDF |
|---|---|---|
| Priority Assignment | Static, inversely proportional to period | Dynamic, based on absolute deadline |
| Optimality | Optimal in fixed-priority class | Optimal in dynamic-priority class |
| Schedulability Bound | $U(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ ≈ 69% (asymptotic) | $U \le 1$ (100%) |
| Implementation | Simple, low overhead | Higher overhead due to runtime priority updates |
| Predictability | High (stable) | Can suffer from domino effect under overload |

(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for key contrast.)

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`
**Define Laxity. How is it used in scheduling?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Laxity** (or Slack) of a job at time $t$ is defined as:

$$L(t) = D - t - e_{\text{remaining}}$$

where $D$ is the absolute deadline, $t$ is the current time, and $e_{\text{remaining}}$ is the remaining execution time.

(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for interpretation, 1 mark for usage.)

**Interpretation:** It represents the maximum time the scheduler can defer the job without missing its deadline.
**Usage:** In **Least Laxity First (LLF)** scheduling, the job with the *smallest* laxity is dispatched first. A job with zero or negative laxity has already missed or is about to miss its deadline.

---

### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 scheme, Part B offers an internal choice. Attempt **ONE** of the two 14-mark questions below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the Rate Monotonic Scheduling algorithm. State and prove the Liu & Layland utilization bound. (7 marks)

**(b)** Consider three periodic tasks with the following parameters: $\tau_1: T_1=5, C_1=2$; $\tau_2: T_2=10, C_2=3$; $\tau_3: T_3=15, C_3=3$. Assume deadlines equal periods. (7 marks)
- (i) Apply the Liu-Layland utilization bound test.
- (ii) If the bound is not sufficient, perform exact Response Time Analysis.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) RMS Explanation (7 marks):**

Rate Monotonic Scheduling is a **static, fixed-priority, preemptive** scheduling algorithm for periodic real-time tasks. The fundamental rule is:

> **The task with the shorter period gets the higher priority.**

Mathematically, if $T_i < T_j$, then $\text{Priority}(\tau_i) > \text{Priority}(\tau_j)$.

**Liu & Layland Bound Proof (Sketch):**

- Define the critical instant for task $\tau_n$ as the time instant when all higher-priority tasks release simultaneously with $\tau_n$ at $t=0$. At this instant, the response time of $\tau_n$ is maximized.
- The processor demand on $\tau_n$ in interval $[0,t]$ is:

$$W_n(t) = C_n + \sum_{j=1}^{n-1} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

- For $\tau_n$ to be schedulable, there must exist $t \in [0, D_n]$ with $W_n(t) \le t$.
- The tightest case is when all tasks have equal periods $T$ and equal execution times. In the limit $T_n \to T_{n-1}$, we obtain:

$$U_{RMS}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$$

- As $n \to \infty$, this approaches $\ln 2 \approx 0.6931$.

This bound is **sufficient but not necessary**: a task set with $U > U(n)$ may still be schedulable, requiring exact analysis.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Liu-Layland Test:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{2}{5} + \frac{3}{10} + \frac{3}{15} \\
&= 0.400 + 0.300 + 0.200 = 0.900
\end{aligned}
$$

For $n=3$: $U(3) = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) = 3(0.2599) = 0.7798$.

Since $U = 0.900 > 0.7798$, the bound test is **inconclusive**. We must proceed to RTA.

**(ii) Response Time Analysis:**

Priorities: $P_1 > P_2 > P_3$ (since $T_1 < T_2 < T_3$).

**For $\tau_3$ ($C_3=3$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(0)} &= 3 \\
R_3^{(1)} &= 3 + \lceil 3/5 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 3/10 \rceil \cdot 3 = 3 + 2 + 3 = 8 \\
R_3^{(2)} &= 3 + \lceil 8/5 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 8/10 \rceil \cdot 3 = 3 + 4 + 3 = 10 \\
R_3^{(3)} &= 3 + \lceil 10/5 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 10/10 \rceil \cdot 3 = 3 + 4 + 3 = 10 \quad \text{(converged)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$R_3 = 10 \le D_3 = 15$ ✓ **Schedulable.**

**For $\tau_2$ ($C_2=3$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_2^{(0)} &= 3 \\
R_2^{(1)} &= 3 + \lceil 3/5 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 = 5 \\
R_2^{(2)} &= 3 + \lceil 5/5 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 = 5 \quad \text{(converged)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$R_2 = 5 \le D_2 = 10$ ✓ **Schedulable.**

**For $\tau_1$ ($C_1=2$):** $R_1 = C_1 = 2 \le D_1 = 5$ ✓ **Schedulable.**

**Conclusion:** The task set is schedulable under RMS, despite the utilization bound being inconclusive.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the problem of **priority inversion** in real-time systems. Describe how **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** solves it. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare and contrast **clock-driven**, **event-driven**, and **hybrid scheduling** approaches in RTOS. Mention the conditions under which each is most suitable. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Priority Inversion & PIP (7 marks):**

**Problem Definition:**
Priority inversion occurs when a higher-priority task is *indirectly* preempted by a lower-priority task, effectively inverting the relative priorities of the two tasks. The classic scenario:

- Task H (high) and Task L (low) share a resource R.
- L acquires R, then H becomes ready and preempts L.
- H attempts to acquire R, but R is held by L → H blocks.
- Task M (medium priority) becomes ready and preempts L (since L is runnable but blocked on H's release of R).
- M runs to completion. L finally gets CPU, finishes, releases R.
- H finally runs. The net effect: H waited for M, even though $P_H > P_M$.

**Unbounded priority inversion** can occur if there are arbitrarily many medium-priority tasks, leading to potentially unbounded blocking of H.

**Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) Solution:**
When a task $\tau_H$ blocks on a resource held by $\tau_L$, the protocol **dynamically raises the priority of $\tau_L$ to $P_H$** for the duration of the resource hold. This prevents medium-priority tasks from preempting $\tau_L$, effectively *inverting* the inheritance chain.

Properties:
- Prevents unbounded inversion.
- Does not prevent deadlocks (only PCP and SRP do that).
- Adds runtime overhead for priority boosts.

**(b) Comparison of Scheduling Approaches (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Clock-Driven | Event-Driven | Hybrid |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Schedule Generation** | Offline, precomputed | Online, dynamic | Both |
| **Decision Time** | At major/minor frame boundaries | On every event (release, completion) | Periodic + on-demand |
| **Predictability** | Highest (fully deterministic) | Lower (depends on event patterns) | Moderate |
| **Overhead** | Minimal at runtime | Higher (priority changes) | Moderate |
| **Task Types** | Pure periodic | Periodic + aperiodic + sporadic | Mixed workload |
| **Example** | Cyclic Executive | RMS, EDF | Slack Stealing, Polling Servers |

**Suitability Conditions:**
- **Clock-driven:** When all tasks are periodic with known parameters and the system is static (e.g., flight control loops).
- **Event-driven:** When tasks are aperiodic/sporadic with unpredictable arrivals (e.g., industrial interrupts).
- **Hybrid:** When a mix of periodic sensor sampling and bursty event handling is required (e.g., multimedia systems).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Marks Are Lost:**
> 1. **Confusing "sufficient" with "necessary"** for the Liu-Layland bound — it is *only sufficient*. If $U > U(n)$, you cannot declare "not schedulable" without performing RTA.
> 2. **Forgetting to use $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ (ceiling function)** in RTA iterations — this is a 1–2 mark deduction per error.
> 3. **Mis-defining Laxity** as $D - r$ instead of $D - t - e_{\text{remaining}}$. The current time $t$ matters — laxity is dynamic.
> 4. **Priority Inversion vs Deadlock:** Students often state PIP "prevents deadlock." PIP does **not** prevent deadlock — only PCP, SRP, or stack resource policy do.
> 5. **Missing units** in numerical answers — always attach ms/μs/etc. in the final answer.
> 6. **Not drawing the timeline diagram** for the RMS scheduling example — examiners often allocate 1–2 marks purely for the visual.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Final-Exam Night Review)**

- [ ] **Task model:** Periodic (T, C, D), Aperiodic, Sporadic — know the difference cold.
- [ ] **Three axes of classification:** Static/Dynamic, Preemptive/Non-preemptive, Offline/Online.
- [ ] **RMS formula:** $U(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$; remember values for n = 1, 2, 3, 4, ∞.
- [ ] **Liu-Layland bound is sufficient, NOT necessary** — always do RTA when $U > U(n)$.
- [ ] **RTA recursion:** $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i^{(k)}/T_j \rceil \cdot C_j$. Iterate until $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$.
- [ ] **EDF utilization bound:** $U \le 1$ is both necessary and sufficient on a uniprocessor.
- [ ] **Laxity definition:** $L(t) = D - t - e_{\text{remaining}}$ — note the dynamic $t$.
- [ ] **Priority Inversion:** A *runtime* anomaly, not a design flaw. Solved (bounded) by PIP.
- [ ] **PIP vs PCP:** PIP only bounds inversion; PCP prevents deadlock too.
- [ ] **Clock-driven vs Event-driven:** Table-driven vs runtime decisions. Trade-off is predictability vs flexibility.
- [ ] **Numerical answer format:** Show every iteration of RTA; never skip "$\lceil \cdot \rceil$" or units.
- [ ] **Asymptotic RMS limit:** $\ln 2 \approx 0.6931$ — this is a frequent "fill in the blank" item.
- [ ] **Common exam traps:** Static priority ≠ Static schedule; EDF is dynamic priority, not dynamic scheduling algorithm (the algorithm class is online/priority-driven).
- [ ] **Real-world tags:** VxWorks (RMS), Mars Pathfinder (used EDF, but bug caused resets), FreeRTOS (configurable, often RMS-based).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CPU Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT741 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** CPU Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**CPU Scheduling** in the context of Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS) is defined as the mechanism by which the kernel's scheduler determines which ready-to-execute task/process gets allocated to the CPU at any given instant, while simultaneously ensuring that all **hard real-time deadlines** are met and timing constraints of the workload are strictly satisfied.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PECPT741 – Module 3), CPU scheduling in RTOS is treated as a **deterministic, priority-driven allocation problem** rather than a fairness-driven one (as in general-purpose OS like Windows/Linux). The fundamental objective is to guarantee **bounded response time**, **predictable latency**, and **deadline adherence** under all system load conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In RTOS, the goal of CPU scheduling is *not* to maximize throughput or minimize average waiting time, but to **guarantee that no task misses its deadline**. This single philosophical shift differentiates RTOS scheduling from general-purpose OS scheduling.

The primary inputs to any RTOS CPU scheduler are:

1. **Task arrival time** ($a_i$) – When the task becomes ready.
2. **Computation time / Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)** ($C_i$) – Maximum CPU time the task may consume.
3. **Period / Relative deadline** ($T_i$ or $D_i$) – How frequently the task recurs.
4. **Priority** ($P_i$) – Static (assigned at design time) or Dynamic (computed at runtime).

The scheduler's output is a **preemptive or non-preemptive dispatch decision** at each scheduling event (release, completion, or interrupt).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **air traffic controller at a busy airport**. Multiple flights (tasks) are queued for landing (CPU). Each flight has:
- A **scheduled arrival time** (release time)
- An **estimated landing duration** (WCET)
- A **fuel reserve / strict landing window** (deadline)

A *commercial OS* is like a controller who minimizes **average wait time** for all planes — a small private jet might be kept circling while a large commercial jet lands first, even if the small jet is running low on fuel.

An *RTOS controller*, however, **prioritizes the plane closest to running out of fuel** (closest deadline) — because letting a plane crash is a *catastrophic, unrecoverable failure*. Throughput is irrelevant; safety (deadline adherence) is everything.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** RTOS scheduling is essentially a **"deadline-protection" system**, not a "throughput-optimization" system. Every scheduling decision is evaluated against the question: *"Will this cause any task to miss its deadline?"*

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

The following metrics are **officially evaluated** in KTU university examinations for this module:

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Worst-Case Execution Time | $C_i$ | Time units (ms/µs) | Maximum CPU burst of task $i$ |
| Period | $T_i$ | Time units | Inter-arrival time of task $i$ |
| Relative Deadline | $D_i$ | Time units | Deadline measured from release |
| Absolute Deadline | $d_i$ | Time units | $a_i + D_i$ |
| Response Time | $R_i$ | Time units | Finish time minus release time |
| CPU Utilization | $U$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | Fraction of CPU used |
| Schedulable Utilization Bound | $U_{bound}$ | Dimensionless | Theoretical guarantee threshold |
| Number of Tasks | $n$ | Count | Tasks in the system |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Liu & Layland's Constant (1973):** The famous schedulability bound $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$ is a **derived mathematical constant** specifically for Rate Monotonic Scheduling. It is **not arbitrary** and should be memorized along with its meaning.
>
> $$\ln 2 \approx 0.6931...$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of RTOS CPU Scheduling Algorithms

CPU scheduling algorithms in RTOS are classified along **two orthogonal axes**:

### Axis 1: Preemption Behavior
- **Non-Preemptive Scheduling:** Once a task starts executing on the CPU, it runs to completion (or until it voluntarily yields via a blocking system call). Simple to implement, but risky for hard real-time systems.
- **Preemptive Scheduling:** The currently running task can be **interrupted and displaced** if a higher-priority task becomes ready. Mandatory for hard real-time guarantees.

### Axis 2: Priority Assignment Strategy
- **Static (Fixed) Priority:** Priority is assigned at **design/compile time** and never changes. Example: Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS).
- **Dynamic Priority:** Priority is **recomputed at runtime** based on current parameters (e.g., proximity to deadline). Example: Earliest Deadline First (EDF).

## 2.2 The Four Canonical RTOS Scheduling Algorithms

### A. Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) — Liu & Layland, 1973
- **Static priority** assignment.
- **Shorter period ⇒ Higher priority.**
- **Preemptive** by default.
- **Optimal** among all static-priority algorithms (no other static scheme can schedule a task set that RMS cannot).

### B. Earliest Deadline First (EDF) — Jackson's Rule, 1955
- **Dynamic priority** assignment.
- At every scheduling decision point, the task whose **absolute deadline is closest** is chosen.
- **Optimal** among all scheduling algorithms (both static and dynamic) on a single processor, **provided no task ever exceeds its WCET**.

### C. Deadline Monotonic Scheduling (DMS)
- Static priority based on **deadline**, not period.
- Used when $D_i < T_i$ (constrained deadlines).
- Priority ordering: **Shorter $D_i$ ⇒ Higher priority.**

### D. Least Laxity First (LLF)
- Dynamic priority based on **laxity** (slack time).
- **Laxity** = (Deadline) − (Current Time) − (Remaining Computation).
- The task with the **smallest laxity** is scheduled next.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table contains **every formula a KTU student must know** for CPU scheduling problems in Module 3.

| # | Formula | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Total CPU utilization | Check load before scheduling |
| 2 | $U_{RMS}(n) = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Liu-Layland bound for RMS on $n$ tasks | Schedulability sufficient test |
| 3 | $\lim_{n \to \infty} U_{RMS}(n) = \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ | Asymptotic RMS bound | Large task sets |
| 4 | $U_{EDF} = 1$ | EDF schedulability bound (exact) | Full utilization possible |
| 5 | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | Response time equation (fixed-priority) | Exact schedulability test |
| 6 | $R_i \leq D_i$ | Schedulability condition | Task $i$ meets deadline |
| 7 | $L_i(t) = D_i - t - C_i^{rem}$ | Laxity of task $i$ at time $t$ | LLF scheduling |
| 8 | $P_i = \text{rank by } T_i \text{ ascending}$ | RMS priority assignment | Static priority computation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The response time equation (Formula 5) is an **iterative fixed-point equation**, not a closed-form solution. Students are expected to solve it by **iteration starting from $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$** until convergence ($R_i^{(k)} = R_i^{(k-1)}$) or until $R_i > D_i$ (failure).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

CPU scheduling theory directly governs the design of:

- **Aerospace flight control systems** (e.g., NASA CFS, SpaceX Dragon — uses RMS variants).
- **Automotive ECUs** (Engine Control Unit, ABS braking — uses OSEK/VDX OS with fixed-priority preemptive scheduling).
- **Medical devices** (Pacemakers, infusion pumps — hard real-time, EDF-based).
- **Industrial robotics** (PLC controllers, motion control — RMS-based).
- **Smartphone audio/video pipelines** (Android AudioFlinger uses priority inheritance with RMS).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Context:** AUTOSAR OS (used by BMW, Bosch, Continental) implements a **fixed-priority preemptive scheduler** based on the OSEK/VDX standard — directly descended from Liu & Layland's RMS theory.

## 2.5 Why RMS Works: Intuitive Logic

RMS works because **high-frequency tasks need to run more often, hence need faster reaction time**. Giving them higher priority minimizes the time they wait for the CPU, reducing their worst-case response time. Mathematically, this minimizes the worst-case response time for any task set — a result formally proven by Liu & Layland using the *critical instant theorem*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Liu & Layland's RMS Utilization Bound

We wish to find the **maximum possible total utilization $U_{max}(n)$** such that **any** set of $n$ tasks with $U \leq U_{max}(n)$ is guaranteed to be schedulable under RMS on a single processor.

### Step 1: Define the Worst-Case Scenario

A task $\tau_i$ suffers its **longest response time** when **all higher-priority tasks release simultaneously with it** and re-release at their maximum rate. This moment is called the **critical instant** (proven by Liu & Layland).

At the critical instant, task $\tau_i$ must complete its execution while being preempted by all $hp(i)$ higher-priority tasks.

### Step 2: Set Up the Response Time Inequality

The response time of $\tau_i$ must satisfy:

$$
R_i \leq C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

For the bound derivation, we **drop the ceiling** (this gives an upper bound on the RHS, which is conservative for the bound itself):

$$
R_i \leq C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \frac{R_i}{T_j} C_j
$$

### Step 3: Solve for $R_i$

Factor out $R_i$ on the right-hand side:

$$
R_i \leq C_i + R_i \cdot \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \frac{C_j}{T_j}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
R_i - R_i \cdot \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \frac{C_j}{T_j} \leq C_i
$$

$$
R_i \left( 1 - \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \frac{C_j}{T_j} \right) \leq C_i
$$

$$
R_i \leq \frac{C_i}{1 - \sum_{j \in hp(i)} U_j}
$$

### Step 4: Apply the Deadline Condition

The schedulability condition requires $R_i \leq T_i$ (for implicit deadlines where $D_i = T_i$):

$$
\frac{C_i}{1 - \sum_{j \in hp(i)} U_j} \leq T_i
$$

$$
C_i \leq T_i \left( 1 - \sum_{j \in hp(i)} U_j \right)
$$

$$
\frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq 1 - \sum_{j \in hp(i)} U_j
$$

$$
U_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} U_j \leq 1
$$

### Step 5: Find the Worst-Case Sum (Liu-Layland Lemma)

The hardest case is when periods are **harmonically related**, specifically:

$$
T_1 < T_2 < T_3 < \dots < T_n \quad \text{with} \quad T_{k+1} < 2 T_k
$$

In the worst case, the utilization that just satisfies schedulability for all $n$ tasks is the solution to:

$$
\prod_{i=1}^{n} \left( \frac{C_i}{T_i} \right) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left( 2^{1/i} - 1 \right) \cdot \frac{1}{2^{1/i}}
$$

Following the standard Liu-Layland derivation, the result is:

$$
U_{bound}(n) = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)
$$

### Step 6: The Asymptotic Limit

As $n \to \infty$:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{2^{1/n} - 1}{1/n}
$$

Using L'Hôpital's rule, let $x = 1/n$, so $x \to 0$:

$$
\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{2^{x} - 1}{x} = \ln 2
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\lim_{n \to \infty} U_{bound}(n) = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** The derivation above is the **canonical board-exam derivation**. Students are expected to know the critical instant theorem, the response time equation, and the asymptotic $\ln 2$ result. Memorize the formula, understand the meaning, and you will get full marks.

## 3.2 Worked Example: RMS Schedulability Test

**Problem:** Three tasks with the following parameters are given. Determine if they are schedulable under RMS.

| Task | $C_i$ | $T_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 1 | 4 |
| $\tau_2$ | 2 | 6 |
| $\tau_3$ | 3 | 10 |

### Step 1: Priority Assignment (RMS)

Sort by increasing $T_i$:

- $\tau_1$: $T_1 = 4$ → **Highest priority**
- $\tau_2$: $T_2 = 6$ → **Medium priority**
- $\tau_3$: $T_3 = 10$ → **Lowest priority**

### Step 2: Compute Total Utilization

$$
U = \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{10}
$$

$$
U = 0.2500 + 0.3333 + 0.3000 = 0.8833
$$

### Step 3: Apply Liu-Layland Bound for $n = 3$

$$
U_{bound}(3) = 3 \cdot \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7798
$$

### Step 4: Compare

$$
U = 0.8833 \quad \text{vs} \quad U_{bound}(3) = 0.7798
$$

Since $U > U_{bound}$, the **sufficient test is inconclusive**. We must use the **exact response time test**.

### Step 5: Exact Response Time Test

**For $\tau_1$:** No higher-priority tasks exist.

$$
R_1 = C_1 = 1 \leq T_1 = 4 \quad \checkmark
$$

**For $\tau_2$:** Higher-priority task is $\tau_1$.

Iteration 0: $R_2^{(0)} = C_2 = 2$

Iteration 1:
$$
R_2^{(1)} = C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2^{(0)}}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 3
$$

Iteration 2:
$$
R_2^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 3
$$

Converged. $R_2 = 3 \leq T_2 = 6$ ✓

**For $\tau_3$:** Higher-priority tasks are $\tau_1, \tau_2$.

Iteration 0: $R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 3$

Iteration 1:
$$
R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 + 1 \cdot 2 = 6
$$

Iteration 2:
$$
R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7
$$

Iteration 3:
$$
R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7
$$

Converged. $R_3 = 7 \leq T_3 = 10$ ✓

### Step 6: Conclusion

All three tasks meet their deadlines, so the system **IS schedulable** under RMS, even though the sufficient Liu-Layland test failed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** A common KTU question asks "Is the system schedulable?" and the naive answer is "No, because $U > U_{bound}$." The correct answer requires the **exact response time test** as demonstrated above. **Always attempt the exact test** before concluding.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Comparison

| Property | RMS | EDF | DMS | LLF |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Priority Type | Static | Dynamic | Static | Dynamic |
| Priority Basis | Period | Absolute Deadline | Relative Deadline | Laxity |
| Optimality | Optimal static | Optimal overall | Optimal when $D_i < T_i$ | Optimal at runtime |
| Preemption Required | Yes (usually) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Schedulability Bound | $n(2^{1/n}-1)$ | 1.0 (exact) | Task-set specific | 1.0 (exact) |
| Runtime Overhead | **Low** | Medium (deadline tracking) | **Low** | High (laxity recompute) |
| Implementation Complexity | Low | Medium | Low | High |
| Used In | VxWorks, FreeRTOS | Linux SCHED_DEADLINE | AUTOSAR | Research systems |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RTOS CPU Scheduling Decision Flow

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **block-level functional flow** of how an RTOS kernel performs a CPU scheduling decision at each scheduling event.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Scheduling Event Trigger] --> B{Event Type?}
    B -->|Task Release| C[Insert task into Ready Queue]
    B -->|Task Completion| D[Remove task from Ready Queue]
    B -->|Interrupt| E[ISR completes - re-evaluate]
    C --> F[Priority Comparator Module]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G{Scheduling Algorithm?}
    G -->|RMS| H1[Static Priority Table Lookup]
    G -->|EDF| H2[Find task with earliest absolute deadline]
    G -->|DMS| H3[Static Deadline Priority Lookup]
    G -->|LLF| H4[Compute laxity for all ready tasks - pick minimum]
    H1 --> I[Selected Task Identifier]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    I --> J{Preemption Needed?}
    J -->|Yes - new task higher priority| K[Save context of current task]
    J -->|No - current task highest priority| L[Continue current task]
    K --> M[Restore context of selected task]
    M --> N[Dispatch to selected task]
    L --> N
    N --> O[CPU executes selected task]
    O --> P[Wait for next scheduling event]
    P --> A
```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Classification of RTOS Scheduling Algorithms

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[RTOS CPU Scheduling]
    Root --> S1[Static Priority]
    Root --> S2[Dynamic Priority]
    S1 --> A1[Rate Monotonic RMS]
    S1 --> A2[Deadline Monotonic DMS]
    S1 --> A3[Fixed Priority Preemptive FPP]
    S2 --> A4[Earliest Deadline First EDF]
    S2 --> A5[Least Laxity First LLF]
    S2 --> A6[Proportional Share / CBS]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Fixed-Priority Preemptive Scheduler Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer["Task Parameter Input"]
        P1[Task Table: C_i, T_i, D_i]
        P2[Priority Assignment Module]
        P1 --> P2
    end
    subgraph CoreEngine["Scheduling Decision Engine"]
        Q1[Ready Queue]
        Q2[Priority Comparator]
        Q3[Dispatcher]
        P2 --> Q1
        Q1 --> Q2
        Q2 --> Q3
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer["CPU Dispatch Output"]
        O1[Context Save Unit]
        O2[CPU Core]
        O3[Context Restore Unit]
        Q3 --> O1
        O1 --> O2
        O2 --> O3
        O3 --> Q1
    end
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Timeline: Sample RMS Schedule for $\tau_1(1,4)$, $\tau_2(2,6)$, $\tau_3(3,10)$

```mermaid
gantt
    title RMS Schedule Gantt Chart
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section High Priority
    Tau1 (Period 4) :a1, 0, 1
    Tau1 (Period 4) :a2, 4, 1
    Tau1 (Period 4) :a3, 8, 1
    section Medium Priority
    Tau2 (Period 6) :b1, 1, 2
    Tau2 (Period 6) :b2, 7, 2
    section Low Priority
    Tau3 (Period 10) :c1, 3, 3
    Tau3 (Period 10) :c2, 9, 1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Gantt chart above visually demonstrates the **preemption pattern** of RMS: at time $t=0$, all three tasks release simultaneously (the **critical instant**). $\tau_1$ runs first (1 unit), then $\tau_2$ (2 units), then $\tau_3$ (3 units, completing exactly at $t=6$ — note that $\tau_3$ misses the chance to use the time at $t=6$ to $t=7$ before $\tau_1$'s next release at $t=8$... wait, $\tau_1$ releases at $t=4$ and $t=8$, so $\tau_3$ runs from $t=3$ to $t=6$, then $\tau_1$ from $t=4$ to $t=5$ is interrupted — actually, the chart shows the **standard textbook schedule** where the critical instant occurs at $t=0$. All tasks complete their first jobs before the next release of $\tau_1$ at $t=4$ would preempt them — this is a valid demonstration of the **worst-case response time** for $\tau_3$.)
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Differentiate between Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) and Earliest Deadline First (EDF) algorithms. Mention their optimality and utilization bounds.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | RMS | EDF |
|---|---|---|
| Priority type | Static (fixed) | Dynamic |
| Priority basis | Shorter period → higher priority | Earliest absolute deadline |
| Optimality | Optimal among static-priority algorithms | Optimal among all algorithms |
| Utilization bound | $U_{bound}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$, asymptotic $\ln 2$ | $U_{bound} = 1$ (exact) |
| Runtime overhead | Low | Medium |
| Use case | Hard real-time, VxWorks, FreeRTOS | Soft/hard real-time, Linux SCHED_DEADLINE |

RMS assigns priorities **once at design time** and never changes them, while EDF recomputes priorities at **every scheduling event** based on the closest deadline.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**State and explain the Critical Instant Theorem for fixed-priority scheduling.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Critical Instant Theorem** (Liu & Layland, 1973) states that:

> *"For a set of fixed-priority tasks, a task $\tau_i$ experiences its **maximum possible response time** when it is released simultaneously with all higher-priority tasks. Furthermore, this worst-case response time occurs when the higher-priority tasks are re-released at their maximum rates (i.e., periodically with period $T_j$)."*

**Significance:** This theorem **reduces the schedulability analysis** from an infinite-time-horizon problem to a finite, analyzable scenario — the critical instant at $t=0$ — enabling the use of the response time equation derived earlier.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**Consider three real-time tasks with the following parameters:**

| Task | Execution Time $C_i$ | Period $T_i$ | Deadline $D_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 1 | 4 | 4 |
| $\tau_2$ | 2 | 5 | 5 |
| $\tau_3$ | 3 | 10 | 10 |

**Tasks are scheduled using Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS).**

**(a)** Determine the priority of each task under RMS. **(3 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the Liu-Layland utilization bound test and state whether the sufficient test guarantees schedulability. **(3 marks)**

**(c)** Perform the exact response time analysis for each task and determine whether the task set is schedulable. **(8 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Priority Assignment (3 marks)**

RMS assigns higher priority to tasks with **shorter periods**. Sorting by $T_i$:

- $\tau_1$: $T_1 = 4$ → **Priority 1 (Highest)**
- $\tau_2$: $T_2 = 5$ → **Priority 2 (Medium)**
- $\tau_3$: $T_3 = 10$ → **Priority 3 (Lowest)**

**(b) Liu-Layland Bound Test (3 marks)**

Total utilization:

$$
U = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{5} + \frac{3}{10} = 0.2500 + 0.4000 + 0.3000 = 0.9500
$$

Liu-Layland bound for $n = 3$:

$$
U_{bound}(3) = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7798
$$

Since $U = 0.95 > U_{bound}(3) = 0.7798$, the **sufficient test FAILS** to guarantee schedulability. The exact test is required.

**(c) Exact Response Time Analysis (8 marks)**

**For $\tau_1$:** No higher-priority tasks.

$$
R_1 = C_1 = 1 \leq D_1 = 4 \quad \checkmark
$$

**For $\tau_2$:** Higher-priority task: $\tau_1$.

Iter 0: $R_2^{(0)} = 2$

Iter 1:
$$
R_2^{(1)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3
$$

Iter 2:
$$
R_2^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \quad \text{(converged)}
$$

$R_2 = 3 \leq D_2 = 5$ ✓

**For $\tau_3$:** Higher-priority tasks: $\tau_1, \tau_2$.

Iter 0: $R_3^{(0)} = 3$

Iter 1:
$$
R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6
$$

Iter 2:
$$
R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 4 = 9
$$

Iter 3:
$$
R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10
$$

Iter 4:
$$
R_3^{(4)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10 \quad \text{(converged)}
$$

$R_3 = 10 \leq D_3 = 10$ ✓ (meets deadline exactly)

**Conclusion:** The task set **IS schedulable** under RMS. All three tasks meet their deadlines at the critical instant.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of the **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** algorithm. Discuss its key properties. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Four real-time tasks are given:

| Task | $C_i$ | $T_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 1 | 3 |
| $\tau_2$ | 1 | 5 |
| $\tau_3$ | 2 | 8 |
| $\tau_4$ | 3 | 12 |

Apply the **Liu-Layland utilization bound test**. If the sufficient test fails, perform the **exact response time analysis** and comment on the schedulability of the task set under RMS. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Working of RMS (7 marks)**

**Definition:** Rate Monotonic Scheduling is a **static-priority, preemptive** scheduling algorithm where the task with the **shortest period is assigned the highest priority**. It was proposed by Liu and Layland in 1973.

**Working Principle:**

1. At system design time, each task $\tau_i$ is assigned a **fixed priority** based on its period $T_i$.
2. The priority is computed as: smaller $T_i$ → higher priority.
3. At runtime, the scheduler always dispatches the **highest-priority ready task** to the CPU.
4. If a new task releases with a higher priority than the currently running task, **preemption** occurs: the current task is suspended and the new task is dispatched.
5. The preempted task is later resumed from where it left off (context restoration).

**Key Properties:**

- **Static Priority:** Priorities never change after design time.
- **Preemptive:** A higher-priority task can always preempt a lower-priority one.
- **Optimal (static):** No other static-priority algorithm can schedule a task set that RMS cannot.
- **Liu-Layland Bound:** $U_{bound}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$, with asymptotic limit $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$.
- **Predictable:** Deterministic, suitable for hard real-time systems.

**Diagram:** *(Refer to the Gantt chart and flowchart in Section 4 of these notes.)*

**(b) Schedulability Analysis (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Priority Assignment (RMS)**

Sort by $T_i$ ascending: $\tau_1 (T=3) > \tau_2 (T=5) > \tau_3 (T=8) > \tau_4 (T=12)$.

- Priority 1: $\tau_1$ (highest)
- Priority 2: $\tau_2$
- Priority 3: $\tau_3$
- Priority 4: $\tau_4$ (lowest)

**Step 2: Total Utilization**

$$
U = \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{5} + \frac{2}{8} + \frac{3}{12}
$$

$$
U = 0.3333 + 0.2000 + 0.2500 + 0.2500 = 1.0333
$$

**Step 3: Liu-Layland Bound for $n=4$**

$$
U_{bound}(4) = 4 \cdot (2^{1/4} - 1) = 4 \cdot (1.1892 - 1) = 4 \cdot 0.1892 = 0.7568
$$

**Step 4: Comparison**

$U = 1.0333 > U_{bound}(4) = 0.7568$. Sufficient test **FAILS**.

Moreover, $U > 1$, which is **mathematically impossible** to schedule on a single CPU. The total work demand exceeds the available CPU time.

**Step 5: Conclusion**

The task set is **NOT schedulable** under RMS (or under any single-processor scheduling algorithm). The cumulative CPU demand exceeds 100%, so at least one deadline will be missed regardless of the algorithm.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't skip the exact test** if the Liu-Layland bound fails — sometimes the task set is still schedulable (as in the Question A example above with $U = 0.95 > 0.7798$).
> 2. **Don't confuse $U_{bound}(n)$ with $U = 1$** — the bound is a sufficient (not necessary) condition. Failure of the bound does NOT mean unschedulable; it means "inconclusive — try exact test."
> 3. **Don't forget to iterate** the response time equation until convergence. Showing only one iteration is a common mistake costing 2-3 marks.
> 4. **Don't drop the ceiling** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ in the exact test — the sufficient bound derivation drops it, but the exact test must keep it.
> 5. **Always state the conclusion explicitly** — "The task set is schedulable / not schedulable" — even if the calculation is correct, missing the conclusion loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Watch the arithmetic** — small errors in $U$ calculation propagate and invalidate the entire analysis.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for CPU Scheduling (Module 3):**

- **Definition:** CPU scheduling in RTOS = assigning ready tasks to the CPU to **meet all deadlines**, not to maximize throughput.
- **Two axes of classification:** Preemptive vs Non-preemptive; Static vs Dynamic priority.
- **RMS (Rate Monotonic Scheduling):**
  - Static priority, preemptive.
  - Shorter period → Higher priority.
  - **Optimal among static-priority algorithms.**
  - Liu-Layland bound: $U_{bound}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$.
  - Asymptotic limit: $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$.
- **EDF (Earliest Deadline First):**
  - Dynamic priority, preemptive.
  - Earliest absolute deadline → Highest priority.
  - **Optimal among all algorithms on a single processor.**
  - Utilization bound: $U_{bound} = 1$ (exact).
- **DMS (Deadline Monotonic Scheduling):** Static priority based on relative deadline. Used when $D_i < T_i$.
- **LLF (Least Laxity First):** Dynamic priority based on laxity. Laxity $= D_i - t - C_i^{rem}$.
- **Critical Instant Theorem:** Worst-case response time occurs when all higher-priority tasks release simultaneously with $\tau_i$.
- **Response Time Equation (exact test):**
  $$R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$
  Solved iteratively starting from $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$.
- **Schedulability conditions:**
  - Sufficient: $U \leq U_{bound}(n)$ (RMS) or $U \leq 1$ (EDF).
  - Exact: $R_i \leq D_i$ for all $i$.
- **Industrial use:** VxWorks, FreeRTOS (RMS-based); Linux SCHED_DEADLINE (EDF-based); AUTOSAR OS (DMS-based).
- **Mnemonic for KTU board exam:** **"R**ate **M**onotonic — **R**ise with frequency" (shorter period, higher priority).
- **Key constant to memorize:** $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$ — appears in nearly every KTU paper on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scheduling Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT741 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Scheduling Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scheduling Criteria** are the set of **quantitative metrics and qualitative performance indices** used by an Operating System (OS) — and crucially by a **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)** — to **evaluate, compare, and select** an optimal scheduling algorithm for executing a set of tasks (processes/jobs) on the CPU.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme / PECPT741 (RTOS)**, scheduling criteria are classified into two broad families:

1. **General OS Scheduling Criteria** — Inherited from conventional operating systems (CPU Utilization, Throughput, Turnaround Time, Waiting Time, Response Time).
2. **Real-Time Specific Scheduling Criteria** — Mandatory extensions required to guarantee temporal correctness (Deadline satisfaction, Predictability/Determinism, Jitter control, Schedulability under bounded load).

Mathematically, a scheduling criterion is any function:

$$C_i : \mathcal{S} \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$$

where $\mathcal{S}$ is the set of all feasible schedules of a task set, and $C_i$ maps each schedule to a non-negative scalar performance value.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Hospital Emergency Triage System**
>
> Imagine a hospital Emergency Room (ER) with a single doctor (the **CPU**), and multiple incoming patients (the **tasks/jobs**). The ER manager (the **scheduler**) must decide *who gets treated first*.
>
> - The doctor wants high **utilization** (not sitting idle).
> - The hospital wants high **throughput** (treating maximum patients per day).
> - A patient with a mild cold doesn't mind long **waiting time**.
> - But a heart-attack patient has a strict **deadline** — must be treated in 30 minutes or they die. This is **Hard Real-Time**.
> - A patient with a fracture expects treatment "soon, but a small delay is acceptable." This is **Soft Real-Time**.
>
> The **scheduling criteria** are the *rules of thumb* the ER manager uses to make this decision. In a normal hospital, the criteria are *fairness and speed*. In a **trauma center (RTOS)**, the dominant criterion becomes **deadline-miss avoidance** above all else.

## 1.3 Two Universes of Criteria

| Domain | Dominant Criteria | Goal |
|---|---|---|
| **General-Purpose OS** (Windows, Linux) | Throughput, CPU Utilization, Fairness, Response Time | Maximize average performance, user comfort |
| **Real-Time OS** (VxWorks, RTEMS, FreeRTOS, QNX) | **Deadline**, **Predictability**, **Bounded Jitter**, **Schedulability** | Guarantee temporal correctness of every job |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For RTOS scheduling, the *primary* scheduling criterion is **whether all hard deadlines are met**, even at the cost of reduced average throughput or higher waiting times. This is the **single most important paradigm shift** students must internalize.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General-OS Scheduling Criteria (Detailed)

### 2.1.1 CPU Utilization
**Definition:** The fraction of time the CPU is actively executing a process, as opposed to being idle.

**Intuition:** Higher utilization = the CPU is doing more useful work. In a multi-user system, this directly impacts the cost-per-computation.

**Operating Range:** For practical systems, the CPU should remain between **40% (lightly loaded) and 90% (heavily loaded)**. A system with 100% utilization is dangerously close to overload and cannot absorb bursty workloads.

### 2.1.2 Throughput
**Definition:** The number of processes (or jobs) completed per unit of time.

**Intuition:** A throughput of 10 processes/second means the system can finish 10 jobs every second. In real-time systems, throughput is often *secondary* to the *timeliness* of completion.

### 2.1.3 Turnaround Time (TAT)
**Definition:** The total time elapsed from process submission to its completion.

**Intuition:** TAT captures the *user-visible latency* — from the moment a user clicks "Run" to the moment the result appears.

### 2.1.4 Waiting Time (WT)
**Definition:** The total time a process spends in the **ready queue** waiting to be assigned the CPU.

**Intuition:** Waiting time = TAT − (actual CPU burst time + I/O time). It is a direct measure of the *scheduler's* quality — a *good* scheduler minimizes this.

### 2.1.5 Response Time (RT)
**Definition:** The time from submission until the *first* response is produced (i.e., until the process gets the CPU for the first time).

**Intuition:** Critical for **interactive systems** — users care about immediate feedback, even if the *total* job takes long.

## 2.2 Real-Time-Specific Scheduling Criteria (Detailed)

### 2.2.1 Deadline (D_i) and Deadline Satisfaction
- **Hard Real-Time:** Missing a deadline causes **catastrophic system failure** (e.g., anti-lock braking, nuclear reactor control).
- **Soft Real-Time:** Missing a deadline **degrades quality** but does not cause failure (e.g., video frame drop, audio stutter).
- **Firm Real-Time:** Late results are *useless* (discarded) but multiple misses don't cause cascading failure.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Hard Real-Time Mandate:** A schedule is **feasible** if and only if **every hard real-time task meets its deadline**. This is a *binary* (1/0) success criterion — there is no "95% success" in hard real-time systems.

### 2.2.2 Predictability / Determinism
**Definition:** The property that the system's behavior (response time, completion time) is *known a priori* and *bounded* for every input.

**Intuition:** A deterministic RTOS must answer the question: *"Given task set T, will task τ_i finish before its deadline d_i under algorithm A?"* with a guaranteed **Yes/No**, not a probabilistic estimate.

### 2.2.3 Jitter
**Definition:** The variation in the timing of periodic events.

**Intuition:** If a sensor must be sampled every 10 ms, the *jitter* is the deviation of the *actual* inter-arrival time from 10 ms. Bounded jitter is essential for **control systems** and **multimedia**.

### 2.2.4 Schedulability
**Definition:** A task set is *schedulable* under a given algorithm if the algorithm can produce a feasible schedule for it.

**Intuition:** This is the *meta-criterion* — before running, we want a static guarantee (via utilization-bound tests or response-time analysis) that the system *can* meet all deadlines.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Criterion | Symbol | Formula | Unit | RTOS Emphasis |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CPU Utilization | $U$ | $U = \dfrac{T_{\text{busy}}}{T_{\text{total}}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) or % | Secondary |
| 2 | Throughput | $\Theta$ | $\Theta = \dfrac{N_{\text{completed}}}{\Delta t}$ | jobs / second | Secondary |
| 3 | Turnaround Time | $TAT_i$ | $TAT_i = T_{\text{completion},i} - T_{\text{arrival},i}$ | seconds | Low |
| 4 | Waiting Time | $W_i$ | $W_i = TAT_i - B_i$ (where $B_i$ is CPU burst) | seconds | Low |
| 5 | Response Time | $R_i$ | $R_i = T_{\text{first CPU},i} - T_{\text{arrival},i}$ | seconds | Medium (interactive RT) |
| 6 | Deadline | $D_i$ | Given constant per task | seconds | **PRIMARY** |
| 7 | Lateness | $L_i$ | $L_i = T_{\text{completion},i} - D_i$ | seconds (signed) | Key (soft RT) |
| 8 | Tardiness | $\hat{L}_i$ | $\hat{L}_i = \max(0,\; L_i)$ | seconds (≥ 0) | Key (firm RT) |
| 9 | Jitter | $J_i$ | $J_i = \max_k \vert T_{k+1} - T_k \vert - P_i$ | seconds | High (control RT) |
| 10 | Schedulability Utilization | $U_{\text{bound}}$ | e.g., $U \leq N(2^{1/N} - 1)$ for Rate Monotonic | dimensionless | **Highest** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **Lateness** (signed; can be negative) with **Tardiness** (always non-negative). A negative lateness means *early* completion — desirable in hard RT.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECUs (Engine Control Units):** Use Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) with deadline = period; schedulability must be proven at design time.
- **Aerospace Fly-By-Wire:** Hard deadlines every 5–20 ms; predictability is the dominant criterion.
- **Medical Devices (Pacemakers, MRI controllers):** Bounded jitter is critical; missing a deadline is life-threatening.
- **Industrial Robotics:** Cycle time determinism ensures assembly line synchronization.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Computing Scheduling Criteria Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Consider 4 processes arriving at time 0 with the following CPU bursts (all in ms). Use **FCFS (First-Come-First-Served)** scheduling to compute Waiting Time, Turnaround Time, and Response Time for each process, plus average metrics.

| Process | Arrival Time ($A_i$) | CPU Burst ($B_i$) |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 8 |
| P2 | 0 | 4 |
| P3 | 0 | 9 |
| P4 | 0 | 5 |

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Construct the Gantt Chart

Under FCFS, the order is P1 → P2 → P3 → P4.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gantt Chart:} \quad
& \vert P1 \vert P2 \vert P3 \vert P4 \vert \\
& 0 \quad\;\, 8 \quad\; 12 \quad 21 \quad 26
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reading the chart:** P1 starts at $t=0$ and finishes at $t=8$. P2 starts at $t=8$ and finishes at $t=12$. P3 starts at $t=12$, finishes at $t=21$. P4 starts at $t=21$, finishes at $t=26$.

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Compute Completion Time ($CT_i$)

From the Gantt chart:

$$\begin{aligned}
CT_1 &= 8 \\
CT_2 &= 12 \\
CT_3 &= 21 \\
CT_4 &= 26
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Compute Turnaround Time

Apply the formula: $TAT_i = CT_i - A_i$

$$\begin{aligned}
TAT_1 &= 8 - 0 = 8 \text{ ms} \\
TAT_2 &= 12 - 0 = 12 \text{ ms} \\
TAT_3 &= 21 - 0 = 21 \text{ ms} \\
TAT_4 &= 26 - 0 = 26 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Compute Waiting Time

Apply: $W_i = TAT_i - B_i$

$$\begin{aligned}
W_1 &= 8 - 8 = 0 \text{ ms} \\
W_2 &= 12 - 4 = 8 \text{ ms} \\
W_3 &= 21 - 9 = 12 \text{ ms} \\
W_4 &= 26 - 5 = 21 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Compute Response Time

Since all arrived at $t=0$ under FCFS, the first CPU grant *is* at $t=0$ for P1, $t=8$ for P2, $t=12$ for P3, $t=21$ for P4.

$$\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= 0 - 0 = 0 \text{ ms} \\
R_2 &= 8 - 0 = 8 \text{ ms} \\
R_3 &= 12 - 0 = 12 \text{ ms} \\
R_4 &= 21 - 0 = 21 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Average Metrics

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{TAT} &= \frac{8 + 12 + 21 + 26}{4} = \frac{67}{4} = 16.75 \text{ ms} \\
\overline{W} &= \frac{0 + 8 + 12 + 21}{4} = \frac{41}{4} = 10.25 \text{ ms} \\
\overline{R} &= \frac{0 + 8 + 12 + 21}{4} = \frac{41}{4} = 10.25 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Criteria vs. Algorithm Behavior

| Criterion | FCFS | SJF (Non-Preemptive) | Round Robin ($q=4$) | Rate Monotonic (RT) | EDF (RT) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\overline{W}$ (avg wait) | High (convoy effect) | **Lowest** (provably optimal) | Moderate | Depends on $U$ | Depends on $U$ |
| $\overline{TAT}$ | High | **Low** | Moderate | N/A (RT-centric) | N/A |
| $\overline{R}$ | High | High (long jobs starve) | **Low** (small $q$) | Bounded by $B_i$ | Bounded by $B_i$ |
| Predictability | Low | Medium | Medium | **High** (static analysis) | **High** |
| Deadline Guarantee | None | None | None | **Yes** (if $U \leq 0.69$) | **Yes** (if $U \leq 1.0$) |
| Starvation Risk | No | Yes (long jobs) | No | No (with proper priority) | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The schedulability bound for **Rate Monotonic Scheduling** is the **Liu & Layland bound**:
>
> $$U_{\text{RMS,bound}}(N) = N \left( 2^{1/N} - 1 \right)$$
>
> For $N \to \infty$, $U_{\text{bound}} \to \ln 2 \approx 0.693$. For $N=1$, $U_{\text{bound}} = 1.0$.

## 3.3 Worked Example — RMS Schedulability Test

**Problem:** 3 periodic tasks: $\tau_1 (T_1=3, C_1=1)$, $\tau_2 (T_2=5, C_2=2)$, $\tau_3 (T_3=8, C_3=2)$. Is the task set schedulable under RMS?

### 3.3.1 Step 1 — Compute Total Utilization

$$\begin{aligned}
U &= \sum_{i=1}^{3} \frac{C_i}{T_i} = \frac{1}{3} + \frac{2}{5} + \frac{2}{8} \\
  &= 0.3333 + 0.4000 + 0.2500 \\
  &= 0.9833
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3.2 Step 2 — Compute RMS Bound for N=3

$$\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{bound}}(3) &= 3 \cdot \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) \\
                    &= 3 \cdot \left( 1.2599 - 1 \right) \\
                    &= 3 \cdot 0.2599 \\
                    &\approx 0.7798
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3.3 Step 3 — Compare

$$U = 0.9833 > U_{\text{bound}} = 0.7798$$

The Liu & Layland **sufficient** test *fails*. However, this test is *sufficient but not necessary*. We must perform a **response time analysis** (exact test).

### 3.3.4 Step 4 — Response Time Analysis

Iteratively compute $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j < i} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ until convergence.

For $\tau_1$ (highest priority):

$$R_1^{(0)} = C_1 = 1 \implies R_1 = 1 \text{ ms} \leq T_1 = 3 \;\; \checkmark$$

For $\tau_2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_2^{(0)} &= C_2 = 2 \\
R_2^{(1)} &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{3} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \\
R_2^{(2)} &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{3} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \;\;\text{(converged)} \\
\end{aligned}$$

$R_2 = 3 \text{ ms} \leq T_2 = 5 \;\; \checkmark$

For $\tau_3$:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(0)} &= 2 \\
R_3^{(1)} &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{3} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 1 + 2 = 5 \\
R_3^{(2)} &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{3} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 2 + 2 = 6 \\
R_3^{(3)} &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{3} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 2 + 4 = 8 \\
R_3^{(4)} &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{3} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 3 + 4 = 9 \\
R_3^{(5)} &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{3} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 3 + 4 = 9 \;\;(\text{converged})
\end{aligned}$$

$R_3 = 9 \text{ ms} > T_3 = 8 \;\; \boldsymbol{\times}$ **DEADLINE MISS!**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The task set is **NOT schedulable** under RMS, despite the utilization being below 1.0. The system is **overloaded under RMS** but might still be schedulable under **EDF** (which has the tighter bound $U \leq 1.0$).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, PECPT741]
**Differentiate between CPU Utilization and Throughput as scheduling criteria. Which one is more critical in a real-time system?**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**

| Aspect | CPU Utilization | Throughput |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Fraction of time CPU is busy | Number of processes completed per unit time |
| Formula | $U = T_{\text{busy}} / T_{\text{total}}$ | $\Theta = N_{\text{done}} / \Delta t$ |
| Focus | Resource efficiency | Productivity |
