### 2.7.1 Priority Inversion
A **higher-priority thread** is indirectly preempted by a **lower-priority thread**, effectively "inverting" the relative priorities.

**Classic Case (Mars Pathfinder Bug, 1997):**
- Low-priority thread acquires a shared lock.
- High-priority thread blocks on the same lock.
- Medium-priority thread preempts the low-priority thread.
- High-priority thread now waits indefinitely → **unbounded priority inversion**.

### 2.7.2 Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)
- When a high-priority thread blocks on a resource held by a lower-priority thread, the **lower-priority thread inherits the priority** of the highest-priority waiter.
- Prevents unbounded priority inversion.
- Does **not** prevent deadlocks or chained blocking.

### 2.7.3 Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)
- Each resource/mutex is assigned a **priority ceiling** = the maximum priority of any thread that may access it.
- A thread acquires the mutex only if its priority is **strictly higher** than the ceilings of all mutexes currently held by other threads.
- **Prevents deadlocks** and bounded blocking.
- Two variants: **Immediate PCP** and **Original PCP**.

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes all key equations, conditions, and metrics for Threading Issues — **memorize these for KTU 2024 ESE**.

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Unit / Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Priority Inversion Duration Bound (PIP) | $T_{inv} \leq T_{cs}^{max}$ | Time |
| 2 | Priority Inversion Duration Bound (PCP) | $T_{inv} \leq (N-1) \cdot T_{cs}^{max}$ | Time |
| 3 | Coffman Deadlock Conditions | $D = M \land H \land NP \land C$ | Boolean |
| 4 | Thread Pool Throughput | $\lambda_{pool} = \dfrac{N_{threads}}{T_{service}}$ | Tasks/s |
| 5 | Context Switch Overhead | $T_{cs} = 2 \cdot T_{save} + T_{load}$ | Time |
| 6 | Race Condition Existence | $\exists \ interleaving : R \neq R_{expected}$ | Logical |
| 7 | TLS Lookup Cost | $O(1)$ with key-based hash | Complexity |
| 8 | Scheduler Activations | $SA = N_{processors}$ allocated | Count |
| 9 | Cancellation Latency | $T_{cancel} = T_{checkpoint} \cdot P$ | Time |
| 10 | Priority Ceiling Rule | $P_{ceiling}(R) = \max\{P(t) \vert t \text{ accesses } R\}$ | Priority |

> Where $T_{cs}^{max}$ = max critical section time, $N$ = number of mutexes, $P$ = cancellation point frequency, $P(t)$ = priority of thread $t$, $R$ = resource.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bounded Priority Inversion Duration (PCP)

We want to show that under the **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)**, a high-priority task can be blocked by at most one lower-priority task at a time, and the total blocking time is bounded.

### Step 1 — Define the System
Let:
- $T_H$ = high-priority thread (priority $P_H$)
- $T_L$ = low-priority thread (priority $P_L$)
- $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_N$ = set of shared mutexes/resources
- $C_i$ = priority ceiling of resource $R_i$, defined as:

$$ C_i = \max\{P(t) \;\vert\; t \text{ accesses } R_i\} $$

### Step 2 — State the PCP Rule
A thread $T$ may lock resource $R_i$ only if:

$$ P(T) > \max\{C_j \;\vert\; R_j \text{ is currently held by another thread}\} $$

Otherwise, $T$ is **blocked** and inherits the priority ceiling of the conflicting resource.

### Step 3 — Show the Blocking Bound
Suppose $T_H$ tries to enter its critical section but is blocked by $T_L$ (which currently holds $R_i$). The ceiling rule guarantees:

$$ C_i \geq P_H $$

Since $T_L$ inherits $P_H$ (or higher), **no medium-priority thread** $T_M$ with $P_L < P_M < P_H$ can preempt $T_L$. Hence, $T_L$ executes its critical section without preemption.

### Step 4 — Compute the Maximum Blocking Time
The maximum time $T_H$ can be blocked is the time for $T_L$ to finish **one critical section**:

$$ T_{block}^{max} = T_{cs}^{max}(T_L) $$

### Step 5 — Extend to Multiple Resources
If a thread accesses $N$ resources, the cumulative blocking due to priority inversion under PCP is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{inv}^{max} &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{cs,i}^{max} \\
              &= (N-1) \cdot T_{cs}^{max} \quad \text{(worst case, sequential)} \\
              &\leq T_{cs}^{max} \cdot (N - 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Final Bounded Inversion Formula

$$ \boxed{T_{inv}^{PCP} \;\leq\; (N-1) \cdot T_{cs}^{max}} $$

> **Key Insight:** Unlike **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** where $T_{inv}$ can grow with chained blocking, **PCP** guarantees a hard upper bound — a critical property for **hard real-time systems**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Threading Issue → Cause → Solution

| Issue | Root Cause | Symptom | RTOS Solution |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Race Condition** | Unsynchronized shared memory access | Non-deterministic output | Mutexes, Semaphores, Disabling Interrupts |
| **Deadlock** | Circular wait on locks | System hang | Banker's Algorithm, PCP, Lock Ordering |
| **Starvation** | Unfair scheduling | Thread never gets CPU | Aging, Round-Robin within priority |
| **Priority Inversion** | Low-priority thread holds shared lock | High-priority task delayed | PIP, PCP |
| **Chained Blocking** | Nested lock acquisitions | Cascading delays | Lock-free programming, PCP |
| **Cancellation Race** | Asynchronous cancellation | Resource leak | Deferred cancellation, cleanup handlers |
| **Signal Loss** | Signal blocked by all threads | Asynchronous event lost | Dedicated signal-handling thread |
| **Scheduler Activations Overhead** | Excessive upcalls | Context switch thrashing | Tune LWP count to CPU count |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Priority Inversion with PIP

**Problem:** Consider three tasks:
- $T_H$ (High priority) — Period 5 ms, WCET 1 ms
- $T_M$ (Medium priority) — Period 20 ms, WCET 5 ms
- $T_L$ (Low priority) — Period 50 ms, WCET 20 ms

All share mutex $M$. $T_L$ acquires $M$ at $t=0$ for 10 ms. $T_H$ arrives at $t=1$ ms and tries to lock $M$.

### Without PIP:
- $T_H$ blocks at $t=1$ ms.
- $T_M$ preempts $T_L$ at $t=1$ ms (since $P_M > P_L$).
- $T_M$ runs for 5 ms (till $t=6$ ms).
- $T_L$ resumes, releases $M$ at $t=10$ ms.
- $T_H$ acquires $M$ at $t=10$ ms. **Blocking time = 9 ms** → catastrophic for real-time.

### With PIP:
- $T_H$ blocks at $t=1$ ms.
- $T_L$ inherits $P_H$ (effectively).
- $T_M$ cannot preempt $T_L$ (since $P_M < P_H$).
- $T_L$ continues critical section, releases $M$ at $t=10$ ms.
- $T_H$ acquires $M$ at $t=10$ ms. **Blocking time = 9 ms** — but no $T_M$ interference during this interval.

> **Observation:** PIP bounds the *unbounded* interference, but the absolute blocking time still depends on $T_L$'s critical section length. This is why **PCP** is preferred for hard real-time.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation: Thread Pool Sizing

For an RTOS serving tasks at rate $\lambda$ with service time $T_s$, by **Little's Law**:

$$ N_{pool}^{min} = \lambda \cdot T_s $$

To maintain **utilization** $U < 1$ (avoid saturation):

$$ N_{pool} = \left\lceil \frac{\lambda \cdot T_s}{U_{target}} \right\rceil $$

For **hard real-time**, target $U_{target} \leq 0.7$ to absorb jitter.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Thread Lifecycle & Cancellation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thread Created] --> B[Thread Runnable]
    B --> C{Scheduler Dispatch}
    C --> D[Thread Running]
    D --> E{Cancel Request?}
    E -- No --> F[Continue Execution]
    E -- Yes --> G{Cancellation Type}
    G -- Async --> H[Immediate Termination]
    G -- Deferred --> I[pthread_testcancel checkpoint]
    I -- Flag Set --> J[Cleanup Handlers Run]
    I -- Flag Not Set --> F
    J --> K[Resources Released]
    H --> L[Thread Terminated]
    K --> L
    F --> D
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TH[Task TH - High Priority] -->|Request Lock| M[Mutex M]
    TL[Task TL - Low Priority] -->|Holds Lock| M
    TM[Task TM - Medium Priority] -->|Preempts TL| TL

    M -.->|Blocked| TH

    TL -->|Inherits Priority| P[Priority Booster]
    P -->|Sets P_L = P_H| TL
    TM -->|Cannot Preempt| TL
    TL -->|Releases M| TH
    TH -->|Acquires M| TH
    TH -->|Completes| EXIT[Task Exit]
    TL -->|Original Priority Restored| END[TL Resumes]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Scheduler Activations Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph K[Kernel Space]
        K1[Processor Allocator]
        K2[Scheduler Activation Upcall]
        K3[Signal Delivery]
    end

    subgraph U[User Space - Thread Library]
        U1[User-Level Scheduler]
        U2[Thread Pool]
        U3[Thread T1]
        U4[Thread T2]
        U5[Thread T3]
    end

    K1 -->|Allocates CPU| U1
    U1 -->|Selects Thread| U2
    U2 --> U3
    U2 --> U4
    U2 --> U5
    K2 -->|Preemption Notification| U1
    K3 -->|Event Notification| U1
    U1 -->|Thread Migration| U2
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Thread-Local Storage (TLS) Access Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Thread T1] --> K[Thread-Specific Key]
    T2[Thread T2] --> K
    T3[Thread T3] --> K
    K --> V1[Value 1 - For T1]
    K --> V2[Value 2 - For T2]
    K --> V3[Value 3 - For T3]

    V1 -.-> D1[Thread T1 Local Data]
    V2 -.-> D2[Thread T2 Local Data]
    V3 -.-> D3[Thread T3 Local Data]
```

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Deadlock Detection State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Running
    Running --> HoldingResource: Acquire Lock
    HoldingResource --> WaitingResource: Request Another Lock
    WaitingResource --> HoldingResource: Lock Granted
    WaitingResource --> Deadlocked: Circular Wait Detected
    HoldingResource --> Running: Release All Locks
    Deadlocked --> Aborted: Recovery Action
    Aborted --> [*]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style – 3 Marks]**

### Q1. What is thread cancellation? Differentiate between asynchronous and deferred cancellation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Thread cancellation is the mechanism by which one thread terminates the execution of another thread before it has completed naturally.

| Aspect | Asynchronous Cancellation | Deferred Cancellation |
|---|---|---|
| Timing | Immediate | At defined cancellation points |
| Cleanup | None | Cleanup handlers run |
| Safety | Unsafe in RTOS | Safe, preferred |
| Mechanism | Forced kill | Flag check (`pthread_testcancel`) |

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark differentiation table + 1 mark RTOS significance)*

---

### Q2. Define priority inversion. How does the Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) solve it?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Priority Inversion** is a scheduling anomaly in which a higher-priority task is indirectly preempted by a lower-priority task, effectively "inverting" the priority order, usually due to a shared mutex.

**PIP Solution (1 mark):**
When a high-priority task $T_H$ blocks on a resource held by a lower-priority task $T_L$, $T_L$ **temporarily inherits** the priority of $T_H$. This prevents medium-priority tasks from preempting $T_L$, bounding the inversion to a single critical section.

**Limitation (1 mark):**
PIP does not prevent deadlocks or chained blocking — only unbounded priority inversion.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – End Semester, 14 Marks]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the different threading issues encountered in a Real-Time Operating System. Discuss thread pools and thread-specific data in detail.**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Threading issues in RTOS arise due to concurrent execution of multiple threads sharing the same address space. Key issues include thread cancellation, thread pools, thread-specific data, scheduler activations, signal handling, and priority inversion.

**Thread Pools (3 marks):**
A thread pool is a collection of pre-created threads that wait for tasks from a work queue. When a task arrives, an idle thread executes it and returns to the pool. **Advantages in RTOS:**
- Eliminates thread creation overhead.
- Provides predictable response time.
- Bounds resource usage (memory, file descriptors).
- Improves throughput under bursty loads.

**Pool sizing formula:**
$$ N_{pool} = \left\lceil \frac{\lambda \cdot T_s}{U_{target}} \right\rceil $$

**Thread-Specific Data (3 marks):**
Thread-Specific Data (TSD), also called Thread-Local Storage (TLS), allows each thread to maintain its own copy of a data item. The data appears global in scope but is logically per-thread.

**POSIX API:**
- `pthread_key_create()` — creates a key visible to all threads.
- `pthread_setspecific(key, value)` — sets per-thread value.
- `pthread_getspecific(key)` — retrieves per-thread value.

**Use cases in RTOS:**
- Per-thread `errno`.
- Transaction IDs in financial RTOS.
- Per-thread state in industrial controllers.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**With a neat diagram, explain the Scheduler Activations mechanism. How does it overcome the limitations of both user-level and kernel-level threads?**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Scheduler Activations is a hybrid threading model that provides the **flexibility of user-level threads** with the **scheduling correctness of kernel-level threads**.

**Architecture (2 marks):**
- The kernel allocates **processors** (abstracted as Lightweight Processes, LWPs) to a user-level thread package.
- When the kernel makes a scheduling decision (e.g., a thread blocks on I/O), it sends a **scheduler activation** (upcall) to the user-level scheduler.
- The user-level scheduler then re-evaluates which user thread to run.

**Diagram (2 marks):** (Refer to Section 4.3 block diagram)

**Overcoming Limitations (2 marks):**
| Limitation | User-Level Threads | Kernel-Level Threads | Scheduler Activations |
|---|---|---|---|
| Blocking system call | Blocks entire process | No issue | Resolved via upcall |
| Context switch cost | Low | High | Moderate |
| Parallelism on multiprocessor | Limited | Full | Full |
| Scheduling flexibility | Full | Limited | Full |

**Conclusion:** Scheduler Activations achieve the best of both worlds but require **complex coordination** between kernel and user-level schedulers, increasing implementation overhead.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain Priority Inversion in Real-Time Systems. Describe the Priority Inheritance Protocol and the Priority Ceiling Protocol with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

**Priority Inversion (2 marks):**
Priority inversion occurs when a higher-priority task is forced to wait for a lower-priority task to release a shared resource, while medium-priority tasks continue to execute. This leads to **unbounded** delays for the high-priority task.

**Classic Example — Mars Pathfinder (1997):**
A low-priority meteorological task held a shared bus semaphore. The high-priority bus management task was blocked, and a medium-priority communication task preempted the low-priority task, causing system resets.

**Priority Inheritance Protocol — PIP (2 marks):**
- When a high-priority task $T_H$ blocks on a resource held by a low-priority task $T_L$, $T_L$ **inherits** $P_H$ temporarily.
- $T_L$ runs at $P_H$ until it releases the resource.
- **Limits** inversion to a single critical section. **Does not** prevent deadlocks.

**Priority Ceiling Protocol — PCP (2 marks):**
- Each mutex $M_i$ is assigned a **ceiling** $C_i = \max$ priority of tasks accessing it.
- A task $T$ can lock $M_i$ only if $P(T) > \max$ ceiling of all currently held mutexes.
- **Prevents deadlocks** and ensures **bounded blocking**.

**Comparison (1 mark):**

| Feature | PIP | PCP |
|---|---|---|
| Deadlock prevention | No | Yes |
| Blocking bound | $T_{cs}^{max}$ (per chain) | $(N-1) \cdot T_{cs}^{max}$ |
| Implementation | Simple | Moderate |

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**What is a race condition? Explain the Coffman conditions for deadlock. How can deadlocks be prevented in an RTOS?**

**Model Answer:**

**Race Condition (2 marks):**
A race condition occurs when two or more threads access shared data concurrently, and the final outcome depends on the **non-deterministic order** of execution. It leads to data corruption and unpredictable system behavior.

**Prevention in RTOS:**
- Disabling interrupts during critical sections (single-core).
- Using mutexes/semaphores.
- Atomic operations.
- Lock-free data structures.

**Coffman Conditions for Deadlock (3 marks):**
All four conditions must hold simultaneously for a deadlock to occur:
1. **Mutual Exclusion** — At least one resource is held in a non-sharable mode.
2. **Hold and Wait** — A thread holds at least one resource and waits for additional resources held by others.
3. **No Preemption** — Resources cannot be forcibly taken from a holding thread.
4. **Circular Wait** — A circular chain of threads exists, where each thread holds a resource the next one needs.

**Deadlock Prevention in RTOS (2 marks):**
- **Lock ordering** — Always acquire locks in a predefined global order.
- **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)** — Statically prevents circular wait.
- **Banker's Algorithm** — Resource allocation based on safe state checking.
- **Watchdog timers** — Detect and abort deadlocked threads.
- **Timeout-based lock acquisition** — `pthread_mutex_timedlock()`.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PIP and PCP** — Many students incorrectly state that PIP prevents deadlocks. PIP **only bounds** inversion; **PCP** prevents deadlocks.
> 2. **Omitting the four Coffman conditions** — A 7-mark question on deadlock expects **all four** named and briefly defined. Skipping one = –1.5 marks.
> 3. **No diagram in Threading Issues questions** — KTU examiners *always* reward a well-labeled block diagram or state diagram. Missing it can cost 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Confusing thread-specific data with global variables** — TLS has a **global key** but **per-thread value**. Conflating these = conceptual error.
> 5. **Forgetting the cancellation cleanup handler** — When discussing deferred cancellation, mention `pthread_cleanup_push()` and `pthread_cleanup_pop()`.
> 6. **Ignoring the "bounded blocking" formula** — Memorize $T_{inv}^{PCP} \leq (N-1) \cdot T_{cs}^{max}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Threading Issues:**

- **Thread Cancellation**: Two types — *Asynchronous* (immediate, unsafe) and *Deferred* (at cancellation points, safe). Use `pthread_testcancel()`.
- **Thread Pools**: Pre-created thread set with a work queue. Size via Little's Law: $N = \lceil \lambda T_s / U \rceil$. Use for predictable, bounded resources.
- **Thread-Specific Data (TSD/TLS)**: One global key, per-thread values. APIs: `pthread_key_create`, `pthread_setspecific`, `pthread_getspecific`.
- **Scheduler Activations**: Hybrid model. Kernel upcalls user-level scheduler on blocking/preemption events. Solves issues of pure user/kernel threads.
- **Signal Handling**: Per-thread signal masks. Synchronous signals → faulting thread. Asynchronous → any eligible thread.
- **Race Condition**: Non-deterministic outcome due to interleaving. Solve with mutexes, atomics, or interrupt disabling.
- **Deadlock (Coffman)**: Mutual Exclusion, Hold & Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait — **all four** required.
- **Starvation**: Thread denied CPU indefinitely. Solve with **aging**.
- **Priority Inversion**: High-priority task blocked by low-priority task. **PIP** bounds it; **PCP** prevents it.
- **PIP Formula**: $T_{inv} \leq T_{cs}^{max}$ (single critical section).
- **PCP Formula**: $T_{inv} \leq (N-1) \cdot T_{cs}^{max}$. Priority ceiling $C_i = \max\{P(t)\}$.
- **Thread Fork-Exec Issue**: After `fork()`, only the calling thread exists; calling `exec()` immediately in child is safe.
- **RTOS Implications**: Always prefer **deferred cancellation**, **PCP for shared mutexes**, and **thread pools for hard real-time** to ensure predictability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thread Libraries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT741 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thread Libraries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thread Library**: A thread library provides a programmer with an **API (Application Programming Interface)** for creating and managing threads. Thread libraries are invoked from a higher-level language (e.g., C, C++, Java) or from an assembly-language program. The two primary approaches for implementing a thread library are:
> 1. **User-Level Library** — All thread management code is contained in user space; the kernel is unaware of the threads.
> 2. **Kernel-Level Library** — Thread management is performed by kernel system calls; the kernel is aware of all threads.

The three dominant thread libraries widely used in industry and covered in the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

- **POSIX Pthreads** (IEEE 1003.1c) — may be provided as user-level or kernel-level.
- **Win32 Threads** — kernel-level library for the Microsoft Windows family.
- **Java Threads** — managed by the **JVM (Java Virtual Machine)**, typically built on top of the host OS's underlying thread library (Pthreads on UNIX/Linux, Win32 on Windows).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a **restaurant kitchen** as your process, and the **chefs** inside it as threads.

- A **process** is like an entire restaurant kitchen — it has its own ingredients (memory), tools (resources), and recipes (code).
- A **thread** is a single chef working on one dish. Multiple chefs can work simultaneously in the same kitchen, sharing ingredients and tools.
- A **thread library** is the **kitchen manager's rulebook** — it tells each chef *how* to start a new dish (create a thread), *when* to take a break (yield/sleep), *how* to clean up (exit), and *how* to wait for another chef to finish (join).
- The **kitchen manager** represents either the user-space scheduler (user-level threads) or the head chef representing the owner (kernel-level threads). This determines whether the kitchen owner (kernel) even knows the chefs exist.

## 1.3 Why Thread Libraries Matter in RTOS

In **real-time embedded systems** (a major KTU focus), thread libraries are critical because:

- They provide a **standardized, portable interface** for building concurrent real-time applications.
- They allow the programmer to express **parallelism** in control loops, sensor polling, and actuator management.
- They interface directly with **scheduling policies** (Rate Monotonic, EDF) studied in earlier modules.
- The choice of user-level vs. kernel-level threads affects **determinism, blocking behavior, and system call performance** — all vital in hard real-time systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standardized Metrics Used in KTU 2024 Evaluations**:
> - **API call overhead**: typically **microseconds (µs)** for kernel-level libraries.
> - **Thread creation latency**: standard benchmark on Linux ≈ **20–100 µs**.
> - **POSIX Standard**: **IEEE 1003.1c (1995)**.
> - **Minimum POSIX guaranteed thread stack**: **2 MB (default, configurable)**.

---

## 1.4 The Two Implementation Approaches — At a Glance

| Approach | Location of Code | Kernel Awareness | Speed | Blocking Problem |
|----------|------------------|------------------|-------|------------------|
| **User-Level (ULT)** | User space | Kernel unaware of threads | Fast (no syscall) | One thread blocks → whole process blocks |
| **Kernel-Level (KLT)** | Kernel space | Kernel aware of threads | Slower (syscall) | Individual threads can block independently |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pure user-level thread libraries** are rare today. Most modern systems (Linux `pthread`, Windows threads) use **kernel-level threads**, though they may expose a **user-level API** (like Pthreads) for portability.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Approaches to Implementing a Thread Library

There are **two distinct strategies** a library designer may adopt:

### 2.1.1 Approach A: Library Entirely in User Space (User-Level Threads)

The library provides all thread-management functions without kernel support. The kernel sees only **one process** with a **single execution context**.

**Operational Steps:**

- The library maintains its own **thread table** (TCB array) tracking each thread's: *stack pointer, PC, registers, state*.
- Thread switching is performed by the library's local scheduler by **swapping the registers/PC** between threads inside the same process.
- A **local scheduler** decides which thread to run next within the process.
- If a thread makes a blocking system call, the **entire process blocks** (because the kernel sees only the process, not its threads).

**The "Why" Behind Each Step:**

- **Why user space?** → No kernel-mode transitions → **faster thread creation/management**.
- **Why is the table in user space?** → Cheaper memory accesses and full programmer control.
- **Why does blocking hurt?** → Kernel cannot resume just that thread — it only knows the process.

### 2.1.2 Approach B: Kernel-Level Library (Kernel-Supported Threads)

The kernel itself contains the thread management code, and threads are **kernel-level entities** (sometimes called **lightweight processes — LWPs**).

**Operational Steps:**

- The kernel maintains a **kernel thread table** (per-thread kernel stack, TCB, scheduling info).
- Thread creation is performed via a **system call** (e.g., `clone()` on Linux, `PsCreateSystemThread` on Windows).
- A blocking call in one thread only blocks that thread, not the entire process.
- The kernel scheduler directly schedules individual threads.

**The "Why" Behind Each Step:**

- **Why kernel-level?** → True parallelism on multiprocessors; clean blocking semantics.
- **Why a system call?** → Crosses the user-kernel boundary to allocate kernel resources.
- **Why LWPs?** → Each LWP can be independently scheduled on a separate CPU core.

## 2.2 The Three Major Thread Libraries

### 2.2.1 POSIX Pthreads (IEEE 1003.1c)

Pthreads is the **POSIX standard** thread API, primarily for UNIX/Linux systems. It may be implemented as either user-level or kernel-level. The reference specification is **IEEE 1003.1c-1995**.

### 2.2.2 Win32 Threads

The **Windows API** (Win32) provides thread management routines. Threads are kernel objects. Common functions: `CreateThread`, `WaitForSingleObject`, `ExitThread`, `GetExitCodeThread`.

### 2.2.3 Java Threads

Java provides a **high-level, object-oriented** threading model. The `java.lang.Thread` class and `java.lang.Runnable` interface are the building blocks. **JVM threads are typically mapped to host OS threads** (Pthreads on Linux, Win32 threads on Windows).

## 2.3 Pthreads — Core API Function Reference

The following table lists the most-tested Pthreads routines in KTU examinations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The tables below use `\vert` and `\mid` to render absolute-value bars so that the markdown table structure is preserved.

| Pthread Routine | Purpose | Header File |
|-----------------|---------|-------------|
| `pthread_create` | Create a new thread | `pthread.h` |
| `pthread_exit` | Terminate calling thread | `pthread.h` |
| `pthread_join` | Wait for thread termination | `pthread.h` |
| `pthread_yield` | Release CPU to let another thread run | `pthread.h` |
| `pthread_attr_init` | Initialize thread attributes | `pthread.h` |
| `pthread_attr_destroy` | Destroy attributes object | `pthread.h` |
| `pthread_self` | Get ID of calling thread | `pthread.h` |
| `pthread_equal` | Compare two thread IDs | `pthread.h` |
| `pthread_cancel` | Request thread cancellation | `pthread.h` |
| `pthread_cleanup_push` | Register cleanup handler | `pthread.h` |
| `pthread_cleanup_pop` | Remove cleanup handler | `pthread.h` |

## 2.4 Pthread Attribute Object — Key Fields

| Attribute Field | Description | Typical Default |
|-----------------|-------------|-----------------|
| `detachstate` | Joinable or detached | `PTHREAD_CREATE_JOINABLE` |
| `schedpolicy` | Scheduling policy (e.g., SCHED_FIFO, SCHED_RR) | `SCHED_OTHER` |
| `inheritsched` | Inherit scheduler from creating thread | `PTHREAD_INHERIT_SCHED` |
| `scope` | Contention scope (process or system) | `PTHREAD_SCOPE_PROCESS` |
| `stacksize` | Stack size of new thread | `2 MB` (configurable) |

## 2.5 Thread Cancellation — Two Models

| Cancellation Type | Behavior | Use Case |
|-------------------|----------|----------|
| **Asynchronous Cancellation** | Target thread terminated **immediately**, at any point | Rarely used — unsafe; may leave resources locked |
| **Deferred Cancellation** | Target thread checks at **cancellation points** whether to terminate | **Preferred** — clean resource cleanup |

**Cancellation Points** are predefined safe locations in POSIX where a thread checks for pending cancellation requests (e.g., `pthread_testcancel`, blocking I/O calls).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded RTOS design** — VxWorks, FreeRTOS, and QNX expose POSIX Pthreads as their primary API.
- **IoT & Robotics** — Sensor fusion tasks split across multiple Pthreads with **SCHED_FIFO** policy.
- **Aerospace & Automotive (AUTOSAR)** — Threads in AUTOSAR OS map closely to POSIX threads with priority ceiling.
- **High-performance servers** — Linux Apache/Nginx use Pthreads to handle thousands of simultaneous client requests.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Conceptual Distinctions

| Concept | User-Level | Kernel-Level |
|---------|------------|--------------|
| Where is TCB stored? | User space (library) | Kernel space |
| Thread creation cost | Low (no syscall) | High (syscall) |
| Blocking calls | Block entire process | Block only calling thread |
| SMP parallelism | Limited | Yes |
| Custom scheduling | Full flexibility | Constrained by kernel scheduler |
| Example library | GNU `Pth`, `Green threads` | Linux `NPTL`, Win32 threads |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step API Analysis & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Pthread Creation — Full Operational Breakdown

The signature of `pthread_create` is:

$$
\text{int pthread\_create(pthread\_t \star thread, const pthread\_attr\_t \star attr,}
$$
$$
\text{void \star (\star start\_routine)(void \star), void \star arg);}
$$

**Step-by-step explanation of each parameter:**

- **`pthread_t *thread`** → Output: receives the **thread identifier (TID)** of the newly created thread. TID is of type `unsigned long` and is unique within the process.
- **`const pthread_attr_t *attr`** → Input: pointer to an attribute object. Pass `NULL` to use **default attributes** (joinable, default stack size, default scheduling).
- **`void *(*start_routine)(void *)`** → Input: pointer to the function the new thread will execute. The thread starts running this function.
- **`void *arg`** → Input: single argument passed to `start_routine`. If multiple values are needed, pass a pointer to a struct.

**Return value:**

$$
\text{return} = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{on success} \\ \text{nonzero error code} & \text{on failure} \end{cases}
$$

Note: Pthreads functions return the **error code directly** instead of setting the global `errno`.

## 3.2 Pthread Lifecycle — Sequence of Operations

The complete lifecycle of a typical Pthread follows this sequence:

**Step 1: Declaration**

$$
\text{pthread\_t tid;}
$$

A variable of type `pthread_t` is declared. This variable will hold the identifier assigned by the kernel/library when the thread is created.

**Step 2: (Optional) Attribute Initialization**

$$
\text{pthread\_attr\_init(\&attr);}
$$
$$
\text{pthread\_attr\_setdetachstate(\&attr, PTHREAD\_CREATE\_DETACHED);}
$$
$$
\text{pthread\_attr\_setstacksize(\&attr, 1 \cdot 1024 \cdot 1024); \quad \text{(1 MB stack)}
$$

Attributes are configured **before** thread creation. After creation, attributes can be destroyed to free memory.

**Step 3: Thread Creation**

$$
\text{rc = pthread\_create(\&tid, \&attr, runner, arg);}
$$

- If `rc == 0` → thread creation succeeded; the new thread begins executing `runner(arg)` **concurrently** with the parent.
- If `rc != 0` → error; the function returns the error number directly (e.g., `EAGAIN`, `EINVAL`, `EPERM`).

**Step 4: Thread Execution (Concurrent)**

Both the original (calling) thread and the new thread execute **independently**. Either may finish first. The runtime interleaves their execution based on the scheduler.

**Step 5: Wait for Termination — `pthread_join`**

$$
\text{void \star status;}
$$
$$
\text{rc = pthread\_join(tid, \&status);}
$$

- The calling thread **blocks** until the thread with ID `tid` terminates.
- `status` receives the value passed to `pthread_exit` (or the return value of `start_routine`).
- After `pthread_join` returns, the resources of the joined thread are reclaimed.
- Only **one thread** can call `pthread_join` for a given target thread.

**Step 6: (Optional) Attribute Cleanup**

$$
\text{pthread\_attr\_destroy(\&attr);}
$$

Frees internal storage used by the attribute object. Does **not** affect threads already created with it.

**Step 7: Implicit or Explicit Exit**

A thread can terminate in two ways:

$$
\text{// Implicit — return from start\_routine}
$$
$$
\text{return (void \star) result;}
$$
$$
\text{// Explicit — call pthread\_exit from anywhere}
$$
$$
\text{pthread\_exit((void \star) result);}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`pthread_exit` vs. `exit`**: `pthread_exit` terminates **only the calling thread**, leaving other threads in the process alive. The standard C `exit()` function terminates the **entire process**, killing all threads.

## 3.3 Thread Cancellation — Step-by-Step Logic

### 3.3.1 Asynchronous Cancellation

$$
\text{pthread\_cancel(target\_tid);}
$$

**Operational Behavior:**

- Cancellation request is delivered **immediately** at an arbitrary machine instruction.
- No opportunity for the target thread to run cleanup handlers.
- **Dangerous** — can leave mutexes locked, memory allocated, files open.
- The KTU 2024 syllabus flags this as a **pitfall scenario**.

### 3.3.2 Deferred Cancellation (Recommended)

**Step 1: Disable cancellation at critical sections**

$$
\text{int oldtype;}
$$
$$
\text{pthread\_setcancelstate(PTHREAD\_CANCEL\_DISABLE, \&oldtype);}
$$

**Step 2: Re-enable at safe points**

$$
\text{pthread\_setcancelstate(PTHREAD\_CANCEL\_ENABLE, \&oldtype);}
$$

**Step 3: Explicit cancellation-point check**

$$
\text{pthread\_testcancel();}
$$

The thread will only terminate when it calls `pthread_testcancel()` (or hits a built-in cancellation point such as `read`, `write`, `sleep`, `pthread_cond_wait`).

**Step 4: Register cleanup handlers (recommended for safety)**

$$
\text{void cleanup\_handler(void \star arg) \{} \quad \text{munlock((int)arg);} \quad \text{free(shared\_resource);} \quad \text{\}}
$$
$$
\text{pthread\_cleanup\_push(cleanup\_handler, (void \star)lock\_id);}
$$
$$
\text{// ... critical section ...}
$$
$$
\text{pthread\_cleanup\_pop(1);  \quad // 1 = execute handler on exit/cancel}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in RTOS**: Deferred cancellation guarantees **bounded cleanup time** — a hard requirement for real-time systems. Asynchronous cancellation violates this, which is why the KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes deferred cancellation.

## 3.4 Java Threads — Step-by-Step Mapping

Java supports two ways to create a thread:

**Method 1: Extend `Thread` class**

$$
\text{class Worker extends Thread \{} \quad \text{public void run() \{} \quad \text{// task code} \quad \text{\}} \quad \text{\}}
$$
$$
\text{Worker w = new Worker();}
$$
$$
\text{w.start();  \quad // creates new JVM thread, calls run()}
$$

**Method 2: Implement `Runnable` interface (preferred)**

$$
\text{class Task implements Runnable \{} \quad \text{public void run() \{} \quad \text{// task code} \quad \text{\}} \quad \text{\}}
$$
$$
\text{Thread t = new Thread(new Task());}
$$
$$
\text{t.start();}
$$

**Step-by-step mapping to Pthreads:**

| Java Construct | Pthreads Equivalent |
|----------------|--------------------|
| `new Thread(r)` | — (object creation only) |
| `t.start()` | `pthread_create(...)` |
| `Thread.sleep(ms)` | `nanosleep()` or `usleep()` |
| `t.join()` | `pthread_join()` |
| `Thread.yield()` | `pthread_yield()` or `sched_yield()` |
| `t.interrupt()` | `pthread_cancel()` |
| `Thread.currentThread()` | `pthread_self()` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The JVM itself uses Pthreads (or Win32 threads) on the host. So a Java thread is ultimately a wrapper around an OS thread — this is the "Java on top of host OS" model.

## 3.5 Win32 Threads — Key API Walkthrough

The Windows API provides the following functions, which the KTU 2024 syllabus asks students to compare with Pthreads.

| Win32 Function | Pthreads Equivalent | Purpose |
|----------------|--------------------|---------|
| `CreateThread` | `pthread_create` | Create new thread |
| `WaitForSingleObject` | `pthread_join` | Wait for thread |
| `GetExitCodeThread` | (return value of `pthread_join`) | Get termination code |
| `ExitThread` | `pthread_exit` | Terminate calling thread |
| `CreateMutex` | `pthread_mutex_init` | Create mutex |
| `CreateEvent` | `pthread_cond_init` | Create synchronization event |
| `ResumeThread`/`SuspendThread` | (no direct equivalent) | Suspend/resume |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus typically asks students to **outline the Win32 API** rather than to write full code. Understanding the **functional mapping** to Pthreads is the key.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Insight — Thread Creation Latency

Consider a real-time system where:

- **Pthread creation time** $T_{c} = 50 \,\mu s$ (typical Linux NPTL)
- **Kernel scheduling tick** $T_{tick} = 1 \,ms$
- **Thread computation** $T_{exec} = 200 \,\mu s$

**Total time from `pthread_create` to thread completion:**

$$
T_{total} = T_{c} + T_{tick} + T_{exec}
$$
$$
T_{total} = 50 + 1000 + 200
$$
$$
T_{total} = 1250 \,\mu s = 1.25 \,ms
$$

**Interpretation:** A real-time designer must ensure that thread creation latency plus execution time fits within the system's **worst-case deadline**. This is a frequent KTU 2024 question in Part A.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

The following Mermaid block renders the **classification hierarchy** of thread libraries as a top-down block-level functional architecture flow.

```mermaid
    A0 --> B2[Win32 Threads]

    B1 --> C2[Kernel Level Implementation]
    B2 --> D1[Kernel Object Model]
    B2 --> D2[Windows API]
    B3 --> E1[JVM Managed Threads]
    B3 --> E2[Built On Host OS Threads]
    E2 --> F1[Maps To Pthreads on Linux]
    E2 --> F2[Maps To Win32 on Windows]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — User-Level vs Kernel-Level Thread Architecture

This block diagram contrasts the **two principal implementation approaches** and how the kernel perceives them differently.

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        UL1[Process P]:::pbox
        UL2[Thread Table In User Space]:::ubox

        UL1 --> UL3
        UL1 --> UL4
    end

    end
```

This functional flow shows the **decision logic** that distinguishes the two cancellation models.

```mermaid
    D1 -->|Yes| E1[Thread Terminates and Runs Cleanup Handlers]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part A questions test **Recall and Understanding** (Bloom Levels L1–L2). KTU 2024 expects concise, definition-grade answers of **40–70 words** per question.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a thread library. List the three common thread libraries discussed in the KTU 2024 syllabus.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A thread library is a software package that provides the programmer with an **API for creating and managing threads** within a process. The three common thread libraries are:

- **POSIX Pthreads** — IEEE 1003.1c standard, used on UNIX/Linux systems.
- **Win32 threads** — provided by the Microsoft Windows API.
- **Java threads** — provided by the JVM and mapped to the host OS thread library.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between user-level threads and kernel-level threads.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | User-Level Threads | Kernel-Level Threads |
|--------|--------------------|----------------------|
| Thread management | In user-space library | In kernel |
| Kernel awareness | Kernel sees one process | Kernel sees each thread |
| Thread creation cost | Low | High (system call) |
| Blocking behavior | Whole process blocks | Only the thread blocks |
| SMP parallelism | Limited | Supported |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part B questions expect **analytical, descriptive, and diagram-supported answers** spanning both **Application** and **Analysis** (Bloom Levels L3–L4). The examiner values **diagrams, comparative tables, and code/structural snippets** as visual evidence of concept mastery.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) Explain the two different approaches to implementing a thread library with neat diagrams. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Approach 1: User-Level Library**

In this approach, all thread management code is in **user space**, and the kernel is unaware of the threads. The library maintains its own **thread table** (PCB-like structures for each thread), including stack pointer, program counter, and register state. A **local scheduler** runs inside the process and decides which thread to execute by **context switching** between threads.

**Advantages:** Thread creation/switching is fast (no system call); the library can use a custom scheduling algorithm.

**Disadvantages:** When one thread makes a blocking system call, the **entire process blocks** because the kernel sees only the process. The system is also **not aware of threads**, so true SMP parallelism is not possible.

**Approach 2: Kernel-Level Library**

In this approach, the kernel provides all thread management via **system calls**. The kernel maintains a **thread table** with one entry per thread. Each thread is an independent schedulable entity.

**Advantages:** Individual threads can block without affecting others. **True SMP parallelism** is supported.

**Disadvantages:** Thread management is **slower** because every operation requires a **system call** (user-to-kernel mode switch).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's tip:** Draw the **User-Level vs Kernel-Level architecture** diagram from Section 4.3 to earn full visual credit.

---

**(b) With a suitable example, illustrate the Pthreads API for thread creation, joining, and cancellation. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Pthreads API follows this pattern:

**Step 1 — Declare thread and attributes:**

$$
\text{pthread\_t tid;}
$$
$$
\text{pthread\_attr\_t attr;}
$$

**Step 2 — Initialize attributes (optional):**

$$
\text{pthread\_attr\_init(\&attr);}
$$
$$
\text{pthread\_attr\_setdetachstate(\&attr, PTHREAD\_CREATE\_JOINABLE);}
$$

**Step 3 — Create the thread:**

$$
\text{rc = pthread\_create(\&tid, \&attr, runner, (void \star)\&arg);}
$$

A new thread starts executing function `runner` with `arg` as its parameter.

**Step 4 — Wait for completion:**

$$
\text{rc = pthread\_join(tid, (void \star \star)\&status);}
$$

The calling thread blocks until `tid` terminates.

**Step 5 — Cancellation:**

- **Asynchronous** — `pthread_cancel(tid)`; cancellation occurs at any point. **Risky** in real-time systems.
- **Deferred** — Cancellation request is delivered only at **cancellation points** (e.g., `pthread_testcancel`, blocking I/O). **Safe** and recommended for RTOS.

**Step 6 — Cleanup attributes:**

$$
\text{pthread\_attr\_destroy(\&attr);}
$$

**Flow Summary:**

$$
\text{Declare} \rightarrow \text{Init Attr} \rightarrow \text{Create} \rightarrow \text{Execute} \rightarrow \text{Join/Cancel} \rightarrow \text{Destroy}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) Discuss the Win32 thread API and Java thread API in detail. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Win32 Thread API**

The Microsoft Windows thread API exposes threads as **kernel objects**. Each thread has a handle, ID, context, and security descriptor.

**Key Functions:**

- `CreateThread()` — Creates a new thread; takes function pointer, parameter, and creation flags.
- `WaitForSingleObject()` — Blocks the calling thread until the target thread terminates.
- `GetExitCodeThread()` — Retrieves the exit code of a terminated thread.
- `ExitThread()` — Terminates the calling thread; the entire process is **not** killed.
- `CreateMutex()`, `CreateEvent()` — Synchronization primitives.

**Distinctive Features:**

- Threads are kernel objects — they can be **passed across processes**.
- The `SuspendThread()` / `ResumeThread()` primitives have **no direct Pthreads equivalent**.

**Java Thread API**

Java provides two ways to create a thread:

- **Extending `Thread` class** — override `run()`.
- **Implementing `Runnable` interface** — preferred because it allows the class to extend another class.

**Key Methods:**

- `start()` — creates a new JVM thread, calls `run()`.
- `run()` — entry point of the new thread.
- `join()` — wait for thread completion.
- `sleep(ms)` — suspend for at least the specified duration.
- `yield()` — hint to scheduler to give up CPU.
- `interrupt()` — sets the interrupt flag; thread checks via `Thread.interrupted()` or `isInterrupted()`.

**Thread States:** New → Runnable → Blocked/Waiting → Timed Waiting → Terminated.

**Host OS Mapping:** JVM threads are mapped to **Pthreads on Linux/UNIX** or **Win32 threads on Windows** — a "many-to-one" or "one-to-one" mapping depending on JVM version.

---

**(b) What is thread cancellation? Differentiate between asynchronous and deferred cancellation. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Thread Cancellation** is the mechanism by which one thread terminates the execution of another thread before its normal completion.

**Asynchronous Cancellation:**

- One thread **immediately** terminates the target thread.
- Resources held by the target thread (mutexes, allocated memory, file descriptors) **may not be released** properly.
- This leads to **inconsistent system state**.
- **Not safe for real-time systems** because worst-case cleanup time is **unbounded**.

**Deferred Cancellation:**

- The target thread is **periodically polled** for cancellation requests.
- Cancellation occurs only at **cancellation points** — predefined safe locations.
- Cleanup handlers (registered via `pthread_cleanup_push`) run before termination.
- **Bounded cleanup time** — required for **real-time predictability**.

**Comparison:**

| Property | Asynchronous | Deferred |
|----------|--------------|----------|
| Timing of termination | Immediate | At next cancellation point |
| Cleanup handlers | Not run | Run automatically |
| Safety | Unsafe | Safe |
| Real-time suitability | Poor | Excellent |
| Default in Pthreads | Not default | Default (`PTHREAD_CANCEL_DEFERRED`) |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 
> **Common reasons students lose marks on Thread Library questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing `pthread_exit` with `exit`** — Pthread-level `pthread_exit` kills only the calling thread; C-level `exit` kills the entire process including all threads. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this confusion.
> 
> 2. **Writing that user-level threads support SMP parallelism** — this is **wrong**; only kernel-level threads can be scheduled in parallel on multiple cores. Common 3-mark deduction.
> 
> 3. **Stating that `pthread_join` can be called by multiple threads for the same target** — this is **undefined behavior**; only **one** thread should join a given target.
> 
> 4. **Recommending asynchronous cancellation in real-time contexts** — examiners **will deduct** for this. Always use **deferred cancellation** in RTOS.
> 
> 5. **Omitting return value semantics** — Pthread functions return the error code directly; they do **not** set `errno`. Mentioning `errno` is incorrect.
> 
> 6. **Skipping the diagram in implementation comparison questions** — KTU 2024 awards **2 marks** specifically for a clearly labelled block diagram contrasting user-level vs kernel-level.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist — Thread Libraries**

- **Definition:** A thread library is an **API for creating and managing threads** without explicit kernel programming.
- **Two implementation approaches:** **User-level (ULT)** and **Kernel-level (KLT)** — know the trade-offs.
- **Three standard libraries:** **Pthreads (POSIX)**, **Win32 threads**, **Java threads**.
- **POSIX Pthreads** are defined in **`pthread.h`** and standardized as **IEEE 1003.1c-1995**.
- **Pthread core operations:** `pthread_create`, `pthread_exit`, `pthread_join`, `pthread_yield`, `pthread_cancel`, `pthread_cleanup_push/pop`, `pthread_attr_init/destroy`.
- **`pthread_create` parameters:** thread, attributes, start function, argument.
- **`pthread_exit` vs `exit`:** Pthread version ends only the calling thread; standard `exit()` ends the whole process.
- **Asynchronous cancellation** = immediate; **deferred cancellation** = at cancellation points. **Deferred is the KTU-preferred answer for real-time systems.**
- **Cancellation points** are predefined safe spots (`pthread_testcancel`, `read`, `write`, `sleep`, `pthread_cond_wait`).
- **Win32 distinctive features:** threads are kernel objects, can cross process boundaries; `Suspend/Resume` has no direct Pthreads equivalent.
- **Java threads** are managed by the **JVM** and ultimately mapped to **Pthreads on Linux** or **Win32 threads on Windows**.
- **Pthread function returns error code directly** — they do **not** set `errno`.
- **User-level threads block the entire process on blocking system calls**; kernel-level threads block **only themselves**.
- **Default thread attribute:** joinable, default stack size **2 MB**, default scheduler `SCHED_OTHER`.
- **`pthread_join` is called by only one thread per target** — calling multiple times is undefined.
- **For KTU RTOS questions**, always emphasize **bounded latency, predictable behavior, and deferred cancellation**.
- **Real-time context:** Use **kernel-level Pthreads with `SCHED_FIFO` or `SCHED_RR`** scheduling policies to achieve real-time responsiveness.
- **Mapping identity to remember:** `Thread.sleep` ↔ `nanosleep`, `t.join` ↔ `pthread_join`, `t.interrupt` ↔ `pthread_cancel`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synchronization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT741 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Synchronization # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Synchronization in Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS)** is defined as the coordination mechanism used to control the concurrent access of multiple tasks to shared resources, ensuring **temporal correctness, mutual exclusion**, and **deterministic execution order** in order to satisfy hard/soft timing constraints prescribed by the application. In a preemptive, multitasking RTOS kernel, tasks (also called *threads of execution*) can be interrupted and rescheduled at any instant. When two or more tasks attempt to access a **Critical Section** (a shared resource like a global variable, hardware register, peripheral, or buffer) simultaneously, the result is a **Race Condition**, leading to non-deterministic behavior — which is unacceptable in a *hard* real-time system. Synchronization primitives therefore enforce three orthogonal guarantees: 1. **Mutual Exclusion** — Only one task may reside in a given critical section at a time. 2. **Conditional Synchronization** — A task may be forced to wait until a specific event/condition becomes true. 3. **Bounded Blocking** — Blocking time must be predictable and within deadline bounds (this is the RT-specific property that distinguishes RTOS synchronization from general-purpose OS synchronization). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners) Imagine a **single-stall public restroom** in a railway station: - The restroom is the **shared resource** (Critical Section). - People waiting to use it are the **tasks**. - The door lock (with a red/green indicator) is the **semaphore/mutex**. Rules enforced: - Only **one person** can be inside at any time → **Mutual Exclusion**. - People form a **queue**; once inside finishes, the next person enters → **FIFO wake-up ordering** (when applicable). - The station manager must guarantee that VIP passengers do not starve general passengers forever, and the queue does not deadlock if two people somehow block each other from the *same* resource → **Priority Inheritance & Deadlock Avoidance**. If the lock is misused (e.g., a person forgets to unlock before leaving), the resource is "leaked," and all subsequent waiters block indefinitely. The RTOS synchronization primitives are the engineering tools that prevent such catastrophes with **deterministic, bounded** behavior. ## 1.3 Synchronization Primitives — Taxonomy The KTU 2024 syllabus primarily covers the following primitives under Module 3: | Primitive | Purpose | Typical Kernel | |---|---|---| | **Binary Semaphore** | Signalling / task-to-task synchronization | VxWorks, μC/OS-II | | **Counting Semaphore** | Resource count management (N identical units) | POSIX, FreeRTOS | | **Mutex (MUT-EX)** | Mutual exclusion with ownership & Priority Inheritance | VxWorks, QNX, RTLinux | | **Event Flags** | Multi-condition AND/OR synchronization | μC/OS-II, VxWorks | | **Message Queue** | Inter-task message passing (synchronization + data) | All major RTOS | | **Spinlock** | Short-duration busy-wait lock (multicore) | Linux-RT, SMP RTOS | > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (PECPT741 / Module 3):** The official KTU 2024 Scheme weightage allocates roughly **15–20%** of the module marks to *Priority Inversion, Inheritance, and Ceiling* problems, which are RTOS-specific phenomena. Mastering these guarantees exam success. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Synchronization ### 2.1.1 Critical Section — The Core Problem A **Critical Section** is any segment of code that accesses a shared resource. The classical correctness conditions (Dijkstra, 1965) are: 1. **Mutual Exclusion** — No two tasks are in the critical section simultaneously. 2. **Progress** — If no task is in the critical section and some task wishes to enter, only that task can decide which other waiting task enters (no outside interference). 3. **Bounded Waiting** — A bound exists on the number of times other tasks can enter the critical section after a task has requested entry and before its request is granted (prevents starvation). ### 2.1.2 Semaphores (Dijkstra, 1965) A semaphore is a non-negative integer variable `S` accessed **only** via two atomic (indivisible) operations: - `P(S)` — **Proberen (Test/Down/Wait/Acquire):** - If `S > 0`, decrement `S` by 1 and proceed. - If `S = 0`, **block** the calling task on `S`'s wait queue. - `V(S)` — **Verhogen (Increment/Up/Signal/Release):** - Increment `S` by 1. - If one or more tasks are blocked, wake **one** of them (FIFO or by priority). Atomicity is guaranteed by disabling interrupts or by hardware-supported atomic instructions like `compare-and-swap` (CAS), `test-and-set`, or `fetch-add`. ### 2.1.3 Types of Semaphores **a) Binary Semaphore** — Initial value `S ∈ {0, 1}`. - Used purely for **signalling** (e.g., "data ready" between ISR and task). - **Has no ownership concept** — any task can `V()` a semaphore that another task `P()`'d. **b) Counting Semaphore** — Initial value `S ∈ {0, 1, 2, ..., N}`. - Represents `N` identical instances of a resource (e.g., 3 UART ports). - Maximum value: `S_max = N`. **c) Mutex (Mutual Exclusion Lock)** — Variant of binary semaphore **with ownership, priority inheritance, and recursive locking**. - Only the **owner task** may release the mutex. - Prevents **unbounded priority inversion** (see §2.1.5). ### 2.1.4 Why Mutex ≠ Binary Semaphore (A Common KTU Trap) | Property | Binary Semaphore | Mutex | |---|---|---| | Ownership | No | Yes (owner tracked) | | Priority Inheritance | No (typically) | Yes | | Recursive Locking | No (undefined) | Yes (counter inside) | | Intent | Signalling | Mutual exclusion | | Clean-up on Task Death | Resource leaked | Auto-released | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Trap:** Many students use the terms *binary semaphore* and *mutex* interchangeably. The KTU valuation key **deducts marks** if the distinction (especially **priority inheritance**) is not stated. ### 2.1.5 Priority Inversion Problem Classic scenario (explained via 3 tasks: L, M, H with priorities Low < Medium < High): 1. **Task L** acquires mutex on shared resource `R`. 2. **Task H** becomes ready, preempts L, tries to lock `R`, **blocks** on L. 3. **Task M** (medium priority) becomes ready, preempts L (which still holds `R`). 4. **H cannot run** (waiting on `R`), **M runs in the middle**, and **L never gets CPU** until M finishes. 5. Result: A medium-priority task delays a high-priority task — a **logical priority inversion**. The blocking time of `H` becomes **unbounded** because it depends on `M`'s execution — a violation of the **bounded blocking** principle required in hard RT. ### 2.1.6 Solutions Implemented in Modern RTOS Kernels **A) Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** - When a high-priority task `H` blocks on a resource held by a lower-priority task `L`, the kernel **temporarily raises L's priority to H's** (or to the maximum of all H's blocked on L's resources). - Effect: `L` cannot be preempted by medium-priority tasks for the duration of resource holding, so it finishes the critical section quickly and releases the mutex. - Maximum blocking time: bounded by the **longest critical section of any lower-priority task**. **B) Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)** - Each resource is assigned a **ceiling priority** = max priority of any task that may lock it. - A task, on locking, is **immediately raised to the ceiling priority** of that resource. - Guarantees deadlock-free mutual exclusion with bounded blocking = at most **one critical section** of any lower-priority task. **C) Stack Resource Policy (SRP)** - Extension of PCP. Allows nested resource access without deadlock. Used in **RTEMS, POSIX with extensions**. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are **exam-grade essentials** and appear frequently in KTU Part A 3-mark questions and Part B derivations. | # | Concept / Formula | Expression | Notes / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Semaphore atomic decrement | $S_{new} = S_{old} - 1$ | Only if $S_{old} \geq 1$ | | 2 | Semaphore atomic increment | $S_{new} = S_{old} + 1$ | Upper bounded by initial value for counting | | 3 | Max blocking time (PIP) | $B_{max} = \max_{L \in \text{tasks}}(CS_L)$ | Bounded by longest lower-priority CS | | 4 | Max blocking time (PCP) | $B_{max} = CS_{single}$ | At most one CS of any L task | | 5 | Resource ceiling | $\Pi_{ceiling}(R) = \max_{T \in \text{users}(R)} \Pi_T$ | $\Pi$ denotes task priority | | 6 | Number of context switches (CS) per `P/V` pair | $N_{ctx} \leq 2$ | One for block, one for wake | | 7 | Mutex internal counter (recursive) | $C = C + 1$ on lock; $C = C - 1$ on unlock | Mutex freed when $C = 0$ | | 8 | Counting semaphore invariant | $0 \leq S \leq S_{max}$ | Maintained atomically | | 9 | Priority inversion duration (unbounded) | $T_{inv} \leq T_{M} + T_{CS(L)}$ | Without PIP | | 10 | Priority inversion duration (PIP) | $T_{inv} \leq T_{CS(L)}$ | Bounded; with PIP | > **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used during **Rate Monotonic Analysis (RMA)** and **response-time analysis** to compute the worst-case blocking term $B_i$ for each task — a mandatory step when certifying hard real-time systems like avionics (DO-178C), automotive ECUs (ISO 26262), and medical devices (IEC 62304). ## 2.3 Why Synchronization Is Harder in Real-Time Systems In a **general-purpose OS** (Windows/Linux), synchronization is allowed to introduce *unbounded* delays (e.g., page faults, disk I/O, scheduler decisions). In a **hard RTOS**, every blocking delay must be **statically provable**. This is why RTOS kernels typically: - Disallow dynamic memory allocation inside the kernel. - Use `O(1)` priority queue implementations (e.g., bitmap-based ready queue in μC/OS-II). - Avoid priority donation chains beyond one level (PIP limitation). - Provide **deadlock detection or prevention** primitives. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Proofs & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bounded Blocking Time under Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) **Theorem:** *Under PIP, the maximum time a high-priority task $\tau_H$ can be blocked by a lower-priority task $\tau_L$ on a shared mutex is bounded by the execution time of $\tau_L$'s longest critical section.* **Given:** - Task $\tau_H$ has priority $\Pi_H$. - Task $\tau_L$ has priority $\Pi_L < \Pi_H$. - $\tau_L$ is in critical section $CS_L$ with duration $T_{CS_L}$. **Proof (Constructive via Case Analysis):** **Step 1 — Initial state:** $\tau_L$ is executing normally at priority $\Pi_L$. **Step 2 — $\tau_H$ preempts** $\tau_L$ (because $\Pi_H > \Pi_L$) and executes until it attempts `P(M)`. **Step 3 — $\tau_H$ blocks** on mutex $M$ held by $\tau_L$. **Step 4 — Inheritance rule triggers:** Kernel detects $M$ held by $\tau_L$, and $\tau_H$ blocked on $M$. Kernel raises $\tau_L$'s effective priority to: $$\Pi_L^{eff} = \max(\Pi_L, \Pi_H)$$ **Step 5 — Medium-priority task $\tau_M$ arrives** with $\Pi_L < \Pi_M \leq \Pi_L^{eff}$. Since $\Pi_M \leq \Pi_L^{eff}$, $\tau_M$ **cannot preempt** $\tau_L$. **Step 6 — $\tau_L$ continues** executing $CS_L$ to completion (only possible preemption is from $\tau_L^{eff} = \Pi_H$, but $\tau_H$ is blocked, so no preemption occurs). **Step 7 — $\tau_L$ executes `V(M)`**, releases the mutex, and kernel reverts $\tau_L$'s priority to $\Pi_L$. **Step 8 — $\tau_H$ is unblocked**, acquires $M$, and resumes. **Step 9 — Total blocking time:** $$B_H = T_{CS_L} - t_{elapsed}$$ At worst, $\tau_L$ had just entered $CS_L$ when $\tau_H$ blocked, so: $$B_H^{max} = T_{CS_L}$$ **Step 10 — Conclusion:** The blocking is bounded by the longest $CS_L$ of any lower-priority task that can lock a resource $\tau_H$ also requires. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Response Time Analysis with Blocking Term (RTA) The standard **response time** $R_i$ of task $\tau_i$ in a fixed-priority preemptive system with synchronization blocking is computed iteratively: $$R_i^{(n+1)} = C_i + B_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(n)}}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$ where: - $C_i$ = worst-case computation time of $\tau_i$. - $B_i$ = worst-case blocking time on shared resources. - $T_j$ = period of higher-priority task $j$. - $hp(i)$ = set of higher-priority tasks. **Iteration procedure:** - **Initial guess:** $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$. - **Iterate** until $R_i^{(n+1)} = R_i^{(n)}$ (converged) or $R_i^{(n+1)} > D_i$ (deadline missed). - **Schedulability test:** $R_i \leq D_i$ for all $i$. **The blocking term $B_i$ is computed differently per protocol:** $$ B_i = \begin{cases} \max_{k \in lp(i)} \bigl( \text{usage}_i(R_k) \cdot CS_k \bigr) & \text{under PIP} \\ \max_{k \in lp(i)} CS_k & \text{under PCP} \end{cases} $$ where $lp(i)$ denotes the set of lower-priority tasks that share at least one resource with $\tau_i$. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Primitive | Scenario / Constraint | Recommended Primitive | Justification | |---|---|---| | ISR → Task notification | Binary Semaphore | Lightweight, no ownership needed | | Single shared resource (single core) | Mutex | Ownership + PIP prevents inversion | | N identical buffer slots | Counting Semaphore | Tracks available instances | | Multiple event conditions (any/all) | Event Flags | AND/OR semantics in one wait | | Inter-task data with synchronization | Message Queue | Bundles sync + data transfer | | Short critical section on multicore | Spinlock | Avoids context switch overhead | | Hard RT with strict ceiling | Mutex + PCP | Provable bounded blocking | | Producer-Consumer with bounded buffer | Counting Semaphore × 2 | One for slots, one for items | ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) **Problem:** A real-time system has three tasks with priorities: - $\tau_H$: priority **10** (highest) - $\tau_M$: priority **5** - $\tau_L$: priority **1** (lowest) All three share a mutex $M$. Compute the worst-case blocking time $B_H$ for $\tau_H$ under: 1. **No synchronization protocol** (naive binary semaphore). 2. **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP).** **Given critical section times:** - $CS_L = 10$ ms - $CS_M = 5$ ms - $CS_H = 3$ ms - Worst-case execution of $\tau_M$ excluding CS: $C_M^{other} = 20$ ms. **Solution Case 1 — No protocol:** From the textbook inversion scenario, $\tau_H$ blocks for the full duration of $CS_L$ **plus** the preemption by $\tau_M$: $$B_H^{(1)} = CS_L + C_M^{other} + CS_M = 10 + 20 + 5 = 35 \text{ ms}$$ This is **unbounded in general** because $C_M^{other}$ depends on application code. **Solution Case 2 — With PIP:** When $\tau_H$ blocks on $M$, $\tau_L$ inherits priority **10**, becoming immune to $\tau_M$'s preemption: $$B_H^{(2)} = CS_L = 10 \text{ ms}$$ The blocking is now **bounded** and predictable. $\square$ > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Insight:** This 10 ms vs 35 ms difference (3.5×) is what makes PIP mandatory in safety-critical RTOS kernels like **VxWorks, QNX Neutrino, FreeRTOS (configurable), and RTEMS**. ## 3.5 Deadlock Conditions (Coffman Conditions) in RTOS Deadlock occurs if **all four** of the following hold simultaneously: 1. **Mutual Exclusion** — Resource is non-sharable. 2. **Hold and Wait** — Task holds one resource while waiting for another. 3. **No Preemption** — Resource cannot be forcibly taken. 4. **Circular Wait** — A cycle exists in the wait-for graph: $T_1 \rightarrow R_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow R_2 \rightarrow T_1$. **RTOS Prevention Strategy:** Use **lock ordering** (always acquire `R1` before `R2`) — this breaks **Circular Wait** and is the most common KTU-recommended method. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 State Transition Diagram — Binary Semaphore ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Unlocked : Initial value S=1 Unlocked --> Locked : P(S) / Wait / Take\n(S becomes 0, task enters CS) Locked --> Unlocked : V(S) / Signal / Give\n(S becomes 1, one waiter wakes) Locked --> Locked : V(S) when S=0\n(signal increments, waiter queued) Unlocked --> Unlocked : P(S) when S=1\n(task enters directly) note right of Locked If a task calls P(S) when S=0, it transitions to BLOCKED state and is added to the wait queue. end note ``` ## 4.2 Priority Inversion Timeline (Naive vs PIP) ```mermaid gantt title Priority Inversion: Naive vs Priority Inheritance Protocol dateFormat X axisFormat %s section Naive (No Protocol) Task L (low) running CS :active, naive1, 0, 10 Task H preempts, blocks on mutex :crit, naive2, 10, 12 Task M preempts L (unbounded!) :active, naive3, 12, 32 Task L resumes CS :active, naive4, 32, 40 Task H finally runs :active, naive5, 40, 50 section With PIP Task L (low) running CS :active, pip1, 0, 10 Task H preempts, blocks on mutex :crit, pip2, 10, 12 Task L inherits H priority, ignores M :active, pip3, 12, 22 Task H unblocks & runs :active, pip4, 22, 32 ``` ## 4.3 Mutex vs Binary Semaphore — Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Need to coordinate tasks?] --> B{Sharing a single
resource?} B -- No, just signalling --> C[Use Binary Semaphore] B -- Yes, mutual exclusion --> D{Need Priority
Inheritance?} D -- No --> E[Spinlock or simple lock] D -- Yes --> F[Use MUTEX] F --> G{Recursive locking
required?} G -- Yes --> H[Recursive Mutex] G -- No --> I[Standard Mutex] C --> J[ISR to Task] I --> K[Task to Task
with ownership] H --> L[Same task locks
multiple times] style A fill:#e1f5ff style F fill:#ffe1e1 style C fill:#e1ffe1 ``` ## 4.4 RTOS Synchronization Architecture Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph AppLayer["Application Layer"] T1[Task T1\nPriority 5] T2[Task T2\nPriority 10] T3[Task T3\nPriority 1] ISR[ISR Handler] end subgraph SyncLayer["RTOS Kernel Synchronization Layer"] SEM1[Binary Semaphore S1] SEM2[Counting Semaphore S2] MUT1[Mutex M1\n+ Priority Inheritance] EF1[Event Flag Group] end subgraph ResLayer["Shared Resources"] R1[UART Peripheral] R2[Buffer Pool\n8 slots] R3[Sensor Data] end T1 -->|P| SEM1 ISR -->|V| SEM1 SEM1 -.->|signals| T1 T2 -->|lock| MUT1 T3 -->|lock| MUT1 MUT1 -->|protects| R1 MUT1 -.->|inheritance| T3 T2 -->|acquire| SEM2 SEM2 --> R2 T1 -->|wait ALL| EF1 T2 -->|set flag| EF1 T3 -->|set flag| EF1 EF1 --> R3 style MUT1 fill:#ffcccc style SEM1 fill:#ccffcc style SEM2 fill:#ccffcc style EF1 fill:#ccccff ``` ## 4.5 Lock Ordering for Deadlock Prevention (RTOS Best Practice) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Task T1] -->|Acquire| R1[Resource R1\nLock Order: 1] A -->|Acquire| R2[Resource R2\nLock Order: 2] A -->|Release| R2 A -->|Release| R1 B[Task T2] -->|Acquire| R1 B -->|Acquire| R2 B -->|Release| R2 B -->|Release| R1 R1 -.->|Prevents circular wait| SAFETY[Deadlock Free] R2 -.->|Prevents circular wait| SAFETY style SAFETY fill:#90ee90 style R1 fill:#fff4cc style R2 fill:#fff4cc ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Differentiate between a binary semaphore and a mutex. Why is a mutex preferred for mutual exclusion in real-time systems?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Binary Semaphore | Mutex | |---|---|---| | Ownership | None — any task can signal | Strict ownership — only owner can release | | Priority Inheritance | Not supported | Supported | | Recursive Lock | Not defined | Allowed (internal counter) | A **mutex** is preferred for mutual exclusion in real-time systems because it includes **priority inheritance** by default, which prevents **unbounded priority inversion** and keeps the high-priority task's blocking time bounded and predictable. *(1 mark for differentiation, 2 marks for priority inheritance reasoning)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is priority inversion? How does the Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) solve it?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1.5 marks):** Priority inversion is a scheduling anomaly where a higher-priority task is indirectly preempted by a lower-priority task through a shared resource, while one or more medium-priority tasks execute, causing the high-priority task's blocking to be unbounded. - **Solution (1.5 marks):** PIP temporarily raises the priority of the lower-priority task holding the resource to that of the highest-priority task blocked on it. This prevents medium-priority tasks from preempting, bounding the blocking time to the duration of the critical section. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]* **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the following synchronization primitives used in RTOS with suitable examples: 1. Binary Semaphore 2. Counting Semaphore 3. Mutex **(b) [7 marks]** Describe the **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** with a clear diagram. Show how it bounds the blocking time of a high-priority task, and compute the worst-case blocking time given the following: - $\tau_H$ (priority = 8), $\tau_M$ (priority = 4), $\tau_L$ (priority = 1) - $CS_L = 12$ ms, $CS_M = 6$ ms, $CS_H = 4$ ms - Worst-case non-CS execution of $\tau_M$ = 18 ms. **Model Solution:** **(a) Primitives (7 marks):** **1. Binary Semaphore (2 marks):** - Non-negative integer `S ∈ {0, 1}`. - `P(S)`: if S>0, S--; else block. `V(S)`: S++, wake one waiter. - **Use case:** ISR signals a task via `V()`; task waits with `P()`. - *Example:* UART receive ISR sets `sem_uart_rx`; the parser task waits on it. **2. Counting Semaphore (2 marks):** - Integer `S ∈ {0, 1, ..., N}`, where N = number of identical resources. - `P()` decrements, `V()` increments. Wake waiters when S transitions 0→1. - *Example:* A pool of 4 identical CAN bus controllers accessed by 10 tasks. **3. Mutex (3 marks):** - Special binary semaphore with **ownership** and **priority inheritance**. - The task that locks the mutex must be the one to unlock it. - Supports **recursive locking** via an internal counter. - **Use case:** Protecting a single shared SPI bus among multiple sensor-reading tasks. **(b) PIP & Computation (7 marks):** **PIP Description (4 marks):** See the timeline diagram and the derivation in §3.1 above. Key points: when a high-priority task blocks on a mutex held by a lower-priority task, the kernel dynamically raises the lower task's priority to the maximum of all blocked higher-priority tasks' priorities. **Computation (3 marks):** Without PIP: $$B_H = CS_L + C_M^{other} + CS_M = 12 + 18 + 6 = 36 \text{ ms}$$ With PIP: $$B_H = CS_L = 12 \text{ ms}$$ Hence, PIP reduces worst-case blocking from **36 ms → 12 ms** (a 3× improvement, and more importantly, a *bounded* value). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Alternative Choice]* **(a) [7 marks]** With neat state diagrams, explain the operation of: 1. Counting Semaphore with wait queue. 2. Mutex with priority inheritance. **(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **four Coffman conditions** necessary for deadlock. How does the **lock ordering** strategy prevent deadlock in an RTOS environment? Illustrate with an example involving two tasks and two resources. **Model Solution Outline:** **(a) State Diagrams (7 marks — 3.5 each):** Refer to the state transition diagram in §4.1 and the priority inversion timeline in §4.2. Describe the wait queue, blocked state, and priority elevation mechanism. **(b) Coffman Conditions (7 marks):** 1. **Mutual Exclusion** (1.5 marks): Resource non-sharable. 2. **Hold and Wait** (1.5 marks): Task holds one resource while waiting for another. 3. **No Preemption** (1.5 marks): Kernel does not forcibly take resources. 4. **Circular Wait** (1.5 marks): Cyclic dependency in wait-for graph. **Lock Ordering Solution (1 mark):** Always acquire resources in a global fixed order. Example: `R1` before `R2` for all tasks. This breaks the circular wait condition. Show the correct sequence for `Task T1` and `Task T2` accessing `R1, R2` in the same order. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Confusing binary semaphore with mutex** — always mention ownership and PIP. > 2. **Forgetting the bounded-blocking requirement** in RTOS — PIP/PCP are *not* optional in hard RT. > 3. **Skipping diagrams** in Part B — a neat timeline/Gantt for priority inversion is worth **1–2 marks** by itself. > 4. **Stating "mutex prevents deadlock"** — mutex *prevents unbounded inversion*, but **does not prevent deadlock** by itself. Use lock ordering. > 5. **Using the term "lock" without specifying atomicity** — examiners expect mention of disabling interrupts / hardware atomic ops (test-and-set, CAS). > 6. **Ignoring ISR safety** — calling blocking primitives (`P`) inside an ISR is illegal in nearly all RTOS kernels; use `V` (signal) only. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Synchronization (RTOS Module 3)** - 🔹 **Critical Section:** Code accessing shared resources; must satisfy mutual exclusion, progress, and bounded waiting. - 🔹 **Semaphore `P/V`:** Atomic test-and-decrement / increment-and-wake. Foundation of all RTOS sync. - 🔹 **Binary vs Counting vs Mutex:** Binary = signal; Counting = N resources; Mutex = owned lock with PIP. - 🔹 **Mutex ≠ Binary Semaphore:** Ownership + priority inheritance are the key differentiators. - 🔹 **Priority Inversion:** A medium-priority task delays a high-priority task indirectly via a shared mutex held by a low-priority task. - 🔹 **PIP (Priority Inheritance):** Boosts holder's priority to the blocked task's priority → bounded blocking. - 🔹 **PCP (Priority Ceiling):** Pre-assigned ceiling priority per resource → deadlock-free, bounded to one CS. - 🔹 **SRP (Stack Resource Policy):** Extension of PCP; allows nested access; used in RTEMS. - 🔹 **Deadlock (Coffman):** Mutual Exclusion + Hold-and-Wait + No Preemption + Circular Wait. - 🔹 **Deadlock Prevention:** Lock ordering (most common); resource pre-allocation; avoid hold-and-wait. - 🔹 **Atomicity:** Kernel achieves `P/V` atomicity by disabling interrupts or hardware CAS/test-and-set. - 🔹 **ISR Rule:** ISR should only **signal** (`V`), never **wait** (`P`). - 🔹 **Bounded Blocking:** Hard RTOS requirement — blocking time must be statically provable. - 🔹 **RTA Blocking Term:** $R_i = C_i + B_i + \sum I_j$; schedulable iff $R_i \leq D_i$. - 🔹 **Event Flags:** Used when a task waits for multiple conditions (AND/OR semantics). - 🔹 **Message Queues:** Combine synchronization + data transfer in one primitive. - 🔹 **Spinlocks:** Suitable only for very short critical sections on multicore RTOS to avoid context switch cost. - 🔹 **Recursive Mutex:** Same task may lock multiple times; counter incremented/decremented; freed when count = 0. - 🔹 **Exam Frequency (KTU 2024):** Synchronization typically appears as **one full 14-mark question** in the ESE, often combined with priority inversion (which carries 3–5 marks within it). > **End of Module 3 — Synchronization Notes** ✔️

topics.title: Mutex: Creating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT741 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Mutex: Creating # Mutex: Creating - Foundational Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) A **Mutex (Mutual Exclusion Semaphore)** is a specialized binary semaphore used in Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS) to enforce exclusive access to a shared resource. In FreeRTOS, *creating* a mutex means dynamically or statically allocating the semaphore data structure, initializing its internal state (available/unlocked), and registering it in the kernel's object registry for use by tasks via the `xSemaphoreTake()` and `xSemaphoreGive()` API calls. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for PECPT741, mutex creation falls under the **Inter-Task Communication & Synchronization** domain, specifically the *Resource Synchronization* sub-module. A mutex is a **locking mechanism** — only the task that successfully *takes* the mutex is permitted to *give* it back, ensuring **mutual exclusion** at the resource level. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** > "A mutex is a binary semaphore that includes a **ownership mechanism** and **priority inheritance protocol**, used to prevent race conditions when multiple tasks compete for a shared resource." ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Washroom Key" Imagine a small office with **a single washroom and one key hanging on a hook outside the door**: - The **hook (mutex)** holds one key (token). - A **task that needs the washroom (shared resource)** must take the key (`xSemaphoreTake()`).
