> **The Critical Observation:** $T_H$ was delayed by **30 ms of $T_M$'s execution** — even though $T_H$ has *no* resource conflict with $T_M$ whatsoever. The 30 ms is *entirely* under $T_M$'s control and could be arbitrarily long. This is the **unbounded** inversion.

### Step 2: Redraw Under Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)

We re-simulate the same scenario, but now the kernel applies PIP:

1. $t = 0$: $T_L$ runs, acquires $M$.
2. $t = 5$: $T_H$ becomes ready, preempts $T_L$, attempts `lock(M)`, blocks.
3. **PIP Inheritance Trigger:** The kernel detects $T_L$ is blocking $T_H$. The kernel sets $P_{\text{eff}}(T_L) \leftarrow P(T_H) = 3$.
4. $t = 8$: $T_M$ becomes ready. The scheduler compares $P_{\text{eff}}(T_L) = 3$ vs $P(T_M) = 2$. Since $3 > 2$, **$T_M$ does NOT preempt $T_L$**. $T_L$ continues to run at inherited priority.
5. $T_L$ runs from $t=5$ to $t=20$ (its remaining 15 ms of critical section), finishes, releases $M$.
6. **PIP De-inheritance:** $T_L$'s effective priority reverts to $P_{\text{base}}(T_L) = 1$.
7. $T_H$ unblocks, runs immediately.
8. $T_M$ runs only after $T_H$ is done.

**Computation of Inversion Duration Under PIP:**

$$\text{Block}_H^{\text{PIP}} = 15 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(only the remaining CS of } T_L \text{)}$$

This is **bounded** — it equals exactly the remaining critical section of the resource holder, no more.

### Step 3: Analytical Matrix — Comparative Blocking Bounds

| Metric | Unprotected | With PIP | With PCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Blocking time of $T_H$ | **Unbounded** (depends on $T_M$) | $\le$ longest CS of any resource | $\le$ longest CS of any resource |
| Deadlock possible? | Yes | Yes (PIP alone does not prevent) | **No** |
| Implementation complexity | None | Medium (dynamic priority update) | High (static analysis + runtime checks) |
| Transitive inheritance? | N/A | Yes | N/A (single-shot ceiling) |
| KTU exam weight | High | **Highest** | Medium |

### Step 4: Deriving the Blocking-Time Bound under PIP (RTA)

For **Response Time Analysis (RTA)** of task $T_i$, the worst-case response time $R_i$ is:

$$R_i = C_i + B_i + \sum_{T_j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

where $hp(i)$ is the set of higher-priority tasks and $B_i$ is the worst-case blocking. Under PIP, the blocking term is bounded by the sum of the *shortest* critical section per resource that $T_i$ shares with any lower-priority task:

$$B_i^{\text{PIP}} = \sum_{M_k \in \text{resources}(T_i)} \min_{T_j : P(T_j) < P(T_i), T_j \text{ uses } M_k} \left( \text{CS}_{M_k, T_j} \right)$$

We solve this equation iteratively (the standard fixed-point iteration):

$$R_i^{(n+1)} = C_i + B_i + \sum_{T_j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(n)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

converging when $R_i^{(n+1)} = R_i^{(n)}$. The task is **schedulable** iff $R_i \le D_i$ (its deadline).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** This RTA equation is the foundation of schedulability analysis in industrial RTOS design. Tools like **RapiTask** (Rapita Systems) and **aiT** (AbsInt) implement this exact iteration to certify that avionics and automotive software meets its hard real-time deadlines.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mutex State Machine — Task-Side View

The following Mermaid state diagram depicts the lifecycle of a task attempting to lock a mutex:

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> READY
    READY --> RUNNING : scheduler_dispatch
    RUNNING --> RUNNING : lock_success_free
    RUNNING --> BLOCKED : lock_blocked_held
    BLOCKED --> READY : unblock_by_release
    READY --> RUNNING : scheduler_dispatch
    RUNNING --> [*] : task_terminates
    BLOCKED --> BLOCKED : timeout_pending
```

## 4.2 Priority Inheritance Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following diagram illustrates the runtime sequence of kernel events when PIP is triggered. It maps the data flow between the scheduler, the mutex control block, and the involved TCBs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task TH becomes ready] --> B[Scheduler dispatches TH]
    B --> C[TH calls lock M]
    C --> D{M is FREE?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Set owner TH, state LOCKED, return]
    D -- No --> F[Insert TH into M wait queue]
    F --> G[Set TH state to BLOCKED]
    G --> H[Find owner TL of M]
    H --> I{P_eff TL less than P TH?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Set P_eff TL = P TH]
    I -- No --> K[No inheritance needed]
    J --> L[Reinsert TL into ready queue at new priority slot]
    K --> M[Dispatch highest priority ready task]
    L --> M
    M --> N[TL runs at inherited priority]
    N --> O[TL calls unlock M]
    O --> P[De-inherit: restore P_eff TL = P_base TL]
    P --> Q[Pop highest waiter TH from queue]
    Q --> R[Transfer ownership TH, set TH READY]
    R --> S[Scheduler dispatches TH]
```

## 4.3 Priority Inversion Comparison — Timeline Schematics

The following Mermaid Gantt-style flow contrasts the three scenarios (Unprotected, PIP, PCP) for the same task set analyzed in Section 3.

```mermaid
gantt
    title Priority Inversion Timeline Comparison
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Unprotected
    TL runs CS (lower): a1, 0, 20
    TH blocked: a2, 5, 48
    TM preempts TL: a3, 8, 38
    TH runs after unblock: a4, 53, 65
    section With PIP
    TL runs CS (inherits P3): b1, 5, 20
    TH blocked: b2, 5, 20
    TH runs after release: b3, 20, 32
    TM runs after TH done: b4, 32, 62
    section With PCP
    TL runs CS at ceiling: c1, 0, 20
    TH blocked: c2, 5, 15
    TH runs: c3, 20, 32
    TM runs: c4, 32, 62
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Notice that under both PIP and PCP, $T_M$ is *forbidden* from running while $T_L$ holds the resource. This is the heart of bounded inversion. The unprotected case shows $T_M$ running in the middle of the inversion window, producing unbounded blocking.

## 4.4 POSIX Mutex Type Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need a mutex?] --> Q1{Nested locking required?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Rec[Use PTHREAD_MUTEX_RECURSIVE]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Need to detect deadlock at runtime?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> EC[Use PTHREAD_MUTEX_ERRORCHECK]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Linux default behavior acceptable?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Def[Use PTHREAD_MUTEX_DEFAULT]
    Q3 -- No --> Norm[Use PTHREAD_MUTEX_NORMAL]
```

---
