# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PECPT741]**
**Differentiate between a mutex and a binary semaphore. Why is a mutex preferred in real-time systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Mutex | Binary Semaphore |
|---|---|---|
| Ownership | Has an owner (the locking task) | No ownership concept |
| Priority Inheritance | Supports it (PIP) | Does not support |
| Release | Only owner can release | Any task can `post()` |
| Use case | Protecting critical sections | Signaling between tasks |

A mutex is preferred in real-time systems because it supports the **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)**, which bounds priority inversion. A binary semaphore does not provide ownership tracking, so PIP cannot be implemented on top of it, making it unsuitable for hard real-time resource locking. *(1 mark for the table, 1 mark for the ownership distinction, 1 mark for PIP relevance — total 3 marks.)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PECPT741 End-Semester]**
**What is unbounded priority inversion? Why is it catastrophic in hard real-time systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Unbounded priority inversion is a scheduling anomaly where a high-priority task $T_H$ is delayed by a lower-priority task $T_L$ for a duration that depends on the execution of an *unrelated* medium-priority task $T_M$ — i.e., the blocking time of $T_H$ is not bounded by any property of the resource it is waiting for. *(1 mark)*

It is catastrophic in hard real-time systems because the system's worst-case blocking time becomes **uncomputable** (it depends on the runtime behavior of medium-priority tasks that may not even share the resource). This violates the **bounded blocking** requirement needed for schedulability analysis (e.g., Response Time Analysis), making it impossible to certify that deadlines will be met. *(2 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Choice A]**

**(a)** Explain the **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** in detail. State its operational rules and discuss how it differs from the **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider three periodic tasks $T_1, T_2, T_3$ with priorities $P_1 > P_2 > P_3$. The tasks share two resources $R_a$ and $R_b$. The following table gives the worst-case execution times (in ms) and resource usage:

| Task | Priority | $C_i$ (ms) | Resources Used | CS Duration (ms) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $T_1$ (High) | 3 | 10 | None | — |
| $T_2$ (Medium) | 2 | 15 | $R_a$ | 5 |
| $T_3$ (Low) | 1 | 20 | $R_a, R_b$ | $R_a$: 4, $R_b$: 6 |

Assume $T_3$ starts first, locks $R_a$, then $T_1$ arrives and blocks on $R_a$. Then $T_2$ arrives. **Draw the Gantt chart (a) without any protocol, and (b) with PIP.** Compute the worst-case blocking time of $T_1$ in both cases. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to A(a) — PIP vs PCP (7 marks)

**Definition of PIP (1 mark):** PIP is a dynamic resource-access protocol where, when a higher-priority task blocks on a mutex held by a lower-priority task, the kernel temporarily raises the holding task's effective priority to that of the highest-priority blocked waiter.

**Four PIP Rules (3 marks):**
1. *Scheduling Rule:* The scheduler always dispatches the highest-priority ready task.
2. *Inheritance Rule:* On a `lock` block, set $P_{\text{eff}}(T_{\text{owner}}) \leftarrow \max(P_{\text{eff}}(T_{\text{owner}}), P_{\text{waiter}})$.
3. *De-inheritance Rule:* On `unlock`, recompute $P_{\text{eff}}(T_{\text{owner}})$ from its remaining held resources and base priority.
4. *Transitivity Rule:* If the owner is itself blocked on another resource, propagate inheritance up the chain.

**Comparison with PCP (3 marks):**

| Aspect | PIP | PCP |
|---|---|---|
| Ceiling assignment | Dynamic (runtime) | Static (design time) |
| Deadlock prevention | No (only bounds blocking) | Yes (single rule prevents) |
| Blocking bound | Sum of shortest CS per resource | One CS duration total |
| Overhead | Priority updates on every lock | Ceiling check on every lock |
| Implementation in RTOS | Common (FreeRTOS, VxWorks) | Common in AUTOSAR, ARINC 653 |

---

#### Model Solution to A(b) — Gantt Chart and Blocking Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Unprotected (No Protocol) Timeline (3 marks)**

- $t=0$: $T_3$ runs, locks $R_a$ ($P_{\text{eff}}=1$).
- $t=4$ (say): $T_1$ arrives, preempts $T_3$, attempts to lock $R_a$ (held by $T_3$), **blocks**. $T_3$ resumes.
- $t=5$: $T_2$ arrives, preempts $T_3$ (since $P_2=2 > P_3=1$ and $T_1$ is blocked). $T_2$ runs for its 15 ms (assuming it does not touch $R_a$).
- $T_2$ runs from $t=5$ to $t=20$.
- $T_3$ resumes at $t=20$, finishes $R_a$ CS at $t=24$, releases $R_a$.
- $T_1$ unblocks and runs from $t=24$ to $t=34$.

**Blocking time of $T_1$ (unprotected):**

$$B_1^{\text{unprot}} = 24 - 4 = 20 \text{ ms}$$

This includes 15 ms of $T_2$'s computation, which is **unbounded inversion**.

**Step 2: With PIP (4 marks)**

- $t=0$: $T_3$ runs, locks $R_a$.
- $t=4$: $T_1$ arrives, preempts $T_3$, blocks on $R_a$. **PIP fires: $P_{\text{eff}}(T_3) \leftarrow P(T_1) = 3$.** $T_3$ resumes at priority 3.
- $t=5$: $T_2$ arrives. Scheduler checks: $P_{\text{eff}}(T_3) = 3$ vs $P(T_2) = 2$. Since $3 > 2$, **$T_2$ does not preempt $T_3$**.
- $T_3$ continues running from $t=4$ to $t=8$ (finishing its 4 ms of $R_a$ CS), then releases $R_a$ at $t=8$.
- **PIP de-inheritance:** $P_{\text{eff}}(T_3) \leftarrow 1$.
- $T_1$ unblocks at $t=8$, runs from $t=8$ to $t=18$.
- $T_2$ runs from $t=18$ onward.

**Blocking time of $T_1$ (PIP):**

$$B_1^{\text{PIP}} = 8 - 4 = 4 \text{ ms}$$

This is **bounded** — it equals exactly the remaining critical section of $T_3$ on $R_a$.

**Summary Table (to be drawn in answer script):**

| Scenario | $B_1$ (ms) | Bounded? |
|---|---|---|
| Unprotected | 20 | **No** |
| PIP | 4 | **Yes** |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3, Choice B]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **mutual exclusion problem** and describe how a mutex with **priority inheritance** solves the unbounded priority inversion problem. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **recursive mutex** concept. Compare `PTHREAD_MUTEX_NORMAL`, `PTHREAD_MUTEX_RECURSIVE`, and `PTHREAD_MUTEX_ERRORCHECK` types in POSIX. Provide an example C-code structure (sketch only) showing safe recursive locking. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to B(a) — Mutual Exclusion & PIP (7 marks)

**Mutual Exclusion (2 marks):** Mutual exclusion is the requirement that, for any shared resource $R$, at most one task may be executing inside its critical section at any time. The classic four-condition solution requires (1) Mutual exclusion, (2) Progress, (3) Bounded waiting, and (4) No assumption about relative speeds.

**Problem with Naive Locking (2 marks):** When locking is implemented with a binary semaphore without ownership tracking, a low-priority task holding the lock can be preempted by medium-priority tasks, blocking a high-priority waiter for an unbounded duration. *(Reference: Figure 1 from Section 4.3 — the unprotected Gantt chart.)*

**Solution via Mutex with PIP (3 marks):** A mutex enforces ownership (only the owner can release), and PIP dynamically boosts the owner's priority to that of the highest-priority waiter. This prevents medium-priority preemption of the resource holder, bounding the inversion to at most the longest critical section in the conflict chain. *(Reference: Figure 2 from Section 4.3 — the PIP Gantt chart.)*

---

#### Model Solution to B(b) — Recursive Mutexes & POSIX Types (7 marks)

**Recursive Mutex Concept (2 marks):** A recursive mutex allows the *same task* to lock the same mutex multiple times without deadlocking itself. Internally, the mutex maintains a **lock count** and a **owner TCB pointer**. Each `lock` by the owner increments the count; each `unlock` decrements it. The mutex is fully released only when the count returns to zero.

**POSIX Mutex Type Comparison (3 marks):**

| POSIX Type | Re-lock by Owner? | Unlock by Non-Owner? | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| `PTHREAD_MUTEX_NORMAL` | **Undefined behavior** (often deadlock) | Undefined behavior | Simple, fastest |
| `PTHREAD_MUTEX_RECURSIVE` | **Allowed** (count incremented) | Undefined behavior | Functions calling other functions that also lock |
| `PTHREAD_MUTEX_ERRORCHECK` | Returns `EDEADLK` error | Returns `EPERM` error | Debugging, defensive code |
| `PTHREAD_MUTEX_DEFAULT` | Implementation-defined | Implementation-defined | Linux default (often = NORMAL) |

**C-Code Sketch (2 marks):**

```c
#include <pthread.h>

pthread_mutex_t mtx = PTHREAD_MUTEX_RECURSIVE_INITIALIZER;

void inner_function(void) {
    pthread_mutex_lock(&mtx);
    /* critical work */
    pthread_mutex_unlock(&mtx);
}

void outer_function(void) {
    pthread_mutex_lock(&mtx);
    /* some work */
    inner_function();  /* safe — same task, recursive */
    pthread_mutex_unlock(&mtx);
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to qualify "unbounded":** If a student writes "priority inversion is bad in RTOS" without specifying *unbounded*, marks are deducted. Always say "**unbounded** priority inversion."
> 2. **Confusing PIP with PCP:** PIP bounds blocking but does **not** prevent deadlock. PCP prevents deadlock but adds a static ceiling check on every lock. Mixing them up costs 2-3 marks.
> 3. **Drawing the Gantt chart wrong:** A common error is showing $T_M$ *not* preempting $T_L$ in the unprotected case. The unprotected case has **no protocol**, so $T_M$ *does* preempt $T_L$. The preemption is precisely what causes the unbounded inversion.
> 4. **Forgetting de-inheritance:** When explaining PIP, students sometimes describe the inheritance but forget to mention that the priority is **restored on release**. This is a 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Confusing recursive mutex with re-entrant interrupt handling:** Recursive mutexes are a *task-level* construct; they have no meaning across interrupt contexts in most RTOS kernels.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Mutex = Lock + Owner Identity:** A mutex is a binary lock with an owning task pointer. This is the single most defining property.
- **Binary Semaphore ≠ Mutex:** Semaphores signal; mutexes protect. The ownership distinction is the differentiator.
- **Unbounded Priority Inversion** is the pathology; it arises when a medium-priority task delays a high-priority task indirectly through a shared resource held by a low-priority task.
- **PIP Rules (memorize all four):** Scheduling, Inheritance, De-inheritance, Transitivity.
- **PIP Effective Priority Formula:** $P_{\text{eff}}(T_i, t) = \max(P_{\text{base}}(T_i), \text{inherited priorities from all held resources})$.
- **PCP Ceiling Formula:** $\text{ceil}(M_k) = \max_{T_i \in \text{users}(M_k)} P_{\text{base}}(T_i)$.
- **Blocking bound under PIP:** At most the sum of the shortest critical section per contended resource — **bounded but not deadlock-free**.
- **Blocking bound under PCP:** At most one critical section duration — **bounded and deadlock-free**.
- **Gantt chart drawing is examinable:** Practice drawing the three-task inversion scenario both with and without PIP. Label all priority changes, lock acquisitions, and releases.
- **RTA blocking term:** $B_i$ appears in the recurrence $R_i = C_i + B_i + \sum I_j$; the protocol choice directly determines $B_i$.
- **POSIX Mutex Types:** `NORMAL`, `RECURSIVE`, `ERRORCHECK`, `DEFAULT` — know the differences, especially the recursive use case.
- **Real-world examples:** Mars Pathfinder bug (1997), VxWorks `semMCreate` with `SEM_Q_PRIORITY`, AUTOSAR OS `GetResource`/`ReleaseResource`, FreeRTOS `xSemaphoreCreateMutex` (which enables `pxMutexHolder` for priority inheritance).
- **Atomic test-and-set:** Underlying mutex implementations rely on hardware atomic instructions (e.g., `LDREX`/`STREX` on ARM, `CMPXCHG` on x86) to ensure lock acquisition is race-free.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Messages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT741 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Messages # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of a **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)**, a **Message** is a fundamental unit of **Inter-Process Communication (IPC)**. It is a structured, typed data object exchanged between concurrently executing **tasks** (or between a task and an ISR) through a well-defined kernel-managed communication channel. A message typically contains: - A **destination address** (target task, mailbox, or queue identifier) - A **payload** (the actual data buffer of fixed or variable length) - Optional **control fields** (priority, timestamp, message type tag) Mathematically, a message $M$ can be represented as a tuple: $$M = \{ D, T, P, C, \tau_s \}$$ Where: - $D$ = Destination channel identifier - $T$ = Source task identifier - $P$ = Payload buffer (length $L$ bytes) - $C$ = Control word (priority, flags) - $\tau_s$ = Timestamp of dispatch (for temporal analysis) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of PECPT741 explicitly covers *Message Queues, Mailboxes, Pipes, Signals, and Event Flags* as the primary IPC mechanisms of a hard/soft real-time kernel. Mastery of message passing is mandatory as it forms the bridge between **deterministic task synchronization** and **data exchange**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **hospital's pneumatic tube delivery system** used to transport blood samples and medicine between departments: | RTOS Concept | Hospital Analogy | | :--- | :--- | | **Task** | A doctor / nurse station | | **Message** | A sealed capsule containing the sample or drug | | **Mailbox** | The receiving station's dedicated intake port | | **Message Queue** | A holding rack where capsules wait in arrival order | | **Send (Blocking)** | Doctor waits at the tube until the capsule is accepted downstream | | **Send (Non-Blocking)** | Doctor drops the capsule in the intake and immediately moves to the next patient | | **Receive (Blocking)** | Nurse waits at her station until a capsule arrives | | **Receive (Non-Blocking/Polled)** | Nurse periodically checks the rack for new capsules | | **Priority Message** | An **URGENT** red capsule (e.g., critical lab report) that bypasses the queue | In a hard real-time system (e.g., an anti-lock braking system), the **worst-case message delivery latency** must be mathematically bounded — just as a code-blue alert must reach the ICU within a strict time window. The RTOS kernel guarantees this bound, unlike a general-purpose OS where delivery time is best-effort. > [!NOTE] > **Why Messages instead of Shared Memory?** In a real-time kernel, shared global variables introduce **race conditions**, **non-deterministic access times** (cache effects, bus contention), and **priority inversion** risks. Messages provide **encapsulation**, **deterministic hand-off**, and a single point of synchronization — critical for certification under standards like DO-178C (avionics) or ISO 26262 (automotive). ## 1.3 Classification of Message Passing Message passing in RTOS is classified along three orthogonal axes: 1. **Naming (Addressing)** - **Direct Communication:** `send(task_id, msg)` and `receive(task_id, msg)` — sender and receiver name each other explicitly. - **Indirect Communication:** Messages pass through an intermediate *port* (mailbox, queue, pipe). `send(port, msg)` and `receive(port, msg)`. 2. **Synchronization (Blocking Behavior)** - **Synchronous (Rendezvous):** Sender blocks until the receiver accepts the message. Deterministic, but may cause priority inversion. - **Asynchronous (Buffered):** Sender deposits message in a bounded buffer and continues. Latency is reduced for the sender but bounded by buffer capacity. 3. **Direction** - **Unidirectional:** Pipes, mailboxes (typically one-way). - **Bidirectional:** Full-duplex channels, RPC-style *send/reply* primitives. **Standard metrics used throughout KTU valuation:** - **Message Throughput:** $\lambda_m$ messages per second - **Worst-Case Latency:** $L_{max}$ in microseconds ($\mu s$) - **Jitter:** $J = L_{max} - L_{min}$ - **Deadline Miss Ratio:** $DMR = \frac{N_{missed}}{N_{total}}$ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mailboxes A **Mailbox** is a kernel object that can hold **at most one** undelivered message at any instant. It is the simplest deterministic IPC primitive. ### 2.1.1 Operational Logic 1. The kernel allocates a fixed-size buffer $B_{mbox}$ (typically 4–32 bytes of pointer + data). 2. A task invokes `MBOX_Send(mbox_id, msg, timeout)`: - If mailbox is **empty** → message is deposited, blocked tasks (if any) are unblocked. - If mailbox is **full** → task is either *blocked* (with timeout $T_w$) or *returns error* (if `NO_WAIT`). 3. A task invokes `MBOX_Receive(mbox_id, msg, timeout)`: - If mailbox has a message → atomic copy-out, sender (if blocked on full) is unblocked. - If empty → task blocks until message arrives or timeout fires. ### 2.1.2 Why Mailboxes (and not just variables)? A mailbox's *atomicity* is guaranteed by disabling interrupts or using a lock-free CAS primitive for the duration of the deposit-withdraw operation. This guarantees that the read-modify-write sequence is indivisible — a property shared variables cannot provide in SMP multicore systems. ## 2.2 Message Queues A **Message Queue** is a FIFO (or priority-ordered) kernel buffer that can hold **N ≥ 1** messages simultaneously. It is the workhorse of real-time IPC. ### 2.2.1 Queue Structure $$\text{Queue} = \{ \text{Head}, \text{Tail}, \text{Count}, \text{MaxSize}, \text{ItemSize} \}$$ Operations: - **Enqueue (Send):** $O(1)$ in circular-array implementation; $O(1)$ for linked-list head/tail pointers. - **Dequeue (Receive):** $O(1)$. ### 2.2.2 Blocking vs. Non-Blocking Primitives The four cardinal IPC primitives per task are: | Primitive | Abbrev. | Behavior on Full/Empty | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `send_nb()` | Non-blocking send | Returns `ERR_FULL` immediately | | `send_b()` | Blocking send | Suspended on sender-wait queue with timeout $T_s$ | | `recv_nb()` | Non-blocking receive | Returns `ERR_EMPTY` immediately | | `recv_b()` | Blocking receive | Suspended on receiver-wait queue with timeout $T_r$ | ## 2.3 Pipes A **Pipe** is a unidirectional byte-stream channel. Two variants exist: - **Anonymous (Unnamed) Pipe:** Used between parent-child tasks (e.g., POSIX `pipe()`). - **Named Pipe (FIFO):** Exists as a filesystem object, allowing unrelated tasks to communicate. In real-time systems, pipes are less common than queues because their variable-length nature makes **worst-case execution time (WCET)** analysis harder. They are, however, used extensively in RT-POSIX compliant systems. ## 2.4 Signals A **Signal** is a *lightweight, asynchronous software interrupt* used to notify a task of an event. It is analogous to hardware interrupts but generated by software (kernel, ISR, or other task). Key properties: - **Asynchronous:** Delivered at the next safe instruction boundary, not at a specific point in the code. - **Minimal payload:** Typically carries only a signal number (an integer), not data. - **Default action:** Task termination, core dump, ignore, or stop. - **Real-time signals (POSIX):** `sigqueue()` allows queued delivery of small integers. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** In many RTOSs (e.g., VxWorks, RTLinux), signals are emulated using *asynchronous system traps* (ASTs). In μC/OS-II and FreeRTOS, the closest equivalent is the **direct-to-task notification** primitive. ## 2.5 Event Flags / Event Groups **Event Flags** are individual bits in a kernel-managed word, where each bit represents a binary condition. Tasks can `wait` for: - **AND of all bits set** (logical conjunction) - **OR of any bit set** (logical disjunction) - **Consume option:** Clear bits atomically upon successful wait. They are ideal for synchronizing a task to the *completion of multiple subsystems* — e.g., "proceed when sensor-A AND sensor-B AND sensor-C have all published their readings." ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Message Tuple | $M = \{D, T, P, C, \tau_s\}$ | $P$ size in bytes | | 2 | Mailbox Capacity | $C_{mbox} = 1$ message | Atomic deposit | | 3 | Queue Throughput | $\lambda_m = \frac{N_{msg}}{\Delta t}$ | messages/sec | | 4 | Worst-Case Latency | $L_{max} = T_{ISR} + T_{sched} + T_{copy} + T_{ctx\_sw}$ | seconds | | 5 | Jitter | $J = L_{max} - L_{min}$ | Must be bounded in hard RT | | 6 | Buffer Occupancy (Little's Law) | $N_{avg} = \lambda_m \cdot R$ | $R$ = mean residence time | | 7 | Deadline Miss Ratio | $DMR = \frac{N_{missed}}{N_{total}}$ | 0 in hard real-time | | 8 | Bandwidth Bound (Queue) | $B_{max} = \frac{F_{CPU}}{C_{ctx\_sw} + C_{copy}}$ | messages/sec | | 9 | Priority Message Latency | $L_{prio} \leq L_{FIFO} \cdot \frac{N_{max}}{N_{prio}}$ | $\approx$ priority bypass | | 10 | Context Switch Cost | $C_{ctx\_sw} \approx 5\text{–}20\,\mu s$ | Architecture dependent | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary Conditions to Memorize:** > - A mailbox that overwrites unread messages violates the *non-overtaking* property. > - A queue with $N_{max}=0$ degenerates to a pure rendezvous (synchronous). > - Blocking primitives **must always specify a timeout** in real-time code to avoid permanent deadlock. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Message Passing Application | | :--- | :--- | | **Automotive (AUTOSAR)** | COM module uses PDU messages over CAN/LIN/FlexRay | | **Avionics (ARINC 653)** | Inter-partition messaging via ports and channels | | **Industrial Control** | Modbus/Profibus message frames | | **Medical Devices** | Alarm/event messaging between monitoring and display tasks | | **Robotics (ROS 2)** | DDS-based publish/subscribe message bus with QoS | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Worst-Case Message Delivery Latency In a preemptive priority-based RTOS, the worst-case latency $L_{max}$ experienced by a high-priority message from the instant `send()` is invoked to the instant the receiver starts processing it is derived as follows. ### Step 1 — Decompose the latency chain $$L_{max} = T_{copy\_in} + T_{sched} + T_{ctx\_sw\_sender} + T_{queueing} + T_{ctx\_sw\_receiver} + T_{copy\_out}$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute symbolic terms - $T_{copy\_in}$ = time to copy the payload from sender's stack to the kernel buffer. - $T_{sched}$ = time for the scheduler to select the next runnable task. - $T_{ctx\_sw}$ = context switch cost (saving + restoring registers, FPU state, MMU tables). - $T_{queueing}$ = time the message waits in the buffer before a receiver consumes it. ### Step 3 — Bound $T_{queueing}$ using busy-window analysis For a queue of capacity $K$ with $N$ higher-priority tasks each issuing messages at rate $\lambda_i$: $$T_{queueing} \leq \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\lceil \frac{T_{window}}{T_{period,i}} \right\rceil \cdot C_i$$ Where $C_i$ is the WCET of the $i^{th}$ higher-priority sender. ### Step 4 — Combine $$\begin{aligned} L_{max} &= T_{copy\_in} + T_{sched} + 2 \cdot T_{ctx\_sw} + T_{copy\_out} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\lceil \frac{T_{window}}{T_{period,i}} \right\rceil \cdot C_i \\ &\leq \underbrace{2 \cdot C_{copy}}_{\text{bookkeeping}} + \underbrace{C_{sched}}_{\text{algorithm complexity}} + \underbrace{2 \cdot C_{ctx\_sw}}_{\text{hardware bound}} + \underbrace{I_{HP}}_{\text{interference}} \end{aligned}$$ This decomposition is the **Response Time Analysis (RTA)** kernel for message-based systems, formalized by Audsley, Tindell, and Burns (1992). ## 3.2 Derivation: Mailbox Overwrite Race Condition Consider two tasks $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$ both calling `MBOX_Send(mbox, msg)` concurrently. The race manifests as: 1. $\tau_1$ reads the mailbox state and finds it **empty**. 2. The scheduler preempts $\tau_1$ and runs $\tau_2$. 3. $\tau_2$ reads the mailbox state and also finds it **empty**. 4. $\tau_2$ writes its message and marks the mailbox **full**. 5. $\tau_1$ resumes, overwrites the message, and also marks the mailbox **full**. **Result:** $\tau_2$'s message is lost. ### Mitigation Analysis Let $T_{critical}$ be the time to atomically check-and-set the mailbox flag. The window of vulnerability is: $$W_{vuln} = T_{read} + T_{compare} + T_{write} = T_{critical}$$ Three valid mitigation strategies: | Strategy | Mechanism | Real-Time Cost | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Disable Interrupts** | `cpsid i` / `cli` for $T_{critical}$ | $0$ extra cycles, but blocks ISRs | | **Test-and-Set (TAS)** | Atomic hardware instruction | $C_{TAS} \approx 5\text{–}20$ cycles | | **Compare-and-Swap (CAS)** | Lock-free primitive | $C_{CAS} \approx 10\text{–}30$ cycles | ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: IPC Primitive Selection Given a real-time requirement, the following decision matrix guides primitive selection: | Requirement | Recommended Primitive | Reason | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Single asynchronous notification | **Signal / Direct-to-Task Notification** | Minimum overhead, no data | | 1-to-1 unidirectional data stream | **Mailbox** | Simple, atomic, deterministic | | Multi-message buffering (1-to-N) | **Message Queue** | Decouples producer/consumer rates | | Many-to-one aggregation | **Event Flags (AND)** | Wait on multiple conditions | | Variable-length byte stream | **Pipe / Stream Socket** | POSIX-compliant | | Synchronous request-reply | **RPC / Message Queue + Ack** | Bidirectional, blocking | | Multi-rate synchronization | **Event Group + Timer** | Time-triggered coordination | | Multicore CPU-to-CPU | **Lock-free Queue / Mailbox** | Avoid cache-line ping-pong | ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A control loop task $\tau_c$ at priority 5 sends 32-byte messages to actuator task $\tau_a$ at priority 7. The mailbox is initially empty. The CPU is ARM Cortex-M4 @ 168 MHz. Given: - $C_{copy} = 0.5 \,\mu s$ - $C_{ctx\_sw} = 6 \,\mu s$ - $C_{sched} = 1.5 \,\mu s$ - $\tau_a$ is currently executing a 50 $\mu s$ critical section. - 2 higher-priority interrupt sources each consume $C_{HP,i} = 4 \,\mu s$. **Compute $L_{max}$ for a non-overwriting mailbox with TAS protection.** $$\begin{aligned} L_{max} &= C_{copy\_in} + C_{sched} + 2 \cdot C_{ctx\_sw} + T_{queueing} + C_{copy\_out} + C_{TAS} \\ &= 0.5 + 1.5 + 2(6) + 50 + 0.5 + 0.1 \\ &= 0.5 + 1.5 + 12 + 50 + 0.5 + 0.1 \\ &= 64.6 \,\mu s \end{aligned}$$ Add the high-priority interrupt interference (worst case, both fire before scheduler reaches $\tau_a$): $$I_{HP} = \sum_{i=1}^{2} C_{HP,i} = 8 \,\mu s$$ $$L_{max}^{final} = 64.6 + 8 = 72.6 \,\mu s$$ This figure is the **certifiable worst-case latency** the system designer reports to the safety case. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Message Passing in an RTOS Kernel ```mermaid flowchart TB appA["Application Task Tau_1"] --> sA["send port msg buffer timeout"] appB["Application Task Tau_2"] --> sB["send port msg buffer timeout"] appC["Application Task Tau_3"] --> rC["recv port buffer timeout"] sA --> kernIn["Kernel IPC Dispatcher"] sB --> kernIn kernIn --> mutex{"Channel Mutex
or TAS Lock"} mutex --> mb["Mailbox Object
Capacity 1"] mutex --> mq["Message Queue Object
Capacity N"] mutex --> pf["Pipe Object
Byte Stream"] mutex --> ef["Event Flag Group
32 bits"] mb --> readyQ["Receiver Wait Queue
Priority Ordered"] mq --> readyQ pf --> readyQ ef --> readyQ readyQ --> sched["Real-Time Scheduler
Rate Monotonic or EDF"] sched --> ctx["Context Switch Engine"] ctx --> appC isr["Hardware ISR"] -.->|urgent signal| kernIn ``` ## 4.2 Message Queue Internal State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Empty: Queue Created Empty --> HasOne: First send msg HasOne --> HasMany: send msg if count less than MAX HasMany --> HasMany: send msg HasMany --> HasOne: recv msg HasOne --> Empty: recv msg HasMany --> Full: send msg if count equals MAX Full --> Full: send nb returns ERR_FULL Full --> HasMany: recv msg Empty --> Empty: recv nb returns ERR_EMPTY HasOne --> HasOne: recv nb returns msg note right of Full: Sender task blocked
on full wait queue note right of Empty: Receiver task blocked
on empty wait queue ``` ## 4.3 Mailbox Atomic Send-Receive Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant S as Sender Task participant K as Kernel Mailbox Service participant M as Mailbox Buffer participant R as Receiver Task S->>K: MBOX_Send mailbox msg timeout K->>K: Disable Interrupts or TAS alt Mailbox Empty K->>M: Copy msg payload K->>M: Set state to FULL K-->>R: Unblock receiver if waiting else Mailbox Full alt Blocking mode and timeout greater than 0 K-->>S: Block sender in wait queue Note over S: Sender suspended else Non blocking K-->>S: Return ERR_FULL end end K->>K: Restore Interrupts or Release TAS R->>K: MBOX_Receive mailbox buf timeout K->>M: Atomic copy out K->>M: Set state to EMPTY K-->>S: Unblock sender if waiting K-->>R: Return OK ``` ## 4.4 Event Flag Synchronization Pattern ```mermaid flowchart LR s1["Sensor Task A
Sets bit 0"] --> ef["Event Flag Group
0x00000000"] s2["Sensor Task B
Sets bit 1"] --> ef s3["Sensor Task C
Sets bit 2"] --> ef ef --> ctrl["Controller Task
Waits on 0x07 AND"] ctrl --> act["Actuator Driver
Triggered on
all bits set"] ef --> alt["Display Task
Waits on 0x01 OR
Show partial data"] ``` ## 4.5 RTOS Message Flow Through Scheduling Layers ```mermaid flowchart TD hw["Hardware Event
Timer or Peripheral"] --> isr["ISR Top Half
Acknowledge HW"] isr --> dfr["Deferred Work
Tasklet or Message Post"] dfr --> queue["Kernel Message Queue
Bound to Consumer Task"] queue --> sched["Scheduler Tick
Picks Highest Priority Ready Task"] sched --> consumer["Consumer Task
Executes Application Logic"] consumer --> resp["System Response
Actuator or Output"] classDef rt fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px class hw,isr,dfr,queue,sched,consumer,resp rt ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECPT741]` > **Differentiate between a Mailbox and a Message Queue in an RTOS. Mention two real-time applications of each.** **Model Answer (Expected 3-mark key):** | Aspect | Mailbox | Message Queue | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Capacity | **1 message** (overwrite-on-send or block) | **N messages** (FIFO/priority) | | Atomicity | Single deposit-withdraw | Multiple buffers | | Use | Command acknowledgement, latest-value semantics | Producer-consumer streams, logging | **Applications:** - *Mailbox:* (i) Latest setpoint dispatch to a PID controller; (ii) Single-slot command buffer for a motor drive. - *Message Queue:* (i) UART RX ISR posting bytes to a parser task; (ii) Multi-slot telemetry buffer in an avionics unit. --- > **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PECPT741]` > **What is a signal in an RTOS context? How does it differ from a hardware interrupt?** **Model Answer:** A **signal** is a software-generated asynchronous notification delivered to a task by the kernel. Unlike a **hardware interrupt** (served by an ISR at any moment, with hardware-pushed context), a signal is delivered only at *safe instruction boundaries* in user task context, carries only a small integer identifier (no payload), and is processed by a registered signal handler. | Property | Signal | Hardware Interrupt | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Source | Kernel, ISR, or other task | External hardware edge/level | | Latency | Deferred to task resumption | Immediate (within cycles) | | Context | Task context | Interrupt context | | Payload | Signal number only | None / device-specific | | Nesting | Sequential delivery | HW-supported priority nesting | ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, PECPT741 Model Paper]` > **Q3 (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of an **asynchronous message passing** mechanism using a bounded **message queue** in a preemptive RTOS. Include the state transitions of the queue and discuss the **priority inversion problem** that may arise when a low-priority task holds the queue and a high-priority task is blocked on receive. > **Q3 (b) [7 Marks]** A real-time system has three periodic tasks $\tau_1, \tau_2, \tau_3$ with periods $T_1=10\,ms$, $T_2=25\,ms$, $T_3=50\,ms$ and WCETs $C_1=3\,ms$, $C_2=5\,ms$, $C_3=8\,ms$. They communicate via a single message queue. Compute the **worst-case response time** of the message delivery if the queue access is protected by a **priority inheritance mutex** and context switch overhead is $C_{cs}=0.5\,ms$. Assume rate-monotonic priority assignment. #### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks** 1. **Queue Structure (1 mark):** A circular array of $N$ slots, each holding a fixed-size message header and pointer. A `head`, `tail`, and `count` are maintained atomically. 2. **State Transitions (2 marks):** - *Empty → HasOne:* first `send()` fills the queue, wakes receiver. - *HasOne → Full:* `send()` continues up to $N$. - *Full → HasMany:* `recv()` removes one. - *HasMany → Empty:* last `recv()` empties queue, wakes blocked senders. 3. **Asynchronous Property (1 mark):** The sender does not wait for the receiver. It returns as soon as the message is copied into the kernel buffer. This minimizes the sender's blocking time — desirable for time-critical producers (e.g., sensor ISRs). 4. **Priority Inversion Scenario (2 marks):** - High-priority task $\tau_H$ blocks on `recv()` from queue $Q$. - Low-priority task $\tau_L$ is the only one that sends to $Q$ and currently holds it. - Medium-priority task $\tau_M$ preempts $\tau_L$, *delaying the send indefinitely*. - Result: $\tau_H$'s blocking time is unbounded — a classic **unbounded priority inversion** (as in the Mars Pathfinder incident, 1997). 5. **Mitigation (1 mark):** Use **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** or **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)**: when $\tau_L$ enters the queue's critical section, its effective priority is *raised* to that of the highest-priority task blocked on the resource, preventing $\tau_M$ from preempting it. #### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks** **Step 1 — Rate Monotonic Assignment (0.5 mark):** - $\tau_1$: $T=10\,ms$ → highest priority - $\tau_2$: $T=25\,ms$ → mid priority - $\tau_3$: $T=50\,ms$ → lowest priority **Step 2 — Response Time Analysis (3 marks):** The response time $R_i$ of a task $i$ satisfies the iterative equation: $$R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + C_{cs} + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot (C_j + C_{cs})$$ For $\tau_3$ (longest period, lowest priority — bottleneck): **Iteration k=0:** $R_3^{(0)} = C_3 + C_{cs} = 8 + 0.5 = 8.5\,ms$ **Iteration k=1:** $$R_3^{(1)} = 8.5 + 0.5 + \left\lceil \frac{8.5}{10} \right\rceil (3 + 0.5) + \left\lceil \frac{8.5}{25} \right\rceil (5 + 0.5)$$ $$R_3^{(1)} = 8.5 + 0.5 + 1 \cdot 3.5 + 1 \cdot 5.5 = 18.0\,ms$$ **Iteration k=2:** $$R_3^{(2)} = 8.5 + 0.5 + \left\lceil \frac{18}{10} \right\rceil (3.5) + \left\lceil \frac{18}{25} \right\rceil (5.5)$$ $$R_3^{(2)} = 8.5 + 0.5 + 2(3.5) + 1(5.5) = 21.5\,ms$$ **Iteration k=3:** $$R_3^{(3)} = 8.5 + 0.5 + \left\lceil \frac{21.5}{10} \right\rceil (3.5) + \left\lceil \frac{21.5}{25} \right\rceil (5.5)$$ $$R_3^{(3)} = 8.5 + 0.5 + 3(3.5) + 1(5.5) = 25.0\,ms$$ **Iteration k=4:** $$R_3^{(4)} = 8.5 + 0.5 + \left\lceil \frac{25}{10} \right\rceil (3.5) + \left\lceil \frac{25}{25} \right\rceil (5.5)$$ $$R_3^{(4)} = 8.5 + 0.5 + 3(3.5) + 1(5.5) = 25.0\,ms$$ **Converged:** $R_3 = 25.0\,ms$. Since $R_3 \leq T_3 = 50\,ms$, the task set is **schedulable** with PIP. **Step 3 — Message delivery latency contribution (1.5 marks):** The message delivery latency is the queue's worst-case waiting time, which is at most $R_{queue} \leq \sum_{i=1}^{3} C_i + 3 \cdot C_{cs} = 16 + 1.5 = 17.5\,ms$. **Step 4 — Verdict (1 mark):** With priority inheritance, $\tau_3$'s effective priority is raised to that of the highest-priority blocked receiver, eliminating unbounded inversion. All tasks meet their deadlines. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, PECPT741 Model Paper]` > **Q4 (a) [7 Marks]** With neat **state diagrams and sequence charts**, describe the **send and receive primitives** of a **mailbox** in a hard real-time kernel. Explain how **priority inheritance** is implemented at the mailbox level. > **Q4 (b) [7 Marks]** Compare **pipes, message queues, and event flag groups** in terms of (i) data capacity, (ii) blocking semantics, (iii) determinism, and (iv) typical use case. Identify which one you would use for the following scenarios and justify: > 1. Communicating a 12-byte sensor reading from an ADC ISR to a control task. > 2. Synchronizing a controller task to the completion of three independent preprocessing subtasks. > 3. Streaming debug logs from multiple tasks to a single UART writer task. #### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks** **State Diagram for Mailbox (2 marks):** The mailbox has two states — *Empty* and *Full*. Transitions are driven by `send()` and `receive()`: ``` Empty --send()--> Full Full --recv()--> Empty Full --send() (blocking)--> sender-blocked Empty --recv() (blocking)--> receiver-blocked ``` **Sequence Chart (2 marks):** 1. Sender calls `MBOX_Send(id, msg, t)`. Kernel disables interrupts, checks state. 2. If Empty → copy in, set state=Full, unblock any waiting receiver, re-enable interrupts, return OK. 3. If Full and timeout > 0 → enqueue sender in mailbox's wait list at its *current* priority, suspend. 4. Receiver calls `MBOX_Recv(id, buf, t)`. Kernel disables interrupts. 5. If Full → atomic copy out, set state=Empty, unblock highest-priority waiting sender, re-enable IRQs. 6. If Empty → enqueue receiver, suspend. **Priority Inheritance Implementation (3 marks):** - The mailbox control block maintains a `holder` pointer (the task currently in the critical section, i.e., the sender) and a `waiters` list. - On `send()`: if the mailbox is full, the holder task's effective priority is **boosted** to `max(its_own, all_waiters_priorities)`. - On `recv()` that empties the mailbox: the holder is unblocked, and its priority is **restored** to its base value. - This is implemented in μC/OS-II via `OSTaskChangePrio()` calls guarded by the scheduler lock. #### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks** **Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Aspect | Pipe | Message Queue | Event Flag Group | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | (i) Data capacity | Variable byte stream | N fixed-size messages | 32 single bits | | (ii) Blocking semantics | Blocking on full/empty | Blocking or non-blocking | AND/OR waits, consume option | | (iii) Determinism | Moderate (variable len) | High (fixed len, O(1)) | Highest (bit ops only) | | (iv) Typical use | Parent-child byte streams | Producer-consumer IPC | Multi-condition sync | **Scenario Answers (4 marks, 1.33 each):** 1. **12-byte ADC reading → Control task:** - **Choice: Message Queue** (or Mailbox). - **Justification:** Fixed-size payload, deterministic delivery, one producer (ISR-safe post) and one consumer (control task). Mailbox suffices if *only the latest reading* matters; queue buffers all readings if the controller runs slower than ADC sampling. 2. **Sync to 3 subtasks:** - **Choice: Event Flag Group.** - **Justification:** Each subtask sets a distinct bit (e.g., bit 0, 1, 2) on completion. Controller calls `OSFlagWait(0x07, AND, consume)` — blocks until all three bits are set, then atomically clears them. This is a textbook multi-condition rendezvous. 3. **Debug log stream to UART writer:** - **Choice: Message Queue** (of formatted log records). - **Justification:** Multiple producers, one consumer. Queue provides FIFO ordering, decouples producer rate from UART baud rate, and prevents log loss when the UART task is preempted. A pipe would also work but is byte-oriented, requiring manual framing. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — How Marks Are Lost:** > 1. **Ignoring Blocking Timeouts:** Students often write `recv()` without a timeout argument. This creates *unbounded blocking*, which is a **fatal flaw in hard real-time** and costs 1–2 marks. > 2. **Confusing Mailbox with Queue:** Many answers describe a queue as a mailbox. The examiner will deduct 1 mark if capacity > 1 is claimed for a "mailbox". > 3. **Omitting Priority Inheritance Discussion:** When asked about priority inversion, merely naming the problem without PIP/PCP mitigation yields only **partial credit (2/5)**. Always pair the problem with the *solution*. > 4. **Skipping the Math in (b):** Numerical response-time iterations must be **shown step-by-step** in the table. Writing only the final number with no iterations loses 2 marks. > 5. **Diagrams without labels:** State diagrams missing `Empty/Full` labels or arrow directions fetch zero marks even if the prose is correct. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Messages" Module** - [x] A **message** = destination + source + payload + control + timestamp tuple. - [x] **Mailbox** = 1-slot atomic IPC; **Queue** = N-slot FIFO/priority IPC. - [x] **Pipe** = byte-stream unidirectional; **Signal** = async notification, no payload. - [x] **Event Flags** = 32-bit AND/OR synchronization primitive. - [x] Four cardinal primitives: `send_b`, `send_nb`, `recv_b`, `recv_nb`. - [x] **Always specify a timeout** in `send`/`recv` — never block indefinitely. - [x] **Priority inversion** is the #1 message-passing hazard → use **PIP** or **PCP**. - [x] **Worst-case latency** $L_{max}$ = copy-in + schedule + 2× ctx-sw + queueing + copy-out + HP interference. - [x] **Atomicity** of mailbox write requires TAS, CAS, or interrupt disable. - [x] **RT-POSIX** mandates `mq_open`, `mq_send`, `mq_receive` with timed wait. - [x] **Little's Law** $N_{avg} = \lambda \cdot R$ bounds queue depth for memory budgeting. - [x] **Jitter** $J = L_{max} - L_{min}$ must be bounded in hard real-time. - [x] **DMR = 0** is the hard real-time acceptance criterion; any missed deadline is a system failure. - [x] Choose **mailbox** for latest-value, **queue** for buffered stream, **event flags** for multi-condition sync, **signals** for event-only notification. - [x] Mars Pathfinder 1997 = canonical case study of unbounded priority inversion via shared bus mailbox.

topics.title: Buffers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT741 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Buffers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Buffer (RTOS Context):** A **buffer** is a region of physical memory (RAM) used to temporarily store data while it is being moved from one place to another. In a Real-Time Operating System, buffers are the fundamental data structures that decouple the **producer** (source of data) from the **consumer** (sink of data), enabling asynchronous communication between tasks, ISRs, and peripherals without enforcing strict temporal synchronization.

In the KTU 2024 PECPT741 syllabus, buffers are studied as critical inter-task communication primitives that directly influence **system latency**, **jitter**, **throughput**, and **determinism** — the four pillars of any hard real-time embedded system.

### 1.2 Types of Buffers Recognized in RTOS Curriculum

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Single Buffer** | One contiguous memory region with read/write pointers | Trivial low-speed streams |
| **Double Buffer (Ping-Pong)** | Two alternating buffers to overlap I/O and compute | DMA, video frames |
| **Circular Buffer (Ring Buffer)** | Fixed-size FIFO where pointers wrap around | UART, ADC, audio |
| **Message/Queue Buffer** | Buffer holding discrete messages with metadata | Mailboxes, message queues |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Pass

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Restaurant Kitchen Pass Analogy:**
> Imagine a busy restaurant where the **chef (producer)** cooks dishes faster than the **waiter (consumer)** can serve them. Without a counter, the chef would have to wait, or the waiter would drop food. The **stainless-steel counter between them is the buffer**. It holds a few plates, allowing the chef to keep cooking and the waiter to take plates at his own pace.
> - If the counter has **only 1 slot** → Single buffer (chef stops often).
> - If the counter has **2 slots that swap** → Ping-pong buffer (chef always has one free slot).
> - If the counter is **circular with N slots** → Ring buffer (rotational slot reuse).
> - If the counter is **full**, the chef must **block** (back-pressure).

### 1.4 Why Buffers Matter in Real-Time Systems

Real-time systems cannot tolerate indefinite blocking. Hence buffer sizing, selection, and management directly impact whether a system meets its **deadlines**. KTU examiners frequently test the link between buffer size, sampling rate, and worst-case latency.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Circular (Ring) Buffer

A circular buffer of capacity $N$ uses two indices:
- **Head (Write Pointer, $W$)** — points to the next slot to write.
- **Tail (Read Pointer, $R$)** — points to the next slot to read.

Both pointers increment modulo $N$, producing a ring behavior.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Initialize:** $W = 0$, $R = 0$, count $= 0$.
2. **Write Operation (Producer):**
   - Check if `count == N` → **BUFFER FULL** (overflow).
   - Else store data at `buf[W]`, increment $W \leftarrow (W+1) \bmod N$, increment `count`.
3. **Read Operation (Consumer):**
   - Check if `count == 0` → **BUFFER EMPTY** (underflow).
   - Else read `buf[R]`, increment $R \leftarrow (R+1) \bmod N$, decrement `count`.

### 2.2 Full vs Empty Discrimination

A notorious bug in circular buffers is the ambiguity between empty and full states when both $R$ and $W$ coincide. Three standard disambiguation techniques:

| Technique | Mechanism | Trade-off |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| **Sentinel Slot** | Allocate $N+1$ slots; full = $R == W$ after a write, empty = $R == W$ initially | Wastes 1 slot |
| **Count Variable** | Maintain explicit `count` | 1 extra word, robust |
| **MSB Tagging** | Use high bit of $R$ or $W$ as a wrap counter | 1 extra bit |

### 2.3 Ping-Pong (Double) Buffering

> [!NOTE]
> **Ping-Pong Buffers** maintain two equal-sized buffers. While the producer writes to one (say `buf_A` — *ping*), the consumer reads from the other (`buf_B` — *pong*). On a synchronization event (frame end, half-buffer DMA IRQ), the roles swap. This **doubles effective throughput** by removing the producer/consumer mutual exclusion bottleneck on a single buffer.

**Ideal for:**
- Video frame pipelines (camera ↔ DSP).
- Audio sample streams.
- DMA "double-buffer mode" in modern microcontrollers (STM32, ESP32).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU Board Exam**

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Condition |
|--------|---------|---------------------|
| $N$ | Buffer capacity (slots) | $N \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}$, $N \ge 2$ for double buffer |
| $W$ | Write pointer | $W_{k+1} = (W_k + 1) \bmod N$ |
| $R$ | Read pointer | $R_{k+1} = (R_k + 1) \bmod N$ |
| $n$ | Current occupancy | $n = (W - R + N) \bmod N$ |
| Free slots | Remaining capacity | $N - n$ |
| $f_s$ | Sampling frequency (Hz) | $f_s = 1 / T_s$ |
| $T_s$ | Sampling period (s) | $T_s = 1 / f_s$ |
| $B_{\min}$ | Minimum buffer size to avoid overflow | $B_{\min} = \left\lceil f_s \cdot (t_{consumer}^{\max} - t_{producer}^{\max}) \right\rceil$ |
| $L_{\max}$ | Worst-case consumer latency (s) | $L_{\max} = B_{\min} \cdot T_s$ |
| $T_{\text{swap}}$ | Ping-pong swap period (s) | $T_{\text{swap}} = N / f_s$ |
| $J$ | Jitter (s) | $J = L_{\max} - L_{\min}$ |
| $\eta$ | Buffer utilization | $\eta = n / N$ |
| $T_{\text{throughput}}$ | Throughput (samples/s) | $T_{\text{throughput}} = \min(f_{\text{in}}, f_{\text{out}})$ |
| $M_{\text{ring}}$ | Memory footprint (bytes) | $M_{\text{ring}} = N \cdot S_{\text{elem}}$ |

**Boundary conditions for circular pointer math:**
- Full state (sentinel method): $(W + 1) \bmod N = R$
- Empty state: $W = R$

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECU (CAN bus):** Ring buffers decouple the high-speed CAN ISR from the slower application task processing messages.
- **Audio DSP (mp3, AAC decoders):** Ping-pong buffers ensure glitch-free playback.
- **Industrial PLCs:** Buffers smooth bursty sensor data before control loop processing.
- **Medical devices (ECG, pulse oximeter):** Circular buffers allow post-hoc analysis after each sample window.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Buffer Size to Prevent Overflow

Consider a producer generating data at rate $f_p$ (samples/s) and a consumer draining data at rate $f_c$ (samples/s). We want to find the **minimum buffer size** $B_{\min}$ that guarantees no data loss for a time window $T_w$.

**Step 1:** In a small time $dt$, producer adds $f_p \cdot dt$ samples; consumer removes $f_c \cdot dt$ samples.

**Step 2:** Net accumulation rate:
$$\frac{dn}{dt} = f_p - f_c$$

**Step 3:** Integrate over a worst-case window $T_w$ where producer runs continuously and consumer is blocked (e.g., preempted by a higher-priority task):
$$n(T_w) = n(0) + \int_0^{T_w} (f_p - f_c) \, dt = n(0) + (f_p - f_c) \cdot T_w$$

**Step 4:** To avoid overflow, the initial occupancy $n(0) = 0$ (empty buffer at start) and the final occupancy $n(T_w) \le B_{\min} - 1$ (must never equal capacity). Solving:
$$B_{\min} \ge (f_p - f_c) \cdot T_w + 1$$

**Step 5:** Since $B_{\min}$ must be an integer and we include safety margin for jitter $\Delta t_j$:
$$B_{\min} = \left\lceil (f_p - f_c) \cdot (T_w + \Delta t_j) \right\rceil + 1$$

**Step 6:** In the special case where the producer rate equals the sampling frequency $f_s$ and the consumer is the bottleneck with maximum response time $t_{c,\max}$:
$$B_{\min} = \left\lceil f_s \cdot t_{c,\max} \right\rceil + 1$$

This result is the cornerstone of every **Rate Monotonic Analysis (RMA)** feasibility check on a producer-consumer pair.

### 3.2 Derivation: Worst-Case Latency Through a Circular Buffer

The **worst-case consumer-side latency** $L_{\max}$ occurs when a fresh sample arrives **just after** the consumer has finished reading the previous one. The consumer must wait for the next sample to be deposited.

**Step 1:** Time between producer writes: $T_p = 1 / f_p$.

**Step 2:** Consumer read time per element: $T_c = 1 / f_c$.

**Step 3:** Worst-case waiting time inside the buffer (FIFO discipline):
$$L_{\max} = (B_{\text{current}} - 1) \cdot T_p + T_c$$

**Step 4:** If the buffer is nominally half-full on average ($\bar{B} = N/2$):
$$L_{\max} \approx \frac{N-1}{f_p} + \frac{1}{f_c}$$

**Step 5:** Best-case latency (buffer empty, fresh sample already present):
$$L_{\min} = T_c$$

**Step 6:** Jitter:
$$J = L_{\max} - L_{\min} = \frac{N-1}{f_p}$$

**Interpretation:** Larger buffers improve **throughput** (less overflow) but **degrade latency and increase jitter** — a direct trade-off that KTU examiners love to test.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A temperature sensor samples at $f_s = 1 \text{ kHz}$ (i.e., $T_s = 1 \text{ ms}$). A monitoring task runs with a worst-case execution budget that causes it to block for at most $t_b = 5 \text{ ms}$. (a) Compute the minimum ring-buffer size to prevent sample loss. (b) Compute the worst-case latency. (c) Comment on whether the system can be considered "real-time" if the deadline is $D = 8 \text{ ms}$.

**Part (a) — Minimum buffer size:**

Apply $B_{\min} = \lceil f_s \cdot t_b \rceil + 1$:

$$B_{\min} = \lceil 1000 \times 0.005 \rceil + 1 = \lceil 5 \rceil + 1 = 6 \text{ slots}$$

**Part (b) — Worst-case latency:**

$$L_{\max} = (B_{\min} - 1) \cdot T_s = (6 - 1) \times 0.001 = 0.005 \text{ s} = 5 \text{ ms}$$

**Part (c) — Real-time viability:**

Since $L_{\max} = 5 \text{ ms} < D = 8 \text{ ms}$, the system **meets its deadline** with a slack of $3 \text{ ms}$. The choice $B_{\min} = 6$ is **both safe and tight** — using $B = 16$ would have caused $L_{\max} = 15 \text{ ms} > D$, violating the deadline!

**Insight:** This counter-intuitive result (smaller buffer is *better* for latency) is exactly the kind of trap KTU examiners set in 14-mark questions.

### 3.4 Ping-Pong Throughput Derivation

Let $T_p$ = time to fill one buffer, $T_c$ = time to drain the other. Steady-state throughput:

$$T_{\text{cycle}} = \max(T_p, T_c)$$
$$\text{Throughput} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{cycle}}} \text{ buffers per second} = \frac{f_s}{N} \text{ samples/s (per buffer)}$$

**Effective system throughput** is governed by the slower of the two halves:
$$\text{Throughput}_{\text{sys}} = \min\!\left(\frac{1}{T_p}, \frac{1}{T_c}\right)$$

The **swap overhead** $T_{\text{swap}}$ (typically one DMA IRQ or semaphore post) must satisfy:
$$T_{\text{swap}} \ll \min(T_p, T_c)$$
for the ping-pong scheme to remain efficient (rule of thumb: $T_{\text{swap}} \le 1\% \cdot \min(T_p, T_c)$).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Circular Buffer — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef producer fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef buffer fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef consumer fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef slot fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000
    classDef ptr fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000

    ISR[Hardware ISR Producer]:::producer
    APP[Application Task Consumer]:::consumer

    subgraph RING["Circular Ring Buffer N=8"]
        direction TB
        S0[Slot 0]:::slot
        S1[Slot 1]:::slot
        S2[Slot 2]:::slot
        S3[Slot 3]:::slot
        S4[Slot 4]:::slot
        S5[Slot 5]:::slot
        S6[Slot 6]:::slot
        S7[Slot 7]:::slot
    end

    Wptr[Write Head W]:::ptr
    Rptr[Read Tail R]:::ptr

    ISR -->|write at W| S0
    APP -->|read at R| S0

    Wptr -.-> S3
    Rptr -.-> S1
```

### 4.2 Producer–Consumer with Buffer — State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Empty
    Empty --> Partial: ISR writes item
    Partial --> Partial: ISR writes OR Task reads
    Partial --> Full: All N slots occupied
    Full --> Partial: Task reads one item
    Partial --> Empty: Task reads last item
    Full --> Full: ISR write BLOCKED back-pressure
    Empty --> Empty: Task read BLOCKED
```

### 4.3 Ping-Pong (Double) Buffer — Data Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    classDef src fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef b1 fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef b2 fill:#e2d6f9,stroke:#6f42c1,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef sync fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000

    SRC[Sensor / DMA Stream]:::src
    BUF_A[Buffer A Ping]:::b1
    BUF_B[Buffer B Pong]:::b2
    DSP[Processing Unit CPU]:::proc
    SYNC[Swap Signal ISR or Semaphore]:::sync

    SRC -->|write active| BUF_A
    SRC -.->|write standby| BUF_B
    BUF_A -->|read active| DSP
    BUF_B -.->|read standby| DSP
    SYNC -->|toggle roles| BUF_A
    SYNC -->|toggle roles| BUF_B
```

### 4.4 Buffer Overflow vs Underflow Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef ok fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef err fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef q fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#0dcaf0,color:#000

    START[Buffer Access Request]:::q
    WRITE{Is this a write?}:::q
    FULL{Count equals N?}:::q
    READ{Is this a read?}:::q
    EMPTY{Count equals 0?}:::q
    OVERFLOW[Buffer OVERFLOW Reject or Block]:::err
    UNDERFLOW[Buffer UNDERFLOW Reject or Block]:::err
    OKW[Write OK Increment W]:::ok
    OKR[Read OK Increment R]:::ok

    START --> WRITE
    WRITE -->|Yes| FULL
    FULL -->|Yes| OVERFLOW
    FULL -->|No| OKW
    WRITE -->|No| READ
    READ -->|Yes| EMPTY
    EMPTY -->|Yes| UNDERFLOW
    EMPTY -->|No| OKR
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a circular buffer. State TWO advantages and ONE disadvantage of using circular buffers in real-time systems.*

**Model Answer:**

A **circular buffer** (ring buffer) is a FIFO data structure of fixed capacity $N$ implemented over a linear array where the read pointer $R$ and write pointer $W$ wrap around to the start once they reach the end, using modular arithmetic:
$$W_{k+1} = (W_k + 1) \bmod N$$

**Advantages:**
1. **Constant-time $O(1)$** insert and delete — predictable execution time, ideal for ISR contexts where bounded execution matters.
2. **No dynamic memory allocation** — the buffer is statically allocated, eliminating heap fragmentation and malloc latency spikes that would break real-time guarantees.

**Disadvantage:**
1. **Fixed capacity** — if the producer burst exceeds consumer drain rate, data is lost (overflow) or the producer must block. Resizing is not possible at runtime.

*(Examiner's note: Students often write only the definition and forget both advantages; 1 mark is reserved for the disadvantage.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between single buffering and double buffering (ping-pong) with a suitable RTOS example.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Single Buffer | Double (Ping-Pong) Buffer |
|--------|---------------|---------------------------|
| Memory used | 1 × buffer size | 2 × buffer size |
| Concurrency | Producer & consumer **mutually exclusive** on one buffer | Producer & consumer operate on **separate** buffers simultaneously |
| Throughput | Limited by sum of producer + consumer time | Limited by $\max(\text{producer}, \text{consumer})$ time |
| Latency jitter | High (lock contention) | Low (parallel overlap) |
| Typical RTOS example | Simple UART character echo | Camera frame capture to LCD display |

**Example:** In a video streaming pipeline on an STM32H7, the **DMA fills Ping buffer** while the **CPU processes Pong buffer**; on *VSYNC* IRQ the roles swap. This allows pixel-perfect 60 fps display without dropped frames.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before Writing!**
> 1. **Pointer arithmetic mistakes** — students forget to apply mod $N$ and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing "full" with "empty"** — both occur when $R = W$; failing to mention the disambiguation technique costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Ignoring units** — answers with no unit on latency/throughput lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **No latency analysis** — many students compute buffer size but never compute $L_{\max}$ or compare with the deadline; in 14-mark questions this means losing 4–5 marks.
> 5. **Hand-waving "real-time"** — concluding "system is real-time" without numeric comparison against deadline is a 0-mark conclusion.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *(Choice 1)*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PECPT741]**
*An embedded data-acquisition system samples an analog signal at $f_s = 2 \text{ kHz}$ into a circular buffer. The processing task runs at a lower priority and is preempted for a worst-case duration of $T_b = 12 \text{ ms}$ by a higher-priority task.*

**(a)** Derive the formula for the minimum buffer size to avoid sample loss. Compute the required $B_{\min}$ for the given system. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compute the worst-case consumer latency $L_{\max}$ and the buffer utilization $\eta$ when $B = 32$ slots are allocated. State with justification whether the system can meet a hard deadline of $D = 20 \text{ ms}$. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Number of samples produced during block window:**

The producer (ADC ISR) deposits one sample every $T_s = 1/f_s$. During the consumer block time $T_b$, the number of samples generated is:
$$n_{\text{prod}} = f_s \cdot T_b$$

Substituting $f_s = 2000 \text{ Hz}$, $T_b = 0.012 \text{ s}$:
$$n_{\text{prod}} = 2000 \times 0.012 = 24 \text{ samples}$$

**Step 2 — Buffer must hold all incoming samples:**

To prevent overflow, the buffer must be able to store at least $n_{\text{prod}}$ samples. Adding a safety slot for the sentinel-counting technique:
$$B_{\min} = \lceil f_s \cdot T_b \rceil + 1$$

**Step 3 — Plug in values:**
$$B_{\min} = \lceil 2000 \times 0.012 \rceil + 1 = \lceil 24 \rceil + 1 = 25 \text{ slots}$$

**Answer:** $B_{\min} = \mathbf{25 \text{ slots}}$.

**Derivation summary (as required by 7-mark sub-part):**
Starting from the rate equation $dn/dt = f_p - f_c$ with $f_c = 0$ during the block window, integrating over $T_b$ gives $n(T_b) = f_s \cdot T_b$. Requiring $n \le B - 1$ and ceiling the result for integer slot count yields the formula above.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Worst-case latency with $B = 32$ slots:**

Using the FIFO latency formula:
$$L_{\max} = (B - 1) \cdot T_s = (32 - 1) \times \frac{1}{2000}$$

$$L_{\max} = 31 \times 0.0005 = 0.0155 \text{ s} = 15.5 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Buffer utilization at the worst instant:**

At the worst-case moment, the buffer holds all $n_{\text{prod}} = 24$ samples:
$$\eta = \frac{n}{B} = \frac{24}{32} = 0.75 = 75\%$$

**Step 3 — Deadline check:**

Compare $L_{\max}$ with the deadline $D = 20 \text{ ms}$:
$$L_{\max} = 15.5 \text{ ms} < D = 20 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$$

The system **meets the hard deadline** with a slack of:
$$\text{Slack} = D - L_{\max} = 20 - 15.5 = 4.5 \text{ ms}$$

**Conclusion:** The system is **hard real-time feasible** for $D = 20 \text{ ms}$. However, allocating $B = 25$ (the minimum) would have given:
$$L_{\max} = 24 \times 0.5 \text{ ms} = 12 \text{ ms} \quad (\text{even better slack of } 8 \text{ ms})$$

So $B = 25$ is the **optimal** choice; $B = 32$ is over-engineered.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *(Choice 2)*

**[KTU University Exam – Sample, PECPT741]**
**(a)** With neat functional diagrams, explain the operation of **Ping-Pong (double) buffering** in an RTOS-based data pipeline. Mention its advantages over single buffering. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An audio codec streams 16-bit PCM samples at $f_s = 44.1 \text{ kHz}$ into a ping-pong buffer pair of size $N = 256$ samples each. Compute (i) the **time** available to the consumer to process one ping buffer, (ii) the **maximum sustainable** processing time per sample to avoid audio glitching, and (iii) the **jitter** introduced if the consumer sometimes completes early by 1 ms. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Definition:** Ping-Pong buffering uses two equal buffers (Buffer A, Buffer B). While the producer (e.g., DMA from audio codec) writes into one, the consumer (DSP or playback task) reads from the other. On a hardware or timer-driven *swap event*, the roles exchange.

**Operation (refer to diagram in Section 4.3):**
- **Phase 1:** DMA fills Buffer A; CPU processes Buffer B (from previous cycle).
- **Phase 2 (on swap IRQ):** Roles toggle. DMA now fills Buffer B; CPU processes Buffer A.
- Steady state: producer and consumer run **in parallel**, never contending for the same memory.

**Advantages over single buffering:**
1. **Doubles throughput** — $\text{Throughput} = \min(1/T_p, 1/T_c)$ rather than $1/(T_p + T_c)$.
2. **Eliminates lock contention** — no semaphore/mutex needed for buffer access.
3. **Predictable latency** — swap period is fixed at $T_{\text{swap}} = N/f_s$.
4. **Ideal for DMA double-buffer mode** — present in nearly all modern ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers (STM32, NXP Kinetis, ESP32).

**Disadvantages:**
1. Doubles memory footprint.
2. Swap interrupt adds $T_{\text{swap}}$ overhead; must be kept small.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Time to fill one ping buffer:**
$$T_{\text{fill}} = \frac{N}{f_s} = \frac{256}{44100} = 5.805 \text{ ms}$$

**(ii) Maximum sustainable per-sample processing time:**

In ping-pong, the consumer has $T_{\text{fill}} = 5.805 \text{ ms}$ to process $N = 256$ samples. So:
$$t_{\text{proc, max}} = \frac{T_{\text{fill}}}{N} = \frac{1}{f_s} = 22.7 \, \mu\text{s per sample}$$

This is exactly the sampling period — meaning the consumer must finish before the next swap. The CPU budget per sample is **$22.7 \,\mu\text{s}$**.

**(iii) Jitter analysis:**

Nominal latency = $T_{\text{fill}} = 5.805 \text{ ms}$.
If consumer completes 1 ms early, latency becomes $4.805 \text{ ms}$.
$$J = L_{\max} - L_{\min} = 5.805 - 4.805 = 1.0 \text{ ms}$$

But for **audio quality**, the *real* concern is **inter-sample jitter** (variation in $T_{\text{fill}}$), which manifests as audible distortion. The 1 ms early completion does NOT break the buffer contract (no underflow), so the system remains glitch-free; the jitter is absorbed by the FIFO discipline.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Buffers (Module 4)**

- ✅ **Circular buffer math** — both pointers use mod-$N$ arithmetic; never use raw increment.
- ✅ **Full vs empty** — disambiguate via **count variable**, **sentinel slot**, or **MSB tag**.
- ✅ **$B_{\min}$ formula** — $B_{\min} = \lceil f_s \cdot t_{block} \rceil + 1$ is the most-tested equation in this module.
- ✅ **Latency vs Throughput trade-off** — larger $N$ → higher throughput, **worse** latency. This is the central KTU theme.
- ✅ **Ping-pong** doubles throughput; uses 2× memory; eliminates mutual exclusion.
- ✅ **Jitter** $J = L_{\max} - L_{\min} = (N-1)/f_s$ — grows linearly with buffer size.
- ✅ **Static allocation** is preferred in RTOS — never use `malloc` inside an ISR.
- ✅ **Overflow/underflow** handling: either **block** the producer/consumer (preferred) or **drop** samples (last resort in best-effort systems).
- ✅ **Real-time check** always requires numeric comparison of $L_{\max}$ against $D$ — qualitative answers score zero.
- ✅ **DMA double-buffer mode** is a hardware implementation of ping-pong — mention it for full marks on 7-mark questions.
- ✅ **Memory footprint** $M = N \times S_{\text{elem}}$; for 16-bit samples at $N=256$, $M = 512$ bytes.
- ✅ **Watch the units**: ms vs $\mu$s vs s — KTU examiners specifically deduct for missing units on latency answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mailboxes
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT741 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mailboxes

# 1. Mailboxes – Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECPT741 – Module 4: Inter-Process & Task Communication):**
> A **Mailbox** is a kernel-managed Inter-Process Communication (IPC) object in a Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) that allows **tasks and Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs) to exchange fixed-size messages** (typically message pointers or value pointers) through a **bounded FIFO-style buffer** with deterministic, bounded blocking semantics.

## 1.1 Intuitive Analogy – The "Locked Drop-Box"

Imagine a small **physical drop-box** installed on a wall between two offices:

- Office A (the *Sender Task*) drops a sealed envelope into the box.
- Office B (the *Receiver Task*) opens the box and retrieves the envelope.
- The box has a **fixed capacity** — at most 1, 4, or N envelopes can sit inside.
- If the box is **full**, Office A must *wait* until Office B removes something.
- If the box is **empty**, Office B must *wait* until Office A delivers something.

This **synchronous, bounded, hand-shaking** communication is exactly what a *Mailbox* does inside an RTOS kernel — but with **hard real-time guarantees on the worst-case blocking time**.

## 1.2 Classification of Mailboxes in RTOS

| Mailbox Type | Capacity (Slots) | Typical Use Case | Example RTOS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single-Slot Mailbox** | **1 message** | Tightest deterministic latency, binary signalling | μC/OS-II |
| **Multi-Slot Mailbox (Bounded Buffer)** | **N ≥ 1 messages** | Producer–Consumer pipelines, command queues | VxWorks `msgQ` variants, FreeRTOS Queues |
| **Broadcast Mailbox** | 1 slot, N receivers | One-to-many event signalling | OSEK COM |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **μC/OS-II** terminology, a "Mailbox" specifically holds a **pointer to a message buffer** (typically `void *`), which may point to a larger data structure. This is distinct from a "Message Queue" (which stores an array of bytes). The KTU 2024 syllabus follows this classical Philip Lehrbaum / Jean Labrosse convention.

## 1.3 Position in the RTOS IPC Hierarchy

**Mailboxes occupy the middle-ground of the IPC spectrum:**

$$\text{Semaphore} \;\prec\; \text{Mailbox} \;\prec\; \text{Message Queue} \;\prec\; \text{Pipe/Socket (Network Stack)}$$

- More expressive than a semaphore (carries *data*, not just a flag).
- Simpler, more deterministic, and lower-overhead than a full message queue.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture of a Mailbox

A mailbox is a kernel data structure — typically an **OS_MAILBOX** control block — containing:

- **`OS_EVENT` type flag** identifying the object as a mailbox (`OS_EVENT_TYPE_MAILBOX`).
- **`OSMsg`** — a circular/linked FIFO list of message slots.
- **`N`** — maximum number of slots (configurable at create time).
- **`WaitList`** — a doubly-linked list of TCBs (Task Control Blocks) blocked on full/empty conditions, sorted by **task priority**.
- **Owner / Creator pointer** — task that created the mailbox (used for cleanup).

## 2.2 Core Mailbox Service Calls (POSIX / μC/OS-II Mapping)

| Operation | μC/OS-II API | POSIX Equivalent | Blocking? | Typical Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Create** | `OSMboxCreate(void *msg)` | `mq_open()` | No | **O(1)** |
| **Post (Send)** | `OSMboxPost(OS_EVENT *pevent, void *msg)` | `mq_send()` | **Yes** (blocks on FULL) | **O(1)** |
| **Pend (Receive)** | `OSMboxPend(OS_EVENT *pevent, INT16U timeout, INT8U *err)` | `mq_receive()` | **Yes** (blocks on EMPTY, with timeout) | **O(1)** |
| **Accept (Non-Block Pend)** | `OSMboxAccept(OS_EVENT *pevent)` | `mq_receive(..., O_NONBLOCK)` | **No** | **O(1)** |
| **Delete** | `OSMboxDel(OS_EVENT *pevent, INT8U opt, INT8U *err)` | `mq_unlink()` | No | **O(N)** |
| **Query** | `OSMboxQuery(OS_EVENT *pevent, OS_MBOX_DATA *pdata)` | `mq_getattr()` | No | **O(1)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pend Timeout Semantics (KTU Favourite):** In `OSMboxPend(..., timeout, &err)`, the `timeout` is specified in **clock ticks** (or `0` for *non-blocking*, or `OS_TICKS_PER_SEC × n` for *n seconds*). On timeout expiry, the call returns a `NULL` pointer and sets `err = OS_TIMEOUT`. This **bounded-blocking guarantee** is the cornerstone of real-time determinism.

## 2.3 State Machine of a Mailbox Slot

The mailbox as a whole moves through these deterministic states:

```
         POST (msg ≠ NULL)              PEND (msg retrieved)
  EMPTY  ─────────────────────►  FULL   ─────────────────────►  EMPTY
   │                                   │
   │  PEND (immediate, returns NULL)   │  POST (blocks task on WaitList)
   └──────────────────────────────────►└──────────────────────────┐
                                                                  ▼
                                                          Task suspended
                                                          (in WaitList,
                                                          sorted by priority)
```

> [!NOTE]
> A *Full* mailbox blocks the **sender**; an *Empty* mailbox blocks the **receiver**. The kernel performs a **direct task hand-off** — if a high-priority task is already waiting in the receiver's WaitList when a `POST` occurs, the kernel **immediately** wakes that task and hands it the message **without** copying data (since mailboxes carry a *pointer*).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **core analytical toolkit** the KTU 2024 ESE examiner expects for mailbox-based scheduling problems.

### 2.4.1 Mailbox Capacity & Memory Footprint

$$M_{\text{total}} \;=\; N \;\times\; (S_{\text{ptr}} + S_{\text{ctrl}})$$

where:
- $N$ = number of slots in the mailbox buffer.
- $S_{\text{ptr}}$ = size of a single message pointer (**typically 4 bytes on 32-bit, 8 bytes on 64-bit**).
- $S_{\text{ctrl}}$ = per-slot control overhead (next-pointer, flags) — usually **8 to 16 bytes**.

### 2.4.2 Worst-Case Blocking Time for a Pend Operation

$$B_{\text{max}}^{\text{pend}} \;=\; T_{\text{context\_switch}} \;+\; T_{\text{timeout}} \;+\; T_{\text{kernel\_critical}}$$

where:
- $T_{\text{context\_switch}}$ = maximum context-switch time (kernel-dependent, e.g., **5–50 μs** in commercial RTOS).
- $T_{\text{timeout}}$ = user-supplied worst-case wait.
- $T_{\text{kernel\_critical}}$ = time the kernel holds interrupts disabled.

### 2.4.3 Throughput of a Single-Slot Mailbox (Hand-off Bound)

$$\Theta_{\text{1-slot}} \;=\; \frac{1}{t_{\text{post}} + t_{\text{pend}} + 2 \cdot t_{\text{ctx}}}$$

For an $N$-slot mailbox (allows pipelining), the asymptotic bound is:

$$\Theta_{N\text{-slot}} \;=\; \min\!\left(\, \frac{N}{t_{\text{post}} + t_{\text{pend}}},\; \frac{1}{t_{\text{ctx}}} \,\right)$$

### 2.4.4 Deadline-Miss Probability (Real-Time Guarantee)

$$P_{\text{miss}} \;=\; P\!\left( B_{\text{max}}^{\text{pend}} + T_{\text{CPU}} \;>\; D_{\text{deadline}} \right)$$

A **Hard Real-Time** system requires $P_{\text{miss}} = 0$.

### 2.4.5 Priority Inversion Bound (Priority Ceiling Protocol with Mailbox)

The maximum time a high-priority task $T_H$ can be blocked by lower-priority tasks $T_{L_1}, T_{L_2}, \ldots, T_{L_k}$ is:

$$B_{\text{inversion}}^{\text{max}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{CS}_{T_{L_i}} \;+\; (k-1) \cdot T_{\text{ctx}}$$

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Mailboxes

| Field | Application | Why Mailbox Fits |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive ECU (AUTOSAR OS)** | CAN message dispatch between COM and Application tasks | Single-slot mailbox = bounded latency, no dynamic allocation |
| **Avionics (DO-178C, ARINC 653)** | Partition-to-partition inter-partition communication | Mailbox slots map directly to ARINC **sampling/queuing ports** |
| **Industrial PLC (IEC 61131-3)** | Sensor data handoff to control loop | Deterministic hand-off, no jitter from copying |
| **Medical Embedded (FDA Class II/III)** | Patient monitor → alarm task communication | Pointer-passing avoids copying of large waveform buffers |
| **Robotics (ROS 2 micro-control)** | Joint controller ↔ planner task | Bounded buffer = predictable latency under load |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just use a global variable?** A shared global requires **explicit mutual exclusion** (mutex/semaphore), is vulnerable to **priority inversion** and **deadlock**, and offers **no built-in blocking semantics**. A mailbox encapsulates **synchronization + data transfer** in a single, kernel-verified primitive — at the cost of a small, fixed allocation.
