# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Necessary Conditions for Deadlock (Coffman Conditions)

A deadlock can occur **only if all four** of the following conditions hold simultaneously. These are known as the **Coffman Conditions**, named after Edward G. Coffman Jr. who defined them in 1971.

### Condition 1: Mutual Exclusion
At least one resource must be held in a **non-shareable (exclusive)** mode. Only one process at a time can use the resource. If another process requests it, it must be delayed until the resource is released.

- **RTOS Example:** A mutex lock protecting a UART peripheral. Only one task can hold the UART mutex at any given instant.

### Condition 2: Hold and Wait
A process must be holding at least one resource **and** waiting to acquire additional resources that are currently held by other processes.

- **RTOS Example:** Task T1 holds a CAN bus mutex and waits for an ADC mutex held by Task T2.

### Condition 3: No Preemption
Resources cannot be preempted. A resource can only be released **voluntarily** by the process holding it after that process has completed its critical section.

- **RTOS Example:** You cannot forcibly take a semaphore from a task in FreeRTOS; the task must call `xSemaphoreGive()`.

### Condition 4: Circular Wait
A set of waiting processes $\{P_0, P_1, \ldots, P_k\}$ must exist such that:
$$P_0 \rightarrow P_1 \rightarrow P_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow P_k \rightarrow P_0$$

- $P_0$ is waiting for a resource held by $P_1$
- $P_1$ is waiting for a resource held by $P_2$
- …
- $P_k$ is waiting for a resource held by $P_0$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Breaking **any one** of these four conditions is sufficient to prevent deadlocks. The most commonly broken condition in real systems is **Circular Wait** (by enforcing a strict global ordering of lock acquisition).

## 2.2 Resource Allocation Graph (RAG) — Detailed Analysis

A **Resource Allocation Graph (RAG)** is a directed bipartite graph used to visualize the state of a system with respect to resource allocation. It has two node types:

- **Circles (○)** represent Processes: $P_1, P_2, \ldots$
- **Squares (□)** represent Resource Types. A dot (•) inside the square represents an instance of that resource.

**Two types of edges exist:**

1. **Assignment Edge (Allocation Edge):** $P_i \rightarrow R_j$ — This means process $P_i$ has been allocated an instance of resource $R_j$. The arrow points **from process to resource**.
2. **Request Edge:** $P_i \rightarrow R_j$ — This means process $P_i$ is requesting an instance of resource $R_j$. The arrow points **from process to resource** as well, but is differentiated by being drawn from a process waiting for the resource.

### Key Theorems for RAG:
- **Theorem 1:** If the graph contains **no cycles**, then **no deadlock exists** in the system.
- **Theorem 2:** If the graph contains a cycle, then:
  - If each resource type has **only one instance**, then a cycle $\Rightarrow$ **deadlock is definite**.
  - If a resource type has **multiple instances**, then a cycle $\Rightarrow$ **deadlock is possible but not certain**.

## 2.3 Deadlock Handling Strategies — Complete Taxonomy

| Strategy | When Applied | Approach | RTOS Suitability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Deadlock Prevention** | At design time | Negate one of the 4 Coffman conditions | ★★★★★ Excellent |
| **Deadlock Avoidance** | At runtime, before allocation | Use Banker's Algorithm; ensure system stays in safe state | ★★★ Moderate |
| **Deadlock Detection** | At runtime, periodically | Build WFG, check for cycles | ★ Poor (runtime cost) |
| **Recovery after Detection** | After detection | Abort a process or preempt resources | ★ Poor for hard RT |
| **Ostrich Algorithm** | Ignore the problem | Pretend deadlocks never occur | Used in some Unix |

## 2.4 Deadlock Prevention — Breaking Each Condition

| Coffman Condition | Prevention Technique | Drawback |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Mutual Exclusion** | Make resources shareable (e.g., read-only files) | Not always possible (printers, mutexes) |
| **Hold and Wait** | Request **all** resources at once before execution | Poor resource utilization; starvation |
| **No Preemption** | If a process holding resources requests another unavailable resource, **preempt** all its held resources | Only works for state-restorable resources (CPU, memory) |
| **Circular Wait** | Enforce a **total ordering** of resource types; each process requests resources in increasing order | Requires global knowledge; hard to enforce in large systems |

## 2.5 Deadlock Avoidance — Banker's Algorithm

Proposed by **Edsger Dijkstra (1965)**, the Banker's Algorithm models a banker who lends money to customers, ensuring that the bank can always satisfy all outstanding requests by keeping a safe reserve.

### Key Definitions:
- **Available Vector ($A$):** $A[j] = $ number of available instances of resource type $R_j$.
- **Max Matrix ($M$):** $M[i][j] = $ maximum number of instances of $R_j$ that process $P_i$ may request.
- **Allocation Matrix ($C$):** $C[i][j] = $ number of instances of $R_j$ currently allocated to $P_i$.
- **Need Matrix ($N$):** $N[i][j] = M[i][j] - C[i][j] = $ remaining maximum need of $P_i$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Invariant of Banker's Algorithm:** $N[i][j] \geq 0$ for all $i, j$, AND the system must always remain in a **safe state** before granting any request.

### Safe State Definition:
A state is **safe** if there exists a **safe sequence** of all processes $\{P_{\pi(1)}, P_{\pi(2)}, \ldots, P_{\pi(n)}\}$ such that for each $P_{\pi(k)}$, the resources it needs can be satisfied by the currently available resources plus the resources held by all $P_{\pi(j)}$ for $j < k$.

### Safety Algorithm (used to test for safe state):

```
1. Let Work = Available          // Initially available resources
   Let Finish[i] = false for all i
2. Find an i such that:
       Finish[i] == false  AND
       Need[i] ≤ Work
   If no such i exists, go to step 4
3. Work = Work + Allocation[i]
   Finish[i] = true
   Go to step 2
4. If Finish[i] == true for all i, then the system is in a SAFE state.
   Otherwise, the system is in an UNSAFE state (deadlock possible).
```

### Resource Request Algorithm (for process $P_i$ requesting $R_j$):

```
1. If Request[i] ≤ Need[i], go to step 2.
   Otherwise, raise an error (process exceeded its max claim).
2. If Request[i] ≤ Available, go to step 3.
   Otherwise, P_i must WAIT (resources not available).
3. Pretend to allocate (modify state):
   Available  = Available  - Request[i]
   Allocation[i] = Allocation[i] + Request[i]
   Need[i]    = Need[i]    - Request[i]
4. Run the Safety Algorithm on the new state.
   If safe → grant the request permanently.
   If unsafe → roll back the pretend allocation; P_i must WAIT.
```

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Total Instances of $R_j$** | $E[j] = A[j] + \sum_{i=1}^{n} C[i][j]$ | Total = Available + Allocated |
| **Need of $P_i$ for $R_j$** | $N[i][j] = M[i][j] - C[i][j]$ | Maximum - Currently held |
| **Safety condition** | $\exists \pi$ such that $N[\pi(k)] \leq A + \sum_{j<k} C[\pi(j)]$ | A safe sequence must exist |
| **Resource Request validity** | $Req_i \leq N_i$ | Cannot exceed max claim |
| **Grant validity** | $Req_i \leq A$ | Resources must be physically available |
| **Number of RAG cycle conditions** | Cycle + all single-instance $\Rightarrow$ definite deadlock | Multiple instances $\Rightarrow$ possible deadlock |
| **Minimal Resources to avoid deadlock** | $k$ processes need $m$ resources each, system safe if $k(m-1) + 1 \leq R$ | Insufficient-resource theorem |
| **Priority Ceiling ($\Pi$)** | $\Pi(R) = \max(\text{priority of any task that may lock } R)$ | Used in PCP protocol |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Banker's Algorithm — Safety State Computation

### Problem Setup:
Consider a system with **5 processes** $P_0, P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4$ and **3 resource types** $A, B, C$ with the following snapshot at time $T_0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Available } A &= (3,\ 3,\ 2) \\
\text{Max } M &= \begin{bmatrix} 7 & 5 & 3 \\ 3 & 2 & 2 \\ 9 & 0 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 2 \\ 4 & 3 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \\
\text{Allocation } C &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 3 & 0 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1: Compute the Need Matrix $N = M - C$

For each entry $N[i][j] = M[i][j] - C[i][j]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N[0] &= (7-0,\ 5-1,\ 3-0) = (7,\ 4,\ 3) \\
N[1] &= (3-2,\ 2-0,\ 2-0) = (1,\ 2,\ 2) \\
N[2] &= (9-3,\ 0-0,\ 2-2) = (6,\ 0,\ 0) \\
N[3] &= (2-2,\ 2-1,\ 2-1) = (0,\ 1,\ 1) \\
N[4] &= (4-0,\ 3-0,\ 3-2) = (4,\ 3,\ 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

So the Need Matrix is:

$$
N = \begin{bmatrix} 7 & 4 & 3 \\ 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 6 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 4 & 3 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2: Initialize Safety Algorithm
$$
\text{Work} = \text{Available} = (3,\ 3,\ 2)
$$
$$
\text{Finish} = [\text{false},\ \text{false},\ \text{false},\ \text{false},\ \text{false}]
$$

### Step 3: Iteration 1 — Find a process whose Need $\leq$ Work

| Process $P_i$ | Need $N[i]$ | Work = (3,3,2) | Need $\leq$ Work? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $P_0$ | (7,4,3) | (3,3,2) | ❌ No ($7 > 3$) |
| $P_1$ | (1,2,2) | (3,3,2) | ✅ Yes |
| $P_2$ | (6,0,0) | (3,3,2) | ❌ No ($6 > 3$) |
| $P_3$ | (0,1,1) | (3,3,2) | ✅ Yes |
| $P_4$ | (4,3,1) | (3,3,2) | ❌ No ($4 > 3$) |

We can choose $P_1$ (or $P_3$). Let's pick $P_1$ first.

After running $P_1$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Work}_{\text{new}} &= \text{Work} + C[1] = (3,3,2) + (2,0,0) = (5,3,2) \\
\text{Finish}[1] &= \text{true}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Iteration 2 — Work = (5, 3, 2)

| Process | Need | Need $\leq$ Work? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $P_0$ | (7,4,3) | ❌ |
| $P_3$ | (0,1,1) | ✅ |
| $P_4$ | (4,3,1) | ✅ |

Choose $P_3$:
$$
\text{Work} = (5,3,2) + (2,1,1) = (7,4,3)
$$

### Step 5: Iteration 3 — Work = (7, 4, 3)

| Process | Need | Need $\leq$ Work? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $P_0$ | (7,4,3) | ✅ |
| $P_2$ | (6,0,0) | ✅ |
| $P_4$ | (4,3,1) | ✅ |

Choose $P_4$:
$$
\text{Work} = (7,4,3) + (0,0,2) = (7,4,5)
$$

### Step 6: Iteration 4 — Work = (7, 4, 5)

Choose $P_0$:
$$
\text{Work} = (7,4,5) + (0,1,0) = (7,5,5)
$$

### Step 7: Iteration 5 — Work = (7, 5, 5)

Choose $P_2$:
$$
\text{Work} = (7,5,5) + (3,0,2) = (10,5,7)
$$

### Step 8: Final Verification

All Finish values are now true. The **safe sequence** is:
$$
\langle P_1,\ P_3,\ P_4,\ P_0,\ P_2 \rangle
$$

**Conclusion:** The system is in a **SAFE state**.

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Banker's Algorithm — Resource Request Handling

### Continuation: $P_1$ requests $(1, 0, 2)$

#### Step 1: Check $Request_1 \leq Need_1$
$$
(1,0,2) \leq (1,2,2) \quad \checkmark
$$

#### Step 2: Check $Request_1 \leq Available$
$$
(1,0,2) \leq (3,3,2) \quad \checkmark
$$

#### Step 3: Pretend Allocation (Modify State)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Available}_{\text{new}} &= (3,3,2) - (1,0,2) = (2,3,0) \\
C[1]_{\text{new}} &= (2,0,0) + (1,0,2) = (3,0,2) \\
N[1]_{\text{new}} &= (1,2,2) - (1,0,2) = (0,2,0)
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4: Run Safety Algorithm on New State

| Iteration | Work | Selected Process | Reason |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Init | (2,3,0) | — | — |
| 1 | (2,3,0) | $P_1$ | Need=(0,2,0) ≤ Work ✓ |
| 2 | (2+3, 3+0, 0+2) = (5,3,2) | $P_3$ | Need=(0,1,1) ≤ (5,3,2) ✓ |
| 3 | (5+2, 3+1, 2+1) = (7,4,3) | $P_0$ | Need=(7,4,3) ≤ (7,4,3) ✓ |
| 4 | (7+0, 4+1, 3+0) = (7,5,3) | $P_2$ | Need=(6,0,0) ≤ (7,5,3) ✓ |
| 5 | (7+3, 5+0, 3+2) = (10,5,5) | $P_4$ | Need=(4,3,1) ≤ (10,5,5) ✓ |

**Safe sequence:** $\langle P_1, P_3, P_0, P_2, P_4 \rangle$

**Conclusion:** The request can be **granted**. The system remains in a safe state.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — RAG Cycle Detection (Multiple Instances)

**Scenario:** A system has resources $R_1$ (2 instances), $R_2$ (2 instances), $R_3$ (2 instances), $R_4$ (3 instances). Four processes $P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4$ are present.

| Edge | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $P_1 \rightarrow R_1$ | $P_1$ requests an instance of $R_1$ |
| $P_2 \rightarrow R_3$ | $P_2$ requests an instance of $R_3$ |
| $R_1 \rightarrow P_2$ | An instance of $R_1$ is allocated to $P_2$ |
| $R_2 \rightarrow P_1$ | An instance of $R_2$ is allocated to $P_1$ |
| $R_2 \rightarrow P_2$ | An instance of $R_2$ is allocated to $P_2$ |
| $R_3 \rightarrow P_3$ | An instance of $R_3$ is allocated to $P_3$ |
| $P_3 \rightarrow R_4$ | $P_3$ requests an instance of $R_4$ |
| $P_4 \rightarrow R_4$ | $P_4$ requests an instance of $R_4$ |
| $R_4 \rightarrow P_1$ | An instance of $R_4$ is allocated to $P_1$ |

**Cycle found?** $P_1 \rightarrow R_1 \rightarrow P_2 \rightarrow R_3 \rightarrow P_3 \rightarrow R_4 \rightarrow P_1$ — **YES, a cycle exists.**

**Is it a deadlock?** Since $R_2$ and $R_4$ have **multiple instances** (R_2 has 2, R_4 has 3), the cycle does NOT necessarily mean deadlock. Let us verify:

- $P_4$ is waiting for $R_4$, but $R_4$ has a free instance. So $P_4$ can be satisfied and complete, releasing its held resources.
- After $P_4$ completes, $P_1$ (or $P_3$) can get $R_4$.

**Conclusion:** The cycle exists, but there is **no deadlock** (it's only a "potential" deadlock).

---

## 3.4 Sufficient Condition for Deadlock — Insufficient Resources Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Insufficient Resources):** If there are $k$ processes each requiring a **maximum of $m$ instances** of a single resource type, then the system is **deadlock-free** if the total number of instances $R$ satisfies:
> $$R \geq k(m - 1) + 1$$

**Example:** Suppose 4 processes each need at most 3 instances of a resource. Then the minimum number of instances to guarantee no deadlock is:
$$R \geq 4(3-1) + 1 = 4 \times 2 + 1 = 9 \text{ instances}$$

If the system has only 8 instances, the worst case is 3 processes each holding 2 and the 4th needing 3 — a circular wait could arise.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define deadlock. State and briefly explain the four necessary conditions for deadlock.**

**Model Answer:**

**Deadlock Definition:** A deadlock is a condition in which a set of processes are permanently blocked because each process in the set is waiting for an event that can only be triggered by another process in the same set. No process can proceed, and the system is stuck.

**Four Necessary Conditions (Coffman Conditions):**

1. **Mutual Exclusion:** At least one resource is held in a non-shareable mode; only one process at a time can use the resource.
2. **Hold and Wait:** A process holding at least one resource is waiting to acquire additional resources currently held by other processes.
3. **No Preemption:** Resources cannot be forcibly taken; they can only be released voluntarily by the holding process.
4. **Circular Wait:** A circular chain of processes exists where $P_0$ waits for $P_1$, $P_1$ waits for $P_2$, …, $P_k$ waits for $P_0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing and explaining the 4 conditions.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is a Resource Allocation Graph (RAG)? How is a deadlock detected from it?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Resource Allocation Graph (RAG)** is a directed graph used to represent the state of resource allocation in a system. It contains two types of nodes:
- **Process nodes** (circles): $P_1, P_2, \ldots$
- **Resource nodes** (squares) with **instance dots** inside.

**Two types of edges:**
- **Assignment edge** $P_i \rightarrow R_j$: $P_i$ holds an instance of $R_j$.
- **Request edge** $P_i \rightarrow R_j$: $P_i$ is waiting for $R_j$.

**Deadlock Detection Rules:**
- If the RAG has **no cycle** $\Rightarrow$ **no deadlock**.
- If the RAG has a cycle and **every resource type has only one instance** $\Rightarrow$ **deadlock is definite**.
- If the RAG has a cycle but **some resource type has multiple instances** $\Rightarrow$ **deadlock is possible but not certain**; further investigation is needed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** 1.5 marks for RAG definition and components, 1.5 marks for detection rules.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **Banker's Algorithm** for deadlock avoidance. Clearly define the data structures used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following snapshot of a system:

| Process | Allocation (A B C D) | Max (A B C D) | Available (A B C D) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| P0 | 0 0 1 2 | 0 0 1 2 | 1 6 2 2 |
| P1 | 2 0 0 0 | 2 7 5 0 | |
| P2 | 0 0 3 4 | 6 6 5 6 | |
| P3 | 1 0 3 2 | 4 3 5 6 | |
| P4 | 1 2 0 0 | 2 6 5 2 | |

**(i)** Compute the Need matrix.
**(ii)** Is the system in a safe state? If yes, give a safe sequence.
**(iii)** If P1 requests (0, 4, 2, 0), can the request be granted immediately? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a): Banker's Algorithm Explanation

The **Banker's Algorithm**, proposed by Edsger Dijkstra, is a deadlock avoidance algorithm that simulates the maximum possible resource requests of each process to test for a **safe state** before allocating resources.

**Data Structures Used:**

1. **Available (vector of size m):** $A[j]$ = number of available instances of resource type $R_j$.
2. **Max (n × m matrix):** $M[i][j]$ = maximum instances of $R_j$ that process $P_i$ may request.
3. **Allocation (n × m matrix):** $C[i][j]$ = instances of $R_j$ currently held by $P_i$.
4. **Need (n × m matrix):** $N[i][j] = M[i][j] - C[i][j]$ = remaining need of $P_i$.

**Two Sub-Algorithms:**

1. **Safety Algorithm:** Tests whether the system is in a safe state by trying to find a safe sequence.
2. **Resource Request Algorithm:** Handles a process's request by tentatively allocating and then running the Safety Algorithm.

**Step-by-step Safety Algorithm:**

- Initialize $\text{Work} = \text{Available}$, $\text{Finish}[i] = \text{false}$ for all $i$.
- Find an $i$ such that $\text{Finish}[i] = \text{false}$ AND $N_i \leq \text{Work}$.
- Set $\text{Work} = \text{Work} + C_i$, $\text{Finish}[i] = \text{true}$.
- If all $\text{Finish}[i] = \text{true}$, the system is **safe**.

### Part (b)(i): Compute the Need Matrix

Using $N[i][j] = M[i][j] - C[i][j]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N[P_0] &= (0,0,1,2) - (0,0,1,2) = (0,0,0,0) \\
N[P_1] &= (2,7,5,0) - (2,0,0,0) = (0,7,5,0) \\
N[P_2] &= (6,6,5,6) - (0,0,3,4) = (6,6,2,2) \\
N[P_3] &= (4,3,5,6) - (1,0,3,2) = (3,3,2,4) \\
N[P_4] &= (2,6,5,2) - (1,2,0,0) = (1,4,5,2)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
N = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 7 & 5 & 0 \\ 6 & 6 & 2 & 2 \\ 3 & 3 & 2 & 4 \\ 1 & 4 & 5 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Part (b)(ii): Safety Check

**Work = (1, 6, 2, 2)** initially.

| Iteration | Process | Need | Work | Need $\leq$ Work? | New Work = Work + Allocation |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | $P_0$ | (0,0,0,0) | (1,6,2,2) | ✅ | (1,6,2,2) + (0,0,1,2) = (1,6,3,4) |
| 2 | $P_3$ | (3,3,2,4) | (1,6,3,4) | ✅ | (1,6,3,4) + (1,0,3,2) = (2,6,6,6) |
| 3 | $P_4$ | (1,4,5,2) | (2,6,6,6) | ✅ | (2,6,6,6) + (1,2,0,0) = (3,8,6,6) |
| 4 | $P_1$ | (0,7,5,0) | (3,8,6,6) | ✅ | (3,8,6,6) + (2,0,0,0) = (5,8,6,6) |
| 5 | $P_2$ | (6,6,2,2) | (5,8,6,6) | ✅ | (5,8,6,6) + (0,0,3,4) = (5,8,9,10) |

**Safe Sequence: $\langle P_0, P_3, P_4, P_1, P_2 \rangle$**

The system is in a **SAFE state**.

### Part (b)(iii): Request Handling for P1 → (0, 4, 2, 0)

**Step 1:** Check $Request_1 \leq Need_1$:
$(0, 4, 2, 0) \leq (0, 7, 5, 0)$ — **TRUE** ✅

**Step 2:** Check $Request_1 \leq Available$:
$(0, 4, 2, 0) \leq (1, 6, 2, 2)$ — **TRUE** ✅

**Step 3:** Pretend Allocation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Available}_{\text{new}} &= (1,6,2,2) - (0,4,2,0) = (1,2,0,2) \\
C[P_1]_{\text{new}} &= (2,0,0,0) + (0,4,2,0) = (2,4,2,0) \\
N[P_1]_{\text{new}} &= (0,7,5,0) - (0,4,2,0) = (0,3,3,0)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Run Safety Algorithm on the new state.

| Iteration | Process | Need (new) | Work | Need $\leq$ Work? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | $P_0$ | (0,0,0,0) | (1,2,0,2) | ✅ |
| After P0 | — | — | (1,2,0,2) + (0,0,1,2) = (1,2,1,4) | — |
| 2 | $P_1$ | (0,3,3,0) | (1,2,1,4) | ✅ |
| After P1 | — | — | (1,2,1,4) + (2,4,2,0) = (3,6,3,4) | — |
| 3 | $P_2$ | (6,6,2,2) | (3,6,3,4) | ✅ |
| After P2 | — | — | (3,6,3,4) + (0,0,3,4) = (3,6,6,8) | — |
| 4 | $P_3$ | (3,3,2,4) | (3,6,6,8) | ✅ |
| After P3 | — | — | (3,6,6,8) + (1,0,3,2) = (4,6,9,10) | — |
| 5 | $P_4$ | (1,4,5,2) | (4,6,9,10) | ✅ |

**Safe sequence found: $\langle P_0, P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4 \rangle$**

**Conclusion:** The request **CAN be granted immediately** because the system remains in a safe state.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the different **methods for handling deadlocks** in detail, comparing deadlock prevention, avoidance, and detection-recovery. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given the following Resource Allocation Graph with multi-instance resources, determine whether a deadlock exists. Explain your reasoning with full analysis. **(7 Marks)**

**Setup:**
- 3 resource types: $R_1$ (2 instances), $R_2$ (3 instances), $R_3$ (2 instances)
- 3 processes: $P_1, P_2, P_3$
- Edges:
  - $P_1 \rightarrow R_1$ (P1 requests R1)
  - $P_2 \rightarrow R_3$ (P2 requests R3)
  - $R_1 \rightarrow P_2$ (R1 allocated to P2)
  - $R_2 \rightarrow P_1$ (R2 allocated to P1)
  - $R_2 \rightarrow P_3$ (R2 allocated to P3)
  - $R_3 \rightarrow P_3$ (R3 allocated to P3)
  - $P_3 \rightarrow R_1$ (P3 also requests R1)

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a): Methods for Handling Deadlocks

The four primary strategies for handling deadlocks are:

1. **Deadlock Prevention (Design-time):**
   - Ensures at least one of the four Coffman conditions can **never hold**, by imposing restrictions on process behavior.
   - **Mutual Exclusion:** Made false by making resources shareable where possible.
   - **Hold and Wait:** A process must request all resources before starting; or release all held resources before requesting new ones.
   - **No Preemption:** If a process cannot get a new resource, the OS preempts all its current resources.
   - **Circular Wait:** A total ordering of resource types is enforced; processes request resources in strictly increasing order.
   - **Drawbacks:** Reduced device utilization; possible starvation.

2. **Deadlock Avoidance (Runtime, with future knowledge):**
   - The OS needs **a priori** knowledge of the maximum resource requirements of each process.
   - Uses the **Banker's Algorithm** to ensure the system never enters an unsafe state.
   - **Drawbacks:** Difficult to know maximum requirements in advance; overhead of running safety check on every request.

3. **Deadlock Detection and Recovery (Runtime, periodic):**
   - The system allows deadlocks to occur but periodically runs a **detection algorithm** (e.g., Wait-For Graph cycle check).
   - Upon detection, recovery is performed by either:
     - **Process termination:** Abort one or more deadlocked processes.
     - **Resource preemption:** Selectively take resources from deadlocked processes and give them to others.
   - **Drawbacks:** Runtime overhead; potential loss of work; not suitable for hard real-time systems.

4. **Deadlock Ignorance (Ostrich Algorithm):**
   - Assume deadlocks never occur. Used in many general-purpose OS like UNIX and Windows for user-level processes.
   - Justification: Detection cost > frequency of occurrence.
   - **Drawback:** Not acceptable in safety-critical RTOS.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Prevention | Avoidance | Detection-Recovery |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Resource utilization | Low-Medium | Medium | High |
| Implementation cost | Medium | High | Medium |
| Runtime overhead | Low | Medium-High | Periodic |
| Suitable for RTOS? | Yes (PCP, PIP) | Sometimes | No |
| Pre-knowledge needed | No | Yes (max claims) | No |

### Part (b): RAG Cycle Analysis

**Resource State:**
- $R_1$: 2 instances total; 1 instance allocated to $P_2$, 1 free.
- $R_2$: 3 instances total; 1 each allocated to $P_1, P_3$; 1 free.
- $R_3$: 2 instances total; 1 instance allocated to $P_3$, 1 free.

**Identify cycles in the RAG:**

**Cycle Candidate 1:** $P_1 \rightarrow R_1 \rightarrow P_2 \rightarrow R_3 \rightarrow P_3 \rightarrow R_2 \rightarrow P_1$
- $P_1$ requests $R_1$, $R_1$ allocated to $P_2$, $P_2$ requests $R_3$, $R_3$ allocated to $P_3$, $P_3$ holds $R_2$, $R_2$ allocated to $P_1$? 
- Wait: $R_2$ is allocated to $P_1$, and $P_3$ holds an instance of $R_2$. So there are two allocation edges from $R_2$ — to $P_1$ AND to $P_3$.

**Cycle Found:** $P_1 \rightarrow R_1 \rightarrow P_2 \rightarrow R_3 \rightarrow P_3 \rightarrow R_1 \rightarrow P_1$? Let us re-trace carefully.

Trace: $P_1$ (request) $\to$ $R_1$ (allocation) $\to$ $P_2$ (request) $\to$ $R_3$ (allocation) $\to$ $P_3$ (request) $\to$ $R_1$. But $R_1$ has only 1 free instance? Let's see: $R_1$ has 2 total, 1 allocated to $P_2$, 1 free.

**Check if the cycle is a true deadlock:**

Apply the detection algorithm:
- $P_1$ is waiting for $R_1$ (which has 1 free instance available). $P_1$'s **other held resource** is $R_2$ (1 instance). If $P_1$ can get $R_1$, it can complete and release $R_1$ and $R_2$.
- But $P_1$ holds an instance of $R_2$. The other instance of $R_2$ is held by $P_3$, and the third is free.
- $P_3$ is waiting for $R_1$. Since $R_1$ has 1 free instance, $P_3$'s request can be granted immediately if it weren't already reserved for $P_1$.
- $P_1$ is also waiting for $R_1$, and $P_3$ is also waiting for $R_1$. Only one of them can get the free $R_1$.

**Detailed Check using Banker's Approach:**

| Process | Allocation | Max Need | Currently Holding | Waiting For | Status |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $P_1$ | 1 of $R_2$ | 1 of $R_2$ + 1 of $R_1$ | $R_2$ | $R_1$ | Wait for $R_1$ |
| $P_2$ | 1 of $R_1$ | 1 of $R_1$ + 1 of $R_3$ | $R_1$ | $R_3$ | Wait for $R_3$ |
| $P_3$ | 1 of $R_2$ + 1 of $R_3$ | $R_2$ + $R_3$ + $R_1$ | $R_2, R_3$ | $R_1$ | Wait for $R_1$ |

- $R_1$ has 1 free instance. Either $P_1$ or $P_3$ can take it.
- Suppose $P_3$ takes $R_1$. Then $P_3$ completes, releases $R_2$ and $R_3$ (and $R_1$). Now $R_1$ has 2 free, $R_2$ has 2 free, $R_3$ has 2 free.
- $P_1$ takes $R_1$, completes, releases $R_1, R_2$. Now $R_1, R_2$ are free.
- $P_2$ takes $R_3$, completes, releases $R_1, R_3$. All complete!

**Conclusion:** Although a cycle exists in the RAG, the system is **NOT in a deadlock state**. The cycle exists due to multiple instances of resources, and there is a valid execution order (P3 → P1 → P2) that allows all processes to complete.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't assume cycle = deadlock.** This is the single most common mistake. With **single-instance** resources, a cycle $\equiv$ deadlock. With **multi-instance** resources, a cycle only means **possible** deadlock.
> 2. **Always verify with the Banker's approach** (or by attempting to find a completion sequence) before declaring a deadlock in multi-instance RAGs.
> 3. **In part (a) Banker's problems**, students often forget to **update the Need matrix** after a pretend allocation in part (b)(iii). Marks are lost for not showing the new Need and Allocation values.
> 4. **Safe sequence matters:** A wrong safe sequence costs 2-3 marks. Always verify by running the safety algorithm to completion.
> 5. **Allocation arithmetic:** Many students make off-by-one errors in the Need matrix calculation. Double-check $N[i][j] = M[i][j] - C[i][j]$ entry-by-entry.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **Deadlock:** Permanent blocking of a set of processes; no process can proceed.
- **4 Coffman Conditions (all must hold):** Mutual Exclusion, Hold & Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait.
- **Breaking any ONE condition** prevents deadlock.
- **Resource Allocation Graph (RAG):** Circles = processes, Squares = resource types, Dots = instances.
- **RAG Cycle Interpretation:**
  - No cycle → No deadlock.
  - Cycle + single-instance → Deadlock definite.
  - Cycle + multi-instance → Possible deadlock; needs further analysis.
- **Banker's Algorithm Data Structures:** Available (vector), Max (matrix), Allocation (matrix), Need = Max − Allocation.
- **Safety Algorithm:** Iteratively find a process whose Need ≤ Work; if found, Work = Work + Allocation; repeat until all processes Finish[i] = true.
- **Safe State:** A state with at least one safe sequence. Safe $\not\equiv$ deadlock-free, but **Unsafe** means deadlock is **possible**.
- **Request Handling:** Validate $Req \leq Need$, $Req \leq Available$, then **pretend-allocate** and run **Safety Algorithm**. Grant only if safe.
- **Insufficient Resources Theorem:** $R \geq k(m-1) + 1$ ensures deadlock-free for $k$ processes each needing $m$ instances of a single resource.
- **Deadlock Ignorance (Ostrich Algorithm):** Used in general-purpose OS like UNIX/Windows for user processes; **not** for RTOS.
- **RTOS-Specific Protocols:** PIP (Priority Inheritance Protocol) and PCP (Priority Ceiling Protocol) are preferred over Banker's algorithm for real-time systems because Banker's is too conservative and runtime-expensive.
- **Deadlock vs. Starvation:** Deadlock = **all** processes in the set are blocked permanently. Starvation = a **single** process waits indefinitely due to unfair scheduling.
- **Real-Time Implications:** In hard RTOS, deadline misses due to deadlock can cause **catastrophic system failure**; hence prevention at design time is preferred.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for the 4 Coffman Conditions — "MHNC":** **M**utual **H**old-and-wait, **N**o preemption, **C**ircular wait.
> **Mnemonic for RAG cycle rule — "OS Multi":** **O**ne cycle, **S**ingle-instance → definite deadlock. **Multi**-instance → investigate further.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Priority Inversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT741 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Priority Inversion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Priority Inversion** is a scheduling anomaly in real-time and multitasking systems in which a higher-priority task is *indirectly* preempted by a lower-priority task, thereby *inverting* the relative priorities of the two tasks — the lower-priority task effectively executes before the higher-priority task, even though neither is directly related.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, **Priority Inversion** is defined as a situation in which the OS scheduler violates the *strict priority ordering* of tasks due to contention over **shared mutually exclusive resources** (protected by semaphores, mutexes, or critical sections).

**Formal Statement:**
Let three tasks exist with priorities $P_H > P_M > P_L$ (High, Medium, Low). Priority Inversion occurs when:
- $T_H$ (high) and $T_L$ (low) share a resource $R$.
- $T_L$ acquires $R$ first.
- $T_H$ becomes ready and attempts to lock $R$ → blocks.
- $T_M$ (medium, unrelated to $R$) preempts $T_L$.
- Result: $T_M$ runs *before* $T_H$ despite $P_M < P_H$ — **the priorities are inverted**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (PECPT741 Module 4):** The 2024 Scheme emphasizes three classical protocols to combat priority inversion: **(i) Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)**, **(ii) Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)**, and **(iii) Stack Resource Policy (SRP)**. Students are expected to compare these in the **ESE (End Semester Examination)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway Toll Booth

Imagine a **3-lane toll booth** on a national highway:

- **Lane H (High Priority):** Ambulance carrying a critical patient.
- **Lane M (Medium Priority):** A government VIP convoy.
- **Lane L (Low Priority):** A regular cargo truck.

Scenario:
1. The **truck (L)** enters the toll lane first but the payment machine jams — it is **stuck** at the booth.
2. The **ambulance (H)** arrives and is *blocked* behind the truck because the lane is occupied.
3. The **VIP convoy (M)** now arrives. Since it has a higher priority than the truck, it is allowed to pass through a *parallel* lane — **going ahead of the ambulance**.

🚨 **Result:** The ambulance (highest priority) is stuck behind a truck (lowest priority) while a medium-priority convoy overtakes it. This is **Priority Inversion** — the most critical entity is *indirectly* delayed by the least critical one.

## 1.3 Types of Priority Inversion

| Type | Description | Bounded? |
|------|-------------|----------|
| **Unbounded Priority Inversion** | Duration depends on unrelated medium-priority tasks; theoretically infinite | ❌ No |
| **Bounded Priority Inversion** | Capped using protocols (PIP, PCP, SRP); maximum delay is calculable | ✅ Yes |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:** The famous **Mars Pathfinder (1997)** spacecraft anomaly — which caused system resets on the Martian surface — was traced to **unbounded priority inversion** in its VxWorks RTOS. This is a frequently-asked 14-mark question.

## 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

The following metrics (commonly tested in KTU numericals) are used to characterize priority inversion:

- **$T_{PI}$** — Duration of priority inversion (seconds or task-time units)
- **$B_i$** — Worst-case blocking time of task $T_i$
- **$C_i$** — Worst-case computation time of task $T_i$
- **$P_i$** — Period of task $T_i$
- **$R_i$** — Worst-case response time of task $T_i$
- **$H_i$** — Maximum duration a task can hold any resource
- **$U$** — CPU utilization factor: $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{P_i}$

These constants must be memorized for the **KTU 2024 ESE**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Unbounded Priority Inversion — Operational Analysis

**Preconditions (must all hold for inversion to occur):**
1. At least **two tasks** share a mutually exclusive resource.
2. **Preemptive priority scheduling** is used.
3. **No priority inheritance** mechanism is in place.
4. A **third intermediate-priority task** exists that does not require the shared resource.

**Step-by-Step Operational Logic:**

- **Step 1 — Acquisition:** $T_L$ (low priority) starts executing and acquires a lock on resource $R$ at time $t_0$.
- **Step 2 — Preemption Attempt:** At time $t_1 > t_0$, $T_H$ (high priority) is released by an event and preempts $T_L$.
- **Step 3 — Blocking:** $T_H$ attempts to lock $R$, finds it held by $T_L$, and is **forced to block** (sleep state). $T_L$ resumes.
- **Step 4 — Medium Interference:** At time $t_2 > t_1$, $T_M$ (medium priority, unrelated to $R$) is released. The scheduler preempts $T_L$ in favor of $T_M$.
- **Step 5 — Critical Delay:** $T_M$ runs for an arbitrary duration. $T_H$ remains blocked. $T_L$ cannot release $R$ until $T_M$ finishes.
- **Step 6 — Resolution:** Once $T_M$ completes, $T_L$ resumes, releases $R$ (at $t_3$), and *only then* can $T_H$ acquire $R$ and run.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why':** $T_L$ cannot release $R$ because it is preempted by $T_M$. The medium-priority task acts as an *amplifier* of the inversion. **The 'How':** The blocking chain $T_H \rightarrow T_L$ is exploited by $T_M$ through the preemption mechanism, causing the inversion window to grow unboundedly with $T_M$'s execution time.

## 2.2 Bounded Priority Inversion Protocols

### 2.2.1 Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)

**Definition:** When a high-priority task $T_H$ blocks on a resource held by a lower-priority task $T_L$, the scheduler **temporarily elevates** $T_L$'s priority to match $T_H$'s priority.

**Rules of PIP (Sha, Rajkumar, Lehoczky — 1990):**

1. **Rule 1 (Scheduling):** When a task $T_i$ is blocked on a resource held by $T_k$, $T_k$'s *effective* priority $\pi_k^{eff}$ is set to $\max(\pi_k^{eff}, \pi_i)$.
2. **Rule 2 (Transitive Inheritance):** If $T_k$ is itself blocked on another resource held by $T_j$, then $T_j$'s effective priority is also boosted to $\pi_i$ (transitive chain).
3. **Rule 3 (Restoration):** When $T_k$ releases the resource, its effective priority reverts to the highest priority among all tasks *still* waiting for resources held by $T_k$.

**Transitive Inheritance Chain Diagram (logic):**
$$T_H \xrightarrow{\text{blocked on } R_1} T_L \xrightarrow{\text{blocked on } R_2} T_X$$
$$\Rightarrow \pi_X^{eff} = \pi_H, \quad \pi_L^{eff} = \pi_H$$

### 2.2.2 Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)

**Definition:** Each resource/mutex is assigned a **priority ceiling** equal to the highest priority of any task that may lock it. A task may enter a critical section **only if** its priority is *strictly higher* than the ceiling of any resource currently locked by any *other* task.

**Key Property — Deadlock Prevention:** Under PCP, a task can be blocked by **at most one** lower-priority task, eliminating deadlocks and chained blocking.

### 2.2.3 Stack Resource Policy (SRP)

**Definition:** An extension of PCP where each task is pre-assigned a **preemption level** $\lambda_i$. A task $T_i$ is permitted to start only if its preemption level is higher than the system ceiling $\Pi_s$ of all currently locked resources.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Bound** | **Units** | **Used In** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maximum inversion duration (no protocol) | Unbounded — depends on $T_M$ execution | time units | Unbounded PI |
| Maximum blocking under PIP | $B_i \leq \max(\text{duration any lower-priority task holds resource } T_i \text{ needs})$ | time units | PIP analysis |
| Maximum blocking under PCP | $B_i \leq \max(H_k)$ for any $k$ s.t. $P_k < P_i$ and $T_k$ shares resource with $T_i$ | time units | PCP analysis |
| Worst-case response time | $R_i = C_i + B_i + I_i$ | time units | All RTOS |
| $I_i$ (interference from higher-priority tasks) | $I_i = \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{P_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$ | time units | Fixed-priority sched. |
| Response time recurrence | $R_i^{(n+1)} = C_i + B_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(n)}}{P_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$ | time units | Exact analysis |
| Scheduability test (sufficient) | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{P_i} + \frac{B_k}{P_k} \leq 1$ | dimensionless | RM with blocking |
| Scheduability test (exact) | $R_i \leq D_i$ for all $i$ | time units | All RTOS |
| Utilization bound (Liu & Layland) | $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | dimensionless | Rate Monotonic |
| Effective priority (PIP) | $\pi_k^{eff} = \max(\pi_k, \max_{i \text{ blocked on } k} \pi_i)$ | priority | PIP |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value or ceiling symbols in equations are rendered with `\lceil \rceil` and `$\vert$` to prevent breaking the markdown table. **KTU board examiners** expect you to reproduce the **response time recurrence** in full when solving numericals.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Priority inversion mitigation is deployed in:

- **Aerospace:** NASA Mars Pathfinder, SpaceX Crew Dragon flight software.
- **Automotive:** AUTOSAR OS, OSEK/VDX for engine control units (ECUs).
- **Medical:** Pacemakers, infusion pumps (FDA-mandated analysis).
- **Industrial:** PLCs running VxWorks, FreeRTOS, ThreadX, QNX Neutrino.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Standard:** **MISRA-C** and **IEC 61508 (SIL-3/4)** require explicit priority inversion analysis for safety-critical real-time embedded systems. This is a *high-yield* KTU discussion point.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Blocking Time Under Priority Inheritance Protocol

**Problem Setup:**
Consider three tasks $T_1, T_2, T_3$ with priorities $P_1 > P_2 > P_3$ (i.e., $T_1$ is highest). Suppose $T_1$ and $T_3$ share a resource $R$, and $T_3$ holds $R$ for a maximum duration of $H_3$ time units.

**Goal:** Derive the maximum blocking time $B_1$ experienced by $T_1$.

**Step 1 — Identify the worst-case blocking chain:**

The blocking occurs only when:
- $T_3$ has acquired $R$, and
- $T_1$ attempts to lock $R$.

In this configuration, no other task $T_2$ can interfere with $T_3$ during its critical section **if PIP is in effect**, because $T_3$'s effective priority is boosted to $P_1$.

**Step 2 — Express the bound:**

Under PIP, the blocking time is bounded by the longest critical section of any *lower-priority* task that uses a resource contended with $T_1$:

$$
B_1 = \max_{k \in lp(1)} H_k
$$

where $lp(1)$ denotes the set of tasks with priority lower than $P_1$ that share at least one resource with $T_1$.

**Step 3 — Substituting values for our scenario:**

Since only $T_3$ is a lower-priority task sharing $R$ with $T_1$:

$$
B_1 = H_3
$$

**Step 4 — Verification via contradiction:**

If $T_2$ (medium) attempted to preempt $T_3$ during its critical section, PIP would have already boosted $T_3$'s priority to $P_1$, making $P_2 < \pi_3^{eff} = P_1$ → preemption is denied. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation: Response Time Calculation with Blocking

**Given:** A task set $\{T_1, T_2, T_3\}$ with the following parameters.

| Task | Period $P_i$ | Computation $C_i$ | Deadline $D_i$ | Blocks-on |
|------|--------------|-------------------|----------------|-----------|
| $T_1$ (high) | 7 | 3 | 7 | $R_1$ shared with $T_3$ |
| $T_2$ (medium) | 12 | 3 | 12 | None |
| $T_3$ (low) | 20 | 4 | 20 | $R_1$ shared with $T_1$ |

Assume $T_3$ holds $R_1$ for at most $H_3 = 2$ time units.

**Step 1 — Compute blocking time of $T_1$:**

$$
B_1 = H_3 = 2
$$

**Step 2 — Set up the response time recurrence for $T_1$:**

$$
R_1^{(n+1)} = C_1 + B_1 + \sum_{j \in hp(1)} \left\lceil \frac{R_1^{(n)}}{P_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j
$$

Since $T_1$ is the highest priority, $hp(1) = \emptyset$:

$$
R_1^{(n+1)} = C_1 + B_1 = 3 + 2 = 5
$$

**Step 3 — Converge the recurrence:**

$$
R_1^{(0)} = C_1 = 3
$$
$$
R_1^{(1)} = 3 + 2 = 5
$$
$$
R_1^{(2)} = 3 + 2 = 5 \quad \text{(converged)}
$$

**Step 4 — Check schedulability:**

$$
R_1 = 5 \leq D_1 = 7 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{SCHEDULABLE} \quad \checkmark
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Protocols

| **Property** | **No Protocol** | **PIP** | **PCP** | **SRP** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bounds priority inversion? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Prevents deadlock? | ❌ No | ❌ No (transitive) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Prevents chained blocking? | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (max 1) | ✅ Yes |
| Runtime overhead | Lowest | Moderate | Moderate-High | High |
| Implementation complexity | Trivial | Low | Medium | High |
| Blocking bound $B_i$ | Unbounded | $\sum_{k=1}^{m} usage(k,i) \cdot H_k$ | $\max_{k: P_k < P_i} H_k$ | $\max_{k: \lambda_k < \lambda_i} H_k$ |
| Resource pre-allocation? | No | No | No | ✅ Yes |
| Stack sharing? | No | No | No | ✅ Yes |
| KTU 2024 Marks Weight | 1–2 | 4–5 | 4–5 | 2–3 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Usage Function Definition:** $usage(k, i) = 1$ if task $T_k$ uses at least one resource that $T_i$ also uses, and 0 otherwise. This is the standard PIP blocking formula in KTU textbooks.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — KTU Style

**Question:** Three tasks share two resources $R_1, R_2$. Compute the worst-case response time of $T_2$.

| Task | $C_i$ | $P_i$ | $D_i$ | Resources used |
|------|-------|-------|-------|----------------|
| $T_1$ | 1 | 4 | 4 | $R_1$ |
| $T_2$ | 2 | 6 | 6 | $R_1, R_2$ |
| $T_3$ | 3 | 10 | 10 | $R_2$ |

Assume $H_1 = 1$ (max hold of $R_1$ by $T_1$), $H_3 = 2$ (max hold of $R_2$ by $T_3$).

**Step 1 — Compute $B_2$ (blocking time of $T_2$):**

$T_2$ can be blocked by lower-priority tasks $T_3$ via $R_2$:
$$
B_2 = H_3 = 2
$$

**Step 2 — Initialize response time iteration:**

$$
R_2^{(0)} = C_2 = 2
$$

**Step 3 — First iteration (with $T_1$ interference):**

$$
R_2^{(1)} = C_2 + B_2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2^{(0)}}{P_1} \right\rceil \cdot C_1
$$
$$
R_2^{(1)} = 2 + 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 4 + 1 = 5
$$

**Step 4 — Second iteration:**

$$
R_2^{(2)} = 2 + 2 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 4 + 2 = 6
$$

**Step 5 — Third iteration:**

$$
R_2^{(3)} = 2 + 2 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 4 + 2 = 6
$$

Converged. **Final:** $R_2 = 6 \leq D_2 = 6$ → **Scheduable** (just barely).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Unbounded Priority Inversion — Timeline Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant T_L as T_L (Low Priority)
    participant T_M as T_M (Medium Priority)
    participant T_H as T_H (High Priority)
    participant R as Resource R

    Note over T_L,R: t = 0
    T_L->>R: Lock(R) — ACQUIRED
    Note over T_L: Enters critical section

    Note over T_H,R: t = t1 (T_H arrives)
    T_H->>R: Try Lock(R) — BLOCKED
    T_H-->>T_L: Blocks (sleep state)
    Note over T_L: Resumes execution

    Note over T_M,R: t = t2 (T_M arrives)
    T_M->>T_L: Preempts T_L (higher priority)
    Note over T_L: Suspended — CANNOT release R

    Note over T_H: t = t2 to t3
    Note over T_H: CRITICAL: T_H waits while T_M runs
    T_M->>T_M: Executes for arbitrary duration

    Note over T_L,R: t = t3 (T_M done)
    T_L->>R: Releases R
    T_H->>R: Lock(R) — ACQUIRED
    T_H->>T_H: Finally executes
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Priority Inheritance Protocol

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    reqArrival["Task Arrival Event"] --> schedCheck{Scheduler Priority Check}
    schedCheck -->|Higher priority ready| preempt["Preempt Current Task"]
    schedCheck -->|Lower or same| continueRun["Continue Current Task"]
    preempt --> readyQueue["Insert into Ready Queue"]

    readyQueue --> dispCheck{Dispatcher: Highest Priority Task}
    dispCheck --> taskExec["Execute Task"]
    taskExec --> resReq{Resource Requested?}

    resReq -->|No| taskRun["Run to Completion / Preemption Point"]
    taskRun --> releaseCPU["Release CPU"]

    resReq -->|Yes| resCheck{Resource Available?}
    resCheck -->|Yes| acquireRes["Lock Resource, Enter Critical Section"]
    resCheck -->|No| block["Block Task on Resource"]

    block --> pipCheck{Any Task Holds Resource?}
    pipCheck -->|Yes| boostPrio["PIP: Boost Holder's Effective Priority to Requester's Priority"]
    pipCheck -->|No| waitQueue["Place in Resource Wait Queue"]

    boostPrio --> prioUpdate["Update Effective Priority in Scheduler"]
    prioUpdate --> contHolder["Holder Continues with Boosted Priority"]
    contHolder --> releaseRes["Holder Releases Resource"]
    releaseRes --> prioRestore["Restore Original Priority (Rule 3)"]
    prioRestore --> wakeWaiter["Wake Up Highest-Priority Waiter"]
    wakeWaiter --> dispCheck
```

## 4.3 Protocol Decision Tree — When to Use What

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Designing RTOS Application"] --> q1{Need deadlock prevention?}
    q1 -->|No, simple system| pip["Use Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)"]
    q1 -->|Yes, safety-critical| q2{Need stack sharing?}
    q2 -->|No| pcp["Use Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)"]
    q2 -->|Yes, memory-constrained| srp["Use Stack Resource Policy (SRP)"]

    pip --> pipProp["Pros: Easy to implement, bounds inversion"]
    pip --> pipCon["Cons: Transitive blocking, no deadlock prevention"]
    pcp --> pcpProp["Pros: Prevents deadlocks, single blocking"]
    pcp --> pcpCon["Cons: Runtime ceiling computation needed"]
    srp --> srpProp["Pros: Stack sharing, multiple resources"]
    srp --> srpCon["Cons: Complex preemption level assignment"]
```

## 4.4 State Transition Diagram of a Blocked Task

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Ready
    Ready --> Running: Dispatcher selects
    Running --> Ready: Preempted by higher priority
    Running --> Blocked: Resource lock denied
    Blocked --> Ready: Resource released + wait queue head
    Running --> Suspended: System call (e.g., delay)
    Suspended --> Ready: Event/timeout fires
    Blocked --> Suspended: Abort / timeout
    note right of Blocked: Under PIP: resource holder is priority-boosted
    note left of Running: Effective priority may exceed nominal
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The above state diagram aligns with the **OSEK/VDX** and **AUTOSAR OS** specification (Basic Task States). Students should be able to draw this for **2-mark definition questions** in the ESE.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define priority inversion. With a suitable example, explain how it can lead to unbounded delay in real-time systems.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Priority Inversion is a scheduling anomaly in which a higher-priority task is indirectly preempted by a lower-priority task due to shared mutually exclusive resources, effectively reversing the intended priority order.

**Example:** Let $T_H$, $T_M$, $T_L$ have priorities $P_H > P_M > P_L$, all on a preemptive scheduler. $T_H$ and $T_L$ share resource $R$.

**Unbounded Delay Mechanism:**
- $T_L$ acquires $R$.
- $T_H$ arrives, blocks on $R$.
- $T_M$ (unrelated to $R$) preempts $T_L$.
- $T_M$ runs for an *arbitrary* duration before $T_L$ can resume, release $R$, and unblock $T_H$.

Since the delay of $T_H$ depends on $T_M$'s execution time (which is unrelated to $T_H$), the inversion window is **unbounded** — theoretically infinite in pathological cases. The classic real-world instance is the **1997 Mars Pathfinder** system resets caused by this exact anomaly.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write *only* the definition and skip the *mechanism* of unboundedness. Examiners allocate **1 mark for the definition** and **2 marks for the example + unbounded explanation**. Both are mandatory.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**List and briefly explain the THREE protocols used to overcome priority inversion in real-time systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Protocol | Key Mechanism | Blocking Bound |
|---|----------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** | The task holding a resource inherits the priority of the *highest-priority task* blocked on that resource. | $B_i \leq \sum usage(k,i) \cdot H_k$ |
| 2 | **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)** | Each resource is assigned a *ceiling priority* (highest priority of any task that may lock it); a task may enter a critical section only if its priority exceeds the system ceiling. | $B_i \leq \max_{k: P_k < P_i} H_k$ (single blocking) |
| 3 | **Stack Resource Policy (SRP)** | Extends PCP using *preemption levels*; enables stack sharing and pre-allocation of resources. | $B_i \leq \max_{k: \lambda_k \leq \lambda_i} H_k$ |

All three protocols **bound** the priority inversion duration, ensuring predictability required in hard real-time systems.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Confusing **priority** with **preemption level**. In SRP, preemption level $\lambda$ and nominal priority $\pi$ are *separate* concepts. Examiners will deduct **1 mark** for this confusion.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — July 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** in detail. Discuss its advantages and limitations, with a timeline diagram showing how it bounds priority inversion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider three periodic tasks with the following parameters:

| Task | $C_i$ | $P_i$ | $D_i$ | Resource |
|------|-------|-------|-------|----------|
| $T_1$ | 2 | 5 | 5 | $R$ (shared with $T_3$) |
| $T_2$ | 3 | 8 | 8 | — |
| $T_3$ | 4 | 15 | 15 | $R$ (shared with $T_1$) |

Assume $H_3 = 1$ (max hold time of $R$ by $T_3$). Compute the worst-case response time of $T_1$ using the PIP blocking bound. Verify schedulability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Definition:** The Priority Inheritance Protocol (Sha, Rajkumar, Lehoczky — 1990) is a resource synchronization protocol for fixed-priority preemptive systems. When a high-priority task $T_H$ blocks on a resource held by a lower-priority task $T_L$, $T_L$ temporarily inherits $T_H$'s priority.

**Three Rules of PIP:**

1. **Scheduling Rule:** If task $T_k$ holds a resource on which $T_i$ (with $P_i > P_k$) is blocked, then the effective priority $\pi_k^{eff}$ of $T_k$ is raised to $P_i$.
2. **Transitive Rule:** If $T_k$ is itself blocked on another resource held by $T_j$, then $\pi_j^{eff} = \max(\pi_j^{eff}, \pi_i)$ — applied recursively through the entire blocking chain.
3. **Restoration Rule:** When $T_k$ releases a resource, its effective priority reverts to the maximum of the nominal priority $\pi_k$ and the highest priority among tasks still waiting for any resource $T_k$ continues to hold.

**Timeline Diagram (PIP Active):**
```
t=0     T_L acquires R, enters CS
t=1     T_H arrives, blocks on R
        ↓ PIP activates: π_L^eff = π_H
        ↓ T_M CANNOT preempt T_L (now equal to highest priority)
t=2     T_L exits CS, releases R
        ↓ π_L^eff reverts to π_L
t=2     T_H acquires R, executes
t=4     T_H completes
```

**Advantages:**
- **Bounds** priority inversion to $B_i = \sum usage(k,i) \cdot H_k$.
- Simple to implement — only requires effective-priority management in the scheduler.
- No changes to task code (transparent synchronization).

**Limitations:**
- Does **not** prevent **deadlocks** (cyclic resource dependencies still possible).
- Allows **chained blocking** (transitive inheritance can create long chains).
- Susceptible to **priority inversion on release** (a task boosting its priority may cause further blocking).
- Does not support stack sharing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Examiners specifically check whether students draw the **timeline** showing the *absence* of $T_M$'s preemption of $T_L$ during the boosted window. **3 marks** are reserved for the diagram alone.

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Identify blocking sources for $T_1$:**

$T_1$ can be blocked only by lower-priority tasks sharing resource $R$. Only $T_3$ qualifies:
$$
B_1 = usage(3,1) \cdot H_3 = 1 \cdot 1 = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Set up response time recurrence for $T_1$:**

$T_1$ is the highest-priority task, so $hp(1) = \emptyset$:
$$
R_1^{(n+1)} = C_1 + B_1 = 2 + 1 = 3
$$

**Step 3 — Convergence:**

$$
R_1^{(0)} = C_1 = 2
$$
$$
R_1^{(1)} = 2 + 1 = 3
$$
$$
R_1^{(2)} = 2 + 1 = 3 \quad \text{(converged)}
$$

**Step 4 — Schedulability check:**

$$
R_1 = 3 \leq D_1 = 5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{SCHEDULABLE} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** $T_1$ meets its deadline even under the worst-case PIP blocking. The system is schedulable for $T_1$ (full system schedulability would also require similar checks for $T_2$ and $T_3$).

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** and the **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)**. Show, with a timeline, how PCP prevents chained blocking. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A system has three tasks sharing a single resource $R$. Compute the worst-case blocking time of the highest-priority task under **(i)** no protocol, **(ii)** PIP, and **(iii)** PCP. Given: $T_1$ (priority 1, highest), $T_2$ (priority 2), $T_3$ (priority 3, lowest); $H_2 = 2$, $H_3 = 3$. Both $T_2$ and $T_3$ access $R$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Comparison Table:**

| **Aspect** | **PIP** | **PCP** |
|---|---|---|
| Resource ceiling assigned? | No | Yes — equals highest priority of any task that may lock it |
| Blocking per task | Multiple (chained) possible | **At most one** (single critical section) |
| Deadlock prevention | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Implementation overhead | Low (priority boost on block) | Medium (runtime ceiling check on entry) |
| Blocking bound | $\sum usage(k,i) \cdot H_k$ | $\max_{k: P_k < P_i} H_k$ |
| Pre-allocation needed? | No | No |
| Run-time check | None on entry | Mandatory ceiling check |

**Timeline (PCP preventing chained blocking):**
- Resource $R$ has ceiling $\Pi_R = \pi_1$ (highest priority).
- $T_2$ attempts to enter CS of $R$ at $t=0$. No other resource is locked → system ceiling $\Pi_s < \pi_2$ → **admitted**.
- $T_1$ arrives at $t=1$, preempts $T_2$, attempts to lock $R$ → finds it held → **blocks**.
- $T_3$ arrives at $t=2$. Under PIP, $T_3$ would preempt $T_2$ (now boosted) only if $P_2 < P_3$... but in our setup $P_3$ is lowest. **Critically**, in PCP, when $T_2$ was admitted at $t=0$, the *system ceiling* was updated. If $T_1$ is *currently* running and the ceiling check is enforced, $T_2$ would not be preemptible.

**Key Insight of PCP:** Since $T_2$ can only be blocked by $T_3$ if $T_3$ is *already* in a critical section when $T_2$ requests it (and the ceiling check would have *prevented* $T_2$ from starting in that case), **chained blocking is structurally impossible**.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**(i) No Protocol — Unbounded:**

Under no protocol, the inversion duration depends on the medium-priority task $T_2$. If $T_3$ holds $R$ and $T_1$ blocks, $T_2$ can preempt $T_3$ for an *arbitrary* duration.

$$
B_1^{\text{NoProto}} = \text{UNBOUNDED (theoretically } \infty \text{)}
$$

For a *practical worst case* (ignoring medium-task interference):
$$
B_1^{\text{NoProto, practical}} = H_3 = 3
$$

**(ii) Under PIP:**

$PIP$ bounds blocking by the sum of critical-section durations of lower-priority tasks that share $R$ with $T_1$. Both $T_2$ and $T_3$ share $R$ with $T_1$:

$$
B_1^{PIP} = usage(2,1) \cdot H_2 + usage(3,1) \cdot H_3 = (1)(2) + (1)(3) = 5
$$

**(iii) Under PCP:**

$PCP$ bounds blocking by the *single* longest critical section of any lower-priority task sharing $R$:

$$
B_1^{PCP} = \max_{k: P_k < P_1} H_k = \max(H_2, H_3) = \max(2, 3) = 3
$$

**Final Comparison:**

$$
B_1^{NoProto} \geq B_1^{PCP} = 3 \leq B_1^{PIP} = 5
$$

**Conclusion:** $PCP$ provides the **tightest** worst-case bound, confirming it is the most predictable protocol for hard real-time systems.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Valuation Insight:** For the no-protocol case, students often answer $B_1 = 3$ (i.e., $H_3$). Examiners expect the **unbounded** answer with explicit mention of $T_2$'s interference. **2 marks** are reserved for the correct identification of *unboundedness*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Priority Inversion (KTU 2024 Module 4)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Higher-priority task indirectly delayed by lower-priority task via shared resource.
- ✅ **Three conditions required:** (1) shared resource, (2) preemption, (3) medium-priority task.
- ✅ **Two types:** *Unbounded* (no protocol) vs. *Bounded* (PIP, PCP, SRP).
- ✅ **PIP** = priority *boost on block*. Bounded but allows chained blocking. **No deadlock prevention**.
- ✅ **PCP** = static *ceiling per resource* + *admission check on entry*. Bounded, **single blocking**, **deadlock-free**.
- ✅ **SRP** = uses *preemption levels* (separate from priorities). Enables **stack sharing** and resource *pre-allocation*.
- ✅ **Classic case study:** **Mars Pathfinder (1997)** — fixed by enabling priority inheritance in VxWorks.
- ✅ **Key formula — PIP blocking:**
$$B_i = \sum_{k \in lp(i)} usage(k,i) \cdot H_k$$
- ✅ **Key formula — PCP blocking:**
$$B_i = \max_{k: P_k < P_i} H_k$$
- ✅ **Response time recurrence:**
$$R_i = C_i + B_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{P_j} \right\rceil C_j$$
- ✅ **Scheduability test:** $R_i \leq D_i$ for every task $i$.
- ✅ **Industrial standards:** MISRA-C, IEC 61508 (SIL), AUTOSAR OS, OSEK/VDX — all mandate PI analysis.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for protocols — "I Can Stack":** **I**nheritance, **C**eiling, **S**tack Resource → in increasing order of strictness.
- ✅ **Watch out:** Priority ≠ Preemption Level (especially in SRP).
- ✅ **Watch out:** PIP does *not* prevent deadlocks — only PCP and SRP do.
- ✅ **Watch out:** Under no protocol, the worst-case blocking is **unbounded**, not equal to $H_{\text{lowest}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic are typically worth **14 marks**, distributed as **(a) 7 marks for protocol explanation with diagram** and **(b) 7 marks for numerical response-time computation**. Always draw a **timeline diagram** in part (a) — examiners explicitly reward it with **2–3 marks**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
