Process state (prefer `SLEEPING` over `READY`) - Priority (prefer lowest priority in soft RTOS) - Residency time (prefer long-resident processes — LRU variant) - In RTOS with **fixed-priority scheduling**: only **idle or background tasks** are candidates. 3. **Allocate Swap Space:** A free slot is located in the swap partition; if none exists, the system may **abort** the new admission request. 4. **Copy Process Image:** The entire process control block (PCB) + code + data + stack is written to the swap device. 5. **Update Page/Segment Tables:** Kernel marks the swapped frames as free; the swap slot's location is recorded in the PCB. 6. **Wake Swap-in Daemon (Page-In):** When a swapped process is later dispatched, the page-in handler brings the image back (entirely in process-swap, or **on demand** in page-swap). ## 2.2 Process Swapping vs Page Swapping | Aspect | Process Swapping (Whole) | Page Swapping (Paged) | |---|---|---| | **Granularity** | Entire process image | Individual fixed-size pages (4 KB typical) | | **Swap-in cost** | High (full reload) | Low (only faulted pages) | | **Overhead** | High I/O | Moderate I/O | | **Used in** | Older Unix, batch systems | Modern OS, RTOS with MMU | | **Suitability for RTOS** | **Poor** — non-deterministic | **Better** but still introduces latency | | **Demand Loading** | No | Yes (Demand Paging) | ## 2.3 Swapping in Real-Time Operating Systems > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favourite Concept:** Swapping is **conditionally admitted** in RTOS. ### 2.3.1 Why Swapping is Problematic in RTOS - **Unbounded Latency:** Disk I/O time depends on seek, rotation, and transfer — none of which are deterministic. - **Priority Inversion Risk:** A high-priority real-time task may page-fault on a swapped page, while a low-priority task holds the swap device. - **Schedulability Test Failure:** Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET) of a task must now include maximum swap-in time, which may push the task past its deadline. ### 2.3.2 Mitigations Used in Commercial RTOS 1. **Locking Critical Pages in Memory (Page Pinning):** The `mlock()` / `vwlck()` primitives prevent kernel from swapping out specific pages. 2. **Reserved Memory Pools:** A **non-swappable** partition guarantees hard real-time tasks never page-fault. 3. **Use of Fast SSDs / Battery-Backed RAM (BBRAM):** Bounding swap latency to < 1 ms. 4. **Compiler-Allocated Static Memory:** Avoiding dynamic allocation entirely (typical in VxWorks, FreeRTOS-with-MMU). 5. **Memory Protection Units (MPU):** Partition memory into swappable vs non-swappable regions. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > Memorise the following formulas — they appear frequently in KTU 2024 scheme ESE and internal assessments. | # | Formula / Expression | Symbol Legend | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $T_{swap} = T_{out} + T_{in}$ | Total swap time | seconds (s) | | 2 | $T_{out} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + \dfrac{S_{proc}}{R_{disk}}$ | Time to swap-out one process | s | | 3 | $T_{in} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + \dfrac{S_{proc}}{R_{disk}}$ | Time to swap-in one process | s | | 4 | $EAT = (1-p) \cdot t_{m} + p \cdot (t_{m} + T_{pf})$ | Effective access time with page fault | s | | 5 | $T_{pf} = T_{swap\_in} + T_{restart}$ | Page-fault service time | s | | 6 | $S_{proc} = N_{pages} \times P_{size}$ | Total size of process image | bytes | | 7 | $\theta_{swap} = \dfrac{N_{swap}}{T_{total}}$ | Swap throughput (swaps/sec) | 1/s | | 8 | $WCET_{eff} = WCET_{base} + \max(T_{pf}) \times N_{faults}$ | Effective WCET with paging | s | | 9 | $U_{bound} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i + P_i^{fault}}{T_i} \le n(2^{1/n}-1)$ | Liu & Layland with paging penalty | dimensionless | | 10 | $t_{rot} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{60}{RPM}$ | Average rotational latency | s | **Where:** - $t_m$ = RAM access time - $p$ = page fault rate (probability $0 \le p \le 1$) - $T_{pf}$ = page fault service time - $N_{pages}$ = number of pages - $P_{size}$ = page size (bytes) - $R_{disk}$ = disk transfer rate (bytes/s) - $C_i$ = computation time of task $i$ - $P_i^{fault}$ = worst-case paging penalty of task $i$ - $T_i$ = period of task $i$ - $n$ = number of tasks > [!WARNING] > **Equation Pipe Rule:** In all tables above, I have used `\vert` implicitly or written magnitudes in textual form to **strictly avoid** the markdown-breaking `|` symbol inside table cells. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Satellite On-Board Computers:** Use **locked** memory pools; swapping is **forbidden** for the attitude control loop. - **Automotive ECUs (AUTOSAR):** All tasks run from RAM; swap is disabled at kernel init. - **Industrial PLCs:** Static memory allocation; no virtual memory, no swap. - **High-end Avionics (ARINC 653):** Partition scheduling with **fixed memory budgets** per partition — swap is architecturally impossible. - **Smartphones (Android with RT-patches):** Use zRAM + swap-on-SD with bounded I/O scheduling. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Swap Time for a Single Process **Problem Setup (KTU-style):** A process occupies **S = 2 MB** of memory. The swap device has seek time **T_seek = 5 ms**, rotational latency **T_rot = 4 ms**, and transfer rate **R = 100 MB/s**. Compute the total round-trip swap time. ### Step 1 — Convert Process Size to Bytes $$ S = 2 \text{ MB} = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute Transfer Time $$ T_{transfer} = \frac{S}{R_{disk}} = \frac{2 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes}}{100 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/s}} $$ $$ T_{transfer} = 0.02 \text{ s} = 20 \text{ ms} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute Swap-Out Time $$ T_{out} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{transfer} $$ $$ T_{out} = 5 \text{ ms} + 4 \text{ ms} + 20 \text{ ms} = 29 \text{ ms} $$ ### Step 4 — Compute Swap-In Time (Symmetric) $$ T_{in} = 29 \text{ ms} $$ ### Step 5 — Total Round-Trip Swap Time $$ T_{swap} = T_{out} + T_{in} = 29 + 29 = 58 \text{ ms} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Real-Time Implication:** If this process has a deadline of 50 ms, swapping alone **violates** the deadline. The system would either (a) increase RAM, (b) use SSD, or (c) lock the process in memory. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective Access Time with Swapping **Setup:** Memory access time $t_m = 100$ ns. Page fault rate $p = 0.001$ (1 in 1000 accesses). Page-fault service time $T_{pf} = 25$ ms. Find EAT. ### Step 1 — State the General EAT Formula $$ EAT = (1-p) \cdot t_m + p \cdot (t_m + T_{pf}) $$ ### Step 2 — Substitute $$ EAT = (1 - 0.001) \cdot 100 \text{ ns} + 0.001 \cdot (100 \text{ ns} + 25 \text{ ms}) $$ ### Step 3 — Convert Units Consistently Convert $t_m$ to ms: $t_m = 100 \text{ ns} = 0.0001 \text{ ms} = 10^{-4} \text{ ms}$ $$ EAT = 0.999 \cdot 10^{-4} \text{ ms} + 0.001 \cdot (10^{-4} + 25) \text{ ms} $$ ### Step 4 — Evaluate Each Term $$ EAT = 0.9999 \times 10^{-4} \text{ ms} + 0.001 \times 25.0001 \text{ ms} $$ $$ EAT \approx 9.999 \times 10^{-5} \text{ ms} + 0.0250001 \text{ ms} $$ $$ EAT \approx 0.0251 \text{ ms} = 25.1 \text{ \mu s} $$ ### Step 5 — Interpretation The page fault inflates the EAT by a factor of ~251× over pure RAM access. In RTOS terms, a 0.1% fault rate is **catastrophic** for hard deadlines. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Schedulability with Paging Penalty (Liu & Layland Extension) **Setup:** Three periodic real-time tasks under Rate Monotonic Scheduling: | Task | Period $T_i$ | Base WCET $C_i$ | Max page faults $f_i$ | Page-fault cost $P$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | $\tau_1$ | 20 ms | 4 ms | 1 | 2 ms | | $\tau_2$ | 50 ms | 10 ms | 2 | 2 ms | | $\tau_3$ | 100 ms | 20 ms | 3 | 2 ms | Test feasibility. ### Step 1 — Compute Effective Computation Time $$ C_i^{eff} = C_i + f_i \times P $$ $$ C_1^{eff} = 4 + 1 \times 2 = 6 \text{ ms} $$ $$ C_2^{eff} = 10 + 2 \times 2 = 14 \text{ ms} $$ $$ C_3^{eff} = 20 + 3 \times 2 = 26 \text{ ms} $$ ### Step 2 — Apply Liu & Layland Utilisation Bound for n = 3 $$ U_{bound} = 3 \times (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \times (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7798 $$ ### Step 3 — Compute Actual Utilisation $$ U_{actual} = \frac{C_1^{eff}}{T_1} + \frac{C_2^{eff}}{T_2} + \frac{C_3^{eff}}{T_3} $$ $$ U_{actual} = \frac{6}{20} + \frac{14}{50} + \frac{26}{100} $$ $$ U_{actual} = 0.30 + 0.28 + 0.26 = 0.84 $$ ### Step 4 — Conclusion $$ U_{actual} = 0.84 > U_{bound} = 0.78 $$ The **necessary condition fails**, so the task set is **not schedulable** under RMS when paging is considered. The system designer must either reduce page faults, lower $C_i$, or use a faster swap device. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Swapping Decision Table for RTOS | Memory Pressure | Process State | Hard RT Task? | Action | |---|---|---|---| | Low | Sleeping | Yes (locked) | **No swap** — page remains pinned | | Low | Sleeping | No (background) | Candidate for swap-out | | High | Ready | Yes | **Do not swap**; admit no new tasks | | High | Ready | No | Swap out lowest-priority background | | Critical | Any | Yes | **Drop new arrivals**; do not evict hard RT pages | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — RTOS Swap Operation Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Scheduler Dispatches Process] --> B{Process Pages
in RAM?} B -- Yes --> C[Execute Process] C --> D[Preempt or Complete] B -- No --> E[Page Fault Trap] E --> F[Select Victim Frame
via Page Replacement] F --> G[Write Victim Page
to Swap Space] G --> H[Read Required Page
from Swap Space] H --> I[Update Page Table] I --> C D --> J[Memory Free?
Check Watermark] J -- Below Minfree --> K[Activate Swapper] K --> L[Select Sleeping
Low-Priority Process] L --> M[Copy Entire Image
to Swap Device] M --> N[Update PCB with
Swap Location] N --> O[Release RAM Frames] J -- Above Minfree --> P[Continue Normally] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Memory Layout: RAM vs Swap Space ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph RAM[Primary Memory - RAM] R1[Kernel Code
Locked] R2[Hard RT Tasks
Pinned Pages] R3[Soft RT Tasks
Swappable] R4[Free Frame Pool] end subgraph SWAP[Secondary Storage - Swap Partition] S1[Swapped Process A] S2[Swapped Process B] S3[Free Swap Slots] end R3 -. Swap-Out .-> S1 S1 -. Swap-In .-> R3 R4 <--> R3 R1 -. Locked -.- R1 R2 -. Locked -.- R2 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Swapper Subsystem Internal Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD KM[Kernel Monitor Thread] --> WM[Watermark Checker] WM -->|Below Threshold| SS[Swap Scheduler] SS --> VS[Victim Selector LRU] VS --> SA[Swap Allocator] SA -->|Allocates Slot| IO[Disk I/O Driver] IO --> PT[Page Table Updater] PT --> LOG[Swap Log and Stats] PT --> KM WM -->|Above Threshold| IDLE[Idle] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram notes (per Mermaid safeguards):** > - Every node ID is alphanumeric and starts with a letter (`A`, `B`, `KM`, etc.). > - No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`) are used as node names. > - All labels inside double quotes are plain uppercase text — no bold/italic/HTML tags. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Q: Define swapping in an operating system. Why is it generally discouraged in hard real-time systems?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (1 Mark):** Swapping is a memory management technique in which the OS moves entire processes (process swap) or individual pages (page swap) between the main memory and a secondary storage area called the swap space, in order to free up RAM for other processes. > **Why discouraged in hard RTOS (2 Marks):** > - **Non-deterministic latency:** Disk I/O involves seek, rotation, and transfer times that are not bounded, violating hard real-time deadlines. > - **WCET inflation:** Worst-case execution time grows by the maximum page-fault service time, which may push a task past its deadline. > - **Priority inversion risk:** A high-priority real-time task may be blocked on a swap-in while a lower-priority task holds the swap device. > - Hence, hard real-time systems typically use **memory locking (`mlock`)** and **pinned pages** to prevent swap-outs. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q: Differentiate between process swapping and page swapping. Which is more suitable for an RTOS and why?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Process Swapping | Page Swapping | |---|---|---| | **Granularity** | Whole process image | Fixed-size pages (e.g., 4 KB) | | **I/O Volume** | Very large | Smaller, on demand | | **Predictability** | Poor (whole image must be reloaded) | Better (only faulted pages) | > **Suitability (2 Marks):** **Page swapping** is comparatively more suitable for RTOS because it allows **demand loading** — only the faulted page is fetched, reducing the average swap time. However, even page swapping is restricted for **hard real-time tasks** via page-locking. Process swapping is **unsuitable** for any hard RTOS because the entire image transfer time is unbearably large. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper] **Q: (a)** Explain the working of swapping in an operating system with a neat block diagram. Discuss its impact on real-time task scheduling. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A real-time system uses swapping with the following parameters: - Average RAM access time: $t_m = 200$ ns - Page fault rate: $p = 0.0005$ - Page fault service time: $T_{pf} = 20$ ms Compute the Effective Access Time (EAT) and comment on the result from an RTOS perspective. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Part (a) — Model Solution **Step 1 — Working of Swapping (4 Marks):** - The OS maintains a **watermark** of free frames; when the count drops below `minfree`, the swapper is triggered. - A **victim process** (preferably in SLEEPING state) is selected. - Its entire address space is copied to the **swap space** (Roll-out). - Later, when the process is scheduled, it is copied back (Roll-in) and execution resumes. - The **swapper**, **disk scheduler**, and **page-replacement policy** cooperate to perform these actions. **Step 2 — Impact on RT Scheduling (3 Marks):** - **WCET inflation:** Effective WCET becomes $C_i^{eff} = C_i + f_i \times T_{pf}$. - **Schedulability bound tightens:** Liu & Layland bound may be violated. - **Deadline miss risk** for hard real-time tasks if a page fault occurs at a critical instant. - Hence, **page locking** and **static memory allocation** are preferred. --- #### Part (b) — Model Solution **Step 1 — State EAT Formula** $$ EAT = (1-p) \cdot t_m + p \cdot (t_m + T_{pf}) $$ **Step 2 — Convert $t_m$ to consistent units** $$ t_m = 200 \text{ ns} = 200 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} = 2 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 0.0002 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute** $$ EAT = (1 - 0.0005) \times 0.0002 \text{ ms} + 0.0005 \times (0.0002 + 20) \text{ ms} $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate** $$ EAT = 0.9995 \times 0.0002 \text{ ms} + 0.0005 \times 20.0002 \text{ ms} $$ $$ EAT = 0.0001999 \text{ ms} + 0.0100001 \text{ ms} $$ $$ EAT = 0.0102 \text{ ms} = 10.2 \text{ \mu s} $$ **Step 5 — RTOS Comment** > [!IMPORTANT] > Although the EAT is only **10.2 µs** (a 51× slowdown vs pure RAM), in a hard RTOS this worst-case latency must be **added to WCET**. For tasks with deadlines of a few hundred microseconds, even this small worst-case penalty may be unacceptable. Therefore, **critical pages should be locked in memory** to eliminate this uncertainty. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q: (a)** With neat diagrams, explain the memory layout during process swapping. How is swap space managed in the kernel? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A process occupies **3 MB** in RAM. The swap device has $T_{seek} = 8$ ms, $T_{rot} = 4$ ms, transfer rate $R = 50$ MB/s. Compute the total round-trip swap time. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Part (a) — Model Solution **Memory Layout (4 Marks):** ``` +-----------------------------+ <-- Low Address | Kernel Code (Locked) | +-----------------------------+ | Hard RT Task Pages (Pinned) | +-----------------------------+ | Soft RT Task Pages (Swappable) | +-----------------------------+ | Free Frame Pool | +-----------------------------+ <-- High Address (RAM) || (swap boundary) +-----------------------------+ <-- Swap Space | Swapped-Out Process A | +-----------------------------+ | Swapped-Out Process B | +-----------------------------+ | Free Swap Slots | +-----------------------------+ ``` **Swap Space Management in Kernel (3 Marks):** - The kernel maintains a **swap map** (bit-map or array) recording which swap slots are free. - On swap-out, a free slot is allocated; on swap-in, it is freed. - The PCB stores the **swap location** (e.g., device number + block number) for each process. - The **page daemon** (or `kswapd`) periodically balances free pages. --- #### Part (b) — Model Solution **Step 1 — Compute Process Size in Bytes** $$ S = 3 \text{ MB} = 3 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 2 — Compute Transfer Time** $$ T_{transfer} = \frac{S}{R} = \frac{3 \times 10^6}{50 \times 10^6} = 0.06 \text{ s} = 60 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 3 — Compute One-Way Swap Time (Swap-Out)** $$ T_{out} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{transfer} = 8 + 4 + 60 = 72 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 4 — Swap-In Time (Symmetric)** $$ T_{in} = 72 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 5 — Total Round-Trip Swap Time** $$ T_{swap} = T_{out} + T_{in} = 72 + 72 = 144 \text{ ms} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Inference:** A 144 ms swap round-trip means **any hard real-time task with a sub-144 ms deadline is unsafe** if this process is swapped in/out. The designer should use an SSD (≈1 ms swap) or pin the process in RAM. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Swapping" Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting rotational latency:** Many students write $T = T_{seek} + \dfrac{S}{R}$ — this loses **1 mark**. > 2. **Not converting units:** Mixing ns with ms in EAT is a **fatal** error. Always convert to a single unit (ms or µs) before substitution. > 3. **Confusing swap-out and swap-in:** The round-trip is **twice** the one-way time. Marks are split for clarity. > 4. **Omitting the RTOS comment:** Pure numeric answers without a "real-time implication" sentence are treated as incomplete. Always add a concluding remark. > 5. **Using `|` in math within markdown tables:** This breaks the KTU PDF renderer. Use `\vert` or textual phrasing. > 6. **Treating EAT as constant:** In RTOS, **worst-case** page fault rate must be used, not average. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **Swapping** moves process/page images between **RAM** and **swap space** on disk. - [x] Two flavours: **Process Swapping (whole)** and **Page Swapping (on demand)**. - [x] In **hard RTOS**, swapping is **avoided**; critical pages are **locked/pinned**. - [x] Key timing components: **$T_{seek}$, $T_{rot}$, $T_{transfer}$**. - [x] **$T_{transfer} = S / R$** is the dominant component for large processes. - [x] **EAT formula:** $EAT = (1-p)t_m + p(t_m + T_{pf})$. - [x] Paging penalty **inflates WCET** and may **break schedulability** (Liu & Layland). - [x] Mitigations: `mlock()`, BBRAM, SSD, static allocation, MPU partitioning. - [x] **Watermarks** (`lotsfree`, `desfree`, `minfree`) drive swapper activation. - [x] Standard page size: **4 KB**; standard page-fault cost: **10–25 ms** (HDD), **0.1–1 ms** (SSD). - [x] In modern embedded RTOS, **swapping is often disabled** at kernel boot. - [x] Always state the **real-time implication** in numerical answers. - [x] Use **`\vert`** instead of `|` in markdown tables to avoid rendering issues. - [x] Round-trip swap time = **2 × one-way time** (symmetric assumption). ---

topics.title: Overlays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT741 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Overlays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Overlay** is a classical memory management technique in which a single program is logically partitioned into multiple self-contained code/data segments, and only the **currently required segment is resident in physical memory (RAM)** at any given moment, while the remaining segments are kept in secondary storage (typically a disk). When execution flow demands a segment that is not currently resident, a runtime mechanism called the **Overlay Manager (Overlay Supervisor)** swaps the in-memory segment out to disk and brings the required segment into the exact same memory region.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECPT741 – Module 4):** Overlays are studied as a *user-implemented virtualization of memory* — a precursor to modern techniques like paging, segmentation, and virtual memory. It is part of the "Memory Management in RTOS" learning unit.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **small kitchen with only one countertop and three recipe cards**: Soup, Pasta, and Cake. The countertop represents **physical RAM** (limited size). The recipe cards represent **program overlays** (stored in a drawer, i.e., secondary memory).

You cannot prepare all three recipes simultaneously because the countertop is too small. So, you:
1. Place the **Soup recipe** on the countertop (load Overlay 0).
2. Cook the soup, then put the card back in the drawer (swap out).
3. Bring the **Pasta recipe** onto the same countertop (load Overlay 1 into the *same memory region*).
4. Repeat for Cake (Overlay 2).

The **Overlay Manager** is like your hand — it knows *when* to exchange cards and *where* to place them on the countertop. The total recipe content is larger than the countertop, yet execution proceeds seamlessly.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Symbol / Unit | Typical Value (Historical Context) |
|---|---|---|
| Overlay segment size | $S_{ov}$ in **bytes** | $\le$ **64 KB** (typical in 16-bit Intel 8086 era) |
| Memory region shared | $M_{r}$ | Same base address reused |
| Overlay swap latency | $T_{swap}$ | **5 – 50 ms** (disk I/O bound) |
| Number of overlays in a program | $N$ | 2 to 16 (typical) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Overlays were the **only practical solution** before virtual memory was invented. They required the *programmer* — not the OS — to manually partition code and manage loading.

## 1.4 Why Overlays Are Relevant to RTOS

In **embedded/real-time systems** (e.g., automotive ECUs, industrial controllers, IoT devices), RAM may still be **scarce** (a few hundred KB to a few MB). Designers sometimes borrow the *overlay philosophy* — using **Memory Protection Units (MPU)** and **overlay regions** in linker scripts (e.g., ARM `SECTIONS` directives) to place mutually exclusive code in the same physical address range, effectively reducing flash/RAM footprint.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — Stepwise Logic

The overlay mechanism operates through a strict sequence governed by the Overlay Manager:

1. **Program Partitioning (Compile/Link Time)**
   The programmer analyzes the program's call graph and divides it into a **root segment** (always resident) and multiple **overlay segments** (mutually exclusive in time).
   - **Root (Level 0):** Always loaded. Contains the main routine, common subroutines, and the Overlay Manager itself.
   - **Overlays (Level 1, 2, ...):** Logical code units A, B, C, ... placed at the *same physical address range* but stored as *separate files* on disk.

2. **Overlay Tree Construction**
   A tree structure is built (often called the **Overlay Structure**). Two overlays at the *same level* of the tree are guaranteed to be **mutually exclusive** — they are never needed in memory at the same time.

3. **Loading Decision (Run Time)**
   When a function `F()` residing in Overlay B is called, the Overlay Manager checks whether Overlay B is currently in RAM.
   - If **yes**: Direct jump, no I/O.
   - If **no**: Save the currently resident overlay to disk (if modified), then read Overlay B from disk into the **Overlay Region**.

4. **Transparent Branch**
   The jump from caller to callee is indirect via a **trampoline** or **transfer vector** — a small jump table at a fixed address. The actual target address is patched at load time.

5. **Return Path**
   When the callee returns, control reverts to the caller in the root or a higher-level overlay. The return address is preserved on the stack as usual.

## 2.2 Overlay Tree Representation

A typical 2-level overlay structure:

```
            [ Root Segment ]      <-- Always in RAM
           /        |        \
   Overlay A   Overlay B   Overlay C   <-- Share the same memory region
       |           |             |
    Sub-A1      Sub-B1        Sub-C1   <-- Each occupies another shared region
    Sub-A2      Sub-B2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **height** of the overlay tree determines the **maximum nested overlay-swaps** possible at runtime. A deeper tree implies more I/O during deep function calls, increasing **worst-case execution time (WCET)** — a critical concern in hard real-time systems.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum resident memory | $M_{max} = S_{root} + \sum_{i=1}^{L} S_{level_i}$ where $L$ is number of distinct levels | Bytes |
| Program size vs. RAM | $S_{program} \le S_{disk}$ but $S_{program} \gg M_{RAM}$ | $S_{disk}$ = disk capacity |
| Mutual exclusion requirement | $\forall\, i \ne j: \; T_i \cap T_j = \emptyset$ where $T_i$ is execution time interval of overlay $i$ | Boolean invariant |
| Worst-case swap cost | $T_{WCET} = T_{compute} + N_{swaps} \cdot (T_{read} + T_{write})$ | ms |
| Overlay count bound | $N \le \dfrac{S_{disk}}{S_{overlay\_min}}$ | Integer |
| Memory savings | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{M_{RAM}}{S_{program}}$ | Fraction (0 to 1) |

> [!WARNING]
> In an **RTOS**, $T_{WCET}$ must be **bounded and provable**. Poorly designed overlays cause unbounded swap delays — a fatal flaw for hard real-time tasks.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Embedded firmware** for legacy microcontrollers (8051, early ARM7).
- **Bootloaders** that load successive firmware stages.
- **DSP applications** where different filter banks are time-multiplexed.
- **Modern ARM linker overlays** (used in automotive AUTOSAR, medical devices).
- **Historical desktop apps** (1980s) like *Lotus 1-2-3* and *dBASE* that exceeded 640 KB on MS-DOS.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Memory Savings Ratio

**Given:**
- Total program size: $S_{program}$ (bytes)
- Physical RAM reserved: $M_{RAM}$ (bytes)
- Root segment size: $S_{root}$ (bytes)
- Maximum number of overlays resident simultaneously: $k$ (an integer $\ge 1$)

**Step 1.** The RAM must accommodate the root plus all overlays that may be co-resident due to nested calls.

$$
M_{RAM} = S_{root} + \sum_{j=1}^{k} S_{overlay\_j}
$$

**Step 2.** The *memory savings* relative to having the *entire* program resident are:

$$
\eta = 1 - \frac{M_{RAM}}{S_{program}}
$$

**Step 3.** Substitute the expressions:

$$
\eta = 1 - \frac{S_{root} + \sum_{j=1}^{k} S_{overlay\_j}}{S_{program}}
$$

**Step 4.** Since $S_{program} = S_{root} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} S_{overlay\_i}$ where $N$ is the *total* overlay count, we obtain:

$$
\eta = 1 - \frac{S_{root} + \sum_{j=1}^{k} S_{overlay\_j}}{S_{root} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} S_{overlay\_i}}
$$

**Step 5.** For a uniform overlay size $S$ (i.e., $S_{overlay\_i} = S$ for all $i$):

$$
\eta = 1 - \frac{S_{root} + k \cdot S}{S_{root} + N \cdot S}
$$

**Step 6.** Simplify by dividing numerator and denominator by $S$:

$$
\eta = 1 - \frac{(S_{root}/S) + k}{(S_{root}/S) + N}
$$

**Interpretation:** As $N \to \infty$ (many overlays) with $k$ held constant, $\eta \to 1$, meaning **arbitrarily large programs can run in arbitrarily small RAM** — provided overlays are mutually exclusive.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem.** A program has $S_{program} = 512$ KB. It is divided into a root of 32 KB and 8 overlays of 60 KB each. Only 1 overlay is ever resident with the root. Compute the memory savings ratio.

**Solution.**

$$
S_{root} = 32 \text{ KB}, \quad k = 1, \quad N = 8, \quad S = 60 \text{ KB}
$$

$$
M_{RAM} = 32 + (1)(60) = 92 \text{ KB}
$$

$$
\eta = 1 - \frac{92}{512} = 1 - 0.1797 = 0.8203
$$

**Result.** Approximately **82% RAM savings** — a 512 KB program runs in just 92 KB of physical memory.

## 3.3 Derivation: WCET Impact in RTOS Context

For a hard real-time task that traverses $p$ overlay boundaries during a single execution:

$$
T_{WCET} = T_{compute} + p \cdot T_{swap}
$$

where

$$
T_{swap} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + \dfrac{S_{overlay}}{B_{disk}}
$$

- $T_{seek}$ = disk seek time (~5 ms for HDD)
- $T_{rot}$ = rotational latency (~4 ms for 7200 RPM)
- $B_{disk}$ = transfer bandwidth (~100 MB/s)
- $S_{overlay}$ = overlay size in bytes

For a 60 KB overlay on a typical HDD:

$$
T_{swap} \approx 5 + 4 + \frac{60 \times 1024}{100 \times 10^6} \approx 9.0006 \text{ ms}
$$

If $p = 10$ swaps per task, an additional **~90 ms** must be added to the deadline budget — a critical overhead.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Overlays | Paging | Segmentation | Virtual Memory |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Managed by | Programmer | OS (MMU) | OS (MMU) | OS (MMU) |
| Granularity | Whole function/module | Fixed page (4 KB) | Variable segment | Page or segment |
| Hardware support | **Not required** | MMU required | MMU required | MMU required |
| Real-time predictability | High (manual control) | Low (page faults) | Low | Very low |
| Memory savings | High (if exclusive) | High | Moderate | Highest |
| Complexity for programmer | High | Low | Low | Low |
| Use in RTOS today | Rare (linker overlays) | Common (with locked pages) | Rare | Common (with care) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Overlay Tree Structure (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    root["ROOT SEGMENT - Always Resident - main and OverlayManager"]
    ovA["OVERLAY A - Sort Module"]
    ovB["OVERLAY B - Search Module"]
    ovC["OVERLAY C - Report Module"]
    subA1["SUB-OVERLAY A1 - QuickSort"]
    subA2["SUB-OVERLAY A2 - MergeSort"]
    subB1["SUB-OVERLAY B1 - LinearSearch"]
    subB2["SUB-OVERLAY B2 - BinarySearch"]

    root --> ovA
    root --> ovB
    root --> ovC
    ovA --> subA1
    ovA --> subA2
    ovB --> subB1
    ovB --> subB2
```

## 4.2 Runtime Memory Layout (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph DISK["SECONDARY STORAGE - DISK"]
        dA["File: OVERLAY.A"]
        dB["File: OVERLAY.B"]
        dC["File: OVERLAY.C"]
    end

    subgraph RAM["PHYSICAL RAM"]
        rRoot["Address 0x0000 - ROOT - Always Loaded"]
        rOvRegion["Address 0x4000 - OVERLAY REGION - Reused"]
        rData["Address 0x8000 - Data and Stack"]
    end

    subgraph MGR["OVERLAY MANAGER - In Root"]
        m1["1. Check Current Overlay ID"]
        m2["2. If Target not loaded"]
        m3["3. Read Target from Disk"]
        m4["4. Place at Overlay Region"]
    end

    dA -.Swap.-> rOvRegion
    dB -.Swap.-> rOvRegion
    dC -.Swap.-> rOvRegion
    MGR --> rOvRegion
    MGR --> DISK
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow of an Overlay Call

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["Function Call in Root - e.g., sort_and_print"])
    check{"Is Target Overlay B already in RAM?"}
    jumpYes["Direct Branch to Overlay B - No I/O"]
    swapOut["Write currently resident overlay to disk if dirty"]
    readDisk["Read Overlay B from disk into Overlay Region"]
    patchJump["Patch Transfer Vector with new address"]
    jumpNo["Indirect Jump via Transfer Vector to Overlay B"]
    execute["Execute Overlay B code"]
    returnPath["Return to caller via stack - address in root preserved"]
    end(["Execution Resumes Normally"])

    start --> check
    check -- YES --> jumpYes --> execute
    check -- NO --> swapOut --> readDisk --> patchJump --> jumpNo --> execute
    execute --> returnPath --> end
```

## 4.4 Call Graph Partitioning Example (Block-Level)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph T0["Tree Level 0 - Always Resident"]
        T0a["main function"]
        T0b["Overlay Manager"]
        T0c["Common Utils"]
    end

    subgraph T1["Tree Level 1 - Shared Region R1"]
        T1a["Module A - 60 KB"]
        T1b["Module B - 60 KB"]
        T1c["Module C - 60 KB"]
    end

    subgraph T2["Tree Level 2 - Shared Region R2"]
        T2a["A1 - 30 KB"]
        T2b["A2 - 30 KB"]
        T2c["B1 - 30 KB"]
        T2d["B2 - 30 KB"]
    end

    T0a --> T1a
    T0a --> T1b
    T0a --> T1c
    T1a --> T2a
    T1a --> T2b
    T1b --> T2c
    T1b --> T2d
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Edges in the overlay tree represent *legal call relationships*. Two siblings at the same level (e.g., A and B) **must never call each other** — otherwise both would need to be resident simultaneously, violating the mutual-exclusion property.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define overlays in the context of memory management. Mention one advantage and one limitation.**

**Model Answer:**
An *overlay* is a memory management technique in which a program is divided into a root segment (always resident in RAM) and several overlay segments, only one of which resides in a designated memory region at any time. Non-resident overlays are kept in secondary storage and are loaded on demand by an Overlay Manager.

- **Advantage:** Allows programs larger than physical RAM to execute successfully.
- **Limitation:** The programmer must manually design the overlay tree, increasing software complexity and the risk of errors.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition, 1 mark advantage, 1 mark limitation)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is an overlay tree? Why must sibling nodes in an overlay tree be mutually exclusive in execution time?**

**Model Answer:**
An *overlay tree* is a hierarchical representation of a program's call structure, used to determine which code segments may share a common memory region. The root represents the always-resident segment, and children represent overlays that are mutually exclusive in time.

**Why mutual exclusion is required:** Sibling nodes share the *same physical memory region*. If two siblings executed concurrently, they would overwrite each other, causing a runtime fault. Hence, the programmer must ensure that during any interval of execution, at most one sibling is invoked.

*(3 marks: 1 mark tree definition, 2 marks mutual-exclusion reasoning)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of overlays with a neat diagram of the overlay tree. Describe the role of the Overlay Manager in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A program of size **800 KB** is structured as: root segment = 50 KB and **10 overlay segments each of 70 KB**. Only one overlay is resident at any time. Compute:
   (i) Total RAM required.
   (ii) Memory savings ratio.
   (iii) Worst-case execution overhead if 6 overlay swaps occur and each swap takes 12 ms on the storage device. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Conceptual Explanation**

The overlay technique is used when a program's size exceeds available physical memory. The program is split into:
- A **root segment** containing `main`, common libraries, and the **Overlay Manager**.
- Several **overlays** loaded into the same memory region on demand.

The **Overlay Manager** performs these duties:
1. Maintains a **transfer vector** (jump table) at a fixed address.
2. Intercepts calls to functions in non-resident overlays.
3. On demand, **swaps out** the current overlay (if modified) and **swaps in** the requested overlay from disk.
4. **Patches the transfer vector** with the new address and performs an indirect jump.
5. On return, control reverts to the root via the saved return address on the stack.

*Diagram: (Refer to Section 4.1 overlay tree.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**(i) Total RAM required:**

$$
M_{RAM} = S_{root} + 1 \cdot S_{overlay} = 50 + 70 = 120 \text{ KB}
$$

**(ii) Memory savings ratio:**

$$
\eta = 1 - \frac{M_{RAM}}{S_{program}} = 1 - \frac{120}{800} = 1 - 0.15 = 0.85
$$

Hence, **85% RAM savings**.

**(iii) Worst-case swap overhead:**

$$
T_{overhead} = 6 \times 12 \text{ ms} = 72 \text{ ms}
$$

This 72 ms must be added to the task's WCET budget.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **overlays** and **virtual memory** with respect to at least four parameters. State two situations where overlays are still preferred in modern embedded/RTOS design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a block diagram, illustrate the **runtime memory layout** of an overlay-based system. Explain the *transfer vector mechanism* used to make overlay calls transparent to the caller. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison Table**

| Parameter | Overlays | Virtual Memory |
|---|---|---|
| Granularity | Function/module level | Page level (typically 4 KB) |
| Implemented by | Programmer + linker | Operating System + MMU |
| Hardware dependency | None (pure software) | Requires MMU hardware |
| Predictability for RTOS | High (bounded swaps) | Low (page faults are non-deterministic) |
| Memory savings | Depends on manual partitioning | Automatic, demand-based |
| Complexity | Programmer burden | OS burden |

**Two modern scenarios where overlays are still preferred:**
1. **Bare-metal embedded firmware** (e.g., ARM Cortex-M0/M3 without MMU) where the system must run with a few hundred KB of flash.
2. **Bootloader stages** that load successive firmware images into the same flash region to minimize boot ROM size.

**(b) Runtime Memory Layout & Transfer Vector**

*Block diagram (refer to Section 4.2):* The root sits at a low fixed address; the overlay region sits at a higher address range. Disk holds all overlays as separate files.

**Transfer Vector Mechanism:**
The *transfer vector* is a small array of jump instructions at a fixed RAM address. Each entry corresponds to a function that belongs to an overlay. When a function is called, control first goes to its transfer vector entry, which jumps to the actual address (patched by the Overlay Manager). The caller does not need to know whether the function is in RAM or on disk — making the call **transparent**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing overlays with paging** — overlays are *programmer-controlled*, paging is *OS-controlled*. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for this.
> 2. **Forgetting the mutual-exclusion rule** when drawing the overlay tree — always state it explicitly.
> 3. **Missing the WCET implication** in RTOS questions — every swap adds bounded latency that must be budgeted.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert units** in numerical problems (KB vs bytes, ms vs µs).
> 5. **Omitting the transfer vector** in transparency explanations — it is the heart of the mechanism.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Overlay = partition a program** so that only a portion is resident in RAM at a time.
- The **root segment** is always loaded; **overlays** share a common memory region.
- **Mutual exclusion** between siblings in the overlay tree is the *fundamental invariant*.
- The **Overlay Manager** handles swapping, transfer vectors, and disk I/O.
- **Memory savings formula:** $\eta = 1 - M_{RAM}/S_{program}$.
- **WCET in RTOS:** $T_{WCET} = T_{compute} + p \cdot T_{swap}$ — every swap adds bounded delay.
- Overlays are **software-only** — no MMU needed — making them ideal for low-end embedded MCUs.
- **Modern analogues:** ARM linker overlay regions, AUTOSAR ECU firmware layering, bootloader stage loading.
- **Difference from virtual memory:** overlays are *manual and predictable*; virtual memory is *automatic but non-deterministic*.
- **Typical sizes:** Overlays of 30–70 KB, root of 20–50 KB, tree depth 2–3.
- **Avoid in hard real-time** if swap latency cannot be bounded (e.g., on HDDs with bad sectors).
- **Best used when:** the call graph has clear partitioning, mutual exclusion is easy to prove, and storage is reliable (Flash/ROM, not magnetic disk).
- **Exam tip:** Always draw the overlay tree, label the root, and mention the *transfer vector* at least once in a long-answer question.
- **Common confusion:** Overlays are *not* the same as function libraries loaded dynamically (DLLs) — DLLs use a *different* memory region, while overlays *share* the same region.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block/Page Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT741 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Block/Page Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Block Management — Formal Definition

**Block Management** in a Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) refers to a **fixed-size memory partitioning scheme** in which the RTOS kernel pre-divides a contiguous memory region (a *memory pool* or *partition*) into equal-sized chunks called **memory blocks**. Every allocation request is satisfied with one whole block, and the kernel tracks each block using a **Memory Control Block (MCB)** metadata structure.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology:
- A **partition** is a memory pool declared at system initialization.
- A **block** is the smallest allocatable unit inside a partition.
- An **MCB** is a kernel-side descriptor containing the block's address, size, state (free/allocated), and linkage to the next control block.

## 1.2 Page Management — Formal Definition

**Page Management** is a **variable-size memory allocation scheme** in which the memory pool is split into **pages** whose sizes may differ (or are fixed at a power-of-two *page size*). The kernel maintains a free-list (or bitmap) of pages and dynamically carves out contiguous regions on demand. Pages can be **coalesced** with adjacent free pages to form larger contiguous regions, mitigating external fragmentation.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT] **Intuition Check — "The Hotel Room Analogy"**
>
> Imagine a hotel:
> - **Block Management** is like a hotel that **only rents identical single rooms** of fixed size. The receptionist has a fixed board of room numbers. Check-in is O(1) — instant. But if you bring luggage for two people, one room is wasted (internal fragmentation). If you need three rooms, they are scattered across floors and not physically adjacent (potential external fragmentation, but each block is uniform).
> - **Page Management** is like a hotel that has a few **suite sizes** (small, medium, large). You get a size that fits best, and the front desk can merge adjacent vacant suites to fit a VIP later. It is more flexible but check-in takes longer (best-fit search).

## 1.4 Standard KTU Metrics (must memorize)

- **Block size** is chosen at partition creation (e.g., **16, 32, 64, 128, 256, 512, 1024 bytes** — typical powers of two).
- **Page size** in RTOS is usually **aligned to processor word size** (e.g., **4, 8, 16, or 32 bytes**) or a larger page like **4 KB**.
- **Fragmentation metric**: a critical KTU interview question — see Section 2.

> [!NOTE] **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PECPT741 — Module 4)**
> Block/Page Management is a core topic under *Memory Management in RTOS*. It is frequently tested as a 14-mark Part B question in the End Semester Examination (ESE) and as a 3-mark short question in the Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — Block Management

A block-based memory pool is built as follows at kernel initialization:

1. The kernel reserves a contiguous **memory pool** of size `N × B`, where `N` = number of blocks, `B` = block size in bytes.
2. The kernel pre-allocates an **array of N Memory Control Blocks (MCBs)** in kernel space.
3. Each MCB contains:
   - `block_address` — pointer to the start of the data region
   - `state` — FREE or ALLOCATED (1 bit is sufficient; often stored as a flag byte)
   - `block_size` — usually redundant (all blocks equal), but kept for debugging
   - `next_free` — pointer for free-list linking (only valid when FREE)
4. A **free-list head pointer** points to the first available MCB.
5. **Allocation** = `remove head from free-list, mark ALLOCATED, return data address`. This is **O(1)**.
6. **Deallocation** = `mark FREE, insert at head of free-list`. Also **O(1)**.

## 2.2 Operational Architecture — Page Management

A page-based manager uses one of two tracking structures:

- **Free-list of free pages** (each with a `next` pointer) — common in μC/OS-II, VxWorks.
- **Bitmap of pages** — common when page count is large and predictable.

Operational steps:

1. The kernel maintains a sorted or unsorted free-list of free pages with their `size` and `address`.
2. **Allocation** algorithm picks a region from the free-list:
   - **First-Fit**: first region large enough → O(n) worst case
   - **Best-Fit**: smallest region that satisfies the request → O(n) but minimizes leftover waste
   - **Worst-Fit**: largest region → O(n)
3. The chosen region is **split** if it is larger than the request, returning the remainder to the free-list.
4. **Deallocation** marks the page FREE and **coalesces** with adjacent free pages if they are physically contiguous (buddy system variant).

## 2.3 Why It Matters in Real-Time Systems

> [!IMPORTANT] **The "Why" of Block/Page Management in RTOS**
> In RTOS, memory allocation must be **bounded in execution time** (a hard real-time requirement). `malloc()` / `free()` in standard C libraries are **non-deterministic** — they can scan large free-lists, fragment memory, and trigger the OS page fault handler. RTOS therefore provides **predictable, bounded-time allocators** like block and page managers so the worst-case latency (`WCET`) of an allocation call can be proven.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Total pool size | $P = N \times B$ | bytes; $N$ = blocks, $B$ = block size |
| 2 | Number of blocks | $N = \lfloor P / B \rfloor$ | integer |
| 3 | MCB memory overhead | $O_{MCB} = \dfrac{N \times S_{MCB}}{P} \times 100\%$ | percent; $S_{MCB}$ = size of one MCB (typically 8–16 bytes) |
| 4 | Internal fragmentation (block) | $I_{frag} = \dfrac{B - R_{req}}{B} \times 100\%$ | percent; $R_{req}$ = requested size |
| 5 | External fragmentation (page) | $E_{frag} = 1 - \dfrac{L_{largest\_free}}{F_{total}}$ | dimensionless; ratio of largest free block to total free memory |
| 6 | Mean allocation time (block) | $T_{alloc} = T_{pop\_list} \approx O(1)$ | cycles — **deterministic** |
| 7 | Mean allocation time (page, first-fit) | $T_{alloc} = k \cdot T_{cmp} + T_{split}$ | $k$ = list length searched; **worst-case bounded** by total pages |
| 8 | Memory utilization | $U = \dfrac{\sum R_{i}}{\sum B_{i}} \times 100\%$ | percent; $\sum B_i$ = total allocated blocks × $B$ |
| 9 | Buddy coalescing — page address check | $(a_1 \oplus a_2) = 0 \Rightarrow \text{mergeable}$ | bitwise XOR; $a_1, a_2$ are page base addresses |
| 10 | Block pool address alignment | $A_{block} \equiv 0 \pmod{B}$ | required for cache-line friendliness on ARM/RISC-V |

> **Engineering utility:** Block management is used in **automotive ECUs (AUTOSAR)**, **avionics (ARINC 653 partitioned memory)**, and **medical device firmware** where WCET guarantees are mandatory. Page management appears in **VxWorks `memPartAlloc`**, **FreeRTOS `pvPortMalloc` (heap_4 / heap_5)**, and **RTEMS region manager**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Memory Overhead of a Block Pool

**Problem statement:** A KTU 2024 ESE question gives you pool size and block size and asks the *percentage memory consumed by the MCB array alone*.

**Given:**
- Total pool size $P = 4096$ bytes
- Block size $B = 64$ bytes
- Each MCB occupies $S_{MCB} = 12$ bytes

**Step 1 — Compute the number of blocks.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{P}{B} \right\rfloor \\[4pt]
  &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{4096}{64} \right\rfloor \\[4pt]
  &= 64 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

This means 64 user-data blocks exist in the pool.

**Step 2 — Compute the memory consumed by all MCBs.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{MCB} &= N \times S_{MCB} \\[4pt]
        &= 64 \times 12 \\[4pt]
        &= 768 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Express as a percentage of the user pool.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_{MCB} &= \dfrac{M_{MCB}}{P} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
        &= \dfrac{768}{4096} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
        &= 0.1875 \times 100\% \\[4pt]
        &= 18.75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Nearly **one-fifth of the pool is consumed by metadata**, leaving only $4096 - 768 = 3328$ bytes (~81.25%) for user data. This is why experienced RTOS designers pick the *largest* block size that satisfies the worst-case request — overhead is amortized across fewer, larger blocks.

> [!NOTE] **Key insight for KTU exams:** If you double the block size (from 64 → 128 bytes), $N$ halves to 32, MCB memory halves to 384 bytes, and overhead drops from 18.75% to 9.375%. **Block size is the dominant lever for overhead control.**

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Internal Fragmentation Worst-Case

**Problem statement:** A task requests $R_{req} = 17$ bytes from a block pool of $B = 32$ bytes. What is the worst-case internal fragmentation?

**Step 1 — Compute the wasted bytes inside the allocated block.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= B - R_{req} \\[4pt]
  &= 32 - 17 \\[4pt]
  &= 15 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Express as a percentage of the block.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{frag} &= \dfrac{W}{B} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
         &= \dfrac{15}{32} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
         &= 46.875\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the *worst-case* request size that maximizes this waste.**

The worst case is the **smallest non-zero request**, $R_{req} = 1$ byte, giving:

$$
I_{frag,\ max} = \dfrac{32 - 1}{32} \times 100\% = 96.875\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Examiner's Pattern:** When asked "What is the worst-case internal fragmentation of a 32-byte block pool?" always answer with the **1-byte request scenario**, not an average.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — External Fragmentation After a Sequence of Page Operations

**Problem statement:** A page manager has 100 KB of free memory in 5 free pages of sizes 4, 8, 12, 16, 60 KB. Compute external fragmentation.

**Step 1 — Total free memory.**

$$
F_{total} = 4 + 8 + 12 + 16 + 60 = 100 \text{ KB}
$$

**Step 2 — Largest free contiguous page.**

$$
L_{largest\_free} = 60 \text{ KB}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute external fragmentation metric.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{frag} &= 1 - \dfrac{L_{largest\_free}}{F_{total}} \\[4pt]
         &= 1 - \dfrac{60}{100} \\[4pt]
         &= 0.40 = 40\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Even though 100 KB is "free," a request of 70 KB would **fail**, because no single page is large enough. This is the classical external fragmentation problem solved (partially) by **coalescing** in page managers.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Buddy Coalescing (Page Manager)

**Problem statement:** Two free pages with base addresses `0x00002000` and `0x00003000` exist. Page size = 4 KB. Will they be merged?

**Step 1 — Write base addresses in binary (lower 13 bits matter, since 4 KB = 2¹²):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_1 &= \text{0x2000} = 0\ldots 0\,0010\,0000\,0000\,0000_2 \\
a_2 &= \text{0x3000} = 0\ldots 0\,0011\,0000\,0000\,0000_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the bitwise XOR of the two addresses.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_1 \oplus a_2 &= 0x2000 \oplus 0x3000 \\
&= 0x1000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — In a binary buddy system, two pages can merge only if the address XOR equals exactly the page size.**

$$
a_1 \oplus a_2 = 0x1000 = 4096 = \text{page size}
$$

**Conclusion:** They are **buddies and will be coalesced** into a single 8 KB page spanning `0x00002000` to `0x00003FFF`.

> [!NOTE] This is the exact criterion used in the Linux kernel's page allocator and in many RTOS kernels for fast O(1) coalescing.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Choose Block vs. Page

| Decision Criterion | Block Management | Page Management |
|--------------------|:----------------:|:---------------:|
| Allocation time guarantee | **Excellent (O(1))** | Good (bounded worst-case) |
| Internal fragmentation | **High (worst-case 96%+)** | Low (only rounding to page) |
| External fragmentation | Low (uniform blocks) | **High (without coalescing)** |
| Memory overhead (MCB) | Scales with $N$ | Scales with free pages |
| Variable-size requests | **Poor fit** | Excellent fit |
| Best use case | Fixed-size messages, control blocks | Mixed-size buffers, dynamic strings |
| Example RTOS API | `OSMemGet()`, `OSMemPut()` (μC/OS) | `memPartAlloc()` (VxWorks), `pvPortMalloc()` (FreeRTOS heap_4) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Memory Pool Architecture — Block Management

The following Mermaid diagram shows the canonical layout of a block-based memory pool with a free-list of MCBs and the contiguous data region.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Memory Pool Start 0x1000] --> B[MCB Array 64 entries]
    B --> C[MCB 1 state FREE next ptr 2]
    B --> D[MCB 2 state ALLOCATED next NULL]
    B --> E[MCB 3 state FREE next ptr 4]
    B --> F[MCB 4 state ALLOCATED next NULL]
    B --> G[MCB 5 state FREE next NULL]
    C --> H[Data Block 1 64 bytes]
    D --> I[Data Block 2 64 bytes]
    E --> J[Data Block 3 64 bytes]
    F --> K[Data Block 4 64 bytes]
    G --> L[Data Block 5 64 bytes]
    H --> M[Unused Memory]
```

> **Reading guide:** The MCB array lives at the *head* of the pool. Each MCB points to its corresponding data block. Free MCBs are linked via `next_free` pointers (1 → 3 → 5), and allocated MCBs have `next_free = NULL` (2, 4).

## 4.2 Memory Control Block (MCB) Internal Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MCB[Memory Control Block MCB] --> F1[Field block_address 4 bytes]
    MCB --> F2[Field block_size 2 bytes]
    MCB --> F3[Field state FREE OR ALLOCATED 1 byte]
    MCB --> F4[Field next_free 4 bytes]
    MCB --> F5[Field task_owner 2 bytes optional]
    MCB --> F6[Padding 1 byte alignment]
```

> **Field-by-field:** `block_address` points to user data. `state` is a single-byte flag. `next_free` is meaningful only when `state = FREE`. `task_owner` is an optional TCB pointer for debugging (μC/OS-II adds this).

## 4.3 Allocation Flow — Block Manager (O(1))

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start Allocation Request size R] --> S1{Is free-list empty?}
    S1 -- Yes --> S2[Return NULL allocation failure]
    S1 -- No --> S3[Pop head MCB from free-list]
    S3 --> S4[Set MCB state to ALLOCATED]
    S4 --> S5[Update head pointer to next_free]
    S5 --> S6[Return block_address to caller]
    S6 --> END1[End O of 1 time]
```

> **Why O(1)?** The free-list head is a global pointer. Popping it requires only two memory reads and two writes, regardless of pool size.

## 4.4 Page Manager — Free-List with Coalescing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Free Page 4 KB at 0x2000] -->|next| P1[Free Page 8 KB at 0x4000]
    P1 -->|next| P2[Free Page 12 KB at 0x6000]
    P2 -->|next| P3[Free Page 16 KB at 0x9000]
    P3 -->|next| P4[Free Page 60 KB at 0xB000]
    P4 -->|next| P5[NULL end of list]
    P0 -.buddy check.-> P2
```

> **Reading guide:** Each free page is a node in a singly linked list. After every deallocation, the kernel walks the list and attempts buddy-coalescing. Here, P0 and P2 might be buddies (their address XOR equals 4 KB), so they merge into an 8 KB free region.

## 4.5 High-Level Block/Page Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[Application requests memory size R] --> D1{Is R bounded and fixed for all tasks?}
    D1 -- Yes --> D2[Use Block Management O of 1 deterministic]
    D1 -- No --> D2B{Is variable size with WCET bound critical?}
    D2B -- Yes --> D3[Use Page Management with coalescing]
    D2B -- No --> D4[Use Standard Library malloc is acceptable]
    D2 --> DONE[High predictability]
    D3 --> DONE2[Good flexibility]
    D4 --> DONE3[Low predictability]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define a Memory Control Block (MCB). List any four fields stored in it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Memory Control Block (MCB)** is a kernel-maintained descriptor (metadata structure) that the RTOS uses to track every memory block in a partition. It is the *bookkeeping record* the kernel reads/writes whenever a block is allocated or freed.

Four standard fields of an MCB:

1. **`block_address`** — pointer to the start of the user data region of the block (4 bytes on a 32-bit MCU).
2. **`block_size`** — size of the data block in bytes (e.g., 2 bytes for sizes up to 65535).
3. **`state`** — a flag indicating whether the block is **FREE** or **ALLOCATED** (1 byte; sometimes a single bit packed with other flags).
4. **`next_free`** — pointer to the next free MCB; used to chain all free blocks into a free-list for O(1) allocation (4 bytes).

*(Optional 5th field for 1 extra mark: `task_owner` — TCB pointer of the task that currently owns the block, used for leak detection.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between internal fragmentation and external fragmentation in the context of RTOS memory management.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Internal Fragmentation | External Fragmentation |
|--------|------------------------|------------------------|
| **Definition** | Wasted space *inside* an allocated memory block because the request size is smaller than the block size. | Wasted free memory *outside* allocated regions — free memory exists in non-contiguous chunks that cannot satisfy a large request. |
| **Cause** | Fixed-size blocks (e.g., 64-byte block used for a 17-byte request → 47 bytes wasted). | Repeated allocation/deallocation of variable-size pages leaves small gaps between used pages. |
| **Where it occurs** | Block management (partitioned memory). | Page management (heap) without coalescing. |
| **Solution** | Use multiple block sizes (tiered partitions) or page management. | Coalescing, compaction, or buddy system. |
| **Example metric** | $I_{frag} = \frac{B - R}{B}$ | $E_{frag} = 1 - \frac{L_{largest\_free}}{F_{total}}$ |

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **block management scheme** used in RTOS memory management. Describe the structure of a Memory Control Block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A memory pool of size **8 KB** is created with a block size of **128 bytes**. The MCB occupies **16 bytes**. Compute:
  (i) The number of blocks. **(2 marks)**
  (ii) The total memory consumed by MCBs. **(2 marks)**
  (iii) The percentage overhead. **(3 marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Q-A (a):**

**Block Management Scheme — Explanation**

Block management is a fixed-size memory partitioning strategy used in RTOS to achieve **deterministic, bounded-time allocation**. At system initialization, the kernel pre-divides a contiguous memory region (the *partition* or *pool*) into N equal-sized blocks, each of size B bytes. For every block, the kernel allocates a corresponding **Memory Control Block (MCB)** that stores metadata.

**Structure of an MCB:**

| Field | Size (typical, 32-bit MCU) | Purpose |
|-------|---------------------------|---------|
| `block_address` | 4 bytes | Pointer to data region |
| `block_size` | 2 bytes | Block size in bytes |
| `state` | 1 byte | FREE or ALLOCATED |
| `next_free` | 4 bytes | Free-list linkage |
| Padding | 1 byte | Alignment |

**Operations:**
- **Allocation (`OSMemGet`)**: Pop the head MCB from the free-list, set `state = ALLOCATED`, return `block_address`. This is O(1) — the *primary advantage* of block management.
- **Deallocation (`OSMemPut`)**: Set `state = FREE`, insert the MCB at the head of the free-list. Also O(1).

**Advantages:** deterministic timing, low external fragmentation, simple implementation.
**Disadvantages:** high internal fragmentation if request sizes vary widely.

(Refer to diagrams in Section 4.1 and 4.2 of these notes.)

---

**Model Answer for Q-A (b):**

**Given:** $P = 8\,\text{KB} = 8192$ bytes, $B = 128$ bytes, $S_{MCB} = 16$ bytes.

**(i) Number of blocks:**

$$
N = \left\lfloor \dfrac{P}{B} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{8192}{128} \right\rfloor = 64 \text{ blocks}
$$

**(ii) Total MCB memory consumed:**

$$
M_{MCB} = N \times S_{MCB} = 64 \times 16 = 1024 \text{ bytes}
$$

**(iii) Percentage overhead:**

$$
O_{MCB} = \dfrac{1024}{8192} \times 100\% = 12.5\%
$$

**Final results:** 64 blocks; 1024 bytes consumed by MCBs; overhead = **12.5%**.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the **page management scheme** for RTOS memory. Compare **first-fit**, **best-fit**, and **worst-fit** allocation strategies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A page manager has the following free memory: 5 KB, 12 KB, 7 KB, 25 KB, and 3 KB. If a request arrives for 10 KB, determine the block returned by:
  (i) First-Fit **(2 marks)**
  (ii) Best-Fit **(2 marks)**
  (iii) Worst-Fit **(3 marks)**

Also calculate the external fragmentation in each case after allocation. **(3 marks distributed)**

---

**Model Answer for Q-B (a):**

**Page Management Scheme:**

Page management is a *variable-size* memory allocation scheme in which the memory pool is divided into free pages, each of which may have a different size. The kernel maintains metadata (free-list or bitmap) for each free page. Allocation is performed by searching the free-list, selecting a region, and *splitting* it if it is larger than the request. Deallocation marks the page FREE and may *coalesce* it with adjacent free pages to form a larger contiguous region (used in **buddy systems**).

**Comparison of allocation strategies:**

| Strategy | Selection Rule | Pros | Cons |
|----------|---------------|------|------|
| **First-Fit** | First free block whose size ≥ request | Fastest in practice, good locality | May leave small unusable holes at the head |
| **Best-Fit** | Smallest free block that satisfies the request | Minimizes leftover waste per allocation | Slower search; tends to produce many tiny unusable fragments over time |
| **Worst-Fit** | Largest free block | Leaves a sizable remainder that may serve future requests | Wastes large blocks on small requests; slowest recovery from fragmentation |

**RTOS preference:** *First-fit* or *best-fit* are most common because they have **bounded worst-case behavior**, critical for hard real-time systems.

---

**Model Answer for Q-B (b):**

**Given free list (unsorted):** 5, 12, 7, 25, 3 KB. **Request:** 10 KB.

**(i) First-Fit:** First block ≥ 10 KB → **12 KB block** returned. Remaining list: 5, 7, 25, 3 KB + 2 KB split (12 − 10).

**(ii) Best-Fit:** Smallest block ≥ 10 KB → **12 KB block** returned. (Same as first-fit in this specific instance.) Remaining: 5, 7, 25, 3 + 2 KB.

**(iii) Worst-Fit:** Largest block → **25 KB block** returned. Remaining: 5, 12, 7, 3 + 15 KB split (25 − 10).

**External fragmentation after each allocation:**

**Case (i) & (ii) — First-fit / Best-fit:** Largest free block now = **15 KB** (the 25 KB block, untouched). Total free = 5 + 7 + 25 + 3 + 2 = 42 KB. 

$$
E_{frag} = 1 - \dfrac{15}{42} = 0.6428 = 64.28\%
$$

**Case (iii) — Worst-fit:** Largest free block = 15 KB (the new split from 25). Total free = 5 + 12 + 7 + 3 + 15 = 42 KB.

$$
E_{frag} = 1 - \dfrac{15}{42} = 64.28\%
$$

(In this particular problem the metric coincides, but **worst-fit is generally worse** for fragmentation metrics over long workloads.)

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing MCB overhead with usable memory.** Students often forget that the MCB array itself consumes pool memory; if asked for "effective user data," subtract MCB memory first.
> 2. **Forgetting the floor function** in $N = \lfloor P/B \rfloor$ — if $P$ is not an exact multiple of $B$, leftover bytes are wasted (use the `floor` operation explicitly).
> 3. **Worst-case internal fragmentation** is computed with the *smallest non-zero request* (1 byte), not with the average request.
> 4. **External fragmentation metric** is the ratio of *largest single free region* to *total free memory*, **not** the count of free regions.
> 5. **Buddy system coalescing** requires the *address XOR* of two pages to **equal the page size**, not just any common property.
> 6. Always state the **time complexity** of allocation when describing a scheme — KTU examiners explicitly award marks for mentioning "O(1)" or "deterministic."

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Block management** = fixed-size blocks, **O(1) allocation**, **O(1) deallocation**, **high internal fragmentation**, **low external fragmentation**. Best for uniform messages / control structures.
- **Page management** = variable-size pages, **O(n) worst-case allocation**, **coalescing** mitigates external fragmentation, lower internal fragmentation. Best for mixed-size dynamic buffers.
- **MCB** always has: `block_address`, `block_size`, `state`, `next_free`. Optional: `task_owner`.
- **Number of blocks** $N = \lfloor P / B \rfloor$.
- **MCB overhead %** $= \frac{N \times S_{MCB}}{P} \times 100\%$.
- **Internal fragmentation worst case** $= \frac{B - 1}{B} \times 100\%$ (for a 1-byte request).
- **External fragmentation** $= 1 - \frac{L_{largest\_free}}{F_{total}}$.
- **First-fit** is the most common RTOS choice for predictable behavior.
- **Buddy coalescing** rule: addresses XOR = page size → mergeable.
- **RTOS preference:** block/page managers over `malloc()` because of **WCET guarantees** — a hard real-time requirement.
- **Real-world APIs:** `OSMemGet/OSMemPut` (μC/OS-II), `memPartAlloc` (VxWorks), `pvPortMalloc` (FreeRTOS heap_4/heap_5).
- **Diagrams to draw in exam:** (1) MCB array + data block layout, (2) free-list linking, (3) allocation/deallocation flowchart.
- **One-line exam answer:** *"Block management offers deterministic O(1) allocation at the cost of internal fragmentation; page management offers flexibility at the cost of bounded-but-slower allocation and external fragmentation."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Replacement Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT741 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Replacement Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Real Time Operating Systems (RTOS)**, **Replacement Algorithms** refer to the deterministic, time-bounded strategies employed by the memory management subsystem (and the task scheduler) to evict existing entities from a finite resource pool when a new entity must be admitted and the pool is full. The entities being replaced may be **memory pages**, **cache lines**, **I/O buffers**, or in a wider sense, **lower-priority tasks** during preemption.

Formally, a replacement algorithm is a function $R : S \rightarrow E$, where $S$ is the current state of the resource pool and $E$ is the element to be evicted, subject to the constraint that the **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)** of $R$ is bounded by a small constant (typically $O(1)$ for hard real-time systems).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECPT741 - Module 4):**
> Replacement algorithms are studied in RTOS under the dual lens of **memory hierarchy management** (page replacement) and **resource arbitration** (task/buffer replacement). The deterministic behaviour of these algorithms directly impacts **task schedulability analysis** and **deadline miss probability**.

## 1.2 Classification of Replacement Algorithms

| Class | Examples | RTOS Suitability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Recency-Based** | LRU, MRU, Clock | Moderate (requires timestamp tracking) |
| **Frequency-Based** | LFU, MFU | Low (poor deterministic guarantees) |
| **Queue-Based** | FIFO, Second Chance | **High** (constant time, low overhead) |
| **Optimal (Theoretical)** | Belady's OPT | Reference benchmark only |
| **Working-Set Based** | WSClock, Aging | High for soft real-time |
| **Priority-Aware** | Priority Page Replacement | **Highest** for hard real-time |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Desk Drawer"

Imagine your study desk has only **3 drawers**, and you are preparing for **5 different subjects** (Physics, Chemistry, Maths, English, Biology). Every time you need notes for a subject, you must put them in a drawer. When all 3 drawers are full and you need notes for a new subject, you must **remove (replace)** one set of notes to make room.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Drawer = Cache / Physical Memory Frame**
> **Subject Notes = Memory Page / Cache Line**
> **The Rule you use to decide which drawer to empty = Replacement Algorithm**

- **FIFO** → "Always remove the notes that were placed in the drawer *first* (oldest at the bottom)."
- **LRU** → "Always remove the notes that I haven't opened in the *longest time*."
- **LFU** → "Always remove the notes I open the *least number of times*."
- **OPT (Belady's)** → "Magically know the future — remove the notes I will need *farthest in the future*."

The **goal** of every algorithm is the same: **minimise the number of times you have to fetch notes from the cupboard (disk/main memory)** because fetching is slow. Each such fetch is called a **Page Fault**.

## 1.4 Why Replacement Algorithms Matter in RTOS

In a real-time system, every millisecond counts. A poor choice of replacement algorithm can cause:

1. **Unbounded Page Faults** → unbounded task execution time → **deadline miss**.
2. **Cache Pollution** → working set of a critical task is evicted just before its deadline.
3. **Priority Inversion** → low-priority task evicts pages needed by a high-priority task.
4. **Thrashing** → system spends more time swapping than executing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** The performance of a replacement algorithm is measured by its **Hit Ratio ($H$)** and **Page Fault Rate ($P_f$)**:
> $$H = \frac{\text{Number of Hits}}{\text{Total Memory References}} \quad ; \quad P_f = 1 - H$$
> In RTOS, we additionally care about the **Worst-Case Page Fault Bound** — a hard guarantee that no task will suffer more than $K$ page faults in any interval of length $T$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Page Replacement Problem — Formal Setup

Given:
- A process has $n$ pages in its logical address space: $P = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n\}$.
- Physical memory (RAM) can hold at most $m$ frames at any instant, where $m < n$.
- A **page reference string** $R = (r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_k)$ indicates the sequence of pages accessed by the process.
- At time $t$, if $r_t$ is already in a frame → **HIT**.
- If $r_t$ is not in any frame → **PAGE FAULT** → the OS must load $r_t$ into a frame.
  - If a **free frame exists** → use it (no replacement).
  - If **all $m$ frames are full** → invoke the **replacement algorithm** to choose a victim frame, write it to disk (if dirty), and load $r_t$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Page Fault Service Time** $T_{pf}$ has two components:
> $$T_{pf} = T_{disk\_read} + T_{disk\_write} \cdot (1 - \text{clean})$$
> In RTOS, $T_{pf}$ is typically **4 to 8 orders of magnitude** larger than $T_{RAM\_access}$, hence the criticality of choosing the right algorithm.

## 2.2 Algorithm 1 — FIFO (First-In First-Out)

### Operational Logic
- Maintain a **queue** of size $m$ containing the page numbers currently in memory.
- On a page fault with full memory → evict the page at the **head** of the queue (the oldest one).
- Load the new page at the **tail** of the queue.

### Why and How
- **Why:** Simplest possible algorithm. Requires only a queue and a pointer — $O(1)$ decision time, ideal for hard real-time.
- **How:** Implemented using a circular array of size $m$ with head and tail indices. No per-page metadata needed beyond the queue position.

### Advantages & Disadvantages
| Pros | Cons |
| :--- | :--- |
| Constant $O(1)$ time | Suffers from **Belady's Anomaly** |
| Minimal hardware support | Ignores actual usage pattern |
| Deterministic — RTOS friendly | High page fault rate on common patterns |

## 2.3 Algorithm 2 — Optimal Page Replacement (OPT / Belady's Algorithm)

### Operational Logic
- On a page fault, evict the page that will **not be used for the longest time in the future**.
- Requires **clairvoyant knowledge** of the future reference string.

### Why and How
- **Why:** Establishes the **theoretical lower bound** on page faults for any given reference string.
- **How:** Used as a benchmark. For any algorithm $A$, $\text{Faults}(A) \geq \text{Faults}(OPT)$. The ratio $\text{Faults}(A) / \text{Faults}(OPT)$ is a measure of $A$'s sub-optimality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> OPT is **not implementable in practice** because the OS cannot predict the future, but it is the gold standard against which LRU, FIFO, etc. are compared in academic analysis.

## 2.4 Algorithm 3 — LRU (Least Recently Used)

### Operational Logic
- On a page fault, evict the page whose **last access time** is the smallest (i.e., used longest ago).
- Intuitively: *"If you haven't used it in a while, you probably won't need it soon."*

### Implementations
1. **Counter-Based:** Each page entry stores a logical timestamp. On access, update the timestamp. Eviction = minimum timestamp. Requires $O(n)$ search.
2. **Stack-Based:** Maintain a doubly-linked list; on access, move page to top. Evict from bottom. $O(1)$ with hash map.
3. **Matrix Method (Hardware):** $n \times n$ bit matrix; on access of page $i$, set row $i$ to all 1s and column $i$ to all 0s. The page with the smallest binary row is the LRU.

### LRU and Belady's Anomaly
**Stack Property:** LRU is a **stack algorithm**. The set of pages in memory after $k$ faults using $m$ frames is a subset of the pages in memory using $m+1$ frames. Consequently, LRU **does NOT suffer from Belady's Anomaly**.

## 2.5 Algorithm 4 — LFU (Least Frequently Used)

### Operational Logic
- Each page has a **reference counter**.
- On a page fault, evict the page with the **lowest counter** (ties broken by recency).

### Why and How
- **Why:** Captures long-term usage intensity rather than recency.
- **How:** Aging is often added to prevent stale high counts from persisting:
  $$C_{new}(i) = C_{old}(i) \cdot \alpha + 1$$ where $0 < \alpha < 1$ is the aging factor.

## 2.6 Algorithm 5 — MFU (Most Frequently Used)

### Operational Logic
- Evict the page with the **highest** reference counter.
- **Rationale:** The page with the lowest counter has probably just been brought in and will be needed.

> [!NOTE]
> MFU is rarely used in practice. It is primarily of academic interest in OS textbooks.

## 2.7 Algorithm 6 — Clock Algorithm (Second Chance)

### Operational Logic
- Pages arranged in a **circular buffer**.
- Each page has a **reference bit** $R \in \{0, 1\}$ set by hardware on every access.
- A **clock hand** rotates. On eviction:
  - If $R = 1$ → set $R = 0$, advance hand (give a "second chance").
  - If $R = 0$ → evict this page.

### Why and How
- **Why:** Approximates LRU at $O(1)$ cost per page fault — extremely RTOS friendly.
- **How:** A single bit per page and one pointer. Worst-case eviction time = one full rotation $\rightarrow$ bounded.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Enhanced Clock (NRU — Not Recently Used):** Uses two bits $(R, M)$ where $M$ is the modified (dirty) bit, yielding 4 classes with the eviction priority order:
> $$\text{Class 0 } (R=0, M=0) \rightarrow \text{Class 1 } (R=0, M=1) \rightarrow \text{Class 2 } (R=1, M=0) \rightarrow \text{Class 3 } (R=1, M=1)$$

## 2.8 Belady's Anomaly

### Definition
For some algorithms (notably **FIFO**), **increasing the number of memory frames can INCREASE the number of page faults.**

### Classic Counter-Example
Reference string: $1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$

| Frames $m$ | Faults (FIFO) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 3 | 9 |
| 4 | **10** ← More frames, more faults! |

### Condition for Anomaly
A replacement algorithm is anomaly-free (i.e., satisfies the **stack property**) if and only if the set of pages in memory is a function of $m$ alone and is **monotonically non-decreasing** with $m$.

## 2.9 Working Set Model

### Definition
The **Working Set $W(t, \Delta)$** of a process at time $t$ is the set of pages referenced in the time window $(t - \Delta, t)$:
$$W(t, \Delta) = \{ p_i \mid \exists \, t' \in (t - \Delta, t) \text{ such that } r_{t'} = p_i \}$$

### Working Set Replacement Policy
- Maintain $|W(t, \Delta)| \leq m$.
- On a page fault, evict any page **NOT** in the current working set.
- If all pages are in the working set, evict the **oldest** one.

> [!NOTE]
> $\Delta$ is called the **working set window**. Choosing $\Delta$ too small causes thrashing; too large wastes memory.

## 2.10 Thrashing

### Definition
**Thrashing** occurs when a system spends more time swapping pages than executing instructions. It happens when:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} |W_i(t, \Delta)| > m_{total}$$
i.e., the **total working sets of all processes exceed total physical memory**.

### Prevention in RTOS
1. **Working Set Paging** — admit a process only if $|W| \leq m_{available}$.
2. **Page Fault Frequency (PFF) Control** — if PFF rises above threshold, suspend a process.
3. **Reservation** — hard reserve frames for high-priority real-time tasks.

## 2.11 KTU High-Yield Formula & Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Constraint / Domain |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $m$ | Number of physical frames | $m \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| $n$ | Number of pages in process | $n > m$ typically |
| $R$ | Reference string length | $R \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| $H$ | Hit ratio | $0 \leq H \leq 1$ |
| $P_f$ | Page fault rate | $0 \leq P_f \leq 1$ |
| $T_{pf}$ | Page fault service time | $\approx 10^6 \times T_{RAM}$ |
| $\Delta$ | Working set window | $\Delta \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| $\alpha$ | Aging factor (LFU) | $0 < \alpha < 1$ |
| $C_{OPT}$ | Optimal page fault count | $\min$ possible |
| $C_A$ | Algorithm $A$'s fault count | $C_A \geq C_{OPT}$ |
| $S$ | Sub-optimality ratio $C_A / C_{OPT}$ | $S \geq 1$ |

### Key Performance Equation
$$\text{Effective Access Time (EAT)} = (1 - p) \cdot T_{mem} + p \cdot T_{pf}$$
where $p$ is the page fault rate. In RTOS, we additionally bound:
$$\text{EAT}_{worst} = T_{mem} + p_{max} \cdot T_{pf}^{max} \leq T_{deadline} - T_{compute}$$
